topics.title: Indian Energy Scenario
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Indian Energy Scenario

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Indian Energy Scenario** refers to the comprehensive study of India's energy resources, their availability, distribution, consumption patterns, conversion technologies, policy framework, and the long-term strategic planning required to meet the nation's developmental energy demands. It encompasses the quantitative analysis of primary energy reserves, installed power generation capacity, sector-wise end-use consumption, per capita energy usage, and the degree of self-sufficiency versus import dependence.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERAT637 - Module 1):**
> "An overview of the present Indian energy scenario — classification of energy resources, reserves and supply, energy demand, energy consumption pattern, national energy policy, planning and strategy for development, energy security, and the role of renewable and non-renewable sources in meeting India's growing power sector requirements."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **giant household** — a family called India — with **1.4 billion members**. Just like any large family, this household must:

1. **Decide what to cook with** (coal, gas, oil, sunlight, water, wind, nuclear fuel).
2. **Measure how much is in the kitchen pantry** (reserves and resources).
3. **Track how hungry everyone is** (per capita energy consumption).
4. **Decide what to buy from the market vs. what to grow at home** (import dependence vs. domestic production).
5. **Plan meals for the next 25 years** (national energy planning).

In this analogy:
- The **kitchen pantry** = Domestic primary energy reserves.
- **Buying from the market** = Importing crude oil and LNG.
- **Cooking gas and electric stove** = Conversion infrastructure (refineries, power plants).
- **The family members** = Industrial, agricultural, domestic, and transport sectors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this topic matters for KTU:**
> - It is a **frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU Energy Systems (PERAT637).
> - Questions typically ask for classification, statistics, and policy analysis.
> - Numerical data must be cited **approximately**, as exact values change yearly.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally used by the **Ministry of Power (MoP)**, **Central Electricity Authority (CEA)**, and **BP Statistical Review of World Energy**:

- **Primary Energy Supply (PES)** — measured in **Mtoe (Million Tonnes of Oil Equivalent)**.
- **Installed Capacity** — measured in **GW (Gigawatt = $10^9$ W)** or **MW (Megawatt = $10^6$ W)**.
- **Per Capita Energy Consumption** — measured in **kWh/person/year** or **toe/person/year**.
- **Energy Intensity** — measured in **toe per million USD of GDP** ($\text{toe/M\$}$).
- **Standard Conversion Factor:** $1 \text{ Mtoe} = 41.868 \text{ TJ} = 11.63 \text{ TWh}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **India's Standout Macro-Indicator:**
> - **Installed Power Capacity (2024):** ~$\mathbf{430\ GW}$
> - **Per Capita Electricity Consumption:** ~$\mathbf{1,300\ kWh/year}$
> - **World Average Per Capita Consumption:** ~$\mathbf{3,300\ kWh/year}$
> - **Population Share of World:** ~$\mathbf{17.7\%}$
> - **Energy Consumption Share of World:** ~$\mathbf{6\%}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Energy Resources

Indian energy resources are classified along multiple axes. The KTU syllabus expects a clear two-tier classification.

### A. Based on Renewability

| Category | Definition | Examples in India |
|---|---|---|
| **Non-Renewable (Conventional)** | Finite stock; depleted with use | Coal, Lignite, Crude Oil, Natural Gas, Nuclear (U-235) |
| **Renewable (Non-Conventional)** | Inexhaustible on human timescale | Solar, Wind, Hydro (up to 25 MW), Biomass, Tidal, Geothermal, Small Hydro |

### B. Based on Availability and Use

| Category | Definition | Indian Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Energy** | Found in nature; used directly or after conversion | Coal, Crude Oil, Sunlight, Wind |
| **Secondary Energy** | Derived from primary sources after conversion | Electricity, Petrol, Diesel, LPG, Hydrogen |

### C. Based on Commercial vs. Non-Commercial

- **Commercial Energy:** Sold in the market — coal, oil, gas, electricity.
- **Non-Commercial Energy:** Traditional fuels — firewood, agricultural residue, cow-dung cakes.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** About **25–30%** of India's total primary energy still comes from non-commercial biomass (rural households). This makes India's commercial energy statistics understate actual consumption.

## 2.2 India's Primary Energy Reserves

| Resource | Estimated Reserve (Approx.) | Lifespan at Current Production |
|---|---|---|
| **Coal** | $\mathbf{\sim 360\ billion\ tonnes}$ | $\sim 150$ years |
| **Crude Oil** | $\mathbf{\sim 650\ million\ tonnes}$ | $\sim 15\text{–}20$ years |
| **Natural Gas** | $\mathbf{\sim 1.4\ trillion\ m^3}$ | $\sim 30$ years |
| **Thorium** | $\mathbf{\sim 963,000\ tonnes}$ (world's largest) | $\sim 100,000+$ years |
| **Uranium** | $\mathbf{\sim 80,000\ tonnes}$ | $\sim 50$ years |
| **Hydropower Potential** | $\mathbf{\sim 145\ GW}$ (economic) | Renewable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> India sits on the **second-largest coal reserves in the world** (after the USA in extractable terms historically) and the **largest thorium deposits globally**, but is heavily import-dependent for **crude oil (~85%)** and **natural gas (~50%)**.

## 2.3 Installed Power Capacity — Sector-Wise Breakdown

The Indian power sector is dominated by the **thermal (coal + gas + diesel)** segment, with **renewables** rapidly catching up.

| Source | Approx. Share (2024) |
|---|---|
| **Thermal (Coal + Gas + Oil)** | $\mathbf{\sim 50\%}$ |
| **Renewable (Solar + Wind + Biomass)** | $\mathbf{\sim 33\%}$ |
| **Hydro (Large)** | $\mathbf{\sim 11\%}$ |
| **Nuclear** | $\mathbf{\sim 2\%}$ |
| **Total Installed** | $\mathbf{\sim 430\ GW}$ |

## 2.4 Sector-Wise Electricity Consumption

| End-Use Sector | Approx. Share |
|---|---|
| **Industrial** | $\mathbf{\sim 42\%}$ |
| **Domestic (Residential)** | $\mathbf{\sim 25\%}$ |
| **Agricultural** | $\mathbf{\sim 18\%}$ |
| **Commercial** | $\mathbf{\sim 9\%}$ |
| **Others (Traction, Public Lighting)** | $\mathbf{\sim 6\%}$ |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use the formulas below for any numerical problem that appears in the KTU examination. The values are the most commonly tested.

| # | Formula / Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Per Capita Consumption} = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy Consumed}}{\text{Total Population}}$ | Used to compare nations |
| 2 | $\text{Energy Intensity} = \dfrac{\text{Primary Energy Supply (toe)}}{\text{GDP (Million USD)}}$ | Indicates economic efficiency |
| 3 | $\text{Plant Load Factor (PLF)} = \dfrac{\text{Energy Actually Generated (kWh)}}{\text{Energy that could be generated at rated capacity}} \times 100$ | Measures plant efficiency |
| 4 | $\text{Plant Availability Factor (PAF)} = \dfrac{\text{Hours Plant is Available}}{\text{Total Hours in Period}} \times 100$ | Measures reliability |
| 5 | $\text{Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF)} = \dfrac{\text{Energy Generated (kWh)}}{\text{Installed Capacity (kW)} \times 8760\ \text{h}} \times 100$ | Standard for solar / wind plants |
| 6 | $1\ \text{Mtoe} = 41.868\ \text{TJ} = 11.63\ \text{TWh}$ | Energy unit conversion |
| 7 | $\text{Carbon Intensity} = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2\ \text{Emissions (kg)}}{\text{Energy Consumed (toe)}}$ | Used in environmental policy |
| 8 | $\text{Self-Sufficiency Ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Domestic Production}}{\text{Total Consumption}} \times 100\%$ | Energy security metric |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Table Integrity Rule:** Note that all absolute values and vertical delimiters in the table above use $\vert$ syntax or spelled-out expressions to avoid breaking the table parser.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power System Planners** (CEA, POSOCO) use PLF and PAF to decide plant additions.
- **Energy Policy Analysts** use per-capita consumption and energy intensity to set **NDC (Nationally Determined Contributions)** under the Paris Agreement.
- **Project Financiers** use CUF and PAF to compute **Levelized Cost of Electricity (LCOE)** for solar / wind tenders.
- **National Security Planners** use the **Self-Sufficiency Ratio** to assess **energy import vulnerability**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations and Worked Numerical Examples

### 3.1.1 Worked Problem 1 — Per Capita Electricity Consumption

**Problem:** India has a population of **1.4 billion** and an annual electricity generation of **$\mathbf{1{,}800\ TWh}$**. Find the per capita consumption. State the significance.

**Step 1:** Convert total generation to a common unit.
$$1{,}800\ \text{TWh} = 1{,}800 \times 10^9\ \text{kWh} = 1.8 \times 10^{12}\ \text{kWh}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the per capita formula.
$$\text{Per Capita Consumption} = \dfrac{\text{Total Energy}}{\text{Population}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the numerical values.
$$\text{Per Capita Consumption} = \dfrac{1.8 \times 10^{12}\ \text{kWh}}{1.4 \times 10^9} = 1{,}285.7\ \text{kWh/person/year}$$

**Step 4:** Interpret.
$$\boxed{\text{Per Capita Electricity Consumption} \approx 1{,}286\ \text{kWh/person/year}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Significance for KTU answer:** India's value of $\sim 1{,}286\ \text{kWh}$ is **less than half the world average** ($\sim 3{,}300\ \text{kWh}$), indicating significant unmet demand and growth potential — a frequently asked **"Discuss the implications"** question.

---

### 3.1.2 Worked Problem 2 — Plant Load Factor (PLF)

**Problem:** A 500 MW thermal power plant generated **$\mathbf{2{,}628\ GWh}$** in one year. Calculate its **Plant Load Factor**.

**Step 1:** Maximum possible generation in one year.
$$E_{\max} = \text{Rated Capacity} \times \text{Hours in Year} = 500 \times 10^6\ \text{W} \times 8{,}760\ \text{h}$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate.
$$E_{\max} = 4.38 \times 10^{12}\ \text{Wh} = 4{,}380\ \text{GWh}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the PLF formula.
$$\text{PLF} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Generation}}{E_{\max}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4:** Substitute.
$$\text{PLF} = \dfrac{2{,}628}{4{,}380} \times 100\%$$

**Step 5:** Compute.
$$\boxed{\text{PLF} = 60.0\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Indian Benchmark:** The national average PLF for coal-based thermal plants typically lies in the **60%–70%** range. Plants below 50% indicate poor health; plants above 80% indicate excellent operation.

---

### 3.1.3 Worked Problem 3 — Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF) for a Solar Plant

**Problem:** A **100 MW** solar PV plant generated **$\mathbf{180\ GWh}$** in a year. Calculate its **CUF**.

**Step 1:** Maximum possible generation.
$$E_{\max} = 100 \times 10^6\ \text{W} \times 8{,}760\ \text{h} = 8.76 \times 10^{11}\ \text{Wh} = 876\ \text{GWh}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the CUF formula.
$$\text{CUF} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Generation}}{E_{\max}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.
$$\text{CUF} = \dfrac{180}{876} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4:** Compute.
$$\boxed{\text{CUF} = 20.55\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solar CUF Interpretation:** A solar CUF of 20–25% is **typical and expected** because solar irradiance is available only 4–6 peak sunshine hours per day on average. This is **not a sign of plant failure** — unlike thermal PLF, where below 60% indicates inefficiency.

---

### 3.1.4 Worked Problem 4 — Energy Intensity Computation

**Problem:** India's GDP in a given year is **$\mathbf{\$3.7\ trillion\ USD}$** and primary energy supply is **$\mathbf{900\ Mtoe}$**. Compute the energy intensity.

**Step 1:** Convert GDP to million USD.
$$\text{GDP} = 3.7 \times 10^{12}\ \text{USD} = 3.7 \times 10^6\ \text{Million USD}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the formula.
$$\text{Energy Intensity} = \dfrac{\text{Primary Energy Supply (toe)}}{\text{GDP (Million USD)}}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute.
$$\text{Energy Intensity} = \dfrac{900 \times 10^6\ \text{toe}}{3.7 \times 10^6\ \text{M\$} }$$

**Step 4:** Simplify.
$$\boxed{\text{Energy Intensity} = 243.2\ \text{toe/M\$}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** India's energy intensity is **higher** than the global average ($\sim 165\ \text{toe/M\$}$). This indicates a **less energy-efficient economy**, but it is also **decreasing over time** as India modernizes.

---

### 3.1.5 Worked Problem 5 — Self-Sufficiency Ratio

**Problem:** India produces **$\mathbf{30\ Mtoe}$** of crude oil annually but consumes **$\mathbf{260\ Mtoe}$**. Find the self-sufficiency ratio.

**Step 1:** Apply the formula.
$$\text{SSR} = \dfrac{\text{Domestic Production}}{\text{Total Consumption}} \times 100\%$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.
$$\text{SSR} = \dfrac{30}{260} \times 100\%$$

**Step 3:** Compute.
$$\boxed{\text{SSR} = 11.5\%}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strategic Implication:** With an SSR of only ~12% in crude oil, India is the **world's third-largest oil importer**. This drives the strategic push toward **renewables, electric mobility, and biofuels** under the **Atmanirbhar Bharat** energy initiative.

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Dimension | Coal | Oil | Natural Gas | Hydro | Solar / Wind | Nuclear |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Reserves Sufficiency** | High ($\sim 150$ yr) | Low ($\sim 15$ yr) | Moderate ($\sim 30$ yr) | Renewable | Inexhaustible | High (Th) |
| **Generation Cost (₹/kWh)** | $3.0\text{–}4.5$ | N/A (transport) | $4.0\text{–}5.0$ | $3.0\text{–}5.0$ | $2.0\text{–}3.5$ | $4.0\text{–}6.0$ |
| **CO₂ Emissions (g/kWh)** | $\sim 900$ | $\sim 700$ | $\sim 450$ | $\sim 4$ | $\sim 20$ | $\sim 12$ |
| **Capacity Utilization** | $60\text{–}70\%$ | — | $60\text{–}80\%$ | $35\text{–}45\%$ | $15\text{–}25\%$ | $80\text{–}90\%$ |
| **Land Footprint** | Medium | N/A | Low | High (reservoir) | Very High | Low |
| **Import Dependence** | Low | Very High ($\sim 85\%$) | High ($\sim 50\%$) | None | None | None |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper, Module 1]`
**Define per capita energy consumption. Why is it considered a key indicator of a nation's development?**

**Model Answer:**

Per capita energy consumption is the **average energy used per person per year**, computed as:

$$\text{Per Capita Consumption} = \dfrac{\text{Total Primary Energy Consumed}}{\text{Total Population}}$$

It is measured in **kWh/year/person** (for electricity) or **toe/year/person** (for total primary energy). It is a development indicator because:

1. Higher industrial output requires more energy per worker.
2. Modern lifestyles (AC, refrigeration, transport) demand more residential energy.
3. India's value ($\sim 1{,}286\ \text{kWh}$) is far below the world average ($\sim 3{,}300\ \text{kWh}$), reflecting unmet developmental needs.
4. It is a direct proxy for **standard of living** and **industrial maturity**.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]`
**Distinguish between primary and secondary energy, with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Primary Energy | Secondary Energy |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Energy found in nature in its raw form | Energy derived after conversion of a primary form |
| **Examples** | Coal, Crude Oil, Sunlight, Uranium, Wind | Electricity, Petrol, LPG, Hydrogen, Diesel |
| **Usability** | Often not directly usable | Directly usable by end-consumer |

**Example pair 1:** Crude Oil (primary) → Petrol (secondary)
**Example pair 2:** Coal (primary) → Electricity (secondary)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]`

**Q.A (a) [7 Marks]:** Discuss the classification of India's energy resources with suitable examples. Explain why India is described as a "coal-rich but oil-poor" nation.

**Q.A (b) [7 Marks]:** With the help of a table, present the sector-wise installed power capacity in India and identify the dominant sector. State two reasons for the increasing share of renewables.

---

**Q.A (a) Model Solution:**

India's energy resources are classified as:

1. **Conventional (Non-Renewable):** Coal, Lignite, Crude Oil, Natural Gas, Nuclear (U-235).
2. **Non-Conventional (Renewable):** Solar, Wind, Small Hydro, Biomass, Tidal, Geothermal.
3. **Commercial vs. Non-Commercial:** Commercial = sold in market; Non-Commercial = firewood, dung.
4. **Primary vs. Secondary:** Primary = raw form; Secondary = converted form.

**Why "coal-rich but oil-poor":**

- India holds $\sim 360$ billion tonnes of coal reserves — among the **largest globally**.
- Self-sufficiency in coal: $\sim 95\%$ — meets domestic demand internally.
- Crude oil reserves: $\sim 650$ million tonnes — only $\sim 0.3\%$ of world reserves.
- Self-sufficiency in crude oil: $\sim 12\%$ — **88% of crude oil is imported**.
- Natural gas self-sufficiency: $\sim 50\%$ — rest imported as LNG.
- Therefore, India is structurally a **coal-dominant, oil-deficient** economy.

---

**Q.A (b) Model Solution:**

| Source | Approx. Capacity (GW) | Approx. Share |
|---|---|---|
| Coal + Lignite Thermal | $\sim 215$ | $\sim 50\%$ |
| Solar | $\sim 85$ | $\sim 20\%$ |
| Wind | $\sim 47$ | $\sim 11\%$ |
| Large Hydro | $\sim 47$ | $\sim 11\%$ |
| Biomass + Bagasse | $\sim 10$ | $\sim 2\%$ |
| Gas + Diesel | $\sim 25$ | $\sim 6\%$ |
| Nuclear | $\sim 8$ | $\sim 2\%$ |
| **Total** | $\mathbf{\sim 430}$ | $\mathbf{100\%}$ |

**Dominant sector:** Thermal (coal-based), at roughly **50% of installed capacity**.

**Reasons for rising renewable share:**

1. **Falling costs** — Solar tariff dropped from ₹17/kWh (2010) to ₹2/kWh (2024), making it cheaper than new coal.
2. **Policy push** — National Solar Mission target of 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030, plus PLI scheme for solar manufacturing.
3. **Climate commitments** — Panchamrit pledges at COP26: 500 GW non-fossil, 50% renewable share, net-zero by 2070.
4. **Energy security** — Reduced dependence on imported coal, oil, and gas.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 1]`

**Q.B (a) [7 Marks]:** With neat sketches, explain the energy flow from primary source to end-use sectors. Discuss the importance of energy conservation in the Indian context.

**Q.B (b) [7 Marks]:** Define the terms Plant Load Factor, Plant Availability Factor, and Capacity Utilization Factor. A 200 MW thermal plant generates 1,200 GWh in a year. Calculate the PLF and comment on its operational efficiency.

---

**Q.B (a) Model Solution:**

**Energy flow path (sketch in words):**
**Primary sources** (Coal, Oil, Gas, Uranium, Sun, Wind, Water) → **Conversion** (Thermal PP, Hydro PP, Nuclear PP, Solar PV, Refinery) → **Secondary carriers** (Electricity, Petrol, Diesel, LPG, Hydrogen) → **End-use sectors** (Industrial, Domestic, Agricultural, Commercial, Transport).

Refer to **Section 4.1 Block Diagram** for the complete schematic.

**Importance of energy conservation in India:**

1. **Per capita consumption is low** ($\sim 1{,}286\ \text{kWh}$ vs. world average $3{,}300\ \text{kWh}$), so wastage directly impacts growth.
2. **High import dependence** for oil ($\sim 88\%$) and gas ($\sim 50\%$) drains foreign exchange reserves.
3. **Energy Intensity** of $\sim 243\ \text{toe/M\$}$ is high, indicating wasteful industrial processes.
4. **Emission reduction** — conservation reduces GHG emissions, helping meet COP26 commitments.
5. **Cost reduction** for industries and households.
6. **The Energy Conservation Act 2001** mandates **BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency)** star ratings and **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme for industries.

---

**Q.B (b) Model Solution:**

**Definitions:**

1. **Plant Load Factor (PLF):**
$$\text{PLF} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Energy Generated (kWh)}}{\text{Rated Capacity (kW)} \times 8{,}760\ \text{h}} \times 100\%$$

2. **Plant Availability Factor (PAF):**
$$\text{PAF} = \dfrac{\text{Hours Plant is Available for Generation}}{\text{Total Hours in Period}} \times 100\%$$

3. **Capacity Utilization Factor (CUF):**
$$\text{CUF} = \dfrac{\text{Energy Generated (kWh)}}{\text{Installed Capacity (kW)} \times 8{,}760\ \text{h}} \times 100\%$$

(PLF and CUF use the same formula; PAF uses *hours available* not *energy generated*.)

**Calculation:**

Given: Capacity = 200 MW = $200 \times 10^6\ \text{W}$. Generation = 1,200 GWh = $1.2 \times 10^{12}\ \text{Wh}$.

$$\text{Maximum possible generation} = 200 \times 10^6 \times 8{,}760 = 1.752 \times 10^{12}\ \text{Wh} = 1{,}752\ \text{GWh}$$

$$\text{PLF} = \dfrac{1{,}200}{1{,}752} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{PLF} = 68.49\%}$$

**Comment on operational efficiency:**

A PLF of **68.49%** is **above the national average** for coal-based plants (typically 60–65%). This indicates **good operational efficiency**, proper maintenance scheduling, and adequate fuel supply. Plants below 50% are considered technically inefficient.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Confusing PLF with CUF:** PLF is typically for thermal plants; CUF is for renewables. Students write one formula for the other and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the 8,760 hour factor:** The denominator in PLF, PAF, and CUF is always $8{,}760$ hours (for a non-leap year). Missing this is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Using wrong units in per capita calculation:** The numerator and denominator must be in **consistent units** (e.g., kWh in numerator, persons in denominator). Mismatched units = 1-mark deduction.
> 4. **Outdated statistics:** Using pre-2020 installed-capacity numbers in a 2024 exam answer is a 1-mark deduction. Use **approximate current values** ($\sim 430$ GW total).
> 5. **Skipping the policy mention:** A "Discuss" question without naming **NEP 2007, BEE, PAT scheme, or COP26 pledges** loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **No diagram in long answers:** 14-mark questions **always expect at least one schematic**. Missing diagram = 1–2 marks lost.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Indian Energy Scenario = a holistic study of India's energy resources, supply, demand, consumption, and policy.
- **Classification axes:** Renewable vs. Non-Renewable; Primary vs. Secondary; Commercial vs. Non-Commercial.
- **India's largest primary energy reserve:** Coal (~$360$ billion tonnes).
- **Largest thorium reserves:** India has $\sim 25\%$ of global thorium.
- **Self-sufficiency ratios:** Coal $\sim 95\%$, Oil $\sim 12\%$, Gas $\sim 50\%$.
- **Installed power capacity (2024):** $\sim 430$ GW; dominant = thermal ($\sim 50\%$).
- **Per capita electricity consumption:** $\sim 1{,}286$ kWh/year (India) vs. $\sim 3{,}300$ kWh/year (world).
- **Energy intensity:** $\sim 243$ toe/M\$ — higher than world average.
- **Critical formulas:** PLF, PAF, CUF, Per Capita Consumption, Energy Intensity, SSR.
- **Key policies to mention in answers:** National Energy Policy, NEP 2007, BEE star labelling, PAT scheme, UJALA, SAUBHAGYA, PM-KUSUM, National Solar Mission, COP26 Panchamrit, Net-Zero 2070.
- **Key institutions:** NITI Aayog, MoP, MNRE, MoPNG, DAE, CEA, CERC, POSOCO, BEE.
- **National targets:** 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030; 50% renewable share in installed capacity by 2030; Net-Zero by 2070.
- **End-use sectors (descending share):** Industrial → Domestic → Agricultural → Commercial → Others.
- **Always include a diagram** in 14-mark answers — block flow or sector pie chart.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: World Energy Scenario
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** World Energy Scenario

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **World Energy Scenario** refers to the comprehensive quantitative and qualitative snapshot of global primary energy supply, demand, distribution across regions and sectors, resource reserves, consumption patterns, energy mix, per-capita usage, and the projected transition pathways from fossil-fuel-dominated systems toward sustainable, low-carbon energy architectures.

It is studied under three primary pillars:

1. **Energy Resources** — the *stock* available on Earth (coal, oil, gas, uranium, hydro, solar, wind, biomass, geothermal, tidal, etc.).
2. **Energy Consumption** — the *flow* of energy actually utilized by end-users (industry, transport, residential, commercial, agriculture).
3. **Energy Reserves & Reserves-to-Production (R/P) Ratio** — the *duration* for which known reserves can sustain current production rates.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Classification (KTU 2024 Highlight):**
> - **Primary Energy** — Energy found in nature that has *not* been converted (coal, crude oil, sunlight, wind).
> - **Secondary Energy** — Derived from primary sources after conversion (electricity, hydrogen, gasoline, diesel).
> - **Commercial Energy** — Sold commercially (coal, oil, gas, electricity).
> - **Non-Commercial Energy** — Traditional fuels (firewood, agricultural waste, cow-dung).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Earth as a Giant Household"

Imagine Planet Earth as **one big household of ~8 billion members** who all need *food* to survive and *electricity* to work. This "food" is called **energy**.

- The **pantry** (stock) holds coal, oil, gas, uranium, and sunlight.
- The **kitchen** (conversion plants) cooks raw fuel into electricity, gasoline, heat.
- The **dining table** (consumption sectors) feeds **Industry, Transport, Buildings, and Agriculture**.
- The **monthly grocery bill** is the *per-capita energy consumption*.

Different "households" (countries) eat very differently:
- **USA** — a heavy meat-and-AC consumer (high per-capita).
- **Europe** — moderate, efficiency-conscious.
- **China** — rapid growth, shifting from coal-heavy to cleaner mix.
- **India** — growing middle class, still biomass-influenced.
- **Sub-Saharan Africa** — under-fed, mostly traditional biomass.

> [!TIP]
> **Why study the World Energy Scenario?** Because every engineering decision — a turbine design, a building HVAC system, an EV powertrain — is downstream of the global energy story. Without context, you cannot judge whether your technology helps or harms the transition.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Industry-Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard energy units you must memorize for KTU examinations:
> - **1 Tonne of Oil Equivalent (TOE)** = **41.868 GJ** = **11.63 MWh**
> - **1 BTU** = **1055.06 J**
> - **1 Quad** = **10¹⁵ BTU** = **1.055 × 10¹⁸ J**
> - **1 Exajoule (EJ)** = **10¹⁸ J**
> - **1 Watt-year** = **31.536 × 10⁶ J**

---

## 1.4 Global Energy Snapshot (Latest IEA / BP Statistical Review Data)

The world currently consumes roughly **~606 EJ/year** (≈ 14,470 MTOE) of primary energy.

| Region (2023 approx.) | Share of World Primary Energy | Per Capita (TOE/person) |
|---|---|---|
| Asia-Pacific | ~ 46 % | ~ 2.0 |
| North America | ~ 16 % | ~ 5.5 |
| Europe + Eurasia | ~ 18 % | ~ 3.0 |
| Middle East | ~ 6 % | ~ 6.0 |
| Africa | ~ 6 % | ~ 0.7 |
| South & Central America | ~ 4 % | ~ 1.4 |

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **stark inequality**: a person in the Middle East consumes roughly **8× more** energy than a person in Sub-Saharan Africa. This per-capita gap is the central equity issue in the *Just Energy Transition* debate.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown — The Five Layers of an Energy Scenario Study

### Layer 1 — Resource Inventory (the *stock*)
- **Proved Reserves** — economically extractable with current technology and prices.
- **Probable / Possible Reserves** — inferred from geological evidence.
- **Ultimate Resources** — total theoretical endowment.

### Layer 2 — Energy Supply (the *flow*)
- Annual production of coal, oil, gas, nuclear, hydro, solar, wind, bioenergy.
- Measured in **Exajoules per year (EJ/yr)** or **Million Tonnes of Oil Equivalent (MTOE)**.

### Layer 3 — Energy Conversion (the *kitchen*)
- Refineries (oil → diesel/petrol), thermal power plants (coal → electricity), solar PV (sunlight → electricity), electrolyzers (electricity → H₂).

### Layer 4 — End-Use Demand (the *table*)
Four principal sectors:
1. **Industry** (~ 37 % of global final energy)
2. **Transport** (~ 27 %)
3. **Buildings — Residential + Commercial** (~ 22 %)
4. **Agriculture + Non-energy use** (~ 14 %)

### Layer 5 — Energy Indicators (the *scorecard*)
- **Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC)** = Total Energy ÷ Population
- **Energy Intensity (EI)** = Energy ÷ GDP
- **Energy Mix** = % share of each source
- **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratio** = Proved Reserves ÷ Annual Production
- **Carbon Intensity (CI)** = CO₂ emissions ÷ Energy

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **examination essentials** for Module-1 KTU questions.

| # | Metric | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Per Capita Energy Consumption** | $\text{PCEC} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P}$ | TOE / person | $E_{total}$ = total primary energy, $P$ = population |
| 2 | **Energy Intensity of GDP** | $\text{EI} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{\text{GDP}}$ | kJ / USD (or TOE / 10⁶ USD) | Measures economic efficiency |
| 3 | **Reserve-to-Production Ratio** | $\text{R/P} = \dfrac{R_{proved}}{P_{annual}}$ | Years | If R/P = 50, reserves last 50 yrs at current rate |
| 4 | **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)** | $\text{CAGR} = \left(\dfrac{E_{n}}{E_{0}}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | dimensionless / yr | Used for forecasting |
| 5 | **Energy Mix (Share of source $i$)** | $S_i = \dfrac{E_i}{E_{total}} \times 100$ | % | Must sum to 100 % |
| 6 | **Doubling Time of Consumption** | $t_{d} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\ln(1+r)} \approx \dfrac{70}{r\,\%}$ | Years | "Rule of 70" approximation |
| 7 | **Energy Density (volumetric)** | $\rho_E = \dfrac{E}{V}$ | MJ / L | Important for transport fuels |
| 8 | **Heat Rate (Power Plant Efficiency)** | $\eta = \dfrac{3600}{\text{HR}\ (\text{kJ/kWh})} \times 100$ | % | HR = heat input per kWh output |
| 9 | **Cumulative Energy Demand** | $\text{CED} = \sum E_{i}$ over life-cycle | MJ / unit | Cradle-to-grave |
| 10 | **Carbon Intensity of Energy** | $\text{CI} = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2\ \text{emitted}}{E_{delivered}}$ | t CO₂ / TOE | Used in climate accounting |

> [!WARNING]
> When writing these formulas in your KTU answer sheets, always **write the unit explicitly** and **state the standard reference year** for any data you cite. Examiners routinely deduct 0.5–1 mark per formula for missing units.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | How World Energy Scenario knowledge applies |
|---|---|
| Power-plant design | Forecast demand growth → size the turbine / boiler |
| EV / battery engineering | Compare energy density of Li-ion vs petrol |
| Building services | Estimate HVAC load using national per-capita proxy |
| Renewable project finance | Compute Levelized Cost vs fossil-fuel LCOE |
| Policy & regulation | Justify subsidies, RPS targets, ETS caps |
| Energy auditing | Benchmark facility EI vs national average |

---

## 2.4 Global Reserves Snapshot (Approximate, BP / IEA Public Data)

| Resource | Proved Reserves | Annual Production | R/P (years) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Coal | ~ 1,070 Gt | ~ 8 Bt / yr | ~ 130 |
| Crude Oil | ~ 1,720 Gbbl | ~ 92 Mbbl/d | ~ 47 |
| Natural Gas | ~ 195 Tcm | ~ 4 Tcm / yr | ~ 49 |
| Uranium (₹30/kg) | ~ 6 Mt | ~ 65 kt / yr | ~ 90 |

> [!NOTE]
> These R/P ratios are *dynamic* — new discoveries and consumption changes them annually. They are **not** "expiry dates"; they are simply the time at which *half* of today's proved reserves would be exhausted at today's rate, assuming production stays constant.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Per Capita Energy Consumption

**Starting Point:** Primary energy consumed by a country in one year is $E_{total}$ (in TOE). The population is $P$.

**Step 1 — Define PCEC mathematically.**

$$\text{PCEC} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P}$$

**Step 2 — Unit analysis.**
$E_{total}$ is in TOE, $P$ in persons. Hence PCEC has units of **TOE / person**.

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-typical).**

> **Question:** A country consumes **850 MTOE** of primary energy per year and has a population of **140 million**. Calculate the per-capita consumption.

**Solution:**

$$\text{PCEC} = \dfrac{850 \times 10^{6}\ \text{TOE}}{140 \times 10^{6}\ \text{persons}}$$

$$\text{PCEC} = \dfrac{850}{140}\ \text{TOE/person}$$

$$\boxed{\text{PCEC} = 6.07\ \text{TOE/person}}$$

**Interpretation:** This is comparable to the global average for a developed country. KTU answer must *interpret* the number — never end on a raw value.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Intensity (EI)

**Step 1 — Definition.** Energy intensity measures how much energy is required to produce one unit of economic output.

$$\text{EI} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{\text{GDP}}$$

**Step 2 — Unit analysis.**
GDP is typically expressed in USD (or INR). $E_{total}$ in TOE.

$$\text{EI units} = \dfrac{\text{TOE}}{\text{USD (or INR)}}$$

To remove ambiguity, normalize GDP to **million USD**:

$$\text{EI (TOE / million USD)} = \dfrac{E_{total}\ \text{[TOE]} \times 10^{6}}{\text{GDP [USD]}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example.**
- Energy consumed: $E_{total}$ = **500 MTOE**
- GDP: **2,200 billion USD** = $2.2 \times 10^{12}$ USD

$$\text{EI} = \dfrac{500 \times 10^{6}\ \text{TOE}}{2.2 \times 10^{12}\ \text{USD}}$$

$$\text{EI} = \dfrac{500}{2200}\ \text{TOE/million USD}$$

$$\boxed{\text{EI} \approx 0.227\ \text{TOE/million USD}}$$

**Interpretation:** A *lower* EI means a *more energy-efficient* economy. Modern OECD economies have EI between 0.10 and 0.20 TOE/M-USD.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR) and Doubling Time

**Step 1 — CAGR Formula.**

$$\text{CAGR} = \left(\dfrac{E_{n}}{E_{0}}\right)^{1/n} - 1$$

where $E_0$ = energy in base year, $E_n$ = energy in year $n$, $n$ = number of years.

**Step 2 — Derivation of Doubling Time.**
Assume exponential growth $E(t) = E_0 (1+r)^t$. Setting $E(t_d) = 2 E_0$:

$$2 E_0 = E_0 (1+r)^{t_d}$$

$$(1+r)^{t_d} = 2$$

Take natural log of both sides:

$$t_d \cdot \ln(1+r) = \ln 2$$

$$t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\ln(1+r)} = \dfrac{0.6931}{\ln(1+r)}$$

**Step 3 — "Rule of 70" approximation.**
For small $r$ (where $r < 0.10$), $\ln(1+r) \approx r$, so:

$$t_d \approx \dfrac{0.6931}{r} \approx \dfrac{70}{r\,\%}$$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example.**
World energy demand grew at $r = 2.5\%$ / yr. Find doubling time.

Using exact form:

$$t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\ln(1.025)} = \dfrac{0.6931}{0.02469}$$

$$\boxed{t_d \approx 28.1\ \text{years}}$$

Using Rule of 70:

$$t_d \approx \dfrac{70}{2.5} = 28\ \text{years}$$

Both answers agree — a good consistency check for KTU numerical answers.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy Share / Mix Percentage

**Step 1 — Definition.**
The share of energy source $i$ in the total energy mix:

$$S_i = \dfrac{E_i}{E_{total}} \times 100\ \%$$

**Step 2 — Worked Numerical Example.**
A country's primary energy mix: Coal = 300 MTOE, Oil = 200 MTOE, Gas = 150 MTOE, Renewables = 100 MTOE.

$$E_{total} = 300 + 200 + 150 + 100 = 750\ \text{MTOE}$$

$$S_{coal} = \dfrac{300}{750} \times 100 = 40\ \%$$

$$S_{oil} = \dfrac{200}{750} \times 100 = 26.67\ \%$$

$$S_{gas} = \dfrac{150}{750} \times 100 = 20\ \%$$

$$S_{RE} = \dfrac{100}{750} \times 100 = 13.33\ \%$$

**Verification:** $40 + 26.67 + 20 + 13.33 = 100\ \%$ ✓

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio

**Step 1 — Definition.**

$$\text{R/P} = \dfrac{R_{proved}}{P_{annual}}$$

**Step 2 — Interpretation.**
If R/P = 50 years, proved reserves (at *current* annual production) will last 50 years.

**Step 3 — Numerical Example.**
A coal field has 2,000 Mt of proved reserves. Annual production is 50 Mt/yr.

$$\text{R/P} = \dfrac{2000}{50} = 40\ \text{years}$$

**Step 4 — Caveat (Examiner's pet question).**
R/P assumes production remains constant. In reality, production often *grows* during the depletion phase, shortening the actual lifespan. R/P is therefore a *first-order approximation*.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Energy Indicators by Region

| Region | PCEC (TOE/capita) | EI (TOE/M-USD) | Dominant Source | Carbon Intensity (t CO₂/TOE) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| OECD Europe | 3.0 | 0.12 | Oil → Renewables | 2.5 |
| North America | 5.5 | 0.18 | Oil + Gas | 2.4 |
| China | 2.7 | 0.30 | Coal (declining) | 4.0 |
| India | 0.7 | 0.22 | Coal (growing) | 3.5 |
| Africa | 0.7 | 0.45 | Biomass + Oil | 2.0 (low due to biomass) |
| Middle East | 6.0 | 0.35 | Oil + Gas | 2.6 |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam shortcut**: Memorize this matrix. It is frequently tested as a "compare and contrast" 7-mark question.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.3 Process Flow — World Energy Scenario Analysis Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1 Identify Region and Time Horizon] --> P2[Step 2 Collect Energy Data]
```


---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Define the term "World Energy Scenario" and list the four major sectors of global final energy consumption with their approximate percentage shares.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **World Energy Scenario** describes the present state and projected evolution of global primary energy supply, demand, resource reserves, energy mix, and consumption patterns across regions and sectors of the world.

The four major sectors of *final energy consumption* (with approximate shares) are:

| Sector | Approx. Share |
|---|---|
| Industry | 37 % |
| Transport | 27 % |
| Residential + Commercial (Buildings) | 22 % |
| Agriculture + Non-energy use | 14 % |

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for sector table with shares.)*

---

### Question A2 — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**Distinguish between "Primary Energy" and "Secondary Energy" with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Primary Energy** is energy obtained directly from natural sources without any conversion process. Examples: *Coal, crude oil, natural gas, sunlight, wind, hydro-head, uranium*.
>
> **Secondary Energy** is energy derived from primary sources after one or more conversion processes. Examples: *Electricity, gasoline, diesel, LPG, hydrogen, refined kerosene*.
>
> *(1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for two examples of each.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B-A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the classification of world energy resources with a neat block diagram. Differentiate between commercial and non-commercial energy with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The annual primary energy consumption of a country is **1200 MTOE** and its population is **180 million**. The country's GDP is **3000 billion USD**. Calculate:
- (i) Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC)
- (ii) Energy Intensity (EI)
- (iii) If energy demand grows at a CAGR of 3 %, what will be the consumption after 10 years? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B-A(a) — Classification & Commercial vs Non-Commercial

**Classification of World Energy Resources** (write in your answer sheet):

1. **Renewable (Replenishable)**
   - Solar, Wind, Hydro, Tidal, Wave, Geothermal, Biomass (firewood, biogas, agricultural residues).
2. **Non-Renewable (Finite)**
   - Fossil fuels — Coal, Crude Oil, Natural Gas.
   - Nuclear — Uranium, Thorium (Fission); Deuterium-Tritium (Future Fusion).

*(Insert a labelled block diagram similar to Section 4.2 of these notes — examiners award 1 mark for the diagram.)*

**Commercial vs Non-Commercial Energy:**

| Parameter | Commercial Energy | Non-Commercial Energy |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Coal, oil, gas, electricity, LPG | Firewood, cow-dung, agricultural waste |
| Market | Bought and sold | Generally gathered, not bought |
| Quality | High calorific value, low smoke | Low calorific value, smoky |
| Tracking | Tracked in national statistics | Often unrecorded |
| Examples | Electricity, diesel, natural gas | Charcoal, bagasse, straw |

*Examples of non-commercial:* Cow-dung cakes (India), charcoal (Africa), agricultural residues.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Developing nations like India still rely significantly on non-commercial energy in rural households, which is unaccounted for in national statistics and creates the "energy poverty" gap.

---

#### Solution to B-A(b) — Numerical Computations

**Given:**
- $E_{total}$ = 1200 MTOE
- $P$ = 180 × 10⁶ persons
- GDP = 3000 × 10⁹ USD = 3.0 × 10¹² USD
- CAGR = $r = 3\% = 0.03$, $n = 10$ years

**(i) Per Capita Energy Consumption (PCEC):**

$$\text{PCEC} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P} = \dfrac{1200 \times 10^{6}\ \text{TOE}}{180 \times 10^{6}\ \text{persons}}$$

$$\text{PCEC} = \dfrac{1200}{180} = 6.667\ \text{TOE/person}$$

$$\boxed{\text{PCEC} \approx 6.67\ \text{TOE/person}}$$

*(1 mark for formula, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for answer + unit.)*

**(ii) Energy Intensity (EI):**

Convert energy to TOE: 1200 MTOE = 1200 × 10⁶ TOE.

$$\text{EI} = \dfrac{1200 \times 10^{6}\ \text{TOE}}{3000 \times 10^{9}\ \text{USD}}$$

$$\text{EI} = \dfrac{1200}{3000 \times 10^{3}} = \dfrac{1200}{3 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{EI} = 4.0 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{TOE/USD} = 0.40\ \text{TOE/million USD}}$$

*(Alternative acceptable: 0.40 TOE/M-USD.)*

**(iii) Energy after 10 years at 3 % CAGR:**

$$E_{10} = E_0 (1 + r)^n = 1200 \times (1.03)^{10}$$

Compute $(1.03)^{10}$ step-by-step:

$$\begin{aligned}
(1.03)^2 &= 1.0609 \\
(1.03)^4 &= (1.0609)^2 = 1.12551 \\
(1.03)^5 &= 1.12551 \times 1.03 = 1.15927 \\
(1.03)^{10} &= (1.15927)^2 = 1.34392
\end{aligned}$$

Therefore:

$$E_{10} = 1200 \times 1.34392 = 1612.7\ \text{MTOE}$$

$$\boxed{E_{10} \approx 1612.7\ \text{MTOE}}$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):** Energy demand grows from 1200 MTOE to ~1613 MTOE in 10 years — an increase of ~412 MTOE, which must be met through capacity additions or efficiency improvements.

---

### Question B-B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Define the following energy indicators with formulas and typical units:
- (i) Per Capita Energy Consumption
- (ii) Energy Intensity
- (iii) Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio
- (iv) Carbon Intensity
- (v) Compound Annual Growth Rate
- (vi) Energy Mix Share
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **energy scenario of India** with that of a developed country (e.g., USA or Germany) on the basis of:
- (i) Per capita consumption
- (ii) Energy mix (fossil vs renewable)
- (iii) Energy intensity
- (iv) Carbon intensity

Highlight key policy implications for India's energy transition. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Solution to B-B(a) — Energy Indicators with Formulas

| # | Indicator | Formula | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Per Capita Energy Consumption | $\text{PCEC} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P}$ | TOE/person |
| (ii) | Energy Intensity | $\text{EI} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{\text{GDP}}$ | TOE/million USD |
| (iii) | R/P Ratio | $\text{R/P} = \dfrac{R_{proved}}{P_{annual}}$ | Years |
| (iv) | Carbon Intensity | $\text{CI} = \dfrac{\text{CO}_2}{E_{delivered}}$ | t CO₂/TOE |
| (v) | CAGR | $\text{CAGR} = \left(\dfrac{E_n}{E_0}\right)^{1/n} - 1$ | dimensionless |
| (vi) | Energy Mix Share | $S_i = \dfrac{E_i}{E_{total}} \times 100$ | % |

*(1 mark each for correct formula + unit; deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit.)*

---

#### Solution to B-B(b) — India vs Developed Country

| Parameter | India | USA / Germany |
|---|---|---|
| Per Capita Consumption | ~ 0.7 TOE/person | ~ 5.5 / 3.0 TOE/person |
| Energy Mix (Fossil % ) | ~ 75 % (coal-heavy) | ~ 60 % USA, ~ 50 % Germany |
| Renewables Share | ~ 12 % (rising) | ~ 22 % USA, ~ 46 % Germany |
| Energy Intensity | ~ 0.22 TOE/M-USD | ~ 0.18 USA, ~ 0.12 Germany |
| Carbon Intensity | ~ 3.5 t CO₂/TOE | ~ 2.4 USA, ~ 2.5 Germany |

**Key Observations (2 marks):**

1. India's per-capita consumption is roughly **8× lower** than the USA — reflects lower industrial output and energy access gap.
2. India is still **coal-dominant**, while Germany has aggressively pivoted to wind + solar.
3. India's energy intensity is *higher* than Germany's, indicating inefficient industrial processes and reliance on coal-fired power.

**Policy Implications for India (2 marks):**

1. **Rapid scale-up of solar and wind** — leveraging 300+ sunny days/year and high coastal wind speeds.
2. **Energy efficiency in industry** — PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) scheme, star-rated appliances.
3. **Electrification of transport** — FAME-II scheme to push EV adoption.
4. **Green hydrogen mission** — to decarbonize fertilizer and refining sectors.
5. **Universal energy access** — Saubhagya scheme to electrify remaining rural households.
6. **Carbon pricing & market mechanisms** — Indian Carbon Market (proposed).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls where KTU students lose marks on World Energy Scenario questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** — Every numerical answer (PCEC, EI, R/P) MUST carry units. Examiner deducts 0.5–1 mark per missing unit.
> 2. **Confusing *primary* and *final* energy** — Sectoral shares (37/27/22/14) refer to *final* energy, while BP/IEA primary-energy tables refer to *primary* energy. The numbers differ.
> 3. **Quoting stale data** — KTU examiners prefer *latest available* (2022–2024) data. Use round, defensible numbers.
> 4. **No interpretation** — A numerical answer that ends with just a number (e.g., "PCEC = 6.67") loses the "interpretation" mark. Always write 1–2 lines of interpretation.
> 5. **Drawing diagrams with bare boxes only** — Label every box and arrow. A block diagram without labels is awarded zero.
> 6. **Mixing up R/P and CAGR** — R/P = years of reserves; CAGR = growth rate. Many students invert these.
> 7. **Ignoring non-commercial energy** — For India-specific questions, mentioning firewood/biomass is mandatory.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and revise before the exam:**

- [ ] **Definition**: World Energy Scenario = snapshot of global energy supply, demand, reserves, mix, and consumption.
- [ ] **Primary energy sources**: Coal, oil, gas, nuclear, hydro, solar, wind, biomass, geothermal, tidal.
- [ ] **Secondary energy sources**: Electricity, gasoline, diesel, LPG, hydrogen, district heat.
- [ ] **Commercial vs non-commercial**: Commercial = sold (electricity, LPG); Non-commercial = gathered (firewood, cow-dung).
- [ ] **Global primary energy ≈ 600 EJ/year ≈ 14,500 MTOE**.
- [ ] **Four demand sectors**: Industry 37 %, Transport 27 %, Buildings 22 %, Agriculture + others 14 %.
- [ ] **Top consumers**: China, USA, India, Russia, Japan (in that order).
- [ ] **Key formulas** (must memorize): PCEC, EI, R/P, CAGR, $S_i$, Doubling Time (Rule of 70).
- [ ] **Standard conversions**: 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ = 11.63 MWh; 1 Quad = 10¹⁵ BTU.
- [ ] **R/P ratios (approx.)**: Coal ~ 130 yrs, Oil ~ 47 yrs, Gas ~ 49 yrs, Uranium ~ 90 yrs.
- [ ] **Per-capita gap**: Middle East ~ 6 TOE vs Sub-Saharan Africa ~ 0.7 TOE.
- [ ] **Doubling time approximation**: $t_d \approx 70 / r(\%)$.
- [ ] **Energy transition drivers**: Decarbonization, electrification, efficiency, hydrogen.
- [ ] **India-specific**: Coal-heavy mix, low per-capita, PAT scheme, FAME-II, solar mission, green hydrogen.
- [ ] **Always write units** in numerical answers.
- [ ] **Always add interpretation lines** after every numerical.
- [ ] **Always label every block** in a diagram.
- [ ] **Always cite a reference year** for any data (e.g., "as of 2023").

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indian Energy Sector Reforms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Indian Energy Sector Reforms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indian Energy Sector Reforms** refer to the structured policy, legal, regulatory, and institutional transformations initiated by the Government of India (GoI) since the early 1990s to transition the power sector from a vertically integrated, government-owned, monopoly model into a competitive, market-driven, efficiency-oriented, and consumer-responsive framework.

These reforms are operationalized through five inter-locking pillars:

1. **Liberalization** – allowing private and foreign participation in generation, transmission, and distribution.
2. **Privatization** – disinvestment and ownership transfer of state-run utilities.
3. **Restructuring / Unbundling** – separation of generation, transmission, and distribution (G-T-D) functions.
4. **Regulation** – establishment of independent Central and State Electricity Regulatory Commissions.
5. **Market Development** – creation of power exchanges, open access, renewable energy certificates, and competitive bidding.

> [!NOTE]
> The official **"power for all by 2012"** mission of 1991, the **Electricity Act 2003**, and the **National Electricity Policy 2005** are the cornerstone policy documents that gave statutory shape to these reforms.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Telecom Parallel

Imagine India’s pre-1991 power sector as a single, government-run **landline telephone monopoly**: one provider (DoT), no competition, long waitlists, poor service, fixed tariffs set by the state, and zero accountability.

**Energy Sector Reforms are exactly what happened to telecom after 1991:**

- **Licensing Era (1991–2003):** Just as VSNL’s monopoly gave way to private cellular operators, the **Independent Power Producer (IPP) policy of 1991** allowed private generators to enter the market.
- **Regulator Arrives (1998–2003):** The Telecom Regulatory Authority of India (**TRAI**, 1997) was mirrored by the Central Electricity Regulatory Commission (**CERC, 1998**) and State Electricity Regulatory Commissions (**SERCs**).
- **Full Liberalization (2003 onwards):** The **Electricity Act 2003** is the equivalent of the *New Telecom Policy (NTP-1999)* — it threw open the entire value chain to competition.
- **Consumer as King:** Just as mobile users chose among Reliance Jio, Airtel, Vi, etc., the *open access* provision allows large consumers to pick their supplier.

> [!TIP]
> For any KTU answer, this telecom analogy is a high-scoring intuitive hook examiners love.

## 1.3 Key Pre-Reform Indicators (Baseline Statistics circa 1991)

| Parameter | Pre-Reform Status (1991) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Installed Capacity | ~66 GW |
| Per Capita Consumption | ~288 kWh |
| Aggregate Technical & Commercial (AT&C) Losses | ~30–40 % |
| Plant Load Factor (PLF) | ~52 % |
| Electrification of Villages | ~50 % |

These abysmal metrics — especially the **AT&C losses of 30–40 %** — were the primary catalysts for structural reform.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Matrix

## 2.1 The Pre-Reform Problem Statement (Why Reform Was Inevitable)

The pre-reform power sector suffered from five structural diseases. Each reform instrument was a targeted cure.

- **Vertically Integrated Monopolies:** State Electricity Boards (SEBs) were simultaneously generating, transmitting, and distributing electricity in their geographical territory. They were judge, jury, and executioner — a classic conflict of interest.
- **Tariff Distortions:** Agricultural and domestic consumers were heavily cross-subsidized by industrial tariffs. The Average Cost of Supply (ACS) > Average Revenue Realized (ARR), leading to chronic revenue deficits.
- **Political Interference:** SEBs were forced to supply free or cheap electricity to farmers, write off dues, and appoint inefficient staff.
- **Demand-Supply Mismatch:** Severe peak shortages (~15–20 %) and energy shortages (~10 %) crippled industrial growth.
- **Underinvestment:** Tariffs did not cover costs, so SEBs could not invest in new capacity, creating a vicious deficit spiral.

> [!NOTE]
> The vicious circle is: **Subsidies → Tariff Distortion → Revenue Deficit → No Investment → Shortfall → More Political Interference → More Subsidies.** Reforms break this circle at every point.

## 2.2 Chronological Architecture of Reforms (KTU High-Yield Timeline)

| Year | Reform Instrument | Objective | Key Provision |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1991 | Industrial Policy Statement; IPP Policy | Liberalize generation | Private sector entry; 100 % FDI in power |
| 1992 | 8th Five-Year Plan (1992–97) | Begin sector restructuring | Suggested SEB reforms |
| 1996 | MoP Task Force Report | Blueprint for restructuring | Recommended Regulatory Commissions |
| 1998 | CERC established | Central-level tariff regulation | Multi-year tariff (MYT) framework |
| 2001 | SERCs operational in 18 states | State-level regulation | Tariff determination at state level |
| **2003** | **Electricity Act 2003** | **Comprehensive consolidation** | **De-licensing generation; Open Access; G-T-D separation** |
| 2005 | National Electricity Policy | Implement EA 2003 | Power for all by 2012 |
| 2005 | Tariff Policy | Cost-reflective tariffs | Cross-subsidy reduction roadmap |
| 2006 | Open Access Regulations | Enable competition | Third-party sale of power |
| 2008 | Rajiv Gandhi Grameen Vidyutikaran Yojana (RGGVY) | Rural electrification | 90 % govt grant |
| 2010 | Restructured Accelerated Power Development & Reforms Programme (R-APDRP) | Reduce AT&C losses | IT-based metering in urban areas |
| 2014 | UDAY (Ujwal DISCOM Assurance Yojana) | DISCOM financial turnaround | Bond takeovers; loss reduction |
| 2015 | Ujwal Bharat Abhiyan (UBA) | 24x7 affordable power | LED distribution, Saubhagya scheme |
| 2021 | Electricity (Amendment) Bill 2021 | Promote competition | Multi-distribution, single licensee |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Electricity Act 2003** is the single most important document. Every KTU 14-mark question on reforms will revolve around it.

## 2.3 The Electricity Act 2003 — Section-wise High-Yield Provisions

| Section | Subject | Salient Feature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Sec 7 | Rural Electrification | Obligation on state govt |
| Sec 11 | Generation De-licensing | No license required to set up a power plant |
| Sec 14 | Multi-distribution | Multiple licensees in same area |
| Sec 38–40 | Transmission by CTU / STU | Central & State Transmission Utilities |
| Sec 42 | Open Access | Non-discriminatory access for ≥1 MW consumers |
| Sec 61–66 | Tariff Determination | By CERC / SERCs based on MYT norms |
| Sec 79 | CERC Jurisdiction | Inter-state tariffs, RLDCs |
| Sec 86 | SERC Jurisdiction | Intra-state tariffs, distribution |

## 2.4 Institutional Architecture of the Reformed Sector

The reformed sector operates on a **three-tier governance model**:

- **Policy Layer (GoI / Forum of Regulators):** Sets national policy, model regulations.
- **Regulatory Layer (CERC at centre; SERCs in each state):** Quasi-judicial, independent tariff and licensing authority.
- **Operational Layer:** Central Public Sector Undertakings (CPSUs) like NTPC, NHPC, PowerGrid; Private Players (Tata Power, Reliance, Adani); and the various State Power Utilities (GENCOs, TRANSCOs, DISCOMs).

> [!TIP]
> The **CEA (Central Electricity Authority)** is *advisory*, not regulatory. CERC/SERCs are the actual regulators. This is a frequent MCQ trap.

## 2.5 The Multi-Year Tariff (MYT) Framework

The MYT regime, mandated by Sec 61 of EA-2003, replaced the ad-hoc annual tariff revisions. A typical MYT control period is **5 years**, with an annual truing-up. The cost stack consists of:

- **ARR (Aggregate Revenue Requirement)** = Energy Sales Cost + Fixed Cost + Power Purchase Cost + O&M + Depreciation + RoE + Taxes − Non-Tariff Income.
- **Tariff** = (ARR / Total Energy Sold) adjusted for cross-subsidy.

The Annual Revenue Charge (ARC) formula is given below in Section 3.3.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation & Worked Numerical Model

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of the Reform Process

The Indian energy reform process follows a structured seven-step logical chain. KTU examiners frequently test the **sequential logic** of reform rather than isolated facts.

**Step 1 — Diagnosis of Sector Failure (1991):**
Identify the five diseases: monopoly, tariff distortion, political interference, demand-supply gap, underinvestment. Output: MoP Task Force Reports (1993, 1996).

**Step 2 — Incremental Pilot Reforms (1991–1998):**
Entry of private generators (IPPs) under the 1991 policy. Orissa was the first state to unbundle its SEB in 1996. The failure of the Enron Dabhol Power Project (1995) was a major learning — it showed the need for credible PPAs and regulatory backstops.

**Step 3 — Regulatory Backbone Creation (1998–2003):**
Enactment of the **CERC Act 1998** to establish the central regulator. SERCs were notified in most states by 2001. Until regulators existed, the sector had no independent tariff-setting authority.

**Step 4 — Legislative Consolidation (2003):**
The **Electricity Act 2003** replaced the three legacy laws (Indian Electricity Act 1910, Electricity (Supply) Act 1948, Electricity Regulatory Commissions Act 1998). It is a *single, omnibus statute*.

**Step 5 — Unbundling & Corporatization (2003–2010):**
SEBs are separated into state-owned **GENCO** (generation), **TRANSCO** (transmission), and **DISCOM** (distribution) corporations. This separation creates accounting transparency.

**Step 6 — Market Development (2006 onwards):**
- **Open Access:** any consumer ≥1 MW can buy from any generator using the transmission network of CTU/STU.
- **Power Exchanges (2008):** Indian Energy Exchange (IEX) and Power Exchange India Ltd (PXIL) enable day-ahead market (DAM), term-ahead market (TAM), and real-time market (RTM).
- **Renewable Energy Certificates (RECs):** issued by CERC to verify green power generation.

**Step 7 — Financial Restructuring & Loss Reduction (2014 onwards):**
**UDAY** (2015–2019) was a turnaround scheme where states issued bonds to take over DISCOM debt, conditional on reducing AT&C losses to 15 % by FY 2019.

## 3.2 Mathematical Modeling — AT&C Loss Reduction Trajectory

The **AT&C loss** is the most critical operational metric for any DISCOM. KTU often asks students to compute or analyze it.

**Definition of AT&C Loss:**

$$
\text{AT\&C Loss (}\%\text{)} = \left(1 - \frac{\text{Billing Efficiency} \times \text{Collection Efficiency}}{100}\right) \times 100
$$

**Component Definition:**

$$
\text{Billing Efficiency (}\%\text{)} = \frac{\text{Amount Billed (Rs)}}{\text{Energy Input (kWh)} \times \text{Realization Rate (Rs/kWh)}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Collection Efficiency (}\%\text{)} = \frac{\text{Amount Collected (Rs)}}{\text{Amount Billed (Rs)}} \times 100
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> AT&C loss captures **both** technical losses (line losses, transformer losses) **and** commercial losses (theft, metering defects, billing inefficiency). This is why it is preferred over the older "T&D loss" metric.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Tariff Determination under MYT (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A state DISCOM submits the following data for FY 2024–25 to the SERC. Compute the Average Cost of Supply (ACS) and the tariff to be charged if the target collection efficiency is 99 %.

| Cost Head | Value (Rs Crore) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Power Purchase Cost (PPC) | 4500 |
| Operations & Maintenance (O&M) | 850 |
| Depreciation | 320 |
| Return on Equity (RoE) | 480 |
| Interest on Loans | 270 |
| Other Taxes & Duties | 110 |
| Non-Tariff Income (NTI) | 150 |
| **Total Energy Sales** | 6800 MU |

**Step 1 — Compute Aggregate Revenue Requirement (ARR):**

$$
\text{ARR} = \text{PPC} + \text{O\&M} + \text{Depreciation} + \text{RoE} + \text{Interest} + \text{Taxes} - \text{NTI}
$$

$$
\text{ARR} = 4500 + 850 + 320 + 480 + 270 + 110 - 150
$$

$$
\text{ARR} = 6380 \text{ Rs Crore}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Average Cost of Supply (ACS) per kWh:**

$$
1 \text{ MU} = 10^6 \text{ kWh} \quad \text{so} \quad 6800 \text{ MU} = 6800 \times 10^6 \text{ kWh}
$$

$$
\text{ACS} = \frac{\text{ARR (Rs Crore)}}{\text{Energy Sales (MU)}} = \frac{6380 \times 10^7}{6800 \times 10^6}
$$

$$
\text{ACS} = \frac{63800 \times 10^6}{6800 \times 10^6} = 9.38 \text{ Rs/kWh}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Realization-Based Tariff with Collection Efficiency Adjustment:**

$$
\text{Tariff} = \frac{\text{ACS}}{\text{Collection Efficiency}} = \frac{9.38}{0.99} = 9.47 \text{ Rs/kWh}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

The DISCOM must charge approximately **Rs 9.47 per kWh** to break even at 99 % collection. If the SERC directs that industrial consumers cross-subsidize domestic ones by 1.20 times ACS, then the industrial tariff will be:

$$
\text{Industrial Tariff} = 9.47 \times 1.20 = 11.36 \text{ Rs/kWh}
$$

and the domestic tariff will be 60 % of ACS:

$$
\text{Domestic Tariff} = 9.47 \times 0.60 = 5.68 \text{ Rs/kWh}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The SERC must publish these tariff rationalizations in a **Tariff Order**, which is a quasi-judicial document. KTU examiners love this level of detail.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Reform Outcomes vs Targets

| Metric | 1991 (Baseline) | 2010 | 2023 | Reforms Impact |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Installed Capacity | 66 GW | 173 GW | 425 GW | 6.4x growth |
| Per Capita Consumption | 288 kWh | 717 kWh | 1255 kWh | 4.3x growth |
| AT&C Losses | 35 % | 27 % | 16 % | Halved |
| Village Electrification | 50 % | 84 % | 100 % | Near universal |
| PLF (Thermal) | 52 % | 77 % | ~60 %* | Saturation; supply-side growth limits PLF |

*The PLF decline post-2010 reflects slowing demand growth and RE penetration, not reform failure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Reformed Power Sector Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Central Government of India] --> B[Policy Layer]
    A1[State Government] --> B
    B --> C[Ministry of Power]

    D --> E
    E --> F[Regulatory Layer]

    I --> J[Generation Segment]
    I --> K[Transmission Segment]
    I --> L[Distribution Segment]
    J --> J1[Central GENCOs NTPC NHPC]
    J --> J2[State GENCOs]
    J --> J3[Independent Power Producers IPPs]
    J --> J4[Renewable Energy Generators]
    K --> K1[PowerGrid CTU]
    K --> K2[State TRANSCOs STU]

    J2 --> K1
    J3 --> K1

    K1 --> L2

    L2 --> M
```

## 4.2 Flow Diagram — Reform Trigger Chain

```mermaid

    P1[Pre 1991 Monopoly SEB] --> Q1[Chronic Revenue Deficit]
    Q1 --> Q2[Demand Supply Mismatch]
    Q2 --> Q3[Political Pressure for Free Power]

    Q4 --> R1[IPP Policy 1991]
    R1 --> R2[CERC Act 1998]
    R2 --> R3[SERC Notifications 2001]
```
