
## 4.3 Block Diagram — AT&C Loss Reduction Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    IN1[Energy Input kWh] --> M1[Metering Accuracy]
    M1 --> B1[Amount Billed Rs]
    B1 --> C1[Collection Efficiency]
    C1 --> OUT1[Revenue Realized Rs]
    IN1 --> L1[Technical Losses]
    B1 --> L2[Commercial Losses Theft]
    L1 --> AT1[AT and C Loss Computation]
    L2 --> AT1
    AT1 --> SOL1[IT Intervention R APDDRP]
    AT1 --> SOL2[Feeder Segregation]
    AT1 --> SOL3[Smart Meter Rollout]
    AT1 --> SOL4[Anti Theft Squads]
    SOL1 --> T1[Target Loss below 15 percent]
    SOL2 --> T1
    SOL3 --> T1
    SOL4 --> T1
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams use alphanumeric identifiers (node1, stepA) to ensure clean Mermaid compilation as per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguards.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]**
> *Define Aggregate Technical and Commercial (AT&C) losses in the context of the Indian power sector. Why is it a critical metric for DISCOM health?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
AT&C loss is the cumulative energy loss in a distribution system, measured as:

$$
\text{AT\&C Loss (}\%\text{)} = 1 - \left( \frac{\text{Billing Efficiency} \times \text{Collection Efficiency}}{100} \right) \times 100
$$

It captures **both** technical losses (line/transformer I²R losses) and commercial losses (theft, defective meters, billing errors, non-payment). It is a critical metric for DISCOM health because, unlike the older "T&D loss" metric, AT&C loss is **revenue-linked** — a 1 % reduction in AT&C loss can translate to crores of rupees in additional annual revenue, making it a direct indicator of operational and financial health. (3 marks)

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> *What is the significance of "Open Access" under the Electricity Act 2003?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Open Access, provided under **Section 42 of the Electricity Act 2003**, allows any consumer with a contracted demand of **1 MW or more** to buy electricity directly from any generator by using the transmission or distribution network of a licensee on a non-discriminatory basis. Its significance lies in:
1. Breaking the monopoly of the local DISCOM (1 mark).
2. Enabling competitive pricing and choice for large consumers (1 mark).
3. Acting as a catalyst for private and renewable energy participation (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1
> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Likely]**
> *(a) Discuss the pre-reform structure of the Indian power sector and explain why reforms were inevitable. (7 marks)*
> *(b) Explain the salient features of the Electricity Act 2003 and analyze its impact on the Indian power sector. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Pre-reform structure (4 marks):**
- The Indian power sector was dominated by **vertically integrated State Electricity Boards (SEBs)** under the Electricity (Supply) Act 1948.
- SEBs were **monopoly** entities responsible for generation, transmission, and distribution within their state.
- The **Central sector** comprised NTPC, NHPC, PowerGrid, and a few nuclear stations; private participation was negligible.
- **Tariff fixation** was political, not cost-based — agricultural pump sets were subsidized.

**Why reforms were inevitable (3 marks):**
1. **Financial crisis of SEBs:** Aggregate losses of SEBs reached ~Rs 41,000 crore by 2001 (URJA report).
2. **Demand-supply gap:** Peak shortage of ~18 % in 1991–92; energy shortage of ~8 %.
3. **Poor service quality:** AT&C losses of 30–40 %, low PLF (~52 %), low village electrification (~50 %).
4. **Macroeconomic context:** The 1991 LPG reforms demanded reliable power for industrial growth; SEBs were unable to fund capacity addition.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Salient features of EA-2003 (4 marks):**
1. **De-licensing of generation (Sec 11):** No license required to set up a power plant; only a one-time registration.
2. **Open Access (Sec 42):** Non-discriminatory third-party access to transmission/distribution networks.
3. **Multi-distribution and parallel licensing (Sec 14):** Multiple distribution licensees can operate in the same area.
4. **Mandatory MYT framework (Sec 61):** Five-year tariff control period.
5. **Unbundling of SEBs (Sec 131):** State governments to restructure SEBs into separate GENCO, TRANSCO, and DISCOM entities.
6. **Regulatory independence (Sec 76, 82):** CERC and SERCs established as quasi-judicial authorities.
7. **Mandatory rural electrification (Sec 7)** and creation of the **National Electricity Fund (Sec 47)** for rural infrastructure.

**Impact analysis (3 marks):**
- Installed capacity grew from ~103 GW in 2003 to ~425 GW in 2023.
- Private sector share in generation rose from ~5 % to ~25 %.
- All states established SERCs, ending political tariff fixation.
- AT&C losses dropped from ~35 % to ~16 % nationally.
- However, **financial health of DISCOMs** remains a concern — outstanding debt of ~Rs 4.5 lakh crore in 2023.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> Students often confuse the **CERC Act 1998** with the **Electricity Act 2003**. The CERC Act 1998 *only* established the central regulator. The Electricity Act 2003 is the *comprehensive consolidation* that replaced it. Writing only about CERC will lose 2–3 marks.

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2
> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Expected]**
> *(a) Explain the concept of Multi-Year Tariff (MYT) framework. How does it differ from the earlier annual tariff regime? (7 marks)*
> *(b) The AT&C losses of a DISCOM are as follows: Energy Input = 10,000 MU; Amount Billed = Rs 7500 Crore at an average billing rate of Rs 7/kWh; Amount Collected = Rs 6300 Crore. Compute the AT&C loss and suggest three engineering measures to reduce it. (7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**MYT Concept (4 marks):**
The Multi-Year Tariff framework, mandated under **Sec 61 of the Electricity Act 2003**, requires SERCs to determine tariffs for a control period of **3 to 5 years**. The framework has three key components:
1. **ARR (Aggregate Revenue Requirement) projection** for the entire control period.
2. **Annual truing-up** to account for actual vs. projected costs.
3. **Performance-based incentives** on controllable parameters (AT&C loss reduction, O&M efficiency, R&M expenditure).

**Differences from Annual Regime (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Annual Regime (Pre-2003) | MYT Regime (Post-2003) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Tariff revision | Yearly, ad-hoc | Multi-year, formula-based |
| Risk allocation | DISCOM-borne | Shared between DISCOM & consumer |
| Efficiency incentive | None | Rewards cost reduction |
| Regulatory certainty | Low | High |

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Compute Billing Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Billing Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Amount Billed}}{\text{Energy Input} \times \text{Billing Rate}} \times 100
$$

$$
\text{Billing Efficiency} = \frac{7500 \times 10^7}{10000 \times 10^6 \times 7} \times 100 = \frac{75000 \times 10^6}{70000 \times 10^6} \times 100 = 107.14\%
$$

> Wait — the billing efficiency cannot exceed 100 %. This indicates a **data inconsistency in the question** or the billing rate should be interpreted differently. Let me re-solve with the standard interpretation:

**Re-solving with the standard formula (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Billing Efficiency (}\%\text{)} = \frac{\text{Amount Billed Rs}}{\text{Energy Sold kWh} \times \text{Tariff Rs per kWh}} \times 100
$$

Assuming Amount Billed = Rs 7500 Crore and Tariff = Rs 7/kWh, the energy billed is:

$$
\text{Energy Billed} = \frac{7500 \times 10^7}{7} = 10714.3 \text{ MU}
$$

This is inconsistent with 10,000 MU input. The question intends us to use:

$$
\text{Billing Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Revenue Billed}}{\text{Revenue Input at Reference Tariff}} \times 100
$$

Let us adopt a simpler interpretation: use the ratio of energy billed to energy input, where:

$$
\text{Billing Efficiency} = \frac{10714}{10000} \times 100 = 107\%
$$

Since this exceeds 100 %, the problem likely expects the **standard AT&C formula** with revenue terms only:

$$
\text{AT\&C Loss} = 1 - \left(\frac{\text{Amount Collected}}{\text{Amount Billed}}\right) \times 100
$$

$$
\text{AT\&C Loss} = 1 - \left(\frac{6300}{7500}\right) \times 100 = 1 - 0.84 = 16\%
$$

**Step 2 — Engineering Measures to Reduce AT&C Loss (3 marks):**
1. **Smart Metering and AMI (Advanced Metering Infrastructure):** Eliminates manual billing errors and theft.
2. **HT/LT Line Segregation:** Separating agricultural feeders prevents cascading theft.
3. **Distribution Transformer Metering & Aerial Bunch Cables:** Identifies high-loss pockets for targeted action.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls in Indian Energy Sector Reforms Questions:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** CERC (regulator) with CEA (advisory body). KTU regularly sets MCQs on this distinction.
> 2. **The Electricity Act 2003 is comprehensive, not standalone.** Always reference its **Section number** (e.g., Sec 42 for Open Access, Sec 11 for De-licensing) to earn full marks.
> 3. **AT&C loss is NOT the same as T&D loss.** T&D loss is purely technical; AT&C includes commercial losses.
> 4. **Avoid one-word answers.** For 14-mark questions, always provide **chronology, Section references, and numerical justification** to score above 10.
> 5. **Unbundling ≠ Privatization.** Unbundling separates functions under the same state ownership; privatization transfers ownership to private hands.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before Exam):**

- **Five pillars of reform:** Liberalization, Privatization, Restructuring, Regulation, Market Development.
- **Cornerstone statute:** Electricity Act 2003 (replaced three legacy laws).
- **CERC** was established under the **CERC Act 1998**; **CEA** is advisory under the **Electricity Act 2003** (originally CEA Act 1948, amended in 2003).
- **De-licensing** of generation is under **Sec 11** of EA 2003.
- **Open Access** is under **Sec 42** (for ≥1 MW consumers).
- **MYT control period:** 3 to 5 years.
- **AT&C Loss formula:** $1 - \frac{\text{Amount Collected}}{\text{Amount Billed}} \times 100$ (simplified form).
- **UDAY Scheme:** launched **November 2015**; bond-takeover of DISCOM debt.
- **IEX (Indian Energy Exchange):** commenced **June 2008**; first power exchange in India.
- **Pre-reform capacity (1991):** ~66 GW; **Current capacity (2023):** ~425 GW.
- **Telecom analogy:** A great way to anchor any reform answer.
- **Three legacy laws replaced by EA-2003:** Indian Electricity Act **1910**, Electricity (Supply) Act **1948**, Electricity Regulatory Commissions Act **1998**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy and Environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy and Environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Energy — Formal Engineering Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**: Energy is the fundamental physical quantity that measures the *capacity of a system to perform work* or to *transfer heat*. It is a state function — meaning the energy of a system depends only on its current state, not on the path by which it arrived at that state.

In SI units, energy is measured in **Joules (J)**, where **1 J = 1 N·m = 1 kg·m²/s²**. The standard thermodynamic sign convention states that energy *entering* a system is **positive (+)** and energy *leaving* is **negative (−)**.

Other commonly used engineering units include:
- **Kilowatt-hour (kWh)**: 1 kWh = **3.6 × 10⁶ J = 3.6 MJ**
- **British Thermal Unit (BTU)**: 1 BTU = **1055.056 J**
- **Tonne of Oil Equivalent (TOE)**: 1 TOE ≈ **41.868 GJ**
- **Tonne of Coal Equivalent (TCE)**: 1 TCE ≈ **29.288 GJ**
- **Calorie (cal)**: 1 cal = **4.184 J**

## 1.2 Environment — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Environment (KTU 2024 Definition)**: The environment encompasses all external physical, chemical, biological, and socio-economic conditions that surround and influence a living organism or an engineered system. In the context of energy systems, "environment" specifically refers to the natural surroundings — atmosphere, hydrosphere, lithosphere, and biosphere — which are affected by energy extraction, conversion, and consumption activities.

## 1.3 The Energy–Environment Nexus

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept**: Every form of energy production and consumption interacts with the environment. This two-way relationship is called the *Energy–Environment Nexus*. Energy is needed to support environmental services (water purification, climate regulation), while the environment provides the *resources* (coal, sunlight, wind, water) needed to produce energy.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "Energy as the Currency of the Universe"

Imagine a **giant kitchen**:
- **Energy is like money (₹)** — you need it to "buy" heat, motion, light, and electricity.
- **The Environment is like the kitchen itself** — it provides the *ingredients* (coal, oil, sunlight, wind, water) needed to "earn" energy.
- Just as a careless cook dirties the kitchen (oil stains, smoke, wasted food), careless energy use pollutes the environment (CO₂, soot, thermal discharge).
- Just as a smart cook keeps the kitchen clean and uses ingredients efficiently, sustainable energy systems keep emissions low and use resources wisely.

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU exam answers**: Whenever you write about Energy & Environment, always structure your answer as a *two-way street* — energy affects the environment, and the environment (climate, resources) affects energy availability.

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in PERAT637

This is the **foundational module** of the entire Energy Systems course. Every subsequent topic — thermal power plants, renewable energy, energy storage, energy economics — builds on the basics defined here. KTU examiners frequently test this topic because it allows assessment at **all six Bloom's Taxonomy cognitive levels** (Remember → Create).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Energy — The Two Master Categories

Energy resources are broadly divided into two categories based on **replenishment timescale** relative to human consumption rates.

| **Category** | **Definition** | **Examples** | **Replenishment Time** |
|--------------|----------------|--------------|------------------------|
| **Conventional (Non-Renewable)** | Finite resources formed over geological timescales; depleted by use | Coal, Crude Oil, Natural Gas, Uranium | Millions of years |
| **Non-Conventional (Renewable)** | Continuously replenished by natural processes at rates matching/exceeding human use | Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal | Continuous / Daily |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Terminology Note**: KTU officially uses *"Conventional"* and *"Non-Conventional"* rather than "renewable/non-renewable". Use the KTU terms in your exam answers for maximum marks.

### 2.1.1 Sub-classification of Conventional Energy

1. **Primary Energy Sources** — Found in nature (coal, crude oil, sun, wind).
2. **Secondary Energy Sources** — Derived from primary sources via conversion (electricity from coal, gasoline from crude oil).
3. **Tertiary Energy Sources** — End-use forms ready for consumption (light, mechanical shaft work).

## 2.2 Forms of Energy — The Eight Physical Forms

Every energy system in the universe manifests as one (or a combination) of these eight forms:

1. **Mechanical Energy** — sum of kinetic + potential energy
2. **Thermal (Internal) Energy** — molecular kinetic energy
3. **Chemical Energy** — stored in molecular bonds
4. **Electrical Energy** — energy of moving charges
5. **Nuclear Energy** — stored in atomic nucleus
6. **Radiant (Electromagnetic) Energy** — photons, sunlight
7. **Sound Energy** — mechanical wave propagation
8. **Mass Energy** — via Einstein's E = mc²

## 2.3 Energy Chain (Energy Route) — Conceptual Framework

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition**: The **Energy Chain** (also called *Energy Route*) is the complete sequence of stages through which energy flows from its natural source to its final useful application. For any energy system, the chain typically has these stages:

**Source → Extraction → Conversion → Storage/Transmission → Distribution → End Use → Environmental Impact**

## 2.4 Environmental Impacts of Energy Systems

| **Environmental Impact** | **Primary Cause** | **Affected Sphere** |
|--------------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Global Warming** | CO₂, CH₄, N₂O emissions from fossil combustion | Atmosphere |
| **Acid Rain** | SO₂, NOₓ emissions | Atmosphere → Soil/Water |
| **Particulate Pollution** | PM2.5, PM10 from coal/diesel | Atmosphere (local) |
| **Thermal Pollution** | Waste heat discharge from power plants | Hydrosphere |
| **Land Degradation** | Mining, drilling, hydro reservoirs | Lithosphere |
| **Radioactive Waste** | Spent nuclear fuel | Biosphere |
| **Oil Spills** | Extraction/transport accidents | Hydrosphere + Biosphere |

## 2.5 Greenhouse Effect — Mechanism

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition**: The Greenhouse Effect is the natural process by which *greenhouse gases (GHGs)* in Earth's atmosphere absorb and re-emit infrared radiation emitted by Earth's surface, thereby trapping heat and keeping the planet warm enough to support life (~15 °C average). Without it, Earth's average surface temperature would be **≈ −18 °C**.

### Key Greenhouse Gases (GHGs) and Their Global Warming Potential (GWP)

| **Gas** | **Chemical Formula** | **Major Anthropogenic Source** | **GWP (100-year, AR6)** | **Atmospheric Lifetime** |
|---------|----------------------|--------------------------------|-------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Carbon Dioxide** | CO₂ | Fossil fuel combustion, deforestation | **1** (reference) | Hundreds–thousands of years |
| **Methane** | CH₄ | Livestock, landfills, natural gas leaks | **≈ 28** | ~12 years |
| **Nitrous Oxide** | N₂O | Agricultural fertilizers, combustion | **≈ 265** | ~114 years |
| **Sulphur Hexafluoride** | SF₆ | Electrical switchgear | **≈ 23,500** | 3,200 years |
| **Hydrofluorocarbons** | HFCs | Refrigerants, air conditioning | **12 – 14,800** | 1–270 years |

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall**: Students often confuse GWP with atmospheric lifetime. *GWP measures the warming impact of 1 kg of gas over 100 years relative to 1 kg of CO₂*, while *lifetime is how long the gas persists in the atmosphere*.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Energy & Environment Master Table

| **#** | **Formula** | **Meaning** | **Typical Units** |
|-------|-------------|-------------|-------------------|
| 1 | $E = W + Q$ | First Law — energy = work + heat | J |
| 2 | $E = mc^{2}$ | Einstein mass-energy equivalence | J |
| 3 | $GCV = NCV + (9 H \cdot \dfrac{h}{100})$ | Relation between Gross and Net Calorific Value | kJ/kg |
| 4 | $NCV = GCV - 2.442 \cdot \dfrac{M}{8} - 2.442 \cdot \dfrac{9H}{100}$ | NCV correction for moisture + hydrogen | kJ/kg |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{\text{Net Work Output}}{\text{Heat Input}}$ | Thermal efficiency (dimensionless) | — |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{th}} \cdot \eta_{\text{mech}} \cdot \eta_{\text{gen}}$ | Overall plant efficiency product | — |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2} = \dot{m}_{\text{fuel}} \cdot C \cdot 3.667$ | CO₂ emission rate (C → CO₂ ratio = 44/12) | kg/s |
| 8 | $\text{EUA} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{CO}_2}}{\dot{W}_{\text{net}}}$ | Emission per unit electricity | kg CO₂/kWh |
| 9 | $\text{SPF} = \dfrac{\text{Useful Energy Output}}{\text{Primary Energy Input}}$ | Energy efficiency ratio | — |
| 10 | $E_{\text{stored}} = P \cdot t$ | Stored energy = power × time | J or kWh |

> [!TIP]
> In formula 7 above, the constant **3.667 = 44/12** arises because the molar mass of carbon (C) is **12 g/mol** and that of CO₂ is **44 g/mol**. Every 12 kg of carbon burned produces 44 kg of CO₂.

## 2.7 Sustainable Development — The Brundtland Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (UN Brundtland Report, 1987)**: *"Sustainable development is development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs."*

The energy-environment intersection directly supports the **three pillars of sustainable development**:
1. **Economic sustainability** — affordable, reliable energy.
2. **Environmental sustainability** — minimal emissions, low ecological footprint.
3. **Social sustainability** — universal energy access, public health protection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Unit Conversions in Energy Engineering

### Problem Context
A thermal power station consumes **1 tonne of coal per hour** with a Gross Calorific Value (GCV) of **25 MJ/kg**. We need to convert this energy flow into three common engineering units: **(a) kWh, (b) BTU, (c) TOE**, to understand cross-domain energy reporting.

### Given Data
- Mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_{c} = 1 \text{ tonne/h} = 1000 \text{ kg/h}$
- GCV of coal: $G = 25 \text{ MJ/kg}$

### Step 1 — Compute the Energy Released Per Hour

The energy released is the product of mass flow rate and calorific value:

$$
E_{h} = \dot{m}_{c} \cdot G
$$

Substituting values:

$$
E_{h} = 1000 \text{ kg/h} \times 25 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

$$
E_{h} = 25\,000 \text{ MJ/h}
$$

**Logic**: Each kg of coal releases 25 MJ. For 1000 kg, we multiply by 1000 to get total MJ released in one hour.

### Step 2 — Convert MJ to kWh

Using the conversion **1 kWh = 3.6 MJ**, we apply:

$$
E_{\text{kWh}} = \dfrac{E_{h} \text{ (MJ)}}{3.6}
$$

$$
E_{\text{kWh}} = \dfrac{25\,000}{3.6}
$$

$$
E_{\text{kWh}} \approx 6944.44 \text{ kWh/h}
$$

**Logic**: Since 1 kWh equals 3.6 MJ, we divide total MJ by 3.6 to obtain the equivalent kWh.

### Step 3 — Convert MJ to BTU

Using the conversion **1 MJ ≈ 947.817 BTU**:

$$
E_{\text{BTU}} = E_{h} \times 947.817
$$

$$
E_{\text{BTU}} = 25\,000 \times 947.817
$$

$$
E_{\text{BTU}} \approx 2.37 \times 10^{7} \text{ BTU/h}
$$

**Logic**: The BTU is the imperial unit still used in US/UK power plant specifications. We multiply MJ by the standard MJ→BTU factor.

### Step 4 — Convert MJ to TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent)

Using the conversion **1 TOE = 41.868 GJ = 41\,868 MJ**:

$$
E_{\text{TOE}} = \dfrac{E_{h}}{41\,868}
$$

$$
E_{\text{TOE}} = \dfrac{25\,000}{41\,868}
$$

$$
E_{\text{TOE}} \approx 0.5972 \text{ TOE/h}
$$

### Final Summary Table

| **Quantity** | **Value** | **Engineering Context** |
|--------------|-----------|-------------------------|
| Energy per hour | 25 000 MJ/h | Raw SI form |
| Energy per hour | 6 944.44 kWh/h | Indian power-plant billing |
| Energy per hour | 2.37 × 10⁷ BTU/h | US thermal specs |
| Energy per hour | 0.5972 TOE/h | International energy reports |

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Net Calorific Value (NCV) from Gross Calorific Value (GCV)

### Physical Background
When coal is burned, the **hydrogen** in fuel combines with oxygen to form **water vapour**. If this vapour escapes (as in a furnace exhausting hot gases), the latent heat of vaporization is lost. The **Gross Calorific Value (GCV)** assumes this water is condensed back to liquid (recovers latent heat), while the **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** assumes water exits as vapour (no latent heat recovered). Industrial calculations almost always use **NCV**.

### Standard Formula (Unified)

$$
NCV = GCV - 2.442 \cdot \left( \dfrac{M}{8} \right) - 2.442 \cdot \left( \dfrac{9 H}{100} \right)
$$

where:
- $M$ = moisture content of fuel (percentage by mass, **%**)
- $H$ = hydrogen content of fuel (percentage by mass, **%**)
- The factor **2.442 kJ/kg** is the latent heat of vaporization of water at **standard reference conditions** (often at 25 °C).

### Worked Numerical Example

**Given**: A coal sample has **GCV = 25 MJ/kg**, moisture **M = 8%**, hydrogen **H = 4%**.

### Step 1 — Calculate the Moisture Correction Term

$$
L_{M} = 2.442 \cdot \left( \dfrac{M}{8} \right)
$$

$$
L_{M} = 2.442 \cdot \left( \dfrac{8}{8} \right)
$$

$$
L_{M} = 2.442 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

**Logic**: Moisture must first be evaporated (consuming latent heat) before fuel can burn. The 1/8 factor comes from the fact that **1 kg of moisture = 1/9 kg of H equivalent** to produce water from combustion, but the 8 divisor is an empirical engineering average.

### Step 2 — Calculate the Hydrogen Correction Term

$$
L_{H} = 2.442 \cdot \left( \dfrac{9 H}{100} \right)
$$

$$
L_{H} = 2.442 \cdot \left( \dfrac{9 \times 4}{100} \right)
$$

$$
L_{H} = 2.442 \cdot 0.36
$$

$$
L_{H} = 0.879 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

**Logic**: Each kg of hydrogen in fuel produces 9 kg of water upon combustion. Thus, multiplying H% by 9 gives the mass of water formed per kg of fuel, and then latent heat 2.442 MJ/kg is applied.

### Step 3 — Subtract Both Corrections from GCV

$$
NCV = GCV - L_{M} - L_{H}
$$

$$
NCV = 25 - 2.442 - 0.879
$$

$$
\boxed{NCV \approx 21.679 \text{ MJ/kg}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight**: The difference between GCV and NCV (here ≈ 3.32 MJ/kg, or **13.3%**) is significant. Power plant heat-rate calculations must use **NCV**, or efficiency will be over-reported.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: CO₂ Emission Per Unit of Electricity Generated

### Problem Context
A coal-fired plant with **η_plant = 35%** burns coal containing **C = 60% carbon by mass**, with **GCV = 24 MJ/kg**. Find the specific CO₂ emission in **kg CO₂ per kWh** of electricity generated.

### Step 1 — Determine the Carbon Burned Per kWh of Electricity Produced

The heat input required per kWh of electricity, given plant efficiency $\eta$:

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{1 \text{ kWh}}{\eta} = \dfrac{3.6 \text{ MJ}}{\eta}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{3.6}{0.35}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{in}} \approx 10.286 \text{ MJ of fuel energy per kWh}
$$

### Step 2 — Determine the Mass of Fuel Per kWh

$$
m_{\text{fuel}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{in}}}{GCV} = \dfrac{10.286 \text{ MJ}}{24 \text{ MJ/kg}}
$$

$$
m_{\text{fuel}} \approx 0.4286 \text{ kg of coal per kWh}
$$

### Step 3 — Determine the Mass of Carbon in That Fuel

$$
m_{C} = m_{\text{fuel}} \cdot \dfrac{C}{100} = 0.4286 \cdot 0.60
$$

$$
m_{C} \approx 0.2571 \text{ kg of C per kWh}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert Carbon to CO₂ Using the Stoichiometric Factor

Each 12 kg of carbon produces 44 kg of CO₂. Thus the conversion factor is **44/12 ≈ 3.667**:

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2} = m_{C} \cdot \dfrac{44}{12}
$$

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2} = 0.2571 \times 3.667
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{\text{CO}_2} \approx 0.943 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ per kWh}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Benchmark**: A typical sub-critical coal power plant emits **0.85–1.05 kg CO₂/kWh**. Our result (0.943 kg/kWh) is well within this range, validating the calculation. A modern **supercritical** plant operating at **η = 42%** would drop this to ≈ 0.78 kg CO₂/kWh.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Environmental Footprint

| **Energy Source** | **CO₂ Emission (kg/kWh)** | **Water Use (L/kWh)** | **Land Use (m²/MWh)** | **Air Pollutants** |
|-------------------|---------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|--------------------|
| Coal (sub-critical) | **0.95** | 1100 | High | SO₂, NOₓ, PM, Hg |
| Natural Gas (CCGT) | **0.40** | 700 | Low | NOₓ, CH₄ leaks |
| Oil (Diesel) | **0.75** | 800 | Medium | SO₂, NOₓ, PM |
| Nuclear | **0.012** | 2500 | Very Low | None (operational); radioactive waste |
| Solar PV (utility) | **0.045** | 30 | High | None |
| Onshore Wind | **0.011** | <5 | Very High | None |
| Hydroelectric | **0.024** | Large (evap.) | Huge (flooding) | CH₄ from reservoirs |
| Biomass | **0.45** | 800 | Very High | PM (if inefficient) |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Takeaway**: Wind and nuclear have the **lowest life-cycle CO₂ emissions**, while coal has the **highest**. However, hydro and biomass — though renewable — can have significant local ecological impacts.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Hierarchical Classification of Energy Resources

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ENERGY RESOURCES]:::root --> B[CONVENTIONAL]:::branch
    A --> C[NON CONVENTIONAL]:::branch

    B --> B1[Fossil Fuels]:::sub
    B --> B2[Nuclear Fuels]:::sub

    B1 --> B1a["Coal Lignite Bituminous Anthracite"]:::leaf
    B1 --> B1b["Oil Crude Petroleum"]:::leaf
    B1 --> B1c["Natural Gas Methane"]:::leaf

    B2 --> B2a["Uranium 235U"]:::leaf
    B2 --> B2b["Thorium 232Th"]:::leaf

    C --> C1[Solar Energy]:::sub
    C --> C2[Wind Energy]:::sub
    C --> C3[Hydro Energy]:::sub
    C --> C4[Biomass Energy]:::sub
    C --> C5[Geothermal Energy]:::sub
    C --> C6[Tidal and Wave Energy]:::sub

    C1 --> C1a["Photovoltaic PV"]:::leaf
    C1 --> C1b["Concentrated Solar Power CSP"]:::leaf

    C3 --> C3a["Large Hydro above 25MW"]:::leaf
    C3 --> C3b["Small Hydro below 25MW"]:::leaf

    C4 --> C4a["Wood and Agro Residue"]:::leaf
    C4 --> C4b["Biogas and Biodiesel"]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1f2d5a,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef branch fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef sub fill:#f9a825,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leaf fill:#e0e0e0,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
```

## 4.2 Diagram: The Energy–Environment Interaction Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Natural Resources Coal Oil Gas Sun Wind Water Uranium]:::source

    INPUT --> EXTRACT[Extraction Mining Drilling Pumping]:::stage
    EXTRACT --> CONVERT[Conversion Combustion Nuclear Solar PV Wind Turbine]:::stage
    CONVERT --> TRANSMIT[Transmission and Distribution Grid Network]:::stage
    TRANSMIT --> USE[End Use Industrial Transport Residential Commercial]:::stage

    USE --> WASTE1[Emissions CO2 SO2 NOx PM]:::pollute
    USE --> WASTE2[Waste Heat Thermal Discharge]:::pollute
    USE --> WASTE3[Solid Waste Ash Slag Spent Fuel]:::pollute
    USE --> WASTE4[Land Use Mining Footprint Reservoir Flooding]:::pollute

    WASTE1 --> ENVATM[Atmosphere Pollution Climate Change]:::envimpact
    WASTE2 --> ENVHYD[Hydrosphere Thermal Pollution]:::envimpact
    WASTE3 --> ENVLITH[Geosphere Solid Waste Accumulation]:::envimpact
    WASTE4 --> ENVBIO[Biosphere Habitat Loss]:::envimpact

    ENVATM -.-> FEEDBACK[Climate Feedback Loop]:::feedback
    ENVBIO -.-> FEEDBACK
    ENVHYD -.-> FEEDBACK
    ENVLITH -.-> FEEDBACK

    FEEDBACK -.-> INPUT

    classDef source fill:#0d47a1,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef stage fill:#00838f,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pollute fill:#c62828,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef envimpact fill:#4a148c,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef feedback fill:#e65100,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,stroke-dasharray: 5 5
```

## 4.3 Diagram: The Complete Energy Chain (From Source to End Use)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[SOURCE Primary Energy]:::s --> B[EXTRACTION Harvesting and Mining]:::s
    B --> C[CONVERSION and REFINING]:::s
    C --> D[STORAGE Optional]:::s
    D --> E[TRANSMISSION Grid Pipeline]:::s
    E --> F[DISTRIBUTION Last Mile]:::s
    F --> G[END USER Industry Transport Domestic Agriculture]:::s
    G --> H[ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT Emissions Waste Heat Land Use]:::impact
    H --> I[MITIGATION Pollution Control Carbon Capture Renewables]:::mitigate
    I -.-> C

    classDef s fill:#1565c0,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef impact fill:#b71c1c,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef mitigate fill:#2e7d32,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read this diagram**: Each block in the chain incurs energy losses (typically 5–15% per stage for thermal systems; lower for electrical systems). The cumulative loss from source to end-use defines the **overall system efficiency**. The dashed feedback line indicates that mitigation measures (renewables, carbon capture, pollution control) feed back into the conversion stage to reduce emissions.

## 4.4 Diagram: Greenhouse Effect Mechanism (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    SUN[Incoming Solar Radiation Shortwave 342 W per m2]:::sun
    ATMOS[Earth Atmosphere with GHGs]:::atmos
    SURFACE[Earth Surface Absorbs 168 W per m2]:::surface
    REEMIT[Earth Re emits Longwave Infrared 390 W per m2]:::surface
    TRAP[GHGs Absorb and Re emit IR downward]:::atmos

    SUN -->|passes through| ATMOS
    ATMOS -->|partly absorbed partly transmitted| SURFACE
    SURFACE -->|warms and re radiates| REEMIT
    REEMIT -->|IR radiation intercepted| TRAP
    TRAP -->|re emit IR back to surface| SURFACE
    TRAP -->|re emit IR upward to space| ATMOS

    classDef sun fill:#fbc02d,color:#000,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef atmos fill:#558b2f,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef surface fill:#5d4037,color:#fff,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note**: The numbers shown (342 W/m² incoming, 168 W/m² absorbed by surface, 390 W/m² outgoing IR) are the **IPCC-accepted global energy balance values**. They reveal that GHGs trap approximately **155 W/m²** of extra IR radiation — equivalent to **~1 °C of warming** today and rising.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (Each 3 Marks)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**
**Define the term "Energy". Explain its various forms with at least four examples.**

**Model Answer**:
Energy is defined as the capacity of a system to do work or transfer heat. Its SI unit is the **Joule (J)**.

Major forms of energy include:
- **Mechanical Energy** — energy of motion and position (e.g., wind turning a turbine).
- **Thermal Energy** — random molecular motion (e.g., heat from burning coal).
- **Chemical Energy** — stored in molecular bonds (e.g., energy in fossil fuels).
- **Electrical Energy** — flow of electrons (e.g., power from a generator).
- **Nuclear Energy** — energy released from splitting/fusing atoms (e.g., nuclear power plant).
- **Radiant Energy** — energy carried by photons (e.g., sunlight).

**Marks Distribution** (per KTU valuation key):
- Definition: 1 mark
- Minimum 4 forms: 2 marks (0.5 each)

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern]**
**Distinguish between Conventional and Non-Conventional energy sources. Give two examples each.**

**Model Answer**:

| **Basis** | **Conventional** | **Non-Conventional** |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Replenishment | Not replenished on human timescale | Continuously replenished |
| Stock | Finite | Essentially infinite |
| Examples | Coal, Oil, Natural Gas, Uranium | Solar, Wind, Tidal, Biomass |
| Pollution | High (CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ) | Low or zero |
| Capital Cost | Moderate | High (declining) |

**Marks Distribution**:
- One-line definition each: 1 mark
- Tabular distinction (3+ points): 1 mark
- Two examples each: 1 mark

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (Each 14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern**: Part B questions carry 14 marks and offer internal choice. Each sub-question (a) and (b) carries **7 marks**. Model answer below shows full working expected for **7/7 marks**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**Part (a) (7 Marks)**: A coal sample has the following ultimate analysis by mass: C = 65%, H = 4%, O = 8%, N = 1%, S = 2%, Moisture = 6%, Ash = 14%. Calculate the **Net Calorific Value (NCV)** if the GCV is given as **24.5 MJ/kg**.

**Model Solution — Step by Step**:

#### Step 1 — Recall the NCV Formula

$$
NCV = GCV - 2.442 \cdot \left( \dfrac{M}{8} \right) - 2.442 \cdot \left( \dfrac{9H}{100} \right)
$$

**Logic**: The two correction terms account for the latent heat lost in (i) evaporating the inherent moisture, and (ii) the water formed from burning the hydrogen in fuel.

#### Step 2 — Calculate the Moisture Correction

Given M = 6%:

$$
L_M = 2.442 \cdot \dfrac{6}{8}
$$

$$
L_M = 2.442 \cdot 0.75
$$

$$
L_M = 1.8315 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate the Hydrogen Correction

Given H = 4%:

$$
L_H = 2.442 \cdot \dfrac{9 \times 4}{100}
$$

$$
L_H = 2.442 \cdot 0.36
$$

$$
L_H = 0.8791 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

#### Step 4 — Apply the Formula

$$
NCV = 24.5 - 1.8315 - 0.8791
$$

$$
NCV = 24.5 - 2.7106
$$

$$
\boxed{NCV \approx 21.79 \text{ MJ/kg}}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Marks Allocation**: 2 marks for correct formula, 2 marks for each correction term, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final answer, 1 mark for unit. Answer with NCV = 21.79 MJ/kg secures full 7 marks.

---

**Part (b) (7 Marks)**: With the NCV obtained above, calculate the **CO₂ emission per kWh** of electricity generated if the power plant has an overall thermal efficiency of **34%**.

**Model Solution — Step by Step**:

#### Step 1 — Heat Input Required Per kWh

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{1 \text{ kWh}}{\eta} = \dfrac{3.6 \text{ MJ}}{0.34}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{in}} \approx 10.588 \text{ MJ of fuel per kWh}
$$

**Logic**: To produce 1 kWh of electricity at 34% efficiency, we must supply 1/0.34 = 2.94 kWh (or 10.588 MJ) of fuel energy.

#### Step 2 — Mass of Fuel Per kWh

$$
m_{\text{fuel}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{in}}}{NCV} = \dfrac{10.588}{21.79}
$$

$$
m_{\text{fuel}} \approx 0.4859 \text{ kg of coal per kWh}
$$

#### Step 3 — Mass of Carbon Burned

Given C = 65%:

$$
m_C = 0.4859 \times 0.65 \approx 0.3158 \text{ kg C per kWh}
$$

#### Step 4 — Convert Carbon to CO₂

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2} = m_C \cdot \dfrac{44}{12} = 0.3158 \times 3.667
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{\text{CO}_2} \approx 1.158 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ per kWh}}
$$

#### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation

This value (≈ 1.16 kg CO₂/kWh) indicates a **sub-critical, low-efficiency** plant. Modern supercritical units operating at **42% efficiency** would emit only **≈ 0.83 kg CO₂/kWh**. Adopting **carbon capture (CCS)** could reduce this further by **~85–90%**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)

**Part (a) (7 Marks)**: Explain the **Greenhouse Effect** with a neat sketch. Discuss the major greenhouse gases and their Global Warming Potential (GWP).

**Model Answer Outline**:

1. **Sketch** (2 marks): Mermaid-style diagram as in Section 4.4 — incoming solar, re-emitted IR, GHG trapping.
2. **Mechanism (3 marks)**:
   - Sun emits **shortwave radiation** that mostly passes through atmosphere.
   - Earth's surface absorbs this and warms to **~288 K (15 °C)**.
   - Earth re-emits energy as **longwave infrared radiation**.
   - GHGs absorb IR, vibrate, and re-emit in all directions — including **back to Earth**.
   - This **trapped energy** raises Earth's average surface temperature — without GHGs, Earth would be **−18 °C**.
3. **Major GHGs with GWP (2 marks)**:
   - CO₂: GWP = 1, share ≈ 76% of warming.
   - CH₄: GWP ≈ 28, share ≈ 16%.
   - N₂O: GWP ≈ 265, share ≈ 6%.
   - HFCs: GWP 12–14,800, share ≈ 2%.
   - SF₆: GWP ≈ 23,500 (used in electrical switchgear).
4. **Industrial Sources (≈ 0 marks, but adds completeness)**: Power plants, transport, cement, livestock.

---

**Part (b) (7 Marks)**: A 100 MW thermal power plant operates at an overall efficiency of **38%** using coal with carbon content **62%** and GCV **26 MJ/kg**. Calculate: (i) Daily coal consumption, (ii) Daily CO₂ emissions, and (iii) Specific CO₂ emission in kg/kWh.

**Model Solution — Step by Step**:

#### Step 1 — Daily Electrical Energy Output

$$
E_{\text{out}} = 100 \text{ MW} \times 24 \text{ h} = 2400 \text{ MWh/day} = 2.4 \times 10^{6} \text{ kWh/day}
$$

#### Step 2 — Daily Heat Input Required

$$
Q_{\text{in}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{out}}}{\eta} = \dfrac{2400 \text{ MWh}}{0.38}
$$

$$
Q_{\text{in}} \approx 6315.79 \text{ MWh/day} = 6315.79 \times 3600 \text{ MJ/day} = 2.274 \times 10^{7} \text{ MJ/day}
$$

#### Step 3 — Daily Coal Consumption

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{coal}} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{in}}}{GCV} = \dfrac{2.274 \times 10^{7}}{26}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{m}_{\text{coal}} \approx 8.746 \times 10^{5} \text{ kg/day} \approx 874.6 \text{ tonnes/day}}
$$

#### Step 4 — Daily Carbon Burned

$$
m_C = 874.6 \times 10^{3} \times 0.62
$$

$$
m_C \approx 542.3 \text{ tonnes of C per day}
$$

#### Step 5 — Daily CO₂ Emissions

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2} = m_C \times 3.667
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{\text{CO}_2} \approx 1988.7 \text{ tonnes of CO}_2 \text{ per day}}
$$

#### Step 6 — Specific CO₂ Emission

$$
\text{Specific Emission} = \dfrac{1988.7 \times 10^{3} \text{ kg}}{2.4 \times 10^{6} \text{ kWh}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Specific Emission} \approx 0.829 \text{ kg CO}_2 \text{ per kWh}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Common Mistake #1**: Many students use **GCV instead of NCV**. Always use the value provided in the problem statement (here GCV is given; if NCV is given, use NCV).
> 2. **Common Mistake #2**: Forgetting to convert **C → CO₂** using the factor **44/12 = 3.667**. Direct emission of "carbon" is never asked — always express as CO₂.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3**: Units mismatch — mixing tonnes/day with kg/kWh. Keep **SI throughout** until the final answer, then convert for readability.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4**: Not converting **MWh to MJ** when needed (factor = 3600). A surprising number of students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5**: In sketches of the Greenhouse Effect, students often forget to show **both directions** of IR re-emission by GHGs (one part back to Earth, one part to space). This is essential for full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before the Exam**

- [ ] **Energy** = capacity to do work or transfer heat; SI unit = **Joule (J)**.
- [ ] **Environment** = all external conditions surrounding a system (atmosphere, hydrosphere, lithosphere, biosphere).
- [ ] **Energy–Environment Nexus** — two-way relationship; energy production affects environment, environmental conditions affect energy availability.
- [ ] **Conventional (Non-Renewable)**: Coal, Oil, Gas, Uranium — finite, polluting.
- [ ] **Non-Conventional (Renewable)**: Solar, Wind, Hydro, Biomass, Geothermal, Tidal — replenishable, clean.
- [ ] **Eight Forms of Energy**: Mechanical, Thermal, Chemical, Electrical, Nuclear, Radiant, Sound, Mass-energy.
- [ ] **Energy Chain**: Source → Extraction → Conversion → Storage → Transmission → Distribution → End Use → Environmental Impact.
- [ ] **Primary vs Secondary vs Tertiary** energy classifications.
- [ ] **GCV vs NCV**: NCV is what industrial boilers actually deliver. NCV ≈ GCV − 2.442(M/8 + 9H/100).
- [ ] **Conversion factor C → CO₂ = 44/12 = 3.667**.
- [ ] **CO₂ to kWh specific emission benchmark**: Coal ≈ 0.95 kg/kWh, Gas ≈ 0.40 kg/kWh, Wind/Solar ≈ 0.01–0.05 kg/kWh.
- [ ] **Greenhouse Effect**: Natural warming due to GHGs trapping IR. Without GHGs, Earth would be −18 °C; with GHGs, it's +15 °C.
- [ ] **Top GHGs by GWP**: CO₂ (1), CH₄ (28), N₂O (265), HFCs (12–14,800), SF₆ (23,500).
- [ ] **Sustainable Development** (Brundtland 1987): meeting present needs without compromising future generations.
- [ ] **Unit Conversions**: 1 kWh = 3.6 MJ; 1 BTU = 1055.056 J; 1 TOE = 41.868 GJ; 1 TCE = 29.288 GJ.
- [ ] **Energy efficiency = useful output energy / primary input energy** (always dimensionless ratio).
- [ ] **3 Pillars of Sustainable Development**: Economic, Environmental, Social.
- [ ] **IPCC Energy Balance numbers** to memorize: 342 W/m² incoming solar, 168 W/m² absorbed by surface, 390 W/m² outgoing IR.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy Tip**: For 14-mark questions, always (1) state the relevant formula, (2) list all given data with units, (3) substitute values step-by-step, (4) compute intermediate results in SI units, (5) give the final answer with **correct units and 3 significant figures**, and (6) add a **1–2 line engineering interpretation**. This structured approach routinely earns 12–14/14 marks in KTU valuations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Security
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Security

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (IEA – International Energy Agency)**
> *Energy Security is defined as the uninterrupted availability of energy sources at an affordable price.* — Adopted as the benchmark definition in the KTU 2024 Energy Systems syllabus.

**Energy Security** in the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme is treated as a **multi-dimensional macro-engineering concept** that integrates:

1. **Availability** – Physical existence of energy reserves/resources.
2. **Accessibility** – Geographic, geopolitical, and infrastructural reach.
3. **Affordability** – Economic capacity of consumers and nations.
4. **Acceptability** – Environmental and social sustainability of supply.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner Insight:** Examiners expect students to quote the **"4 A's of Energy Security"** (Availability, Accessibility, Affordability, Acceptability) verbatim in 7-mark questions. This is a high-yield, high-frequency keyword combination.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a family that depends on a **single salary earner**. If that person loses their job, the entire household collapses financially. The family is **not financially secure**.

Similarly, a nation that depends on a **single energy source** (e.g., only imported crude oil) is **not energy secure**. If the supplier nation cuts off exports, or if oil prices spike, the importing nation faces an energy crisis.

**Energy Security is therefore the equivalent of a household having multiple income streams, savings, insurance, and the ability to grow its own food.** The nation must:
- **Diversify** its energy sources (solar, wind, hydro, nuclear, fossil).
- **Stockpile** strategic reserves (like a savings account).
- **Produce domestically** when possible (like growing your own food).
- **Build resilient grids and storage** (like having insurance).

### 1.3 Physical & Economic Metrics Used

The following constants and standard metrics are universally used in energy security calculations:

- **Barrel (bbl) = 159 litres** (standard crude oil unit).
- **TOE (Tonne of Oil Equivalent) = 42 GJ ≈ 5.8 MMBtu**.
- **Standard unit of energy** in policy documents: **Mtoe (Million Tonne of Oil Equivalent)**.
- **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratio** — measured in **years**.
- **Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) target** in India: **90 days of net imports** (as per IEA benchmark).

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** The **"4 A's"** are tested in nearly every KTU University Exam cycle. Memorize the order: **A**vailability → **A**ccessibility → **A**ffordability → **A**cceptability.

---

### 1.4 Why Energy Security is the Cornerstone of Module 1

Module 1 of PERAT637 introduces students to the **macro-environment** in which all energy systems operate. Energy Security is the **anchor concept** because:
- Every other topic (renewable integration, grid stability, storage, policy) ultimately serves the goal of energy security.
- It links **geopolitics, economics, environment, and engineering** — the four pillars of modern energy education.
- India is the **world's 3rd largest energy consumer**, making this directly relevant to KTU students entering the workforce.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Energy Security — Operational Logic

Each of the **4 A's** has measurable engineering and policy instruments. The following structured breakdown reflects what KTU examiners expect in **14-mark analytical questions**.

#### 2.1.1 Availability
- Refers to the **physical existence of primary energy resources** within national boundaries or accessible through trade.
- Measured through **proved reserves** (fossil fuels) and **recoverable resource estimates** (renewables).
- Key indicator: **R/P ratio (Reserve-to-Production)**.
- **Why it matters:** A high R/P ratio means the country has long-term supply assurance. India's R/P for crude oil is only ~5–6 years, indicating severe availability constraints.

#### 2.1.2 Accessibility
- Refers to the **infrastructure and geopolitical freedom** required to extract, transport, and deliver energy.
- Pipelines, ports, refineries, transmission grids, and LNG terminals are accessibility enablers.
- **Geopolitical choke points** (Strait of Hormuz, Malacca Strait, Suez Canal) directly threaten accessibility.

#### 2.1.3 Affordability
- Refers to **price stability and consumer purchasing power**.
- Measured through **energy intensity** and **energy cost as % of GDP**.
- Sudden price shocks (e.g., 1973 Oil Crisis, 2022 Russia-Ukraine war) threaten affordability.

#### 2.1.4 Acceptability
- Refers to the **environmental and social sustainability** of the energy mix.
- Renewable energy and clean fossil technologies (CCUS) improve acceptability.
- Carbon taxes, ESG compliance, and SDGs fall under this dimension.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **authoritative formula reference** for all Energy Security numerical problems in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| **Formula / Index** | **Mathematical Form** | **Variables & Units** | **Engineering Interpretation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Import Dependency (ID)** | $ID = \dfrac{I_{net}}{TPES} \times 100$ | $I_{net}$ = Net imports (Mtoe); $TPES$ = Total Primary Energy Supply (Mtoe) | Higher ID = Lower energy security |
| **Reserve-to-Production (R/P)** | $R/P = \dfrac{R_{proved}}{P_{annual}}$ | $R_{proved}$ (tonnes); $P_{annual}$ (tonnes/year) | Result in **years** of remaining supply |
| **Shannon-Wiener Diversity Index (H)** | $H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \ln(p_i)$ | $p_i$ = fractional share of source $i$ in mix | Higher H = More diversified mix = Higher security |
| **Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI)** | $HHI = \sum_{i=1}^{n} s_i^{2}$ | $s_i$ = percentage share of source $i$ (decimal) | Lower HHI = More diversified = Higher security |
| **Energy Intensity (EI)** | $EI = \dfrac{TPES}{GDP}$ | TPES (Mtoe); GDP (Billion \$) | Lower EI = More efficient economy |
| **Self-Sufficiency Ratio (SSR)** | $SSR = \dfrac{D_{domestic}}{TPES} \times 100$ | $D_{domestic}$ = Domestic production (Mtoe) | Higher SSR = Higher security |
| **Strategic Reserve Days (SRD)** | $SRD = \dfrac{S_{reserve}}{I_{daily}} \times 100$ | $S_{reserve}$ (Mtoe); $I_{daily}$ (Mtoe/day) | Result in **days** of import cover |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Safety Protocol:** The vertical pipe $\vert$ character is **NEVER** used inside table cells to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. Use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX expressions.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas are **not academic abstractions**. They are the **decision-making backbone** of:

- **Ministry of Power (India)** — uses EI and ID for the *Energy Security Index* published in the Economic Survey.
- **OPEC** — uses R/P ratios to negotiate production quotas.
- **IEA Member Compliance** — countries must maintain SRD ≥ 90 days as a treaty obligation.
- **World Bank & IMF** — use EI as a macroeconomic efficiency benchmark.
- **Stock Markets** — HHI is used by analysts to assess geopolitical risk of energy companies.

### 2.4 The Energy Security Trilemma (World Energy Council Framework)

A globally accepted framework that **balances three competing goals** — visualized as a triangle:

1. **Energy Security** (reliability of supply)
2. **Energy Equity** (accessibility and affordability for all)
3. **Environmental Sustainability** (clean energy transition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specific:** A perfectly energy-secure nation balances all three vertices. India currently scores **"BBB"** (Balanced) on the WEC Trilemma Index, with strong equity but moderate security and sustainability.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1: Computing the Shannon-Wiener Diversity Index

**Problem:** A country has the following energy mix (in % of total primary energy):
- Coal: 45 %
- Oil: 25 %
- Natural Gas: 18 %
- Renewables: 8 %
- Nuclear: 4 %

Compute the Shannon-Wiener Diversity Index $H$ and comment on the security implication.

#### Step 1: Convert percentages to fractional shares

$$p_{coal} = 0.45, \quad p_{oil} = 0.25, \quad p_{gas} = 0.18, \quad p_{renew} = 0.08, \quad p_{nuclear} = 0.04$$

#### Step 2: Apply the Shannon-Wiener formula

$$H = -\sum_{i=1}^{n} p_i \ln(p_i)$$

#### Step 3: Compute each term separately

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{coal} \ln(p_{coal}) &= 0.45 \times \ln(0.45) = 0.45 \times (-0.7985) = -0.3593 \\
p_{oil} \ln(p_{oil}) &= 0.25 \times \ln(0.25) = 0.25 \times (-1.3863) = -0.3466 \\
p_{gas} \ln(p_{gas}) &= 0.18 \times \ln(0.18) = 0.18 \times (-1.7148) = -0.3087 \\
p_{renew} \ln(p_{renew}) &= 0.08 \times \ln(0.08) = 0.08 \times (-2.5257) = -0.2021 \\
p_{nuclear} \ln(p_{nuclear}) &= 0.04 \times \ln(0.04) = 0.04 \times (-3.2189) = -0.1288
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Sum the absolute values

$$
\begin{aligned}
H &= - [(-0.3593) + (-0.3466) + (-0.3087) + (-0.2021) + (-0.1288)] \\
H &= - [-1.3455] \\
H &= 1.3455
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5: Interpret the result

The maximum possible $H$ for $n = 5$ sources (perfectly equal mix of 20 % each) is:

$$H_{max} = \ln(5) = 1.6094$$

Therefore, the **diversification efficiency** is:

$$\eta_{div} = \frac{H}{H_{max}} \times 100 = \frac{1.3455}{1.6094} \times 100 = 83.6\%$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A value of 1.3455 indicates a **moderately diversified** energy mix. The high coal share (45 %) pulls the index down. To improve energy security, the country should increase renewables and nuclear shares.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2: Import Dependency Calculation

**Problem:** A nation has the following data for 2024:
- Total Primary Energy Supply (TPES) = 800 Mtoe
- Domestic production = 320 Mtoe
- Net energy imports = 480 Mtoe

Compute Import Dependency and Self-Sufficiency Ratio.

#### Step 1: Import Dependency

$$
ID = \frac{I_{net}}{TPES} \times 100 = \frac{480}{800} \times 100 = 60\%
$$

#### Step 2: Self-Sufficiency Ratio

$$
SSR = \frac{D_{domestic}}{TPES} \times 100 = \frac{320}{800} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

#### Step 3: Verification

$$
ID + SSR = 60\% + 40\% = 100\% \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This identity $ID + SSR = 100\%$ is a KTU board examiner favorite** for short conceptual cross-questions. A student is expected to prove or apply this in 3-mark questions.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Energy Security Threat vs Mitigation Strategy

The following matrix maps **real-world threats** to **engineering/policy mitigations**, which is a standard 14-mark descriptive question structure.

| **Threat Category** | **Specific Threat** | **Engineering Mitigation** | **Policy Mitigation** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Geopolitical** | Supplier nation embargo | Pipeline diversification; LNG terminals | Bilateral trade agreements; BRICS energy cooperation |
| **Economic** | Oil price volatility | Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR) | Hedging contracts; futures market participation |
| **Technical** | Grid failure / cyber attack | Smart grids; microgrids; storage | Cybersecurity regulations; CERT-In frameworks |
| **Environmental** | Climate change disruption | Renewables; CCUS; green hydrogen | Paris Agreement NDCs; Carbon tax |
| **Resource** | Reserve depletion | Enhanced Oil Recovery; renewable expansion | R&D subsidies; ISA (International Solar Alliance) |
| **Demand-side** | Demand spikes | Demand response; time-of-use tariffs | Energy conservation building codes (ECBC) |

### 3.4 Derivation of the R/P Ratio Sensitivity

The R/P ratio is **not static** — it changes as production rates evolve. A formal derivation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{proved} &= \int_{0}^{T} P(t) \, dt \quad \text{(Total reserves consumed over lifetime T)} \\
R/P &= \frac{R_{proved}}{P_{annual}} \quad \text{(in years, assuming constant production)} \\
\frac{d(R/P)}{dP_{annual}} &= -\frac{R_{proved}}{P_{annual}^{2}} < 0
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This negative derivative proves that **any increase in annual production rate reduces the R/P ratio** — meaning energy security deteriorates if extraction is accelerated without new discoveries. KTU examiners reward this kind of calculus-based reasoning in 14-mark questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Four A's of Energy Security — Hierarchical Flow

The following Mermaid block renders the **logical hierarchy** of the 4 A's framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    A --> E[ACCEPTABILITY]

    C --> C3[Geopolitical Stability of Trade Routes]

    D --> D1[Energy Intensity]
    D --> D2[Price Stability Mechanisms]
    D --> D3[Subsidies and Tariff Policy]

    E --> E1[Renewable Share]
    E --> E2[Carbon Emissions per TOE]
    E --> E3[ESG Compliance Score]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    ES --> ES1[Supply Reliability]

```

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    GOAL[INDIA ENERGY SECURITY GOAL 2047] --> P1[PRIMARY PILLAR]
    GOAL --> P2[SECONDARY PILLAR]
    GOAL --> P3[TERTIARY PILLAR]

    P1 --> P1A[Diversify Energy Mix]
    P1 --> P1B[Expand Renewables to 500 GW]

    P2 --> P2A[Strategic Petroleum Reserves 90 Days Plus]
    P2 --> P2B[Ethanol Blending 20 Percent]

    P3 --> P3A[Smart Grid and Storage 42 GWh]
```
