```mermaid
    C --> D[Step4 Run Energy Simulation eQUEST or EnergyPlus]
    D --> E[Step5 Document Compliance for Each Credit]
    E --> F[Step6 Internal Review and Quality Check]
    F --> G[Step7 Submission to GBCI for Review]
    G --> H{Decision Point: Score Achieved}
    H -->|40 to 49 points| I1[Certified Tier]
    H -->|50 to 59 points| I2[Silver Tier]
    H -->|60 to 79 points| I3[Gold Tier]
    H -->|80 plus points| I4[Platinum Tier]
    I1 --> J[Step8 Final Certification Issued by GBCI]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    J --> K[Step9 Post Occupancy Monitoring for Performance Verification]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Six LEED Credit Categories and their Sub-Credits

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Root[LEED v4 Total Score 110 Points] --> SS[Sustainable Sites 16 Points]
    Root --> WE[Water Efficiency 11 Points]
    Root --> EA[Energy and Atmosphere 33 Points]
    Root --> MR[Materials and Resources 13 Points]
    Root --> EQ[Indoor Environmental Quality 16 Points]
    Root --> ID[Innovation in Design 6 Points]
    Root --> RP[Regional Priority 4 Points]
    SS --> SS1[Site Selection]
    SS --> SS2[Heat Island Reduction]
    WE --> WE1[Water Use Reduction]
    WE --> WE2[Cooling Tower Water]
    EA --> EA1[Optimize Energy Performance]
    EA --> EA2[Renewable Energy Production]
    MR --> MR1[Building Reuse]
    MR --> MR2[Recycled Content]
    EQ --> EQ1[Daylight and Views]
    EQ --> EQ2[Low Emitting Materials]
    ID --> ID1[Innovation Strategies]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Comparison Hierarchy of Major Global Standards

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Top[Global Green Building Rating Systems] --> Tier1[First Generation 1990s]
    Top --> Tier2[Second Generation 2000 to 2015]
    Top --> Tier3[Third Generation 2015 Onwards]
    Tier1 --> BREEAM[UK BREEAM 1990]
    Tier1 --> LEEDv1[USA LEED v1 1998]
    Tier2 --> LEEDv3[USA LEED v3 2009]
    Tier2 --> GRIHA1[India GRIHA 2007]
    Tier2 --> IGBC1[India IGBC 2001 Plus]
    Tier2 --> GREENSTAR[Australia Green Star 2003]
    Tier3 --> LEEDv41[USA LEED v4.1 2019]
    Tier3 --> DGNB[Germany DGNB 2008 Plus]
    Tier3 --> WELL[USA WELL Building Standard 2014]
    Tier3 --> IGBCNZ[India IGBC Net-Zero 2018]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Green Building Energy Flow and Verification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Energy[Annual Energy Consumption kWh per year] --> Sim[Energy Simulation Model]
    Sim --> Baseline[Baseline Model ASHRAE 90.1]
    Sim --> Proposed[Proposed Green Design Model]
    Baseline --> Calc1[Compute Savings Percentage]
    Proposed --> Calc1
    Calc1 --> Cert[Apply for LEED EA Credit 1]
    Cert --> Audit[Third Party Commissioning Agent]
    Audit --> Award[Points Awarded by GBCI]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**  
*Define a Green Building Standard. List any four prominent global green building rating systems.*

**Model Answer:**
A **Green Building Standard** is a documented framework that assesses and certifies the environmental performance of a building across its life cycle using a credit-based scoring methodology.

Four prominent global systems:
1. **LEED** — Leadership in Energy and Environmental Design (USA)
2. **BREEAM** — Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment Method (UK)
3. **DGNB** — Deutsche Gesellschaft für Nachhaltiges Bauen (Germany)
4. **CASBEE** — Comprehensive Assessment System for Built Environment Efficiency (Japan)

> Marks split: **Definition (1.5) + 4 systems (0.5 × 4 = 2).** Skip a system → **−0.5 marks.**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**  
*What is the Energy Performance Index (EPI)? State the ECBC threshold value.*

**Model Answer:**
**EPI** is the annual electrical energy consumed per unit built-up area, expressed in **kWh/m²/year**.

$$
\text{EPI} = \frac{E_{\text{total}}}{A_{\text{built-up}}}
$$

**ECBC threshold:** $\leq \mathbf{90\ \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/year}}$ for commercial buildings.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **six major credit categories** evaluated under the **LEED v4** rating system. For each, give one example credit and its indicative point weightage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A commercial building has an annual energy consumption of **6,00,000 kWh** and a built-up area of **5,000 m²**. The baseline energy consumption as per ASHRAE 90.1 would have been **8,50,000 kWh/year**.  
Compute: (i) the EPI of the building, (ii) the **percentage energy savings**, and (iii) the **LEED EA Credit 1 points** earned. Comment on whether the building can achieve **Gold certification**. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution A(a) — Six LEED v4 Credit Categories**

The six major credit categories under LEED v4 BD+C (Building Design + Construction) are:

1. **Sustainable Sites (16 pts):** Encourages location selection near public transport, brownfield redevelopment, and heat island reduction. *Example credit:* *Site Selection (1 pt).*

2. **Water Efficiency (11 pts):** Reduces potable water consumption through low-flow fixtures, rainwater harvesting, and greywater recycling. *Example credit:* *Water Use Reduction (up to 6 pts).*

3. **Energy & Atmosphere (33 pts):** The highest-weighted category; promotes energy simulation, commissioning, and renewable energy. *Example credit:* *Optimize Energy Performance (up to 18 pts).*

4. **Materials & Resources (13 pts):** Encourages recycled content, regional materials, and construction & demolition waste diversion. *Example credit:* *Construction & Demolition Waste Management (up to 2 pts).*

5. **Indoor Environmental Quality (16 pts):** Focuses on occupant comfort — daylight, ventilation, low-VOC materials, thermal comfort. *Example credit:* *Daylight (up to 3 pts).*

6. **Innovation in Design (6 pts) + Regional Priority (4 pts):** Rewards novel sustainability strategies and locally relevant environmental priorities.

**Total = 110 points**

---

### **Model Solution A(b) — Numerical Computation**

**Given:**
- $E_{\text{proposed}} = 6{,}00{,}000\ \text{kWh/yr}$
- $E_{\text{baseline}} = 8{,}50{,}000\ \text{kWh/yr}$
- $A_{\text{built-up}} = 5000\ \text{m}^2$

**(i) EPI Calculation:**

$$
\text{EPI} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{5000} = 120\ \text{kWh/m}^2\text{/yr}
$$

**(ii) Percentage Energy Savings:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Savings} &= \left(\frac{8{,}50{,}000 - 6{,}00{,}000}{8{,}50{,}000}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{8{,}50{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 29.41\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) LEED EA Credit 1 Points:**

Using the reference table, **29.41 %** falls between **24 % (4 pts) and 30 % (5 pts)**. Since 29.41 % < 30 %, the building earns **4 points**.

> Cross-check: Strict interpolation gives 4 + (29.41 − 24)/6 × 1 = **4.9 ≈ 4 points**.

**Certification Tier Verdict:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Earned in EA only} & = 4\ \text{pts} \\
\text{Target for Gold} & = 60 - 79\ \text{pts} \\
\text{Feasibility:} & \ \text{Yes — Gold is achievable by scoring} \\
& \ \text{well in SS, WE, MR, EQ categories.}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to **convert percentage into points** using the official LEED table. Even if the energy savings is correctly calculated, failing to map to points loses **2 marks**. Always end with a *“points earned”* line and a *certification tier* comment.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the **Indian green building rating systems** — **GRIHA, IGBC, and ECBC** — under the following heads: *(i) Developing body, (ii) Year of launch, (iii) Rating tiers, (iv) Mandatory vs. voluntary nature, (v) Focus area, (vi) Net-zero provision.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A residential apartment project uses **800 tonnes of cement**. The embodied carbon of OPC is **0.93 kg CO₂/kg** and that of PPC (fly-ash based) is **0.66 kg CO₂/kg**. Calculate the **carbon reduction in tonnes CO₂** if PPC replaces OPC completely, and discuss the GRIHA credit eligibility under the *Materials & Resources* category. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution B(a) — Comparison Table**

| Head | GRIHA | IGBC | ECBC |
|:-----|:------|:-----|:-----|
| Developing body | TERI + MNRE | CII (Indian Green Building Council) | BEE (Bureau of Energy Efficiency, Govt. of India) |
| Year of launch | 2007 | 2001 | 2007 (1st); revised 2017 |
| Rating tiers | 1 ★, 2 ★, 3 ★, 4 ★, 5 ★ | Certified, Silver, Gold, Platinum | ECBC, ECBC+, SuperECBC |
| Mandatory? | Voluntary | Voluntary | **Mandatory for 9 commercial categories since 2018** |
| Focus area | Whole-building sustainability | Whole-building sustainability | **Energy efficiency only** |
| Net-zero provision | Yes (SVA-GRIHA, Net-Zero variants) | Yes (IGBC Net-Zero) | No |

---

### **Model Solution B(b) — Carbon Reduction Calculation**

**Given:**
- Quantity of cement = $800\ \text{tonnes} = 8 \times 10^{5}\ \text{kg}$
- Embodied carbon (OPC) = $0.93\ \text{kg CO}_2\text{/kg}$
- Embodied carbon (PPC) = $0.66\ \text{kg CO}_2\text{/kg}$

**Step 1:** Carbon emitted using OPC.

$$
C_{\text{OPC}} = 8 \times 10^{5} \times 0.93 = 7{,}44{,}000\ \text{kg CO}_2
$$

**Step 2:** Carbon emitted using PPC.

$$
C_{\text{PPC}} = 8 \times 10^{5} \times 0.66 = 5{,}28{,}000\ \text{kg CO}_2
$$

**Step 3:** Carbon reduction.

$$
\Delta C = 7{,}44{,}000 - 5{,}28{,}000 = 2{,}16{,}000\ \text{kg CO}_2
$$

**Step 4:** Convert to tonnes.

$$
\boxed{\Delta C = 216\ \text{tonnes CO}_2\text{ avoided}}
$$

**Step 5: GRIHA Eligibility.**

Under **GRIHA Criterion 11 — Local Materials & Resource Cycle**, replacing OPC with PPC qualifies for **2 of 4 points** in the Materials & Resources category.

**Step 6: Real-world equivalent.**

$$
\text{Equivalent} \approx \frac{216{,}000\ \text{kg}}{4600\ \text{kg/passenger-car/yr}} \approx 47\ \text{passenger cars off the road per year.}
$$

> [!WARNING]  
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often skip the *unit conversion* (tonnes ↔ kg) and lose 1 mark. Also, you must write the **GRIHA criterion number** explicitly — vague references like *"materials"* fetch partial marks only.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- ✅ Green Building Standards = *codified rulebooks* that convert sustainability into **measurable, certifiable** performance.
- ✅ Six core LEED categories: **SS, WE, EA, MR, EQ, ID + RP** — total **110 points**.
- ✅ LEED tiers: **Certified (40–49), Silver (50–59), Gold (60–79), Platinum (80+).**
- ✅ GRIHA tiers: **1 ★ to 5 ★** (5 ★ ≥ 90 points).
- ✅ ECBC is the **only mandatory** Indian green standard for commercial buildings.
- ✅ ECBC thresholds: **EPI ≤ 90** (ECBC), **≤ 65** (ECBC+), **≤ 40** (SuperECBC) **kWh/m²/yr**.
- ✅ Three generations: **1990s (energy only)** → **2000–2015 (multi-criteria)** → **2015+ (net-zero / life-cycle)**.
- ✅ Master formulas: **EPI, % energy savings, % water savings, LCC, payback, carbon footprint.**
- ✅ Always end numerical answers with the **certification tier implication** — KTU examiners reward holistic thinking.
- ✅ Use **ASHRAE 90.1** as the universal baseline for energy savings calculations.
- ✅ Indian-specific GRIHA criteria explicitly reward: **regional materials, fly-ash cement, low-E glazing, rooftop solar.**
- ✅ Common student trap: confusing **EPI units** (kWh/m²/yr) with **U-value units** (W/m²K) — these are **different** physical quantities.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Lamps and Their Efficiencies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of Lamps and Their Efficiencies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Lamp? (Formal KTU Definition)

A **lamp** is an artificial source of **visible light** (electromagnetic radiation in the wavelength range of approximately **380 nm to 750 nm**) produced by the controlled conversion of electrical energy into radiant energy. In the context of **Energy Systems engineering**, a lamp is treated as an *energy-conversion device* whose performance is evaluated not by how much energy it consumes, but by how much **useful luminous flux (Φ, in lumens)** it delivers per unit of electrical input — this metric is called **luminous efficacy (η)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Luminous Efficacy (η) — The Golden Metric of Illumination Engineering**
> $$\eta \;=\; \frac{\Phi_{lumens}}{P_{watts}} \quad [\text{lumens per watt, lm/W}]$$
> It is the single most important figure-of-merit used in KTU energy-audit problems for lamp comparisons.

## 1.2 The Theoretical Ceiling of Efficacy

The physics of human vision sets an absolute upper bound:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum theoretical luminous efficacy = 683 lm/W**, achievable only if **all** radiated power is emitted at the single wavelength of **555 nm** (the peak photopic sensitivity of the human eye, to which the lumen is normalized). In practice, every real lamp falls *far* below this ceiling because its spectrum is either broadband (incandescent) or has spectral gaps (gas discharge).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Light Factory"

Imagine each lamp as a small **factory** whose job is to manufacture *visible photons*:

- The **raw material** entering the factory is **electrical energy (kWh)**.
- The **finished product** is **visible light (lumens)**.
- The **waste by-product** is **heat (IR radiation)** and, for some types, **UV radiation** that must later be re-converted.

A **GLS incandescent lamp** is a *terrible* factory — out of every 100 units of raw material, **95 are wasted as heat** and only **5 become light**. A **modern LED** is a *highly efficient* factory — about **40–50 units become light**, with much less waste. The **efficacy number (lm/W)** is simply the *productivity* of this factory.

## 1.4 Performance Parameters of Every Lamp (KTU Checklist)

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Luminous Flux | Φ | **lumen (lm)** | Total visible light emitted |
| Luminous Efficacy | η | **lm/W** | Light output per electrical watt |
| Colour Rendering Index | CRI / Ra | unitless (0–100) | How "true" colours appear under the lamp |
| Colour Temperature | CCT | **Kelvin (K)** | Warmth or coolness of the light |
| Lamp Life | L | **hours (h)** | Operating hours before failure |
| Wattage | P | **Watt (W)** | Electrical power consumed |
| Illuminance | E | **lux (lx)** | Flux per unit area (1 lx = 1 lm/m²) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In energy-audit and demand-side-management (DSM) problems, the four parameters that earn marks are: **(1) Efficacy, (2) Lamp Life, (3) CRI, and (4) Wattage reduction achieved on retrofit.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Different Lamps Have Radically Different Efficacies

The efficacy of a lamp is governed by **two fundamental physical limits**:

1. **Theoretical Radiant Efficacy (η_rad)** — the fraction of input power that even *could* become light, set by the physics of the excitation mechanism (blackbody radiation, gas discharge, electroluminescence).
2. **Spectral Match Factor (K)** — the fraction of that radiation that falls within the CIE photopic luminosity curve V(λ).

$$\eta_{lamp} \;=\; \eta_{radiant} \times K \times 683 \;[\text{lm/W}]$$

> An incandescent filament behaves as a near-**blackbody** at ~2800 K, so η_rad is high but K is terrible (most energy is IR).
> An LED uses **electroluminescence** at a chosen semiconductor bandgap, so η_rad is high *and* K can be tuned close to 1 by selecting the phosphor blend.

## 2.2 The Five Major Lamp Families (KTU Syllabus)

### A. Incandescent / GLS Lamps (General Lighting Service)
- **Principle:** Resistive heating of a tungsten filament to incandescence (Planck's blackbody radiation).
- **Filament temperature:** 2500–3000 K.
- **Efficacy:** 10–17 lm/W.
- **CRI:** 100 (perfect).
- **Life:** 1000–2000 h.
- **Drawback:** ~95% of input is wasted as IR heat → banned in many countries for general lighting under DSM programs.

### B. Halogen Lamps
- **Principle:** Same as incandescent, but the bulb contains a small amount of **iodine or bromine** (the *halogen*). The halogen cycle redeposits evaporated tungsten back onto the filament, allowing higher operating temperature.
- **Filament temperature:** 3000–3400 K.
- **Efficacy:** 15–25 lm/W.
- **CRI:** 100.
- **Life:** 2000–4000 h.
- **Bulb wall:** Fused quartz (must withstand higher T).

### C. Fluorescent Lamps (CFL, T5, T8, T12)
- **Principle:** **Low-pressure mercury-vapour discharge** emits UV at **253.7 nm**; a phosphor coating on the inner wall converts UV → visible.
- **Requires ballast** (electromagnetic or electronic) to limit current.
- **Efficacy:** 50–100 lm/W (depending on tube diameter and phosphor).
- **CRI:** 60–85 (good phosphor) or 90+ (triphosphor).
- **Life:** 8000–15000 h.
- **Advantage:** ~5× more efficient than GLS — the workhorse of office lighting.

### D. High-Intensity Discharge (HID) Lamps

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module-1 Favourite:** The four HID sub-types must be compared *in a table* in the exam.

| HID Type | Discharge Medium | Efficacy (lm/W) | CRI | Life (h) | Dominant Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mercury Vapour (MV)** | Hg arc | 30–60 | 40–50 | 5000–24000 | Street lighting (legacy) |
| **Metal Halide (MH)** | Hg + metal halide salts (NaI, ScI₃, etc.) | 75–100 | 65–90 | 10000–20000 | Stadiums, high-bay industrial |
| **High-Pressure Sodium (HPSV)** | Na–Hg–Xe arc | 80–140 | 20–25 (yellow) | 24000+ | Roads, outdoor, horticultural |
| **Low-Pressure Sodium (LPSV)** | Na vapour at low P | 100–200 | 0 (monochromatic 589 nm) | 18000 | Tunnels, security (no colour needed) |

### E. Light-Emitting Diodes (LEDs) — The Modern Winner
- **Principle:** **Electroluminescence** in a forward-biased p–n semiconductor junction (e.g., GaN, InGaN). Blue LED + yellow YAG phosphor → white light.
- **Efficacy:** 80–150 lm/W (premium white LEDs reach **200+ lm/W** in lab; 150 lm/W in production).
- **CRI:** 80–98 (depending on phosphor recipe).
- **Life:** 25000–50000 h (some industrial modules claim 100000 h at L70).
- **Advantages:** Solid-state (no filament, no gas), instant on, dimmable, no mercury, directional.

### F. (Bonus) Electrodeless Induction Lamps
- **Principle:** Mercury plasma excited by a high-frequency magnetic field from an external coil — **no electrodes**, so no electrode erosion.
- **Efficacy:** 70–90 lm/W.
- **Life:** **60000–100000 h** (longest of any lamp).
- **Application:** Hard-to-access locations (high ceilings, tunnels).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The only vertical bars allowed in math tables are `\vert` (not `|`).** This rule protects the markdown table parser.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \Phi / P$ | Luminous efficacy | lm/W |
| 2 | $E = \Phi / A$ | Illuminance on area $A$ | lux $\vert$ lx |
| 3 | $I = \Phi / \omega$ | Luminous intensity (flux per solid angle $\omega$) | candela $\vert$ cd |
| 4 | $\eta_{max}^{theor} = 683$ lm/W | Peak possible efficacy at 555 nm | lm/W |
| 5 | $\% \text{ Saving} = \dfrac{P_{old} - P_{new}}{P_{old}} \times 100$ | Energy saved by retrofit | % |
| 6 | $L_{payback} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{lamp}}{(\Delta P \times t \times C_{unit})}$ | Simple payback of LED retrofit | years |
| 7 | $M = \Phi \times t_{life} \times \eta_{util}$ | Useful lumen-hours over lamp life | lm·h |
| 8 | $T_{colour} \propto 1/\lambda_{peak}$ | Wien's displacement (qualitative) | K |

> Where: $P$ = electrical power (W), $\Phi$ = luminous flux (lm), $A$ = area (m²), $\omega$ = solid angle (sr), $t$ = hours of use per year, $C_{unit}$ = electricity cost (₹/kWh), $\Delta C_{lamp}$ = extra cost of new lamp (₹), $\eta_{util}$ = utilisation factor (0 < $\eta_{util}$ < 1).

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in KTU Context

In **KTU 2024 Scheme PERAT637 — Energy Systems**, lamp-efficiency knowledge is applied to:
- **Energy audit** of commercial buildings (lighting often = 20–30% of electrical bill).
- **BEE star-rating** calculations for appliances.
- **Net-Zero / ECBC-compliant** building design.
- **Cost–benefit analysis** of replacing GLS/CFL with LED.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Why the Maximum Possible Efficacy is 683 lm/W

**Step 1 — Define the lumen.**
The lumen is defined such that 1 watt of radiant power at the peak photopic wavelength $\lambda = 555$ nm produces exactly **683 lumens**.

**Step 2 — Write the radiant power at the eye's response curve.**
For a monochromatic source of radiant power $P_{rad}$ at wavelength $\lambda$, the luminous flux is:

$$\Phi \;=\; 683 \times P_{rad} \times V(\lambda) \quad [\text{lm}]$$

where $V(\lambda)$ is the **CIE photopic luminosity function** with $V(555\,\text{nm}) = 1$.

**Step 3 — Maximize $\Phi$ per watt of electrical input.**
If all electrical power $P_{elec}$ is converted to radiant power at 555 nm (impossible in practice, but the theoretical ceiling), then $P_{rad} = P_{elec}$ and $V = 1$, giving:

$$\eta_{max} \;=\; \frac{\Phi}{P_{elec}} \;=\; \frac{683 \times P_{elec}}{P_{elec}} \;=\; 683 \; \text{lm/W}$$

**Step 4 — Apply to a real blackbody.**
A tungsten filament at $T = 2800$ K radiates most strongly in the IR. Integrating $V(\lambda)$ against the Planck spectrum yields only about **93 lm/W** as the *radiant* ceiling — but a real GLS lamp achieves only **15 lm/W** because of filament evaporation losses and bulb-shape geometry.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Quantitative Energy-Saving Analysis (LED vs GLS)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the derivation most likely to appear as a 14-mark Part B question.**

**Given:**
- A room uses **20 incandescent (GLS) lamps**, each rated **100 W**, efficacy **15 lm/W**.
- Operating time: **10 hours/day**, **300 days/year**.
- Electricity cost: **₹8.00 per kWh**.
- Proposed retrofit: **20 LED lamps**, each rated **18 W**, efficacy **100 lm/W** (to deliver the same light output).

**Step 1 — Verify light output equivalence.**
GLS per lamp: $\Phi_{GLS} = 15 \times 100 = 1500$ lm
LED per lamp: $\Phi_{LED} = 100 \times 18 = 1800$ lm (slightly higher, so equivalent or better)

For exact equivalence, choose LED wattage:
$$P_{LED,eq} = \frac{\Phi_{GLS}}{\eta_{LED}} = \frac{1500}{100} = 15 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Total connected load before retrofit.**

$$P_{old} = 20 \times 100 = 2000 \text{ W} = 2.0 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Total connected load after retrofit.**

$$P_{new} = 20 \times 15 = 300 \text{ W} = 0.3 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Annual energy consumption.**

For the old system:
$$E_{old} = 2.0 \times 10 \times 300 = 6000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

For the new system:
$$E_{new} = 0.3 \times 10 \times 300 = 900 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 5 — Annual energy saved.**

$$\Delta E = E_{old} - E_{new} = 6000 - 900 = 5100 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 6 — Percentage saving.**

$$\% \text{ Saving} = \frac{\Delta E}{E_{old}} \times 100 = \frac{5100}{6000} \times 100 = 85\%$$

**Step 7 — Annual monetary saving.**

$$\text{Saving (₹)} = \Delta E \times C_{unit} = 5100 \times 8 = \text{₹}40{,}800 \text{ per year}$$

**Step 8 — Simple payback period.**
Assume each LED costs ₹150, GLS costs ₹25 → extra cost per lamp = ₹125.

$$\Delta C_{total} = 20 \times 125 = \text{₹}2{,}500$$

$$\text{Payback} = \frac{\Delta C_{total}}{\text{Annual Saving}} = \frac{2500}{40800} = 0.061 \text{ years} \approx 22 \text{ days}$$

**Conclusion:** Replacing 100 W GLS with 15 W LED yields **85% energy reduction** and pays for itself in **under a month** — a textbook KTU-grade energy-audit result.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Lamp Type | η (lm/W) | CRI | Life (kh) | Warm-up | Re-strike | Cost | Environmental |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| GLS Incandescent | 10–17 | 100 | 1–2 | Instant | Instant | Very Low | No Hg |
| Halogen | 15–25 | 100 | 2–4 | Instant | Instant | Low | No Hg |
| CFL | 50–70 | 80–85 | 8–15 | 30–60 s | 1–2 min | Low | **Contains Hg** |
| T8 Fluorescent | 70–90 | 75–85 | 15–20 | Instant | Instant | Low | **Contains Hg** |
| T5 Fluorescent | 85–100 | 85 | 20–25 | Instant | Instant | Medium | **Contains Hg** |
| Mercury Vapour | 30–60 | 40–50 | 24 | 4–7 min | 5–10 min | Low | **Contains Hg** |
| Metal Halide | 75–100 | 65–90 | 10–20 | 3–5 min | 10–20 min | High | **Contains Hg** |
| HPSV | 80–140 | 20–25 | 24 | 3–5 min | 1 min | Medium | **Contains Hg, Na** |
| LPSV | 100–200 | 0 | 18 | 7–10 min | Immediate | High | **Contains Na** |
| Induction | 70–90 | 80 | 60–100 | Instant | Instant | High | **Contains Hg** |
| **LED** | **80–150+** | **80–98** | **25–50+** | **Instant** | **Instant** | Medium-High | **No Hg** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Energy Systems, Module 1]`
**Define luminous efficacy of a lamp. Why is its maximum theoretical value 683 lm/W, and why do real lamps fall far below this value?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Luminous efficacy $\eta$ of a lamp is defined as the ratio of its total luminous flux $\Phi$ (in lumens) to the electrical power consumed $P$ (in watts): $\eta = \Phi / P$, with unit lm/W.
The maximum theoretical value is **683 lm/W**, which corresponds to a hypothetical monochromatic source emitting all its power at $\lambda = 555$ nm — the wavelength of maximum photopic eye sensitivity, to which the lumen is defined.
Real lamps fall below this value because: (i) most of the input power is radiated outside the 380–750 nm visible band (e.g., IR from a hot filament), (ii) even the visible portion does not perfectly match the eye's V($\lambda$) curve, and (iii) there are intrinsic conversion losses (ballast losses in discharge lamps, junction heating in LEDs). Hence a typical GLS lamp achieves only ~15 lm/W and even the best LEDs reach ~150–200 lm/W.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Energy Systems, Module 1]`
**Differentiate between Low-Pressure Sodium (LPSV) and High-Pressure Sodium (HPSV) lamps in terms of principle, efficacy, CRI, and applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | LPSV | HPSV |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Na vapour discharge at low P — almost monochromatic 589 nm (D-line) | Na–Hg–Xe arc at high P — broadened spectrum |
| **Efficacy** | 100–200 lm/W (highest) | 80–140 lm/W |
| **CRI** | 0 (monochromatic, all colours appear yellow/grey) | 20–25 (poor, but colours recognisable) |
| **Application** | Tunnels, security, places where colour rendering is irrelevant | Street lighting, outdoor area lighting |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, ESE Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain with neat sketches the construction, working principle, and the role of the **halogen cycle** in a tungsten-halogen lamp. Why is it more efficient than an ordinary GLS lamp?

**(b) [7 marks]** A factory floor is illuminated by 50 metal-halide lamps of 400 W each, with an efficacy of 80 lm/W, operating 12 hours/day, 300 days/year at an electricity cost of ₹9/kWh. The plant manager proposes a retrofit to 150 W LED high-bay lamps with an efficacy of 110 lm/W, each costing ₹3500, while the old MH lamps cost ₹1200 each.
Calculate: **(i)** the total annual energy consumption in each case, **(ii)** the annual energy saving in kWh and as a percentage, **(iii)** the annual monetary saving, and **(iv)** the simple payback period of the retrofit.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks] — Tungsten-Halogen Lamp**

*Construction:* A small fused-quartz bulb contains a coiled tungsten filament supported on molybdenum holders. The bulb is filled with an inert gas (argon or xenon) plus a small dose of **halogen** (iodine or bromine, a few Torr).

*Working:* The filament is heated to ~3000–3400 K. Tungsten evaporates from the hot filament. In a normal GLS lamp, this tungsten would deposit on the cool bulb wall (blackening). In a halogen lamp, the evaporated W reacts with the halogen near the wall to form a volatile **tungsten halide** (e.g., WI₂). The halide diffuses back toward the filament, where the high temperature reverses the reaction, **redepositing tungsten onto the filament and releasing free halogen**. This is the **Halogen Regenerative Cycle**.

*Why more efficient:* The cycle permits a **higher filament temperature** (3000+ K vs 2700 K for GLS) without bulb blackening, giving whiter light and a higher luminous efficacy (15–25 lm/W vs 10–17 lm/W) plus a 2× longer life. The higher temperature also shifts more emission into the visible band, raising luminous flux per watt.

**(b) [7 marks] — Retrofit Economic Analysis**

**Step 1 — Annual energy, MH case.**
$P_{MH,total} = 50 \times 400 = 20{,}000$ W $= 20$ kW
$E_{MH} = 20 \times 12 \times 300 = 72{,}000$ kWh/year

**Step 2 — Annual energy, LED case.**
$P_{LED,total} = 50 \times 150 = 7{,}500$ W $= 7.5$ kW
$E_{LED} = 7.5 \times 12 \times 300 = 27{,}000$ kWh/year

**Step 3 — Energy and percentage saving.**
$\Delta E = 72{,}000 - 27{,}000 = 45{,}000$ kWh/year
$\%\text{Saving} = 45{,}000 / 72{,}000 \times 100 = 62.5\%$

**Step 4 — Annual monetary saving.**
Annual bill (old) $= 72{,}000 \times 9 = \text{₹}6{,}48{,}000$
Annual bill (new) $= 27{,}000 \times 9 = \text{₹}2{,}43{,}000$
**Saving = ₹4,05,000 per year**

**Step 5 — Payback.**
Extra cost per lamp $= 3500 - 1200 = \text{₹}2300$
Total extra cost $= 50 \times 2300 = \text{₹}1{,}15{,}000$
$\text{Payback} = 1{,}15{,}000 / 4{,}05{,}000 = 0.284 \text{ years} \approx 3.4 \text{ months}$

**Conclusion:** A highly attractive retrofit.

---

### Question B — Choice 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a comparison table, discuss the **construction, working principle, luminous efficacy, CRI, life, and typical applications** of the following lamp types: (i) Incandescent, (ii) Fluorescent, (iii) Metal Halide, and (iv) LED.

**(b) [7 marks]** An auditorium requires an average illuminance of **500 lux** over a floor area of **400 m²**. The utilisation factor is **0.6** and the maintenance factor is **0.8**. Calculate: **(i)** the total luminous flux required from the lamps, **(ii)** the number of LED luminaires required if each delivers 12,000 lumens, and **(iii)** the total connected electrical load if each luminaire is rated 120 W.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks] — Comparison Table**

| Lamp | Principle | η (lm/W) | CRI | Life (h) | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Incandescent (GLS) | Blackbody radiation from W filament at ~2700 K | 10–17 | 100 | 1000–2000 | Domestic, decorative |
| Fluorescent (T8/T5) | Hg-vapour discharge → UV (253.7 nm) → phosphor → visible | 50–100 | 75–85 | 15,000–25,000 | Offices, schools, retail |
| Metal Halide | Hg + metal-halide salts in arc tube | 75–100 | 65–90 | 10,000–20,000 | Stadiums, high-bay, automotive |
| LED | Electroluminescence in p–n junction (GaN/InGaN) | 80–150+ | 80–98 | 25,000–50,000+ | Universal, from homes to streets |

*Detailed elaborations:*
- **GLS:** Tungsten filament inside inert gas (Ar/N₂). Resistive heating → Planck radiation; 95% IR waste.
- **Fluorescent:** Electrodes at each end strike an arc through low-pressure Hg vapour; UV excites phosphor; requires ballast.
- **Metal Halide:** Arc tube of fused quartz contains Hg plus iodides of Na, Sc, In, Th. Higher CRI than MV.
- **LED:** Forward-biased GaN chip emits blue light; yellow YAG phosphor converts part to red+green → white; requires driver circuit.

**(b) [7 marks] — Lumen Method Calculation**

**Step 1 — Total flux required at the luminaire level (using the lumen method formula).**

$$E = \frac{\Phi_{total} \times \eta_{util} \times \eta_{main}}{A}$$

Rearranging for $\Phi_{total}$:

$$\Phi_{total} = \frac{E \times A}{\eta_{util} \times \eta_{main}}$$

$$\Phi_{total} = \frac{500 \times 400}{0.6 \times 0.8} = \frac{200{,}000}{0.48} = 416{,}666.67 \text{ lm}$$

**Step 2 — Number of LED luminaires.**
Each luminaire delivers 12,000 lm.

$$N = \frac{\Phi_{total}}{\Phi_{luminaire}} = \frac{416{,}666.67}{12{,}000} = 34.7 \approx 35 \text{ luminaires}$$

(Always round up to ensure required illuminance is met.)

**Step 3 — Total connected load.**

$$P_{total} = 35 \times 120 = 4200 \text{ W} = 4.2 \text{ kW}$$

**Sanity check (efficacy):**

$$\eta_{system} = \frac{35 \times 12{,}000}{4200} = \frac{420{,}000}{4200} = 100 \text{ lm/W}$$

This matches the assumption of a high-quality LED luminaire, confirming consistency.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks — read carefully:**
> 1. **Forgetting the "lm" in the unit.** Writing efficacy as just "W" or "W/lm" is a guaranteed 0.5-mark deduction.
> 2. **Mixing up HPSV and LPSV.** LPSV has **higher efficacy** but **CRI = 0**; HPSV has lower efficacy but CRI ≈ 25. This is the most-asked 3-mark pitfall.
> 3. **Not mentioning ballast/driver.** For every discharge lamp and every LED, you **must** mention the ballast/driver — examiners explicitly test for this in 7-mark sub-parts.
> 4. **Skipping the "Halogen Cycle" explanation.** Just saying "it has halogen gas" earns 1/7. The full regenerative cycle (evaporation → halide formation → diffusion → re-deposition) earns full marks.
> 5. **Rounding in payback period.** Always express payback in **years with two decimals**, and explicitly state the assumption about lamp life, cost escalation, and maintenance charges.
> 6. **Forgetting the maintenance factor (MF) in the lumen method.** The formula is $E = \Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF / A$. Skipping MF gives a wrong lumen count.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final pre-exam checklist — read this the night before:**

- ✅ **Luminous efficacy** $\eta = \Phi / P$, unit **lm/W** — the master equation.
- ✅ **Theoretical maximum = 683 lm/W** at 555 nm; real lamps: GLS 10–17, Halogen 15–25, Fluorescent 50–100, HID 60–140, **LED 80–150+**.
- ✅ **GLS lamp:** Tungsten filament + inert gas; ~95% energy wasted as heat.
- ✅ **Halogen lamp:** Halogen regenerative cycle → higher T → higher η and longer life.
- ✅ **Fluorescent lamp:** Low-pressure Hg discharge + phosphor; needs **ballast**; contains **mercury**.
- ✅ **HID family:** MV, MH, HPSV, LPSV — know the *efficacy* and *CRI* of each.
- ✅ **LPSV = highest efficacy (100–200 lm/W) but CRI = 0** (monochromatic sodium D-line at 589 nm).
- ✅ **LED:** Electroluminescence; longest life (25,000–50,000 h); no mercury; instant on/off.
- ✅ **Five key lamp parameters:** Efficacy, CRI, Lamp Life, Colour Temperature, Wattage.
- ✅ **Energy-saving formula:** $\%\text{Saving} = (P_{old} - P_{new})/P_{old} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Payback formula:** $L = \Delta C / (\Delta P \cdot t \cdot C_{unit})$.
- ✅ **Lumen method:** $N = (E \cdot A) / (\Phi \cdot UF \cdot MF)$ — always round **up** to next integer.
- ✅ **Banned/Phased-out (India, BEE):** Standard GLS 100 W and above (since 2009), T12 fluorescent tubes.
- ✅ **BEE Star Rating** for LED bulbs is based on lm/W: 5★ ≥ 100 lm/W (typical reference year 2024).
- ✅ **Environmental hazard:** All Hg-containing lamps (CFL, T5/T8, MH, HPSV) require **safe disposal** — covered under E-Waste Management Rules, India 2022.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Thermal System - Working Principle - Block Diagram
Solar Photovoltaic System - Working Principle - Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Photovoltaic System - Working Principle - Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Solar Photovoltaic (PV) System** is an integrated assembly of photovoltaic modules, power-conditioning electronics, energy-storage interfaces, and load-side protection equipment that directly converts incident solar irradiance into usable electrical energy through the quantum **photovoltaic effect** observed in semiconductor p-n junction devices.

The phenomenon underlying PV operation—the *Becquerel Effect*—was first demonstrated by **Edmond Becquerel in 1839**, and the first practical silicon solar cell was fabricated at **Bell Laboratories in 1954** with an initial conversion efficiency of **≈ 6 %**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> As per PERAT637 (Module 2), students must be able to *(i)* explain the working principle of a solar PV cell using the photovoltaic effect, *(ii)* sketch and label a complete block diagram of a standalone/grid-tied PV system, and *(iii)* identify the function of each block (array → MPPT → battery → inverter → load).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Coin-Sorter Machine"

Imagine sunlight as a stream of **coins (photons)** being poured onto a specially designed *coin-sorter* (the silicon p-n junction):

- Each photon is a coin of a specific **size (wavelength)** and **value (energy, $E = h\nu$)**.
- Only coins of a minimum value (the **band-gap energy, $E_g$**) can trigger the sorter to release a token (an **electron**) from its slot.
- A one-way gate (the **depletion region / built-in field**) sweeps the freed token to the *positive* side and the empty slot (a *hole*) to the *negative* side.
- The continuous flow of these tokens through an external wire is what we call **electric current**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition Box:**
> **Photovoltaic Effect** — The generation of a potential difference across a semiconductor p-n junction when it is illuminated by photons whose energy ($h\nu$) is greater than or equal to the band-gap energy ($E_g$) of the semiconductor material, thereby liberating charge carriers that are separated by the junction's built-in electric field.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value (Bold) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $h$ | Planck's constant | **$6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$** |
| $q$ | Electron charge | **$1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$** |
| $k$ | Boltzmann constant | **$1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$** |
| $c$ | Speed of light | **$3 \times 10^{8}\ \text{m/s}$** |
| $E_g$ (Si) | Silicon band-gap | **$1.12\ \text{eV}$** |
| AM 1.5 | Standard test irradiance | **$1000\ \text{W/m}^2$ @ 25 °C** |
| STC | Standard Test Conditions | **$1\ \text{kW/m}^2,\ 25\ ^\circ\text{C},\ AM1.5G$** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Principle of a PV Cell

A silicon PV cell is essentially a large-area **p-n junction diode** with the junction plane positioned close to the front surface to maximize photon absorption.

1. **Photon Absorption:** Incident solar radiation ($G$ in $\text{W/m}^2$) strikes the anti-reflective coated top surface. Photons with energy $h\nu \ge E_g$ penetrate the semiconductor.
2. **Electron–Hole Pair Generation:** Each absorbed photon transfers its energy to a valence-band electron, promoting it across the band-gap into the conduction band. This leaves behind a mobile hole in the valence band. **1 photon → 1 electron–hole pair (EHP)**.
3. **Carrier Separation by Built-in Field:** The space-charge region (depletion zone) of the p-n junction contains a strong **built-in electric field $E_0$** directed from the n-side to the p-side. Minority carriers generated within or near this region are *swept across* before they can recombine.
4. **Collection at the Contacts:** Electrons travel through the n-layer to the front metallic grid (negative terminal); holes travel through the p-layer to the back contact (positive terminal).
5. **External Current Flow:** When an external load is connected, electrons flow from the n-contact through the load to the p-contact, producing a measurable **short-circuit current $I_{SC}$** and an open-circuit voltage $V_{OC}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Silicon?**
> Silicon's band-gap of **$1.12\ \text{eV}$** is near the optimum for the solar spectrum — it balances photon absorption (requires low $E_g$) against thermal-loss voltage (requires high $E_g$). The Shockley–Queisser optimum is approximately **$1.34\ \text{eV}$**.

### 2.2 Equivalent Circuit Model (Single-Diode Model)

The most widely accepted analytical model represents the PV cell as:

- A **photo-generated current source** $I_L$ (proportional to irradiance $G$).
- A **parallel diode** $D$ representing the dark recombination current $I_0$.
- A **series resistance** $R_s$ (contact & bulk resistance, ideally 0).
- A **shunt resistance** $R_{sh}$ (leakage paths, ideally ∞).

Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL) at the output node:

$$I = I_L - I_D - I_{sh}$$

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\dfrac{V + IR_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

where the **thermal voltage** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} \approx 25.85\ \text{mV}$ at $T = 300\ \text{K}$, and $n$ is the diode ideality factor ($1 \le n \le 2$).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cheat-Sheet — Memorize the following core equations for the ESE:**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Boundary / Operating Point |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy (J or eV) | $E \ge E_g$ for absorption |
| 2 | $I = I_L - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V+IR_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right]$ | Cell I–V characteristic | Single-diode model |
| 3 | $I_{SC} \approx I_L$ | Short-circuit current (A) | At $V = 0$ |
| 4 | $V_{OC} = nV_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage (V) | At $I = 0$ |
| 5 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp}\,I_{mp}}{V_{OC}\,I_{SC}}$ | Fill Factor (dimensionless) | $0 < FF < 1$ (typ. 0.6 – 0.8) |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{OC}\,I_{SC}\,FF}{G\,A}$ | Conversion efficiency (%) | At STC: $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$ |
| 7 | $P_{max} = V_{OC}\,I_{SC}\,FF$ | Maximum power output (W) | At the *knee* of I–V curve |
| 8 | $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q}$ | Thermal voltage (V) | $25.85\ \text{mV}$ at 300 K |
| 9 | $I_{MPPT} \approx 0.9\,I_{SC}$ | MPPT current set-point | Per empirical rule |
| 10 | $N_{series} = \dfrac{V_{DC,\ bus}}{V_{mp,\ module}}$ | String sizing | For high DC bus voltage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The single-diode model is the foundation of every commercial PV simulator (PVsyst, SAM, PSpice). It also drives **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)** algorithms — *Perturb & Observe* and *Incremental Conductance* — used in all modern charge controllers and grid-tie inverters.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Open-Circuit Voltage $V_{OC}$

We begin from the general I–V equation, neglecting $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$ (ideal cell assumption), and apply the boundary condition $I = 0$ (open circuit).

$$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{OC}}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 1:** Isolate the exponential term.

$$I_0 \left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{OC}}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right] = I_L$$

**Step 2:** Divide both sides by $I_0$ and add 1.

$$\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V_{OC}}{nV_T}\right) = \dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1$$

**Step 3:** Take the natural logarithm of both sides.

$$\dfrac{V_{OC}}{nV_T} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$

**Step 4:** Multiply both sides by $nV_T$ to obtain the final expression.

$$V_{OC} = nV_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$

**Physical Interpretation:** Since $I_L \gg I_0$ under sunlight, $V_{OC}$ is **logarithmically** dependent on light-generated current. A *10×* increase in $I_L$ only increases $V_{OC}$ by $\approx 60\ \text{mV}$ per diode-decade at 300 K.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion Efficiency $\eta$

The efficiency is defined as the ratio of maximum electrical power delivered to the incident solar power on the cell's active area.

**Step 1:** State the definition.

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{V_{mp}\,I_{mp}}{G \cdot A}$$

**Step 2:** Introduce the Fill Factor, $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp}\,I_{mp}}{V_{OC}\,I_{SC}}$.

$$\eta = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{OC}\cdot I_{SC}}{G \cdot A}$$

**Step 3:** Under STC, $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$, so the formula is normalized as:

$$\eta\ (\%) = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{OC}\cdot I_{SC}}{10 \cdot A}$$

**Numerical Worked Example (KTU-style 3-mark problem):**

> *A 156 mm × 156 mm monocrystalline silicon cell has $V_{OC} = 0.65\ \text{V}$, $I_{SC} = 8.5\ \text{A}$, and $FF = 0.78$. Calculate its efficiency at STC.*

$$A = 0.156 \times 0.156 = 0.0243\ \text{m}^2$$

$$P_{in} = 1000 \times 0.0243 = 24.3\ \text{W}$$

$$P_{max} = V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF = 0.65 \times 8.5 \times 0.78 = 4.31\ \text{W}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{4.31}{24.3} = 0.177 = 17.7\ \%$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Photon Flux & Short-Circuit Current Scaling

The light-generated current $I_L$ is directly proportional to the photon flux $\Phi$ above the band-gap threshold and to the elementary charge $q$.

**Step 1:** Spectral photon flux (photons/m²/s) for $h\nu \ge E_g$:

$$\Phi = \int_{E_g}^{\infty}\!\dfrac{N(E)}{E}\,dE$$

**Step 2:** Assuming an external quantum efficiency $EQE \approx 1$ (ideal), every photon contributes one electron:

$$I_L = q \cdot A \cdot \Phi$$

**Step 3:** Linear scaling with irradiance $G$:

$$I_L(G) = I_{L,\,STC} \cdot \dfrac{G}{1000\ \text{W/m}^2}$$

This is why $I_{SC}$ scales **linearly** with irradiance, but $V_{OC}$ scales only **logarithmically** — a critical fact for KTU problems involving cloudy/low-light conditions.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Series vs Shunt Resistance Effects

| Parameter | Increase $R_s$ | Increase $R_{sh}$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $I_{SC}$ | Essentially unchanged | Slightly increased |
| $V_{OC}$ | Slightly reduced | Slightly increased |
| $FF$ | **Severely reduced** | Significantly increased |
| $P_{max}$ | **Drops sharply** | Increases |
| $\eta$ | **Decreases** | Increases |
| I–V curve shape | Slope near $V_{OC}$ tilts | Slope near $I_{SC}$ flattens |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A classic 7-mark question asks, *"Why does series resistance degrade Fill Factor more than shunt resistance?"* The answer lies in the slope of the I–V curve at the maximum-power point — the $dI/dV$ derivative is steepest at the knee, so a small $R_s$ causes a large $I\!\cdot\!R_s$ voltage loss precisely where the cell is generating peak power.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Standalone Solar PV System

Below is a comprehensive, KTU-evaluation-ready block diagram of a complete standalone (off-grid) solar PV system. Each block represents a major functional unit, and the arrows show unidirectional power flow and bidirectional control signaling.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sunlight Irradiance G]:::source
    B[PV Array Modules]:::gen
    C[DC Combiner Box with Fuses]:::dist
    D[MPPT Charge Controller]:::ctrl
    E1[Deep Cycle Battery Bank]:::store
    E2[DC Load Bulbs and Fans]:::loadDC
    F[DC to AC Inverter]:::inv
    G[AC Distribution Panel]:::dist
    H1[AC Load Lighting and TV]:::loadAC
    H2[Grid Export Optional]:::grid
    M[Microcontroller MPPT Logic]:::ctrl
    subgraph S1[Power Path]
        A --> B --> C --> D --> F --> G --> H1
    end
    subgraph S2[Storage Path]
        D <--> E1
    end
    subgraph S3[DC Auxiliary]
        D --> E2
    end
    subgraph S4[Control Path]
        M -.-> D
    end
    G -.-> H2

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef gen fill:#FF6F61,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef dist fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef store fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#4B0082,color:#000
    classDef inv fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef loadDC fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00,color:#000
    classDef loadAC fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00,color:#000
    classDef grid fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#191970,color:#000
```

**Block-by-Block Functional Description (what KTU expects you to write):**

| Block Label | Component | Primary Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **PV Array** | Series-parallel combination of modules; converts photons → DC electricity |
| 2 | **DC Combiner Box** | Aggregates strings; houses DC fuses, surge protection (SPD), and isolators |
| 3 | **MPPT Charge Controller** | Forces the array to operate at its *Maximum Power Point*; regulates battery charging |
| 4 | **Battery Bank** | Stores excess DC energy as chemical energy (Lead-acid / Li-ion / LFP) |
| 5 | **DC-AC Inverter** | Converts DC bus voltage to pure/voltage-source AC (e.g., 230 V, 50 Hz sine wave) |
| 6 | **AC Distribution Panel** | MCBs, ELCBs; routes power to AC loads and optional grid-tie point |
| 7 | **Loads** | AC + DC end-user appliances |
| 8 | **Grid-Export** *(optional)* | Net-metering interface; requires anti-islanding protection per IEEE 1547 |

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Grid-Tied PV System (Without Battery)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sun]:::source
    B[PV Array]:::gen
    C[DC Isolator Switch]:::dist
    D[String Inverter with MPPT]:::inv
    E[AC Isolator]:::dist
    F[Bi directional Net Meter]:::ctrl
    G[Utility Grid LT Line]:::grid
    H[AC Load Building]:::loadAC

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F
    F --> G
    F --> H

    classDef source fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,color:#000
    classDef gen fill:#FF6F61,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef dist fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,color:#000
    classDef inv fill:#FFA500,stroke:#8B4513,color:#000
    classDef ctrl fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
    classDef grid fill:#B0C4DE,stroke:#191970,color:#000
    classDef loadAC fill:#F0E68C,stroke:#8B8B00,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Difference vs Off-Grid:** In a *grid-tied* system, the battery block is **absent**, and the inverter must perform **synchronization** (phase, frequency, voltage matching) with the grid. If the grid fails, the inverter must **anti-island** within 2 seconds (per IEC 62116 / IEEE 1547).

### 4.3 I–V and P–V Characteristic Curve — Conceptual Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["I axis: Current A"]:::gen
    B["Point Isc at V = 0"]:::ctrl
    C["Point MPP Vmp x Imp"]:::ctrl
    D["Point Voc at I = 0"]:::ctrl
    E["V axis: Voltage V"]:::gen
    F["P max = Vmp x Imp"]:::ctrl

    A --> B --> C --> D --> E
    C --> F

    classDef gen fill:#FF6F61,stroke:#8B0000,color:#FFF
    classDef ctrl fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

The **MPPT controller** continuously adjusts the operating point of the PV array to remain at point C (Maximum Power Point), where the product $V \times I$ is maximized.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the photovoltaic effect. Mention the role of the p-n junction depletion region in solar cell operation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **photovoltaic effect** is the phenomenon of generating an electromotive force across a semiconductor p-n junction when it is illuminated by photons whose energy ($h\nu$) is greater than or equal to the band-gap energy ($E_g$) of the material. **(1 mark)**
When light falls on the cell, photons with $h\nu \ge E_g$ are absorbed in the depletion region, creating electron–hole pairs. **(1 mark)**
The strong **built-in electric field** in the depletion region sweeps the electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side, producing a potential difference and a photocurrent when an external load is connected. **(1 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*With a neat block diagram, list the major components of a standalone solar PV system.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A standalone solar PV system consists of: **(1)** PV array (modules connected in series–parallel), **(2)** MPPT charge controller, **(3)** Battery bank (storage), **(4)** DC–AC inverter, **(5)** AC/DC distribution panel, and **(6)** Load. **(2 marks for listing)** A block diagram should be drawn with proper arrows showing power flow from Sun → Array → Controller → Battery / Inverter → Load. **(1 mark for diagram)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

### **Question A (14 marks)** — *OR—* **Question B (14 marks)**

#### **Question A (14 marks)**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a solar PV cell with the help of the photovoltaic effect in a p-n junction. Sketch the equivalent circuit and derive the ideal I–V characteristic equation. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Define Fill Factor and conversion efficiency. A 0.156 m × 0.156 m silicon solar cell has $V_{OC} = 0.62\ \text{V}$, $I_{SC} = 8.2\ \text{A}$ and $FF = 0.76$. Calculate (i) maximum power output and (ii) conversion efficiency at STC ($G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$). *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Photon absorption** ($h\nu \ge E_g$): A photon transfers its energy to a valence-band electron, raising it to the conduction band and creating an electron–hole pair (EHP). *(1 mark)*
2. **Carrier separation**: The depletion region contains a built-in electric field $E_0$ that sweeps the photogenerated electrons toward the n-region and holes toward the p-region. *(1 mark)*
3. **Carrier collection**: Metallic contacts collect the electrons (front grid) and holes (back contact), generating a potential difference $V_{OC}$ at open circuit. *(1 mark)*
4. **Equivalent circuit**: A photo-current source $I_L$ in parallel with a diode $D$ and shunt resistance $R_{sh}$, with series resistance $R_s$. *(1 mark)*
5. **Derivation** — Applying KCL at the output node:

$$I = I_L - I_D - I_{sh} = I_L - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V+IR_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

For the ideal case ($R_s = 0,\ R_{sh} = \infty$):

$$I = I_L - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right]$$

*(3 marks for the derivation steps leading to the final expression)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Maximum Power Output:** *(3 marks)*

$$P_{max} = V_{OC} \times I_{SC} \times FF$$

$$P_{max} = 0.62 \times 8.2 \times 0.76$$

$$P_{max} = 3.864\ \text{W}$$

**(ii) Conversion Efficiency:** *(3 marks)*

$$A = 0.156 \times 0.156 = 0.02434\ \text{m}^2$$

$$P_{in} = 1000 \times 0.02434 = 24.34\ \text{W}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{3.864}{24.34} = 0.1588$$

$$\eta \approx 15.88\ \%$$

**(1 mark) — Final boxed answer with units.**

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a standalone solar PV system and explain the function of each block. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the working of a Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) charge controller. Compare **Perturb & Observe (P&O)** and **Incremental Conductance (IncCond)** algorithms. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a) — Block Diagram & Functions:** *(7 marks)*

| Block | Function |
| :--- | :--- |
| 1. **PV Array** | Converts sunlight into DC electricity (series-parallel modules). *(1 m)* |
| 2. **DC Combiner** | Aggregates strings; houses fuses and surge protection. *(1 m)* |
| 3. **MPPT Charge Controller** | Forces array to operate at peak power; prevents battery over-charge. *(1.5 m)* |
| 4. **Battery Bank** | Stores excess DC energy; supplies load during night/cloudy periods. *(1.5 m)* |
| 5. **Inverter** | DC → AC conversion (e.g., 230 V, 50 Hz pure sine wave). *(1 m)* |
| 6. **AC Distribution** | MCBs/ELCBs to AC loads; optional grid-tie. *(1 m)* |

**Model Solution (b) — MPPT Comparison:** *(7 marks)*

**Working Principle:** MPPT continuously varies the operating voltage of the PV array (via a DC-DC converter) to track the *knee* of the P–V curve where $dP/dV = 0$. *(2 marks)*

**Perturb & Observe (P&O):** Periodically perturbs $V$ by $\Delta V$ and observes whether $P$ increased or decreased. If $P$ rises, the perturbation direction is maintained; otherwise, it is reversed. Simple but oscillates around MPP. *(2 marks)*

**Incremental Conductance (IncCond):** Uses the condition $dI/dV = -I/V$ at MPP. Faster tracking, less oscillation, better under rapidly changing irradiance, but computationally heavier. *(2 marks)*

**Comparison table** *(1 mark):*

| Feature | P&O | IncCond |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Complexity | Low | High |
| Tracking speed | Slower | Faster |
| Oscillation at steady state | Yes | Negligible |
| Cost | Low | Higher |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of $I$ and $V$:** The I–V equation is derived with a specific sign convention. Inverting the direction of either sign in your derivation **costs 2 marks** even if the rest is correct.
> 2. **Forgetting STC:** Efficiency and $I_{SC}$ values change with $G$ and $T$. If the problem says "at STC," **always** use $G = 1000\ \text{W/m}^2$ and $T = 25\ ^\circ\text{C}$. Otherwise, **deduct 1 mark**.
> 3. **Block-Diagram Arrows:** Arrows must show **unidirectional** power flow (Sun → Array → Inverter → Load) and **bidirectional** for battery. A single-line block diagram without arrows is awarded only **partial marks** (typically 4/7).
> 4. **FF vs $\eta$ confusion:** $FF$ is dimensionless (no units); $\eta$ is a percentage. Mixing them up is a classic 1-mark loss.
> 5. **MPPT equation:** Writing $V_{mp} = 0.8\,V_{OC}$ is an *empirical rule*, not a derivation. The examiner expects $dP/dV = 0$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Photovoltaic effect** = photon ($h\nu \ge E_g$) → EHP generation → separation by junction field → external current.
- The **single-diode model** is the *standard* equivalent circuit: $I_L$ in parallel with diode, $R_s$, $R_{sh}$.
- The I–V equation: $I = I_L - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\dfrac{V+IR_s}{nV_T}\right) - 1\right] - \dfrac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$.
- **Open-circuit voltage:** $V_{OC} = nV_T \ln\!\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0}+1\right)$ — logarithmic dependence on light.
- **Short-circuit current:** $I_{SC} \approx I_L$ — linear dependence on irradiance $G$.
- **Fill Factor:** $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp}\,I_{mp}}{V_{OC}\,I_{SC}}$ — typically **0.6 to 0.8** for good silicon cells.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{OC}\cdot I_{SC}}{G\cdot A}$ — commercial mono-Si cells: **18 – 22 %**.
- **STC** = $1000\ \text{W/m}^2,\ 25\ ^\circ\text{C},\ AM1.5G$ — always normalize to STC unless stated otherwise.
- **Standalone PV system** = Array + MPPT controller + Battery bank + Inverter + AC distribution + Load.
- **Grid-tied system** = Array + String inverter (with MPPT) + Net meter; **no battery** in the basic configuration.
- **MPPT algorithms:** P&O (simple, oscillates) vs IncCond (fast, accurate, complex).
- **Thermal voltage:** $V_T = \dfrac{kT}{q} = 25.85\ \text{mV}$ at 300 K — a *must-memorize* constant.
- **Band-gap of Si** = **$1.12\ \text{eV}$** — the most common KTU numerical value for filtering problems.
- **Anti-islanding** protection is mandatory for grid-tie inverters (per IEEE 1547 / IEC 62116).
- **Series $R_s$** degrades **FF** the most; **Shunt $R_{sh}$** affects the slope near $I_{SC}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solar Cell Efficiency Calculation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Solar Cell Efficiency Calculation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Solar Cell Conversion Efficiency (η)** is defined, in accordance with the **KTU 2024 Energy Systems syllabus**, as the ratio of the **maximum electrical power output** delivered by a photovoltaic (PV) device to the **incident solar radiant power** received at its surface under standardized test conditions (STC).

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{A \cdot G}$$

where every term is a fundamental performance descriptor of the solar cell.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Standard Test Conditions (STC) Mandate**
> - **Solar Irradiance (G)** = **1000 W/m²** (equivalent to 1 Sun)
> - **Air Mass** = **AM 1.5 G** (Global standard solar spectrum)
> - **Cell Temperature** = **25 °C (298.15 K)**
> - **Cell Area (A)** must be reported in **m²**
> All numerical efficiency questions in KTU 2024 ESE use these exact STC values unless explicitly stated otherwise.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rain Bucket" Intuition

Imagine a rectangular bucket placed under a steady rainfall. The total rain hitting the bucket per second is the **input power (P_in)**, but the bucket cannot catch every drop — some splashes out, some evaporates. The water actually stored and usable per second is the **output power (P_max)**, and the **efficiency** is simply "what fraction of the falling rain did the bucket successfully convert into usable water?"

A **solar cell** behaves identically:
- The sunlight falling on it is the **rain** → **P_in = A × G**
- The electricity it produces is the **stored water** → **P_max = V_mp × I_mp**
- Splashing, evaporation, and leakage represent the **electrical, optical, and thermal losses** in the cell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU 2024**
> Efficiency is a **dimensionless quantity** (no units), but is conventionally expressed in **percentage (%)**. Always multiply the final decimal value by 100 before reporting it in the answer script.

## 1.3 Foundational Performance Parameters

The following five parameters jointly govern every efficiency calculation question asked in the KTU ESE:

| Parameter | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Open-Circuit Voltage | $V_{oc}$ | Terminal voltage when load is disconnected (current = 0) |
| Short-Circuit Current | $I_{sc}$ | Terminal current when terminals are shorted (voltage = 0) |
| Maximum Power Point Voltage | $V_{mp}$ | Voltage at the "knee" of the I–V curve giving peak power |
| Maximum Power Point Current | $I_{mp}$ | Current corresponding to $V_{mp}$ |
| Fill Factor | $FF$ | "Squareness" measure of the I–V curve |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these five?** The KTU 2024 valuation key explicitly tests whether the student can correctly identify $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$, $V_{mp}$, $I_{mp}$ from a given I–V curve and apply the efficiency formula. Marks are frequently lost when $V_{mp}$ and $I_{mp}$ are confused with $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Chain — From Sunlight to Efficiency

The operation of a solar cell, and the corresponding calculation of its efficiency, can be broken down into seven sequential reasoning steps aligned with KTU Module 2 learning outcomes:

1. **Photon Absorption:** Incident photons of energy $E_{ph} \ge E_g$ (bandgap) strike the semiconductor and generate electron–hole pairs.
2. **Charge Separation:** The p–n junction's built-in electric field sweeps electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side.
3. **Current Generation:** This directional movement constitutes the photo-generated current $I_L$, the largest contributor to $I_{sc}$.
4. **I–V Characteristic Plot:** When a variable load is connected, the terminal voltage $V$ and current $I$ trace a non-linear I–V curve.
5. **Power Curve Derivation:** Multiplying pointwise gives $P(V) = V \cdot I(V)$, a curve with a single maximum — the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**.
6. **Fill Factor Computation:** $FF$ quantifies how "rectangular" the I–V curve is, bounded by $0 \le FF \le 1$.
7. **Efficiency Finalization:** The ratio $\eta = P_{max} / P_{in}$ produces the final percentage.

## 2.2 Why Each Formula Exists — The "Why-How" Explanation

| Formula | Why it exists | How it is used in KTU ESE |
|---|---|---|
| $P_{in} = A \cdot G$ | Quantifies total incident radiant power | Multiplied with given area and irradiance to find input |
| $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | True usable output at the optimal operating point | Computed directly from MPP coordinates on the graph |
| $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Benchmarks the "ideality" of the cell | Computed when only $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$ are given alongside $P_{max}$ |
| $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{A \cdot G}$ | Ultimate performance metric | Final answer in all efficiency problems |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following table verbatim. Every symbol, its standard unit, and the boundary range appears in KTU ESE questions on a regular basis.

| # | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Standard Unit | Boundary / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{in} = A \cdot G$ | $A$ = cell area, $G$ = irradiance | $m^2 \cdot W/m^2 = W$ | $G_{STC} = 1000\ W/m^2$ |
| 2 | $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ | $V_{mp}$, $I_{mp}$ are MPP coordinates | $V \cdot A = W$ | Always $\le V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ |
| 3 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | "Squareness" of I–V curve | Dimensionless | $0.70 \le FF \le 0.85$ (good cell) |
| 4 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | Conversion efficiency | Percentage | $15\% - 22\%$ (Si cells) |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{A \cdot G} \times 100$ | Expanded form for ESE | Percentage | — |
| 6 | $V_{oc} \approx \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$ | Open-circuit voltage (ideal diode) | Volts | $0.5\ V$ to $0.75\ V$ (Si) |
| 7 | $I_{sc} \approx I_L \propto G$ | Photo-generated current | Amperes | Linear with irradiance |
| 8 | $\eta_{SQ} \approx 33.7\%$ | Shockley–Queisser detailed-balance limit | Percentage | Theoretical ceiling for single-junction |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the absolute-value and fraction bars inside formulas use the LaTeX commands `\dfrac` and `\vert` to ensure clean rendering and to prevent the markdown table syntax from being broken by stray `|` characters — a safeguard mandated by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In practical engineering, solar cell efficiency is the **single most cited metric** in PV module datasheets. It directly determines:
- **$/Watt cost** of electricity generated
- **Land footprint** required for a given rated capacity
- **Levelized Cost of Energy (LCOE)** calculations in utility-scale solar farms
- **Payback period** for rooftop photovoltaic installations

A jump from 16% to 22% efficiency roughly halves the panel area needed for the same power output — making efficiency improvement a **multi-million-dollar R&D target** in the global photovoltaic industry.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete Derivation of the Solar Cell Efficiency Formula

We begin from the single-diode equivalent circuit model of a solar cell, which is the canonical model prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 2.

**Step 1 — Write the terminal current equation using Kirchhoff's Current Law (KCL):**

The total current $I$ leaving the cell equals the photo-generated current $I_L$ minus the diode current $I_D$ and the shunt leakage current $I_{sh}$:

$$I = I_L - I_D - I_{sh}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Shockley diode equation for $I_D$:**

$$I_D = I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 3 — Form the complete ideal single-diode model (assuming $R_{sh} \to \infty$, so $I_{sh} \approx 0$):**

$$I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

**Step 4 — Extract the two boundary-condition currents used in efficiency problems:**

When the terminals are **open-circuited**, $I = 0$. Solving for $V$:

$$0 = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{kT}\right) - 1 \right]$$

Rearranging the exponential:

$$\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{oc}}{kT}\right) = \frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1$$

Taking the natural logarithm:

$$V_{oc} = \frac{kT}{q} \ln\!\left(\frac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$$

When the terminals are **short-circuited**, $V = 0$, and the diode term collapses to zero:

$$I_{sc} = I_L - I_0 \left[ e^{0} - 1 \right] = I_L - I_0 (1 - 1) = I_L$$

**Step 5 — Define the Fill Factor geometrically from the I–V curve:**

The power curve $P(V) = V \cdot I(V)$ is differentiated and set to zero to locate the MPP. The resulting maximum power is:

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$$

Dividing by the rectangle area $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ gives the fill factor:

$$FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

**Step 6 — Derive the final efficiency expression:**

The input power is the product of area and incident irradiance:

$$P_{in} = A \cdot G$$

The output power is the maximum extractable electrical power:

$$P_{out} = P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF$$

Therefore, the conversion efficiency is:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{A \cdot G}$$

**Step 7 — Final reporting in percentage form:**

$$\eta\,\left(\%\right) = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{A \cdot G} \times 100$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constants involved are: Boltzmann's constant $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ J/K$, and the elementary charge $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ C$. The thermal voltage at 300 K is $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85\ mV$, which KTU 2024 students are expected to know.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Numerical Pattern)

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 ESE-style):**
A silicon solar cell of area $0.015\ m^2$ is tested under STC. The measured parameters are:
- $V_{oc} = 0.65\ V$
- $I_{sc} = 3.5\ A$
- $V_{mp} = 0.52\ V$
- $I_{mp} = 3.2\ A$
- $G = 1000\ W/m^2$

Calculate: **(a)** the input power, **(b)** the maximum output power, **(c)** the fill factor, and **(d)** the conversion efficiency.

### (a) Input Power Calculation

The total solar radiant power striking the cell is:

$$P_{in} = A \cdot G = 0.015\ m^2 \times 1000\ W/m^2$$

$$\boxed{P_{in} = 15\ W}$$

### (b) Maximum Output Power

$$P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp} = 0.52\ V \times 3.2\ A$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} = 1.664\ W}$$

### (c) Fill Factor Calculation

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{0.52 \times 3.2}{0.65 \times 3.5}$$

Numerator evaluation:

$$0.52 \times 3.2 = 1.664\ W$$

Denominator evaluation:

$$0.65 \times 3.5 = 2.275\ W$$

Therefore:

$$FF = \frac{1.664}{2.275} = 0.7314$$

$$\boxed{FF \approx 0.7314\ (\text{dimensionless})}$$

### (d) Conversion Efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{1.664}{15} \times 100$$

$$\eta = 0.11093 \times 100 = 11.093\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 11.09\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-check using the expanded formula:**
> $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{A \cdot G} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.65 \times 3.5 \times 0.7314}{0.015 \times 1000} \times 100 = \dfrac{1.664}{15} \times 100 = 11.09\%$
> Both forms yield the identical result, confirming the correctness of the derivation.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Varying Parameters

The table below summarizes the directional trend of efficiency with each physical parameter — a **high-frequency KTU 2024 conceptual question**.

| Parameter Increased | Effect on $V_{oc}$ | Effect on $I_{sc}$ | Effect on $FF$ | Net Effect on $\eta$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Irradiance $G$ | Slight logarithmic increase | Linear increase | Marginal change | $\eta$ slightly improves |
| Temperature $T$ | Decreases (~2 mV/°C per cell) | Slight increase | Decreases | **$\eta$ decreases** (critical KTU point) |
| Bandgap $E_g$ | Increases | Decreases | Complex | Optimum near $E_g \approx 1.34\ eV$ |
| Series Resistance $R_s$ | Slight decrease | Slight decrease | **Strong decrease** | **$\eta$ decreases sharply** |
| Shunt Resistance $R_{sh}$ | Slight increase | Slight decrease | Decreases | $\eta$ decreases |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Solar Cell Power Flow

The diagram below illustrates how incident sunlight is converted, conditioned, and measured to yield the efficiency figure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[SUNLIGHT SOURCE G 1000 W per m squared] --> B0[PHOTON ABSORPTION LAYER p n Junction]
    B0 --> C0[CHARGE SEPARATION Built in Electric Field]
    C0 --> D0[PHOTO GENERATED CURRENT I sub L]
    D0 --> E0[TERMINAL I V CHARACTERISTIC]
    E0 --> F0[MAXIMUM POWER POINT Vmp x Imp]
    F0 --> G0[CALCULATE FILL FACTOR FF]
    G0 --> H0[COMPUTE EFFICIENCY eta in percent]
    E0 --> I0[OPEN CIRCUIT VOLTAGE Voc]
    E0 --> J0[SHORT CIRCUIT CURRENT Isc]
    I0 --> G0
    J0 --> G0
    H0 --> K0[KTU FINAL ANSWER]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Sources of Loss in a Real Solar Cell

A "block-level functional architecture" representing the loss mechanisms that cause real efficiency to fall below the Shockley–Queisser theoretical limit.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[INPUT SOLAR POWER 100 percent] --> L1[REFLECTION LOSS 3 to 5 percent]
    L1 --> L2[TRANSMISSION LOSS photons below bandgap 20 percent]
    L2 --> L3[THERMALIZATION LOSS high energy photons 33 percent]
    L3 --> L4[RECOMBINATION LOSS in bulk and surface 5 percent]
    L4 --> L5[RESISTIVE LOSS series and shunt 2 to 5 percent]
    L5 --> OUT[USEFUL ELECTRICAL OUTPUT 15 to 22 percent for silicon]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Choosing the Correct Efficiency Formula Variant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Given Data Type] --> Q2{Is Vmp and Imp directly provided?}
    Q2 -- YES --> F1[Use Pmax equals Vmp times Imp directly]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q3{Is Pmax given in Watts?}
    Q3 -- YES --> F2[Use Pmax directly in numerator]
    Q3 -- NO --> Q4[Use FF times Voc times Isc in numerator]
    F1 --> R1[eta equals Pmax divided by A times G times 100]
    F2 --> R1
    F4[FF times Voc times Isc] --> R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with a letter (e.g., `A0`, `L1`, `Q1`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as standalone node names.
> - Inside double-quoted labels, no markdown formatting tags are inserted; raw uppercase alphanumeric text is used for clarity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define solar cell conversion efficiency and list the standard test conditions used for its measurement.**

**Model Answer:**

Solar cell conversion efficiency ($\eta$) is defined as the ratio of the maximum electrical power output ($P_{max}$) delivered by a photovoltaic cell to the incident solar radiant power ($P_{in}$) on its active area, expressed as a percentage.

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} \times 100\,\%$$

The **Standard Test Conditions (STC)** specified by KTU 2024 are:
- **Irradiance (G)** = **1000 W/m²**
- **Air Mass** = **AM 1.5 G**
- **Cell Temperature** = **25 °C**

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series-2]`
**What is fill factor? Why is it called the "squareness" indicator of the I–V curve?**

**Model Answer:**

The fill factor ($FF$) is the ratio of the maximum obtainable power to the product of open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current:

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

It is called the "squareness" indicator because geometrically, on an I–V plot, $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ represents the **outer rectangle** bounded by the I and V axes, while $V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ is the **inner rectangle** drawn under the actual I–V curve at the MPP. The ratio of these two rectangle areas measures how closely the real curve approaches the ideal rectangular shape.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### `Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive the expression for the conversion efficiency of a solar cell from the single-diode model. Clearly define every symbol used. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Refer to **Section 3.1, Steps 1–7** of these notes for the complete derivation. The expected key points to write in the answer script are:

1. Start from the KCL equation: $I = I_L - I_0 \left[ \exp(qV/kT) - 1 \right]$
2. Apply the boundary condition $I = 0$ to obtain $V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln\left(\dfrac{I_L}{I_0} + 1\right)$
3. Apply the boundary condition $V = 0$ to obtain $I_{sc} = I_L$
4. Define $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$ and $FF = P_{max}/(V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc})$
5. Define $P_{in} = A \cdot G$
6. Combine to yield $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{A \cdot G} \times 100\,\%$

**(b) A silicon solar cell has an area of $0.02\ m^2$. Under STC, the open-circuit voltage is $0.62\ V$, short-circuit current is $4.0\ A$, and the fill factor is $0.74$. Calculate: (i) the input power, (ii) the maximum power output, and (iii) the conversion efficiency. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $A = 0.02\ m^2$
- $G = 1000\ W/m^2$
- $V_{oc} = 0.62\ V$
- $I_{sc} = 4.0\ A$
- $FF = 0.74$

**(i) Input Power:**

$$P_{in} = A \cdot G = 0.02 \times 1000 = 20\ W$$

**(ii) Maximum Power Output:**

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF = 0.62 \times 4.0 \times 0.74$$

$$P_{max} = 2.48 \times 0.74 = 1.8352\ W$$

**(iii) Conversion Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{1.8352}{20} \times 100$$

$$\eta = 0.09176 \times 100 = 9.176\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta \approx 9.18\%}$$

### `Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Series-1]`

**(a) With the help of a labeled I–V characteristic curve, explain the terms: open-circuit voltage, short-circuit current, maximum power point, and fill factor. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The **I–V characteristic curve** of a solar cell plots terminal current $I$ on the y-axis against terminal voltage $V$ on the x-axis. The key labeled points and regions are:

- **Short-Circuit Current ($I_{sc}$):** The y-intercept of the curve at $V = 0$. Represents the maximum current deliverable by the cell.
- **Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$):** The x-intercept of the curve at $I = 0$. Represents the maximum voltage the cell can develop.
- **Maximum Power Point (MPP):** The single point $(V_{mp}, I_{mp})$ on the curve where the product $V \cdot I$ is maximized. The corresponding power is $P_{max} = V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}$.
- **Fill Factor ($FF$):** The ratio of the area of the smaller rectangle under the I–V curve at MPP to the area of the larger rectangle $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$. A higher $FF$ means a more "square" curve and a better cell.

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

**(b) A solar module of total area $0.5\ m^2$ delivers a maximum power of $90\ W$ under an irradiance of $800\ W/m^2$. Calculate its conversion efficiency. If the same module operates under $1000\ W/m^2$ irradiance and the short-circuit current increases linearly to $1.6\ A$ with $V_{oc} = 22\ V$ and $FF = 0.75$, recalculate the efficiency. Comment on the result. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Case 1: $G = 800\ W/m^2$**

$$P_{in} = A \cdot G = 0.5 \times 800 = 400\ W$$

$$\eta_1 = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{90}{400} \times 100 = 22.5\%$$

**Case 2: $G = 1000\ W/m^2$, $I_{sc} = 1.6\ A$, $V_{oc} = 22\ V$, $FF = 0.75$**

$$P_{in} = 0.5 \times 1000 = 500\ W$$

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF = 22 \times 1.6 \times 0.75$$
