$$P_{max} = 35.2 \times 0.75 = 26.4\ W$$

$$\eta_2 = \frac{26.4}{500} \times 100 = 5.28\%$$

**Comment:** In Case 1, the given $P_{max}$ is realistic and yields a reasonable module efficiency. In Case 2, the efficiency is anomalously low because the assumed $I_{sc}$ and $V_{oc}$ are inconsistent with a $90\ W$ rating — illustrating the importance of consistent I–V parameters in efficiency calculations. In practice, $P_{max}$ scales with irradiance, and a well-designed module would deliver about 112 W at 1000 W/m².

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert area to m²:** If the area is given in cm², divide by 10,000 before substitution. A 100 cm² area = 0.01 m².
> 2. **Using $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$ instead of $V_{mp}$ and $I_{mp}$ for $P_{max}$:** These are *different* points on the curve. $P_{max} \le V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$ — always.
> 3. **Omitting the $\times 100$ in the final step:** KTU scripts are penalized when a decimal is reported instead of a percentage.
> 4. **Confusing $G = 1000\ W/m^2$ with $1\ kW/m^2$ for STC:** They are the same value; the conversion is correct, but ensure dimensional consistency in the working.
> 5. **Not reporting FF as dimensionless:** A common 0.5-mark deduction in KTU answer scripts.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize Before ESE**

- The **master efficiency formula** is $\eta = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{A \cdot G} \times 100\,\%$.
- **STC** means $G = 1000\ W/m^2$, **AM 1.5 G**, and $T = 25\,°C$ — KTU 2024 default.
- **$V_{oc}$** is the x-intercept; **$I_{sc}$** is the y-intercept of the I–V curve.
- **$V_{mp}$** and **$I_{mp}$** are the *coordinates of the maximum power point*, NOT the intercepts.
- **Fill Factor** $FF$ ranges from **0.70 to 0.85** for a healthy silicon cell.
- The **Shockley–Queisser limit** for a single-junction solar cell is approximately **33.7%**.
- Silicon cells in production today achieve **15% to 22%**; multi-junction cells can exceed **40%**.
- Efficiency **decreases with rising temperature** (key KTU viva question).
- $I_{sc}$ scales **linearly** with irradiance $G$; $V_{oc}$ scales **logarithmically**.
- Always **multiply the final decimal by 100** to express $\eta$ in **percentage**.
- **Series resistance $R_s$** degrades FF sharply and hence reduces $\eta$.
- **Shunt resistance $R_{sh}$** leaks current and reduces $I_{sc}$, lowering $\eta$.
- The thermal voltage $V_T = kT/q \approx 25.85\ mV$ at 300 K — required in derivations.
- The temperature coefficient of $V_{oc}$ for silicon is approximately **$-2\ mV/°C$** per cell.
- The **bandgap of crystalline silicon** is $E_g = 1.12\ eV$; the optimum for single-junction efficiency is near $E_g = 1.34\ eV$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind Energy Systems - Working Principle - Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wind Energy Systems - Working Principle - Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🌬️ Wind Energy Systems — Core Technical Foundation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Wind Energy Conversion System (WECS)** is an electromechanical apparatus that converts the **kinetic energy** of naturally moving air masses (wind) into useful mechanical shaft work, and subsequently into **electrical energy** through a coupled generator unit. According to KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature, wind energy systems are classified as a subset of *non-conventional / renewable energy resources* governed by aerodynamic principles (lift and drag), electromechanical power conversion, and grid synchronization.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 2 of PERAT637 (Energy Systems) requires students to demonstrate understanding of the working principle of WECS, the block-level functional architecture of horizontal-axis wind turbine systems (HAWT), and the role of each subsystem in the energy conversion chain.

### Governing Standards & Constants
- **Air density (ρ):** Standard value = **1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, 15°C.
- **Swept area (A):** Geometric projection of the rotor disc = **π × R²** (m²), where R is the rotor radius.
- **Wind speed (v):** Measured in m/s at hub height (typically 10 m or 80–120 m reference).
- **Betz Limit (Cₚ,max):** Theoretical maximum = **16/27 ≈ 0.5926 (≈ 59.3 %)**.
- **Power rating range:** Utility-scale WECS = **1.5 MW to 15 MW** (offshore).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — "The River Analogy"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine wind as a *flowing river* of air molecules. A wind turbine behaves exactly like an **old-fashioned water wheel** placed in that river. The faster the river flows, the more work the wheel can perform. However, no matter how perfectly you design the wheel, you can never extract **all** the kinetic energy of the water — the water must leave the wheel with *some* residual velocity, otherwise it would pile up downstream. This is the physical origin of the **Betz Limit (59.3 %)**.

**Why does this matter?**
- If the turbine extracts **100 %** of the wind's kinetic energy, the air stops behind the rotor, creating a "wall" of stagnant air. This blocks new wind from entering. Nature therefore imposes a *thermodynamic ceiling* on extraction.
- The **16/27 factor** is a mathematical consequence of momentum conservation applied to a control volume of air crossing the rotor disc.

---

## 1.3 Categorization of WECS

| Type | Axis Orientation | Typical Application | Capacity Range |
|------|------------------|---------------------|----------------|
| **HAWT** (Horizontal Axis) | Rotor shaft parallel to wind | Utility-scale farms | 1.5 – 15 MW |
| **VAWT** (Vertical Axis) | Rotor shaft perpendicular to wind | Off-grid, urban | 0.1 – 100 kW |
| **Offshore WECS** | HAWT mounted on floating/fixed substructure | Deep-sea wind farms | 5 – 15 MW |
| **Distributed / Micro WECS** | Small HAWT/VAWT | Residential, telecom towers | < 10 kW |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU 2024 examination purposes, the **HAWT (Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine)** working principle and block diagram is the most heavily tested topology, as it dominates global installed wind capacity.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Working Principle (HAWT)

1. **Wind Capture (Aerodynamic Stage):** Wind striking the rotor blades exerts a pressure differential. Modern blades use the **Bernoulli + lift principle** (airfoil shape) to generate a net lift force, causing rotation.
2. **Mechanical Transmission Stage:** The rotor (low-speed shaft, ~15–20 rpm) is coupled via a **gearbox** that steps up the speed (~1500–1800 rpm) required to drive an electrical generator.
3. **Electromechanical Conversion Stage:** The high-speed shaft drives a **synchronous or induction generator**, producing AC electrical power (variable frequency, variable voltage).
4. **Power Conditioning Stage:** A **power electronic converter (AC-DC-AC)** rectifies the wild AC into fixed-frequency, grid-compatible AC.
5. **Grid Interface Stage:** A **step-up transformer** raises the voltage to transmission levels (11 kV / 33 kV / 132 kV) before feeding the **utility grid**.
6. **Control & Safety Stage:** The **SCADA-based controller** continuously adjusts **pitch angle** (blade rotation), **yaw alignment** (nacelle rotation), and engages the **aerodynamic / mechanical brake** during overspeed or fault conditions.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize these equations. Almost every Part B question in PERAT637 Module 2 tests **at least one** of them. **Avoid using the vertical pipe `|` symbol inside table cells** — use `\vert` instead.

| # | Formula | Description / Unit |
|---|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | $P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^{3}$ | Available power in wind (Watts) |
| 2 | $A = \pi R^{2}$ | Swept area of rotor disc (m²) |
| 3 | $C_{p} = \dfrac{P_{\text{turbine}}}{P_{\text{wind}}}$ | Power coefficient (dimensionless) |
| 4 | $C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Betz theoretical ceiling |
| 5 | $P_{\text{turbine}} = C_{p}\,\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^{3}$ | Mechanical power extracted (W) |
| 6 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{v}$ | Tip Speed Ratio (TSR), dimensionless |
| 7 | $E = P_{\text{turbine}} \times T \times \eta_{\text{gen}} \times \eta_{\text{conv}}$ | Annual energy yield (Wh) |
| 8 | $v_{z} = v_{\text{ref}}\left(\dfrac{z}{z_{\text{ref}}}\right)^{\alpha}$ | Wind shear (power law), $\alpha$ ≈ 0.143 (open terrain) |
| 9 | $C_{T} = \dfrac{2 P_{\text{wind,in}} - 2 P_{\text{wind,out}}}{\rho A v^{3}}$ | Thrust coefficient |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{gear}} \times \eta_{\text{gen}} \times \eta_{\text{conv}}$ | System efficiency chain |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for formulae:** *"P = Half Rho A v Cube"* — this single line is the most-asked equation in KTU Renewable Energy papers. A common KTU student trap: writing $v^{2}$ instead of $v^{3}$ in the power equation (a **0-mark mistake**).

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wind Resource Assessment:** Engineers use the $v^{3}$ dependence to justify siting turbines at higher hub heights (every 10 m altitude gain ≈ **+1–2 %** wind speed = **+3–6 %** power).
- **Cut-in / Cut-out Speeds:** Turbines are designed to operate within a band (typically **3 m/s cut-in, 25 m/s cut-out**). Beyond cut-out, the blades are pitched to 90° (feathered) to prevent structural failure.
- **Capacity Factor (CF):** Ratio of actual annual energy produced to the theoretical maximum if operating at rated power 24/7. **Onshore CF ≈ 25–35 %**, **Offshore CF ≈ 45–55 %**.
- **Wind Farm Layout:** Turbines are spaced 5–10 rotor diameters apart to minimize wake losses (≈ 5–10 % per downstream row).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Power Available in Wind

Consider a control volume of air of cross-sectional area $A$ moving with velocity $v$ (m/s).

**Step 1:** Mass of air passing the rotor disc per unit time (mass flow rate):

$$
\dot{m} = \rho A v
$$

where $\rho$ is the **air density** (kg/m³), $A$ is **swept area** (m²), and $v$ is **wind velocity** (m/s).

**Step 2:** Kinetic energy of a moving body of mass $m$ is:

$$
KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the *kinetic energy flux* (i.e., power) carried by the wind per unit time is obtained by replacing $m$ with the mass flow rate $\dot{m}$:

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $\dot{m} = \rho A v$:

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2} (\rho A v) v^{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Final compact form:

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,A\,v^{3}
$$

> **Interpretation:** The cubic dependence on velocity means that **doubling wind speed gives 8× the power**. This is why even marginal improvements in turbine placement (height, terrain, seasonal pattern) yield disproportionately large energy gains.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Power Extracted & The Betz Limit

To find the *maximum* fraction of $P_{\text{wind}}$ that a turbine can extract, we use **1-D momentum theory** (Rankine–Froude actuator disc model).

**Step 1:** Let $v_1$ = upstream wind velocity, $v_2$ = velocity at the rotor disc, $v_3$ = downstream wake velocity. By continuity and energy considerations, the optimal condition is:

$$
v_2 = \frac{v_1 + v_3}{2}
$$

**Step 2:** The thrust (force) on the disc, derived from momentum flux change:

$$
F = \rho A v_2 (v_1 - v_3)
$$

**Step 3:** Power extracted = Force × velocity at the disc:

$$
P_{\text{ext}} = F \cdot v_2 = \rho A v_2^{2} (v_1 - v_3)
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $v_2$ from Step 1 and introducing the *induction factor* $a$ (where $v_2 = v_1(1-a)$ and $v_3 = v_1(1-2a)$):

$$
P_{\text{ext}} = \rho A v_1^{3} \cdot 4a(1-a)^{2}
$$

**Step 5:** Available wind power (from Section 3.1, using $v_1$ as the upstream velocity):

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v_1^{3}
$$

**Step 6:** Power coefficient $C_p$ (ratio of extracted to available):

$$
C_{p} = \frac{P_{\text{ext}}}{P_{\text{wind}}} = 4a(1-a)^{2}
$$

**Step 7:** To find the *maximum* of $C_p$, differentiate with respect to $a$ and equate to zero:

$$
\frac{dC_p}{da} = 4(1-a)^{2} - 8a(1-a) = 0
$$

**Step 8:** Factorising the expression:

$$
4(1-a)\bigl[(1-a) - 2a\bigr] = 0
$$

**Step 9:** Solving $(1 - 3a) = 0$ (rejecting $a=1$ which means no wind left):

$$
a = \frac{1}{3}
$$

**Step 10:** Substituting $a = 1/3$ back into the $C_p$ expression:

$$
C_{p,\max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}
$$

**Step 11:** Final numerical result:

$$
C_{p,\max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.5926 \quad (\text{or } 59.3\%)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **Betz Limit** — the most famous result in wind energy theory. No turbine in the world, no matter how aerodynamically perfect, can extract more than **59.3 %** of the kinetic energy in the wind. Modern utility-scale HAWTs achieve $C_p$ in the range **0.45 – 0.50**.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A HAWT has a rotor diameter of 80 m. The wind speed is 12 m/s, air density is 1.225 kg/m³, and the power coefficient is 0.45. Find (a) the available wind power, and (b) the power extracted by the turbine.

**Solution:**

Swept area:

$$
A = \pi R^{2} = \pi (40)^{2} = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**(a) Available wind power:**

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2} (1.225)(5026.55)(12)^{3}
$$

Intermediate computation $12^{3} = 1728$:

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728
$$

$$
P_{\text{wind}} \approx 5.32 \ \text{MW}
$$

**(b) Power extracted:**

$$
P_{\text{turbine}} = C_{p} \cdot P_{\text{wind}} = 0.45 \times 5.32
$$

$$
P_{\text{turbine}} \approx 2.39 \ \text{MW}
$$

> **Answer:** $P_{\text{wind}} \approx 5.32$ MW, $P_{\text{turbine}} \approx 2.39$ MW.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Structural Block Diagram — Wind Energy Conversion System

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram (HAWT Architecture)

The diagram below maps the *complete energy and signal flow* of a Horizontal-Axis Wind Turbine System, from kinetic wind input to grid injection. Auxiliary control and safety feedback paths are shown as **dashed lines**.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A1[WIND KINETIC ENERGY INPUT] --> A2[ROTOR BLADES AERODYNAMIC LIFT DRAG]
    A2 --> A3[HUB ASSEMBLY]
    A3 --> A4[LOW SPEED SHAFT 15 TO 20 RPM]
    A4 --> A5[GEARBOX SPEED MULTIPLIER]
    A5 --> A6[HIGH SPEED SHAFT 1500 TO 1800 RPM]
    A6 --> A7[GENERATOR AC OUTPUT]
    A7 --> A8[POWER ELECTRONIC CONVERTER AC DC AC]
    A8 --> A9[STEP UP TRANSFORMER]
    A9 --> A10[GRID OR LOCAL LOAD 11 KV 33 KV]
    A11[PITCH CONTROL ACTUATOR] -.-> A2
    A12[YAW DRIVE MOTOR] -.-> A2
    A13[BRAKE SYSTEM AERODYNAMIC AND MECHANICAL] -.-> A3
    A14[SCADA CONTROLLER AND SENSORS] -.-> A7
    A14 -.-> A11
    A14 -.-> A13
    A15[ANEMOMETER AND WIND VANE] --> A14
```

## 4.2 Functional Block Description (Block-by-Block)

| Block | Component | Primary Function |
|-------|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | **Wind Kinetic Energy Input** | Source — bulk airflow with mass flow rate $\dot{m} = \rho A v$ |
| 2 | **Rotor Blades** | Capture kinetic energy via aerodynamic lift; pitch-angle adjustable |
| 3 | **Hub** | Mechanically couples blades to low-speed shaft |
| 4 | **Low-Speed Shaft** | Transmits slow, high-torque rotation to gearbox |
| 5 | **Gearbox** | Steps up rotational speed by factor of ≈ 100 |
| 6 | **High-Speed Shaft** | Drives generator at synchronous speed |
| 7 | **Generator** | Converts mechanical energy to electrical (DFIG or PMSG typical) |
| 8 | **Power Converter** | Rectifies & inverts AC to grid-compatible 50/60 Hz |
| 9 | **Transformer** | Steps up voltage for transmission |
| 10 | **Grid / Load** | End-user delivery at distribution/transmission level |
| A | **Pitch & Yaw Control** | Optimizes blade angle & nacelle orientation |
| B | **Brake System** | Safety shutdown at overspeed / fault |
| C | **SCADA Controller** | Supervisory automation & sensor feedback |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why gearbox is sometimes eliminated?** Direct-drive turbines (e.g., Enercon E-126) use **multi-pole permanent-magnet synchronous generators** to remove the gearbox, reducing maintenance cost and improving reliability — but at the expense of heavier nacelle mass.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**
**Define the term "Power Coefficient" (Cₚ) of a wind turbine. State its theoretical maximum value and explain its physical significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Power coefficient is the ratio of mechanical power extracted by the turbine to the total kinetic power available in the wind:

$$
C_{p} = \frac{P_{\text{turbine}}}{P_{\text{wind}}}
$$

- **Maximum value (1 mark):** Theoretical maximum $C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ (the **Betz Limit**, derived by Albert Betz, 1919).
- **Physical significance (1 mark):** The Betz Limit establishes that no wind turbine, regardless of design perfection, can convert more than **59.3 %** of the wind's kinetic energy into mechanical work. The remaining ~40.7 % must remain in the downstream wake to satisfy conservation of momentum.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**With the help of a neat block diagram, list any six major subsystems of a Horizontal Axis Wind Turbine (HAWT).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — list format):**
1. Rotor Blade Assembly
2. Gearbox
3. Generator
4. Power Electronic Converter
5. Step-up Transformer
6. SCADA / Pitch-Yaw Controller
7. Yaw Mechanism & Brake System *(any six)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Part A):** Do not write vague terms like "rotor" or "controller" alone — the examiner expects **specific subsystem names** such as *"pitch control actuator"* or *"AC-DC-AC power converter"*. A single-word answer can cost you **1 mark**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **power available in wind** passing through a swept area $A$ with velocity $v$ and density $\rho$. State the **Betz Limit** and discuss the physical reasons why 100 % extraction is impossible. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 60 m. The hub-height wind speed is 14 m/s and the air density is 1.2 kg/m³. If the power coefficient is 0.42 and the combined generator + converter efficiency is 92 %, calculate the **electrical power output** in kW. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition & Derivation (5 marks):**

Kinetic energy of a mass $m$ moving with velocity $v$:

$$
KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^{2}
$$

Mass flow rate of air through swept area $A$:

$$
\dot{m} = \rho A v
$$

Power in the wind = rate of kinetic energy flow:

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} v^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}
$$

**Betz Limit Discussion (2 marks):**
- Maximum theoretical $C_p = 16/27 \approx 0.593$ (Betz, 1919).
- If the turbine tried to extract **100 %** of the kinetic energy, the air would have to come to a *complete stop* behind the rotor. This would prevent fresh air from entering the disc, choking the system. Momentum conservation therefore forces the wake to retain at least **1/3** of the upstream kinetic energy.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Given: $D = 60$ m → $R = 30$ m, $v = 14$ m/s, $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $C_p = 0.42$, $\eta = 0.92$.

**Step 1 — Swept area:**

$$
A = \pi R^{2} = \pi (30)^{2} = 900\pi \approx 2827.43 \ \text{m}^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Available wind power:**

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = \tfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(2827.43)(14)^{3}
$$

Compute $14^3 = 2744$:

$$
P_{\text{wind}} = 0.6 \times 2827.43 \times 2744 \approx 4655.5 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical power extracted by rotor:**

$$
P_{\text{mech}} = C_p \times P_{\text{wind}} = 0.42 \times 4655.5 \approx 1955.3 \ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 4 — Electrical power output after generator & converter losses:**

$$
P_{\text{elec}} = \eta \times P_{\text{mech}} = 0.92 \times 1955.3 \approx 1798.9 \ \text{kW}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $P_{\text{elec}} \approx 1.80$ MW (≈ 1799 kW)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Draw and explain the **block diagram of a Horizontal Axis Wind Energy Conversion System (HAWT)**, clearly stating the function of **each block** in the energy conversion chain. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)**? Explain its role in determining the aerodynamic efficiency of a wind turbine. A 3-blade HAWT has a rotor radius of 25 m, runs at 30 rpm in a 10 m/s wind. Find the TSR and comment on whether the operating point is near optimal. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**(5 marks for diagram, 2 marks for explanation)** — Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4 of these notes. The expected answer must include:
- Aerodynamic subsystem (blades + hub)
- Mechanical transmission (low-speed shaft, gearbox, high-speed shaft)
- Electromechanical conversion (generator)
- Power conditioning (converter, transformer)
- Control & safety (SCADA, pitch-yaw, brake)
- Grid interface

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Definition (1 mark):**

$$
\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v}
$$

where $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s), $R$ = rotor radius (m), $v$ = wind speed (m/s).

**Role (2 marks):** TSR determines the angle of attack on the blade airfoil. Each rotor design has an **optimal TSR (λ\*)** at which $C_p$ is maximum (typically 6–8 for 3-blade utility HAWTs). Operating far from λ\* results in aerodynamic stall or undertip loading.

**Step 1 — Convert 30 rpm to rad/s:**

$$
\omega = 30 \times \frac{2\pi}{60} = \pi \ \text{rad/s} \approx 3.142 \ \text{rad/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute TSR:**

$$
\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{v} = \frac{(3.142)(25)}{10} = \frac{78.55}{10} \approx 7.85
$$

**Step 3 — Comment (1 mark):** The optimal TSR for a typical 3-blade HAWT is ≈ 7–8. Therefore, **λ = 7.85 lies within the optimal band**, indicating the turbine is operating near peak aerodynamic efficiency for this wind speed.

> **Final Answer:** $\lambda \approx 7.85$ — operating near optimal.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this topic:**
> 1. **Unit errors (–1 to –2 marks):** Forgetting to convert **diameter to radius** in the swept area formula, or computing rotor area as $\pi D^{2}$ instead of $\pi R^{2}$.
> 2. **Wrong exponent (–2 marks):** Writing $v^{2}$ instead of $v^{3}$ in the wind power formula.
> 3. **Missing constants (–1 mark):** Failing to state the air density value used in calculations. Always write $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³ (sea-level standard).
> 4. **Betz Limit mis-statement (–1 mark):** Writing "Cₚ = 0.59" without the exact fraction **16/27** loses a mark — examiners reward the *exact* form.
> 5. **Block diagram omission (–3 to –5 marks):** In Part B, drawing the block diagram but **not labeling each block's function** is a major deduction. Always pair every block with a one-line functional description.
> 6. **Unit conversion of angular velocity (–1 mark):** Using rpm directly in $\omega R / v$ (a very common error). Convert rpm → rad/s using $\omega = \text{rpm} \times 2\pi / 60$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before walking into the KTU exam hall, ensure you can confidently answer "yes" to every item below:**

- ☐ Can write the **wind power equation** $P = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho A v^{3}$ from memory, with **all three variables** correctly defined and units.
- ☐ Know the **Betz Limit** value: $C_{p,\max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ — and can state **at least one physical reason** why 100 % extraction is impossible (momentum conservation / wake retention).
- ☐ Can sketch and **label every block** of the HAWT block diagram (Rotor → Gearbox → Generator → Converter → Transformer → Grid, plus control & safety feedback).
- ☐ Understand the **role of gearbox** (speed step-up ≈ 100×) and why some modern turbines (e.g., direct-drive) eliminate it.
- ☐ Can define and compute **Tip Speed Ratio (TSR)** $\lambda = \omega R / v$, including **correct unit conversion** from rpm to rad/s.
- ☐ Know typical **operating wind speed bands**: cut-in ≈ 3 m/s, rated ≈ 12–15 m/s, cut-out ≈ 25 m/s.
- ☐ Can state the difference between **HAWT and VAWT** and identify which is dominant in utility-scale applications.
- ☐ Can compute **electrical power output** after applying $C_p$ (mechanical extraction) and $\eta_{\text{gen+conv}}$ (efficiency chain).
- ☐ Know the **wind shear (power law)** formula for vertical wind speed extrapolation: $v_{z} = v_{\text{ref}}(z / z_{\text{ref}})^{\alpha}$.
- ☐ Can list the **four governing aerodynamic forces** on a wind blade: lift, drag, thrust, and torque.

> 🎯 **Final Examiner Tip:** In KTU Part B (14-mark) questions on WECS, the examiner typically allocates **7 marks for derivation** and **7 marks for numerical computation**. Always present the derivation in a **step-numbered, equation-based format** — bullet-pointed text without equations will not receive full credit.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind Power Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wind Power Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Wind Power Equation** is the foundational mathematical relationship in wind energy engineering that quantifies the extractable kinetic energy possessed by a moving mass of air intersecting the swept area of a wind turbine rotor. It establishes that the power available in the wind is directly proportional to the **air density**, the **swept area** of the rotor blades, and the **cube of the wind velocity**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the wind power equation forms the theoretical basis for:

- **Wind Resource Assessment** – Estimating the gross energy potential at any geographical site.
- **Turbine Sizing & Selection** – Matching the aerodynamic capacity of a machine to the prevailing wind regime.
- **Energy Yield Prediction** – Calculating Annual Energy Production (AEP) of wind farms.
- **Capacity Factor Estimation** – Relating installed nameplate capacity to actual generation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The Wind Power Equation is the gateway concept to **Aerodynamics of Wind Turbines**. Every subsequent topic – Betz Limit, Tip Speed Ratio, Power Coefficient curves, and design of rotor blades – is mathematically derived from this single cubic-velocity relationship.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — "The River and the Water Wheel":**
>
> Imagine a flowing river representing the wind. A water wheel placed in the river represents a wind turbine. The **power of the river** depends on three things:
>
> 1. **How fast the water flows** → analogous to **wind velocity (V)**
> 2. **The cross-section area of water hitting the wheel** → analogous to the **swept area (A)** of the turbine blades
> 3. **How dense the water is (how heavy)** → analogous to **air density (ρ)**
>
> If you double the wind speed, you don't get double the power — you get **eight times** the power! This cubic relationship is the single most important fact about wind energy and is why sites with slightly higher wind speeds are dramatically more productive.

### 1.3 Fundamental Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical constants and standard atmospheric parameters are used throughout wind power calculations:

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Air Density (ISA Sea Level) | $\rho$ | **1.225** | kg/m³ |
| Betz Coefficient (Maximum) | $C_{P,max}$ | **16/27 ≈ 0.593** | dimensionless |
| Betz Limit Power Multiplier | $k_{Betz}$ | **0.593** | dimensionless |
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | $p$ | **101.325** | kPa |
| Standard Temperature | $T$ | **288.15** | K |
| Gas Constant for Air | $R$ | **287.05** | J/(kg·K) |
| Air Density Correction Factor | — | **1.225** | kg/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why air density matters:** Air is roughly **800 times less dense than water**, which is why wind turbines must have very large rotor diameters (often **80–180 m**) to capture meaningful amounts of power. Even a 1% change in air density (due to temperature or altitude) translates to a 1% change in available wind power.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Why Wind Carries Power

Wind is simply **air in motion**. Any moving mass possesses **kinetic energy** by virtue of its velocity. When this moving stream of air encounters a turbine rotor, it transfers part of its kinetic energy to the rotating blades.

The energy transfer chain can be broken down into **four sequential stages**:

- **Stage 1 — Kinetic Energy in the Wind:** Air molecules moving at velocity $V$ carry kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$ per unit mass.
- **Stage 2 — Mass Flow Rate Through the Rotor:** A column of air of cross-sectional area $A$ flowing at velocity $V$ delivers a mass per unit time $\dot{m} = \rho A V$.
- **Stage 3 — Power Available in the Wind:** Combining kinetic energy per unit mass with mass flow rate yields the wind power equation.
- **Stage 4 — Practical Power Extraction:** A real turbine cannot extract 100% of this power. The **Betz Limit** caps extraction at **59.3%**, while engineering losses reduce this further.

### 2.2 Why the Cubic Relationship Exists — The "Why" Behind the Equation

The cubic dependence on wind velocity arises from the multiplication of two separate cubic effects:

- **Kinetic energy per unit mass** is proportional to $V^2$ (because $KE = \tfrac{1}{2}mv^2$).
- **Mass flow rate** is also proportional to $V$ (because $\dot{m} = \rho A V$).

Therefore: $P \propto V^2 \times V = V^3$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Consequence of the Cubic Law:**
> - A wind speed of 6 m/s contains **8 times** more power than a wind speed of 3 m/s.
> - A wind speed of 12 m/s contains **64 times** more power than 3 m/s.
> - This is why wind resource assessment is so critical — a 25% error in measured wind speed translates to almost **100% error** in estimated power.

### 2.3 The "How" — Assumptions in the Standard Wind Power Equation

The standard form $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ is built on the following assumptions:

- **Steady, Uniform Flow:** Wind speed is constant across the rotor disk and over time.
- **Incompressible Flow:** Air density $\rho$ does not change as the wind passes through the rotor.
- **No Obstructions:** The wind stream is unaffected by nearby turbines or terrain (used for a single, isolated turbine).
- **Frontal Disk Model:** All air passing through the swept area is treated as a uniform cylindrical column.
- **No Wake Effects:** Air leaving the rotor is assumed to leave cleanly without significant turbulence.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

The Wind Power Equation is not just academic — it directly drives decisions in:

| Industry Application | Use of the Equation |
|---|---|
| **Wind Farm Site Assessment** | Estimating gross wind resource potential from measured anemometer data |
| **Turbine Manufacturing (Vestas, Siemens Gamesa, GE)** | Specifying nameplate rated power for new turbine designs |
| **Offshore Wind Engineering** | Determining tower height required to access stronger, less-turbulent winds |
| **Hybrid Renewable Systems** | Sizing wind component alongside solar PV in microgrids |
| **Grid Integration Studies** | Forecasting variable generation for utility load balancing |
| **Climate & Atmospheric Studies** | Computing global wind energy budgets over landmasses and oceans |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Formulas — High-Yield for KTU University Exam**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ | Available power in the wind (W) | $V$ in m/s, $A$ in m², $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ | Mass flow rate (kg/s) | Through the swept area |
| 3 | $A = \pi R^2$ | Swept area (m²) | $R$ = rotor radius |
| 4 | $P_{turbine} = C_P \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ | Mechanical power extracted (W) | $C_P$ is the power coefficient |
| 5 | $C_P = \dfrac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}}$ | Power coefficient (dimensionless) | $0 \le C_P \le 0.593$ |
| 6 | $C_{P,max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ | Betz Limit / Betz Coefficient | Theoretical maximum |
| 7 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{16}{27} \cdot \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ | Maximum extractable power (W) | Achievable only with ideal turbine |
| 8 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\omega R}{V} = \dfrac{\pi R n}{30 V}$ | Tip Speed Ratio (TSR) | $n$ = rpm, $\omega$ = rad/s |
| 9 | $\rho = \dfrac{p}{R T}$ | Air density from ideal gas law | Temperature & pressure dependent |
| 10 | $P_{wind} \propto V^3$ | Cubic velocity law | Doubling wind speed → 8× power |
| 11 | $E_{annual} = \sum P_i \cdot t_i$ | Annual Energy Production (J or kWh) | Sum over wind speed bins |
| 12 | $\beta = \dfrac{V_{downstream}}{V_{upstream}}$ | Interference factor (Betz analysis) | Optimal $\beta = 1/3$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Always write the **units** explicitly in every step of your numerical solution. Marks are specifically allocated for unit consistency in KTU 2024 scheme answer scripts.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Fundamental Wind Power Equation

We begin from first principles using the kinetic energy of a moving fluid element.

**Step 1 — Kinetic Energy of a Single Air Parcel:**

Consider a small parcel (lump) of air of mass $m$ moving at velocity $V$. By the classical definition of kinetic energy from Newtonian mechanics:

$$KE = \frac{1}{2} m V^2$$

**Step 2 — Expressing Mass in Terms of Density and Volume:**

The mass of any fluid parcel can be expressed as the product of its density and the volume it occupies:

$$m = \rho \cdot \mathcal{V}$$

where $\rho$ is the mass density of air (kg/m³) and $\mathcal{V}$ is the volume (m³).

**Step 3 — Volume Flow Rate Through the Swept Area:**

A wind turbine rotor presents a circular disk of area $A = \pi R^2$ to the incoming wind. In one second, a column of air of length $V$ (metres) passes through this disk. Therefore, the volume of air passing through the rotor per unit time is:

$$\frac{d\mathcal{V}}{dt} = A \cdot V$$

**Step 4 — Mass Flow Rate Through the Rotor:**

Multiplying the volume flow rate by the air density gives the mass flow rate (kg/s):

$$\dot{m} = \frac{dm}{dt} = \rho \cdot A \cdot V$$

**Step 5 — Power as Rate of Kinetic Energy Transfer:**

Power is defined as the time-rate of change of energy. Since each kilogram of air carries kinetic energy $\tfrac{1}{2}V^2$, and $\dot{m}$ kilograms pass per second, the total power carried by the wind is:

$$P_{wind} = \dot{m} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Final Wind Power Equation:**

Substituting $\dot{m} = \rho A V$ into the power expression:

$$P_{wind} = (\rho A V) \cdot \frac{V^2}{2}$$

$$\boxed{P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3}$$

This is the **fundamental wind power equation** and is the most heavily tested formula in this module.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: The Betz Limit (Maximum Theoretical Power Extraction)

This is one of the most frequently asked **14-mark derivations** in KTU University Exams. The German physicist **Albert Betz** (1919) proved that no turbine — no matter how perfectly designed — can extract more than **59.3%** of the kinetic energy in the wind.

**Step 1 — Control Volume Setup:**

Consider an ideal wind turbine enclosed in a "stream tube" (a virtual cylindrical boundary that follows the streamlines of the flow).

- Upstream (far before rotor): velocity $= V_1$, area $= A_1$, pressure $= p_1$
- At the rotor disk: velocity $= V_d$, area $= A_d$, pressure $= p_d$
- Downstream (far after rotor): velocity $= V_2$, area $= A_2$, pressure $= p_2$

**Step 2 — Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation):**

Mass entering the stream tube must equal mass leaving it:

$$\rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A_d V_d = \rho A_2 V_2$$

$$A_1 V_1 = A_d V_d = A_2 V_2$$

**Step 3 — Definition of the Interference Factor (Induction Factor):**

Define the **axial induction factor** $a$ as the fractional reduction in wind speed at the rotor disk:

$$V_d = V_1 (1 - a)$$

The downstream velocity (by continuity in the far wake) is:

$$V_2 = V_1 (1 - 2a)$$

**Step 4 — Force on the Rotor from Momentum Theory:**

By Newton's second law, the thrust force on the rotor equals the rate of change of momentum of the air stream:

$$F = \dot{m}(V_1 - V_2)$$

$$F = \rho A_d V_d (V_1 - V_2)$$

Substituting $V_d$ and $V_2$:

$$F = \rho A_d V_1(1-a) \cdot V_1 \cdot 2a = 2 \rho A_d V_1^2 a (1-a)$$

**Step 5 — Power Extracted by the Rotor:**

Power is force times velocity. The relevant velocity is the velocity **at the rotor disk**:

$$P_{turbine} = F \cdot V_d = 2 \rho A_d V_1^2 a (1-a) \cdot V_1(1-a)$$

$$P_{turbine} = 2 \rho A_d V_1^3 a (1-a)^2$$

**Step 6 — Express Power Extracted as a Fraction of Available Wind Power:**

Divide by the total wind power $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A_d V_1^3$:

$$C_P = \frac{P_{turbine}}{P_{wind}} = \frac{2 \rho A_d V_1^3 a (1-a)^2}{\tfrac{1}{2} \rho A_d V_1^3}$$

$$C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$$

**Step 7 — Maximize $C_P$ with Respect to $a$:**

Differentiate $C_P$ with respect to $a$ and set equal to zero:

$$\frac{dC_P}{da} = 4 \left[ (1-a)^2 + a \cdot 2(1-a)(-1) \right] = 4(1-a)(1-3a) = 0$$

This gives either $a = 1$ (trivial, no flow) or:

$$a = \frac{1}{3}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $a = 1/3$ Back into $C_P$ Expression:**

$$C_{P,max} = 4 \cdot \frac{1}{3} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^2 = \frac{4}{3} \cdot \frac{4}{9} = \frac{16}{27}$$

$$\boxed{C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \text{ or } 59.3\%}$$

**Step 9 — Maximum Power Equation:**

$$P_{max} = \frac{16}{27} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \rho A V^3$$

This is the **maximum theoretical power** any horizontal-axis wind turbine can extract.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Examples (KTU Exam Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 1 — Basic Wind Power Calculation**

**Problem:** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 80 m. The wind speed is 12 m/s and air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Calculate the available power in the wind and the maximum extractable power.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the swept area:**

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi (40)^2 = 1600\pi \approx 5026.55 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute available wind power:**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$$

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times (12)^3$$

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 5026.55 \times 1728$$

$$P_{wind} \approx 5{,}320{,}519 \text{ W} \approx 5.32 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3 — Compute maximum extractable power (Betz Limit):**

$$P_{max} = \frac{16}{27} \times 5.32 \text{ MW} \approx 0.593 \times 5.32 \approx 3.15 \text{ MW}$$

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 2 — Effect of Doubling Wind Speed**

**Problem:** A site has a mean wind speed of 6 m/s. The available wind power is $P_1$. What is the new available wind power $P_2$ if the wind speed increases to 9 m/s? Assume all other parameters are constant.

**Solution:**

Since $P \propto V^3$:

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^3 = \left(\frac{9}{6}\right)^3 = (1.5)^3 = 3.375$$

$$P_2 = 3.375 \, P_1$$

The power increases by **237.5%** — a 50% increase in wind speed nearly **triples** the available power.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Example 3 — Tip Speed Ratio Calculation**

**Problem:** A 3-blade wind turbine has a rotor radius of 45 m. It rotates at 18 rpm in a wind speed of 10 m/s. Calculate the tip speed ratio.

**Solution:**

Convert rotational speed to rad/s:

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi n}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 18}{60} = 1.885 \text{ rad/s}$$

Compute tip speed ratio:

$$\lambda = \frac{\omega R}{V} = \frac{1.885 \times 45}{10} = \frac{84.83}{10} \approx 8.48$$

This is a typical TSR for a modern horizontal-axis wind turbine (optimal range: 7–9).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Wind Energy Conversion Chain

The following diagram maps the energy transformation stages from raw wind to grid electricity. It illustrates how the Wind Power Equation integrates into the full engineering system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["INPUT: WIND STREAM"]
    nodeB["STAGE 1: WIND POWER EQUATION P = 0.5 x rho x A x V cubed"]
    nodeC["STAGE 2: MASS FLOW RATE m dot = rho x A x V"]
    nodeD["STAGE 3: KINETIC ENERGY EXTRACTION"]
    nodeE["STAGE 4: BETZ LIMIT Cp max = 16 over 27"]
    nodeF["STAGE 5: TIP SPEED RATIO lambda = omega R over V"]
    nodeG["STAGE 6: ROTOR MECHANICAL POWER P turbine = Cp x P wind"]
    nodeH["STAGE 7: GEARBOX SPEED MULTIPLICATION"]
    nodeI["STAGE 8: GENERATOR ELECTRICAL OUTPUT"]
    nodeJ["OUTPUT: GRID READY ELECTRICITY"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ

    nodeE -.imposes theoretical ceiling.-> nodeG
    nodeF -.optimizes rotor efficiency.-> nodeG
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Velocity Streamlines Through a Wind Turbine (Betz Model)

This diagram represents the control volume used in the Betz Limit derivation, showing the change in air velocity across the rotor disk.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    upstream["UPSTREAM FAR FIELD\nVelocity V1\nArea A1"]
    rotor["ROTOR DISK\nVelocity Vd equals V1 times 1 minus a\nArea A equals pi R squared"]
    downstream["DOWNSTREAM WAKE\nVelocity V2 equals V1 times 1 minus 2a\nArea A2"]

    upstream -- streamlines expand --> rotor
    rotor -- streamlines contract --> downstream

    rotor -.thrust force F.-> rotor
    rotor -.power extraction P turbine.-> rotor
```

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram: Power Coefficient vs Tip Speed Ratio Curve

The following diagram represents the **$C_P$ vs $\lambda$** characteristic curve that is central to wind turbine design.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pointA["A: LOW TSR - STALLED REGIME\nCp very low\nBlades act like flat plates"]
    pointB["B: OPTIMAL TSR - PEAK CP\nCp max equals 16 over 27 equals 0.593\nIdeal Betz operation"]
    pointC["C: HIGH TSR - ABOVE RATED\nCp falls\nGenerator overspeed limit"]

    pointA -- increasing lambda --> pointB
    pointB -- increasing lambda further --> pointC

    axis["X axis: Tip Speed Ratio lambda\nY axis: Power Coefficient Cp"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Modern pitch-controlled turbines actively regulate blade pitch angle to keep the operating point near **B** (peak $C_P$) for as wide a wind speed range as possible. This is called **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)** in wind energy systems.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define the *Wind Power Equation*. State the physical significance of each variable in the equation.

**Model Answer:**
The Wind Power Equation expresses the kinetic power carried by a moving air stream passing through the swept area of a wind turbine rotor:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$$

where:
- $\rho$ = air density (kg/m³), representing how heavy the air is.
- $A$ = swept area of the rotor (m²), i.e., $\pi R^2$.
- $V$ = wind velocity (m/s), the upstream undisturbed wind speed.
- $P_{wind}$ = available power in the wind (Watts).

The cubic dependence on $V$ means even small increases in wind speed produce large increases in available power.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
State the **Betz Limit** and explain why no wind turbine can extract 100% of the wind's kinetic energy.

**Model Answer:**
The **Betz Limit** states that the maximum fraction of kinetic energy that an ideal wind turbine can extract from the wind is:

$$C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593 \text{ or } 59.3\%$$

A turbine cannot extract 100% of the wind's kinetic energy because:
- If all energy were extracted, the air would have to come to a complete stop at the rotor.
- This would block the incoming wind (like a solid wall), preventing any further flow through the rotor.
- Therefore, the downstream air must retain some velocity ($V_2 > 0$) to allow continued flow, capping extraction at 59.3%.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Regulation:** Part B questions carry **14 marks**, typically split into **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Students must answer **either** the full Question A **or** the full Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** Derive the **Wind Power Equation** $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ starting from the fundamental definition of kinetic energy. Clearly state all assumptions used. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A wind turbine has a rotor diameter of 60 m. At a particular site, the wind speed is 10 m/s and the air density is 1.20 kg/m³. Calculate:
- (i) The total power available in the wind.
- (ii) The maximum theoretical power that can be extracted (using Betz Limit).
- (iii) The actual electrical power output, assuming an overall turbine-generator efficiency of 35%. **[7 Marks]**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation — See Section 3.1 above for full step-by-step derivation.**

Key assumptions to be stated explicitly in the answer script:
- Steady, uniform wind flow.
- Incompressible fluid (constant $\rho$).
- Frontal disk model (uniform velocity across swept area).
- No wake interactions or obstructions.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Swept area calculation:**

$$A = \pi R^2 = \pi (30)^2 = 900\pi \approx 2827.43 \text{ m}^2$$

**Total power available in wind:**

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2}\rho A V^3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.20 \times 2827.43 \times (10)^3$$

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.20 \times 2827.43 \times 1000 = 1{,}696{,}458 \text{ W} \approx 1.696 \text{ MW}$$

**(ii) Maximum theoretical power (Betz Limit):**

$$P_{max} = \frac{16}{27} \times P_{wind} = 0.5926 \times 1{,}696{,}458$$

$$P_{max} \approx 1{,}005{,}334 \text{ W} \approx 1.005 \text{ MW}$$

**(iii) Actual electrical power output (with 35% efficiency):**

$$P_{electrical} = \eta_{overall} \times P_{max} = 0.35 \times 1{,}005{,}334$$

$$P_{electrical} \approx 351{,}867 \text{ W} \approx 352 \text{ kW}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]*

**(a)** State and derive the **Betz Limit**. Show that the maximum power coefficient $C_P$ is $\frac{16}{27}$. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A wind farm consists of 25 identical turbines, each with a rotor diameter of 90 m. The average wind speed at hub height is 9 m/s, air density is 1.225 kg/m³, and the average power coefficient is 0.42. Calculate:
- (i) The mechanical power output of one turbine.
- (ii) The total mechanical power output of the wind farm.
- (iii) The annual energy generation in MWh, assuming the turbines operate at this average power for 75% of the year (capacity factor = 0.75). **[7 Marks]**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**(a) Betz Limit Derivation — See Section 3.2 above for full derivation.**

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Mechanical power of one turbine:**

Swept area:

$$A = \pi (45)^2 = 2025\pi \approx 6361.73 \text{ m}^2$$

Wind power available:

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 6361.73 \times (9)^3$$

$$P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 6361.73 \times 729$$

$$P_{wind} \approx 2{,}841{,}759 \text{ W} \approx 2.84 \text{ MW}$$

Mechanical power extracted (using $C_P = 0.42$):

$$P_{mech} = C_P \times P_{wind} = 0.42 \times 2.84 \text{ MW} \approx 1.19 \text{ MW per turbine}$$

**(ii) Total wind farm mechanical power:**

$$P_{farm} = 25 \times 1.19 \text{ MW} = 29.84 \text{ MW}$$

**(iii) Annual Energy Generation:**

Hours of operation per year (capacity factor = 0.75):

$$t_{operating} = 0.75 \times 8760 = 6570 \text{ hours/year}$$

Annual energy:

$$E_{annual} = P_{farm} \times t_{operating} = 29.84 \times 6570 \approx 196{,}049 \text{ MWh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{annual} \approx 1.96 \times 10^5 \text{ MWh/year}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks on Wind Power Equation Problems:**
>
> 1. **Confusing radius and diameter** — When the rotor **diameter** $D$ is given, students forget to halve it to get radius $R$ before computing $A = \pi R^2$. This error alone causes a **factor-of-4 mistake** in the answer.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to cube the velocity** — A classic error is writing $V^2$ instead of $V^3$. KTU examiners specifically test this — they will deliberately give numerical values that make $V^2$ vs $V^3$ produce widely different answers.
>
> 3. **Skipping the units** — KTU 2024 scheme specifically awards marks for **unit consistency**. Always carry units through every step and write the final answer in MW, kW, or W with explicit units.
>
> 4. **Not stating assumptions in derivations** — In Betz Limit derivation questions, students who do not list the assumptions (steady flow, incompressible flow, uniform disk loading, etc.) lose **2 marks** out of 7.
>
> 5. **Mixing up $C_P$ and efficiency** — The power coefficient $C_P$ is an **aerodynamic** parameter limited by Betz to 0.593. It is NOT the same as overall turbine-generator efficiency, which includes gearbox and generator losses (typically 85–95%).
>
> 6. **Using diameter in $A = \pi R^2$** — A formula sheet pitfall. Always verify which variable is given.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Wind Power Equation**

- [ ] **Core Equation:** $P_{wind} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho A V^3$ — must be memorized verbatim with units.
- [ ] **Cubic Velocity Law:** Power scales with $V^3$ — doubling wind speed = **8× power**.
- [ ] **Swept Area Formula:** $A = \pi R^2$ — diameter must be halved before squaring.
- [ ] **Standard Air Density:** $\rho = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$ at **ISA sea-level conditions** (15°C, 101.325 kPa).
- [ ] **Altitude/Temperature Correction:** Use $\rho = p/(RT)$ when site conditions deviate from standard.
- [ ] **Betz Limit:** $C_{P,max} = \frac{16}{27} \approx 0.593$ — the **theoretical maximum**, never exceeded.
- [ ] **Betz Derivation:** Memorize the key steps — control volume, induction factor $a = 1/3$, $C_P = 4a(1-a)^2$, differentiate to find maximum.
- [ ] **Tip Speed Ratio:** $\lambda = \omega R / V = \pi R n / (30 V)$ — typically **7–9** for modern 3-blade HAWTs.
- [ ] **Power Coefficient Definition:** $C_P = P_{turbine}/P_{wind}$ — dimensionless, $0 \le C_P \le 0.593$.
- [ ] **Practical $C_P$ Values:** Real turbines achieve $C_P \approx 0.35$ to $0.45$ at design wind speed.
- [ ] **Annual Energy Formula:** $E_{annual} = P_{rated} \times 8760 \times \text{Capacity Factor}$ (kWh).
- [ ] **Wind Speed Frequency:** Energy yield calculations use the **Weibull** or **Rayleigh** distribution of wind speeds, not just the mean.
- [ ] **Engineering Use Cases:** Site assessment, turbine sizing, AEP estimation, MPPT design.
- [ ] **Common Exam Pitfalls:** Radius vs diameter, $V^2$ vs $V^3$, missing units, confusing $C_P$ with efficiency.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy from Waves and Tides - Working Principle - Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Energy from Waves and Tides - Working Principle - Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Wave Energy (Ocean Wave Energy)

**Formal Definition:**
Wave Energy is the form of **renewable, mechanical-kinetic energy** harnessed from the surface gravity waves of the ocean. These waves are generated primarily by wind blowing over a vast stretch of seawater (fetch), and they carry **kinetic energy (KE) in the water particle motion** as well as **gravitational potential energy (PE) in the elevated water surface**.

The energy flux transported by ocean waves across a vertical plane (per unit crest length) is given by the well-established linear wave theory (Airy wave theory), which forms the foundation for sizing every wave energy converter (WEC) studied in the KTU syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must be able to (i) draw and explain the **block diagram** of a wave energy conversion system, (ii) state and apply the **wave power equation**, and (iii) compare the **three principal types of WECs** (Oscillating Water Column, Point Absorber, Overtopping).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Rolling Drum"

Imagine you stand at the seashore and watch a wave roll a **large floating drum** up and down. The rising and falling water surface pushes the drum up and down rhythmically. If you connect a piston to that drum and let it pump air through a turbine, the oscillating motion of the waves is converted into **rotating mechanical motion**, which then drives a generator. That is, in essence, how an **Oscillating Water Column (OWC)** wave plant works. The "wave" is the input, the "drum" is the capture chamber, the "piston" is the air column, and the "turbine + generator" is the energy conversion stage.

## 1.3 Tidal Energy

**Formal Definition:**
Tidal Energy is the **predictable form of ocean energy** derived from the periodic rise (flood tide) and fall (ebb tide) of sea level caused by the **gravitational interaction between the Earth, Moon, and Sun**. The usable energy manifests in two distinct physical forms:

1. **Potential (Head) Energy** — exploited by a **Tidal Barrage** or **Tidal Lagoon** (head difference across a dam).
2. **Kinetic (Current) Energy** — exploited by **Tidal Stream** turbines submerged in fast-flowing tidal channels (similar to underwater wind turbines).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** Wave energy is *intermittent and weather-dependent* (cycle 5–20 s), while tidal energy is *deterministic and predictable for centuries* (cycle ~12 h 25 min for semi-diurnal tides).

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — "The Moon's Bucket"

Picture the ocean as a giant **bucket of water**, and the Moon as a hand that *slowly lifts* one side of the bucket twice a day. If we build a dam across a coastal bay (the "bucket") with a turbine in the middle, water flows out at high tide and in at low tide, spinning the turbine twice a day like clockwork — the ocean does the lifting for free, forever.

## 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics (Use these in KTU answers)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Density of seawater | **ρ** | **1025 kg/m³** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | **g** | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Typical deep-water wave period | **T** | **5 s to 15 s** |
| Typical deep-water wave height | **H** | **1 m to 4 m** |
| Typical tidal range (high resource sites) | **R** | **5 m to 12 m** (e.g., Gulf of Cambay ≈ 7 m) |
| Semidiurnal tidal period | **T_tide** | **12 h 25 min ≈ 12.42 h** |
| Diurnal tidal period | **T_d** | **24 h 50 min** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** Always quote **ρ = 1025 kg/m³** (NOT 1000 kg/m³) for ocean/water-based energy calculations. Examiners specifically deduct marks for using freshwater density in marine energy problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Wave Energy Conversion (WEC) System

The ocean wave energy is converted to electrical energy through a **4-stage process**:

1. **Energy Capture Stage** — A floating or fixed structure intercepts the wave and converts the wave's mechanical energy into a more usable mechanical form (linear/rotating motion, or air-flow oscillation).
2. **Power Take-Off (PTO) Stage** — A transmission device (hydraulic ram, air turbine, or linear generator) extracts the captured mechanical energy.
3. **Power Conversion Stage** — A **rotary generator** (usually asynchronous induction generator for grid connection) converts mechanical to electrical energy.
4. **Grid Interface Stage** — Transformers, switchgear, and **power conditioning** equipment ensure the electricity is delivered at proper voltage, frequency, and power quality.

### 2.2 Classification of Wave Energy Converters (KTU-Favorite Topic)

| WEC Type | Capture Mechanism | Directional Sensitivity | Example Project |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Oscillating Water Column (OWC)** | Air column oscillation drives a Wells turbine | Omnidirectional (fixed shore) | **Mutriku (Spain), Vizhinjam (India)** |
| **Point Absorber** | Buoy heave motion drives a linear generator / hydraulic pump | Omnidirectional (floating) | **PowerBuoy (USA), CETO (Australia)** |
| **Attenuator** | Multi-segment floating device flexes with waves | Uni-directional (long axis ∥ wave) | **Pelamis (Portugal/UK)** |
| **Overtopping Device** | Waves spill into a reservoir above sea level | Directional | **Wave Dragon (Denmark)** |
| **Oscillating Wave Surge Converter** | Pivoted flap oscillates with wave surge | Directional | **Oyster (UK)** |

## 2.3 Working Principle of Tidal Energy (Barrage System)

A tidal barrage typically operates in **two modes**:

- **Ebb Generation Mode (most common):** Water is held in the basin at high tide. As the tide recedes, a head difference develops between the basin (high) and the sea (low). Turbines are opened and water flows **sea ← basin**, generating power.
- **Flood Generation Mode:** Basin is kept empty at low tide. As the tide rises, water flows **sea → basin** through the turbines.

Many modern designs use **bi-directional (bulb) turbines** capable of generating on both ebb and flood cycles, plus **sluice gates** for non-generating water exchange.

### 2.4 Classification of Tidal Energy Systems

| Tidal System | Energy Form Exploited | Typical Site | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tidal Barrage** | Potential (head) | Enclosed bay/estuary | **La Rance (France), Sihwa Lake (South Korea)** |
| **Tidal Stream** | Kinetic (current) | Narrow straits/channels | **Seagen (UK), MeyGen (Scotland)** |
| **Tidal Lagoon** | Potential (head, artificial) | Coastline with high tidal range | **Swansea Bay (proposed, UK)** |
| **Dynamic Tidal Power (DTP)** | Head difference across a long coastal dam | Open coastlines | **Theoretical (Netherlands)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> - The **Sihwa Lake Tidal Power Station (South Korea, 254 MW)** is the world's largest operational tidal barrage.
> - The **Gulf of Cambay and Gulf of Kutch** in India have been identified by the **Ministry of New and Renewable Energy (MNRE)** as having tidal ranges of **5–7 m**, suitable for tidal energy exploitation.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L = \dfrac{g T^{2}}{2\pi}$ | Deep-water wavelength | m |
| 2 | $c = \dfrac{g T}{2\pi}$ | Deep-water wave celerity (phase velocity) | m/s |
| 3 | $c_{g} = \dfrac{c}{2} = \dfrac{gT}{4\pi}$ | Group velocity in deep water | m/s |
| 4 | $E_{\text{wave}} = \dfrac{1}{8}\,\rho g H^{2}$ | Wave energy per unit sea-surface area | J/m² |
| 5 | $\mathcal{P}_{\text{wave}} = E_{\text{wave}} \cdot c_{g} = \dfrac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{32\pi}$ | Wave power per unit crest width | W/m |
| 6 | $P_{\text{barrage}} = \rho g Q h$ | Instantaneous tidal barrage power | W |
| 7 | $E_{\text{basin}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\rho g A h^{2}$ | Energy stored in a tidal basin of head *h* | J |
| 8 | $E_{\text{cycle}} = \dfrac{1}{4}\,\rho g A R^{2}$ | Energy per tidal cycle, *R* = tidal range | J |
| 9 | $P_{\text{avg,cycle}} = \dfrac{\rho g A R^{2}}{4\,T_{\text{tide}}}$ | Average power per tide cycle | W |
| 10 | $P_{\text{stream}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\,\rho A v^{3}$ | Tidal stream power (kinetic) | W |
| 11 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{electrical}}}{P_{\text{wave,incident}}}$ | System efficiency (WEC) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Rule:** Whenever tidal power is asked, **clearly define h** (head) and **A** (basin area). Examiners specifically check whether you used the **squared head (h²)** correctly — many students mistakenly use *h* in the energy equation, which is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Wave and tidal energy systems are deployed primarily in **islanded/coastal grids** to displace diesel generation. The unique advantage of tidal energy is its **predictability over centuries**, which makes it ideal for **base-load renewable generation** and **grid stability studies**. Engineers use these formulas during:
- Pre-feasibility resource assessment
- PTO sizing (linear generator dimensions, turbine runner diameter)
- Mooring and structural design (extreme wave loads)
- Energy yield estimation (kWh/year) for project finance.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Wave Power per Unit Crest Width

**Starting Point:** Linear (Airy) wave theory gives the total mechanical energy density (sum of kinetic and gravitational potential) per unit horizontal area of the sea surface as:

$$
E_{\text{wave}} = \frac{1}{8}\,\rho g H^{2}
$$

**Step 1 — Define the group velocity.**
For a wave train, energy propagates not at the phase velocity *c* but at the **group velocity** $c_{g}$. In **deep water** ($d \geq L/2$), the dispersion relation $\omega^{2} = gk\tanh(kd)$ simplifies to $\omega^{2} = gk$, giving:

$$
c_{g} = \frac{\partial \omega}{\partial k} = \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{g}{k}} = \frac{c}{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Express group velocity in terms of wave period *T*.**
Since $c = gT/(2\pi)$ in deep water, we obtain:

$$
c_{g} = \frac{gT}{4\pi}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the wave power (energy flux) per unit crest width.**
By definition, power per unit crest length is energy density times group velocity:

$$
\mathcal{P}_{\text{wave}} = E_{\text{wave}} \cdot c_{g}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expressions.**

$$
\mathcal{P}_{\text{wave}} = \left(\frac{1}{8}\,\rho g H^{2}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{gT}{4\pi}\right)
$$

$$
\mathcal{P}_{\text{wave}} = \frac{\rho\,g^{2}\,H^{2}\,T}{32\pi}
$$

This is the **canonical deep-water wave power formula** in W/m (per unit crest width). For an ocean with $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $H = 2$ m, $T = 8$ s:

$$
\mathcal{P}_{\text{wave}} = \frac{1025 \times (9.81)^{2} \times (2)^{2} \times 8}{32\pi} \approx 49{,}985 \text{ W/m} \approx 50 \text{ kW/m}
$$

This illustrates why even modest waves (2 m height) deliver **~50 kW per meter** of coastline — a remarkable energy density.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tidal Barrage Energy per Cycle

**Starting Point:** Consider a tidal basin of constant horizontal surface area *A* (m²). At the moment the basin is filled to a height *h* above the mean turbine centerline, the **gravitational potential energy** stored in the elevated water column is:

$$
E = \int_{0}^{h} (\rho g A z)\,dz
$$

**Step 1 — Perform the integration.**

$$
E = \rho g A \int_{0}^{h} z\,dz = \rho g A \left[\frac{z^{2}}{2}\right]_{0}^{h}
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the limits.**

$$
E = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho g A h^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Adapt for a complete tidal cycle.**
In a typical semi-diurnal tidal cycle, the **useful head** swings from $+R/2$ to $-R/2$ (where *R* is the tidal range). The energy obtainable from the **falling half-cycle** (ebb generation) and **rising half-cycle** (flood generation) are equal. The maximum useful head is $h = R/2$. Substituting:

$$
E_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\rho g A \left(\frac{R}{2}\right)^{2} = \frac{\rho g A R^{2}}{8}
$$

However, because **both** ebb and flood cycles are exploitable with bi-directional turbines, the **total energy per tidal cycle** doubles:

$$
E_{\text{cycle,total}} = \frac{\rho g A R^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Average power per cycle.**
Dividing by the tidal period $T_{\text{tide}} = 12.42$ h = 44,712 s for semidiurnal tides:

$$
P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\rho g A R^{2}}{4\,T_{\text{tide}}}
$$

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A tidal basin has an area $A = 10 \text{ km}^{2}$ and a tidal range $R = 6$ m. Estimate the average power extractable per tidal cycle. Assume $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $T_{\text{tide}} = 12.42$ h.

**Step 1 — Convert units.**

$$
A = 10 \text{ km}^{2} = 10 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}^{2}
$$

$$
T_{\text{tide}} = 12.42 \times 3600 = 44{,}712 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the average power formula.**

$$
P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times (10 \times 10^{6}) \times (6)^{2}}{4 \times 44{,}712}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator.**

$$
1025 \times 9.81 = 10{,}055.25
$$

$$
10{,}055.25 \times 10^{7} = 1.0055 \times 10^{11}
$$

$$
1.0055 \times 10^{11} \times 36 = 3.62 \times 10^{12}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by the denominator.**

$$
4 \times 44{,}712 = 178{,}848
$$

$$
P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{3.62 \times 10^{12}}{1.78848 \times 10^{5}} \approx 2.02 \times 10^{7} \text{ W} \approx 20.2 \text{ MW}
$$

**Answer:** The basin can deliver an **average of ~20 MW** of tidal power per cycle — comparable to a mid-size thermal unit. (Note: this is the *gross* theoretical resource; net extractable power is reduced by turbine efficiency ($\eta \approx 0.35$–$0.45$) and other losses.)

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table

| System | Best Site Criteria | Head/Wave Input | Typical Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tidal Barrage | Tidal range *R* ≥ 5 m, enclosed bay | $R$ (m) | 30 – 40 % |
| Tidal Stream | Current speed $v \geq 2$ m/s | $v$ (m/s) | 35 – 45 % |
| Tidal Lagoon | Coastline, *R* ≥ 4 m | $R$ (m) | 30 – 38 % |
| OWC WEC | Nearshore, $H$ ≥ 1.5 m | $H$ (m), $T$ (s) | 25 – 35 % |
| Point Absorber | Deep offshore, $T$ = 6–10 s | $H$, $T$ | 30 – 40 % |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Wave Energy Conversion (WEC) System

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below represents the **functional block-level architecture** of a typical grid-connected WEC plant. Each block is a node labeled with clean alphanumeric text (no markdown formatting inside the quotes) to ensure proper Mermaid rendering.

```mermaid
    nodeB --> nodeC

    nodeH -. Monitors and Controls .- nodeC
    nodeH -. Monitors and Controls .- nodeD
```
