# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2]
**Define wave energy. List any four types of Wave Energy Converters (WECs).**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Wave energy is the renewable energy derived from the surface gravity waves of the ocean, generated primarily by wind action on the sea surface. It exists in the form of both **kinetic energy** (moving water particles) and **gravitational potential energy** (elevated water surface).

Four types of Wave Energy Converters:
1. Oscillating Water Column (OWC)
2. Point Absorber
3. Attenuator
4. Overtopping Device

*(Any 4 of the 5 types listed in the syllabus table will earn full marks.)*

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]
**Distinguish between tidal barrage and tidal stream energy systems.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Tidal Barrage | Tidal Stream |
|---|---|---|
| Energy form | Potential (head) | Kinetic (current) |
| Structure | Dam across a bay | Submerged turbine array |
| Head/Current | Head = 3–10 m | Current = 2–5 m/s |
| Site | Enclosed basin | Narrow strait |
| Example | La Rance, France | MeyGen, Scotland |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of an **Oscillating Water Column (OWC) wave energy plant**. State any two advantages and two limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the **wave power per unit crest width** in deep water, starting from the wave energy density formula. **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

An **Oscillating Water Column (OWC)** is a partially submerged hollow structure open to the sea below the waterline. As ocean waves enter the chamber, the water column inside rises and falls, forcing the air above it to oscillate. This oscillating air passes through a **Wells turbine** (which is self-rectifying, i.e., it rotates in the same direction for both inflow and outflow of air).

**Working Principle (4 Marks):**
1. Incoming waves cause the water level inside the OWC chamber to oscillate.
2. The trapped air column above the water is alternately compressed and rarefied.
3. The bidirectional air flow drives a **Wells turbine** connected to an asynchronous induction generator.
4. Electrical power is conditioned (filtered for harmonics), step-up transformed, and fed to the grid.
5. A control system modulates the turbine's guide vanes to match the slowly varying wave amplitude.

**Block Diagram (1 Mark) — Refer to Section 4.4 above (OWC Plant Diagram).**

**Advantages (1 Mark):**
- No moving parts in seawater → **low corrosion & maintenance**
- Works in **any wave direction** (omnidirectional)

**Limitations (1 Mark):**
- Requires **nearshore site with high waves**
- **Low conversion efficiency** (~25–35 %) due to the Wells turbine's limited aerodynamic efficiency

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes. The full derivation is:

Starting with the wave energy density from Airy theory:

$$
E_{\text{wave}} = \frac{1}{8}\,\rho g H^{2}
$$

Deep-water dispersion gives $c_{g} = c/2 = gT/(4\pi)$. Wave power is the product of energy density and group velocity:

$$
\mathcal{P}_{\text{wave}} = E_{\text{wave}} \cdot c_{g} = \left(\frac{1}{8}\rho g H^{2}\right)\left(\frac{gT}{4\pi}\right) = \frac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{32\pi}
$$

Units check: [kg/m³][m/s²]²[m]²[s]/[—] = W/m ✓.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Internal Choice Alternative) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a tidal barrage power plant** with the help of a block diagram. Describe **ebb generation** and **flood generation** modes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A tidal basin has an area of **8 km²** and a tidal range of **5 m**. Estimate the **average power** extractable per tidal cycle, assuming an overall efficiency of 30%. Take $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s². **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**

A **tidal barrage** is a dam-like structure built across a tidal estuary or bay, fitted with **sluice gates** and **reversible bulb turbines**.

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):** Refer to **Section 4.2** above.

**Working Modes (4 Marks):**

**1. Ebb Generation (most common):**
- At high tide, sluice gates are opened to fill the basin until the tide peaks.
- Sluices close, and the basin is impounded.
- As the sea level falls during the outgoing tide, a head difference builds up across the barrage.
- Once the head reaches ~1 m, the bulb turbines are opened; water flows **from basin to sea**, generating power.
- Power generation continues for ~6 hours until the next low tide.

**2. Flood Generation:**
- The basin is emptied at low tide by opening the sluices.
- As the tide rises, water flows **from sea into the basin** through the turbines, generating power during the rising phase.
- Modern bulb turbines (e.g., at La Rance) can operate in both directions with high efficiency.

**Key Components (1 Mark):**
Sluice gates, bulb (reversible Kaplan) turbines, asynchronous generators, step-up transformers, grid connection, and a SCADA control system.

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
$A = 8 \text{ km}^{2} = 8 \times 10^{6} \text{ m}^{2}$, $R = 5$ m, $\eta = 0.30$, $\rho = 1025$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $T_{\text{tide}} = 12.42$ h = 44,712 s.

**Step 1 — Energy per cycle (theoretical, both directions).**

$$
E_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{\rho g A R^{2}}{4} = \frac{1025 \times 9.81 \times 8 \times 10^{6} \times 25}{4}
$$

Compute numerator: $1025 \times 9.81 = 10{,}055.25$; $10{,}055.25 \times 8 \times 10^{6} = 8.044 \times 10^{10}$; $\times 25 = 2.011 \times 10^{12}$.

$$
E_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{2.011 \times 10^{12}}{4} = 5.028 \times 10^{11} \text{ J}
$$

**Step 2 — Gross average power.**

$$
P_{\text{gross}} = \frac{E_{\text{cycle}}}{T_{\text{tide}}} = \frac{5.028 \times 10^{11}}{44{,}712} \approx 1.124 \times 10^{7} \text{ W} \approx 11.24 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply efficiency.**

$$
P_{\text{net}} = \eta \times P_{\text{gross}} = 0.30 \times 11.24 = 3.37 \text{ MW}
$$

**Answer:** The tidal barrage can deliver an **average net power of approximately 3.37 MW** per tidal cycle.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 4** in the tidal energy formula $E = \rho g A R^{2} / 4$ — students often write $\rho g A R^{2}/2$, which is wrong because the head is $R/2$ (squared gives $R^{2}/4$).
> 2. **Mixing up *h* and *R*** — *R* is the total tidal range, while *h* is the head above the turbine (which equals $R/2$ in a symmetric system).
> 3. **Using freshwater density** (1000 kg/m³) instead of **seawater density** (1025 kg/m³). This is an instant 0.5 mark deduction per sub-question in KTU 2024 valuation.
> 4. **Skipping the block diagram** in part (a) answers. The diagram is worth 1–2 marks separately; writing only the text answer loses those marks.
> 5. **Units in final answer** — always state **MW** for tidal/barrage power and **kW/m** for wave power per unit crest width.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Wave energy** is intermittent, weather-dependent (5–20 s cycles), with the canonical formula:
  $$\mathcal{P}_{\text{wave}} = \frac{\rho g^{2} H^{2} T}{32\pi} \text{ W/m}$$
- ✅ **Tidal energy** is **predictable for centuries** (12 h 25 min semidiurnal cycle), with the formula:
  $$E_{\text{cycle}} = \frac{\rho g A R^{2}}{4} \text{ J}, \quad P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\rho g A R^{2}}{4 T_{\text{tide}}}$$
- ✅ Always use **ρ = 1025 kg/m³** for seawater (not 1000).
- ✅ **WEC Block Diagram must include:** Capture structure → PTO → Generator → Power conditioning → Grid, with control loop.
- ✅ **Tidal Barrage Block Diagram must include:** Basin → Sluice gates → Bulb turbine → Generator → Transformer → Grid, with SCADA.
- ✅ **Three principal Tidal systems:** Barrage (potential), Stream (kinetic), Lagoon (potential, artificial).
- ✅ **Five principal WEC types:** OWC, Point Absorber, Attenuator, Overtopping, Oscillating Wave Surge Converter.
- ✅ **Wells turbine** is self-rectifying — used in OWC plants; rotates the same way for both air inflow and outflow.
- ✅ **Bi-directional (bulb) turbines** are used in modern tidal barrages for both ebb and flood generation.
- ✅ **Real-world references for KTU answers:**
  - Wave: **Mutriku (Spain), Vizhinjam (India)**
  - Tidal Barrage: **La Rance (France, 240 MW), Sihwa Lake (South Korea, 254 MW)**
  - Tidal Stream: **MeyGen (Scotland), Seagen (UK)**
  - India: **Gulf of Cambay, Gulf of Kutch** (R ≈ 5–7 m)
- ✅ **Sihwa Lake (South Korea) is the world's largest operational tidal power station (254 MW)**.
- ✅ **Efficiency band to remember:** WEC ≈ 25–40 %; Tidal Barrage ≈ 30–40 %; Tidal Stream ≈ 35–45 %.
- ✅ **Distinction line in exam answers:**
  - Wave → *intermittent, kinetic + potential, devices in mid-ocean or shoreline*
  - Tidal → *predictable, potential (barrage) or kinetic (stream), devices in coastal bays or channels*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ocean Thermal Energy System - Working Principle - Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ocean Thermal Energy System - Working Principle - Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC)** is a renewable energy technology that exploits the **natural thermal gradient** between the sun-warmed upper layers of tropical oceans and the cold, dense deep waters (typically located 800–1000 m below the surface) to drive a heat engine and produce useful work, usually in the form of electricity.

For a viable OTEC plant, the temperature difference ($\Delta T$) between the warm surface water and the cold deep water must be at least **20 °C (often cited as 20 K)**. Because this small temperature differential is inherent to the resource, OTEC systems must operate on low-pressure, low-temperature thermodynamic cycles — predominantly the **Rankine cycle using a low-boiling-point working fluid** such as ammonia (NH₃), or in the case of open-cycle plants, the working fluid is seawater itself.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Ocean as a Giant Refrigerator Running in Reverse"**
> Imagine a refrigerator: it uses electricity to *move* heat from a cold interior to a warm exterior. An OTEC plant does the **exact opposite** — it uses the *existing* temperature difference between warm and cold water (which nature provides for free) to *generate* electricity. Think of warm surface water as the "boiler" of a power plant, and deep cold water as the "condenser." A working fluid (like ammonia) is vaporized by warm water, pushes a turbine like steam, then is condensed back to liquid by cold water — and the loop repeats continuously.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Key Sizing & Performance Metrics for OTEC (KTU 2024 Scheme Standard Values):**
> - **Minimum usable $\Delta T$** = **20 °C** (between surface water ~25–28 °C and deep water ~4–7 °C).
> - **Theoretical maximum (Carnot) thermal efficiency** for a 20 K sink-source span ≈ **6.7 %**.
> - **Practical OTEC efficiency** (real Rankine cycle, considering irreversibilities) = **2 – 3 %**.
> - **Standard reference depths** for cold water intake: 800 m – 1000 m.
> - **Working fluid of choice (Closed Cycle):** **Ammonia (NH₃)** — boiling point ≈ −33 °C at atmospheric pressure.
> - **Tropical operating belt:** between **20° N and 20° S** latitude.

### 1.4 Classification of OTEC Systems

| System Type | Working Fluid | Cycle Type | Primary Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Closed Cycle (CC-OTEC)** | Ammonia / Propane / R-134a | Rankine (indirect) | Electricity |
| **Open Cycle (OC-OTEC)** | Warm seawater itself | Modified Rankine | Electricity + Fresh water |
| **Hybrid Cycle** | Combination of both | Combined | Electricity + Desalinated water |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) closed-cycle plant operates on the **Rankine thermodynamic cycle**. The fundamental "Why" behind each step is to convert a small but *continuous* temperature difference into rotational mechanical work.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 4 Cardinal Thermodynamic Processes of an OTEC Rankine Cycle:**

1. **Heat Addition (Evaporation) — Isobaric Heat Input**
   - Warm surface seawater (sourced from ~20 m depth) is passed through a **heat exchanger called the evaporator**.
   - The working fluid (ammonia) flowing inside the evaporator tubes absorbs this low-grade heat.
   - **Result:** Liquid ammonia vaporizes into high-pressure, low-temperature vapor.
   - **Why:** This creates the *pressure potential* needed to drive the turbine.

2. **Work Output (Expansion) — Isentropic Expansion**
   - The high-pressure ammonia vapor expands across a **turbine**, which is mechanically coupled to a **generator**.
   - **Result:** Kinetic energy of vapor → shaft rotation → electrical energy.
   - **Why:** This is the *only* step where useful work is extracted.

3. **Heat Rejection (Condensation) — Isobaric Heat Output**
   - The low-pressure vapor leaving the turbine enters a **condenser** (another heat exchanger).
   - Cold deep seawater (~5 °C, pumped from 800–1000 m depth) circulates through the condenser, absorbing heat from the ammonia.
   - **Result:** Ammonia vapor condenses back to saturated liquid.
   - **Why:** Completes the thermodynamic loop and readies the fluid for the next cycle.

4. **Pressurization (Pumping) — Isentropic Compression**
   - The condensed liquid ammonia is pressurized by a **feed pump / working-fluid pump** back to the high evaporator pressure.
   - **Result:** High-pressure liquid ammonia ready for re-evaporation.
   - **Why:** Maintains the cyclic pressure differential.

### 2.2 Real-World Utility of OTEC

- **Base-load renewable electricity** for tropical island nations (Hawaii, Maldives, Lakshadweep, Caribbean).
- **Co-product generation:** Fresh water (via Open Cycle OTEC), air-conditioning (using cold deep water as a free chiller), mariculture (cold-water aquaculture of salmon/lobster), and chilled-soil agriculture.
- **Energy security:** Vast, virtually inexhaustible resource — oceans cover **71 %** of Earth's surface and absorb ~70 % of incident solar radiation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations for KTU Board Exams (Use $\vert$ instead of $\vert x \vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity):**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variable Definition | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Carnot (Ideal) Thermal Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}$ | $T_H$ = hot reservoir temp, $T_C$ = cold reservoir temp | K (Kelvin) |
| 2 | **OTEC Practical Efficiency** | $\eta_{\text{OTEC}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{H}}$ | $W_{\text{net}}$ = turbine work − pump work, $Q_H$ = heat absorbed in evaporator | dimensionless |
| 3 | **Net Work Output** | $W_{\text{net}} = W_T - W_P$ | $W_T$ = turbine work, $W_P$ = pump work | kW or MW |
| 4 | **Heat Absorbed in Evaporator** | $Q_H = \dot{m}_{wf} \, (h_1 - h_4)$ | $\dot{m}_{wf}$ = working fluid mass flow rate; $h_1, h_4$ = enthalpies at turbine inlet & pump inlet | kW |
| 5 | **Heat Rejected in Condenser** | $Q_C = \dot{m}_{wf} \, (h_2 - h_3)$ | $h_2, h_3$ = enthalpies at turbine exit & condenser exit | kW |
| 6 | **Available Thermal Resource Power** | $P_{\text{thermal}} = \dot{m}_{sw} \, c_p \, \Delta T$ | $\dot{m}_{sw}$ = seawater mass flow; $c_p$ ≈ 4.18 kJ/kg·K | kW |
| 7 | **Seawater Volumetric Flow** | $\dot{V} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{\eta \, \rho_{sw} \, c_p \, \Delta T}$ | $\rho_{sw}$ ≈ 1025 kg/m³ | m³/s |
| 8 | **Maximum (Carnot) Work from $\Delta T$** | $W_{\max} = Q_H \left(1 - \dfrac{T_C}{T_H}\right)$ | Same as Eq. 1 expanded | kW |
| 9 | **Plant Capacity Factor Assumption** | $\text{CF} \approx 0.85$ to $0.90$ | Continuous operation (no diurnal/seasonal loss) | dimensionless |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum (Carnot) Efficiency of a Tropical OTEC Plant

**Given (KTU Standard Problem Data):**
- Warm surface water temperature: $T_H = 27\,^\circ\text{C} = 300.15\,\text{K}$
- Cold deep water temperature: $T_C = 5\,^\circ\text{C} = 278.15\,\text{K}$
- Required: Maximum theoretical efficiency of the heat engine.

**Step 1 — Convert to Absolute Temperature (Kelvin).**
This conversion is mandatory because the Carnot efficiency is defined only in absolute terms; using Celsius would produce a meaningless, negative denominator.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_H &= 27 + 273.15 = 300.15\ \text{K} \\
T_C &= 5 + 273.15 = 278.15\ \text{K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Carnot Efficiency Equation.**
The Carnot efficiency represents the *theoretical ceiling* of any heat engine operating between two thermal reservoirs. OTEC plants approach but never exceed this limit.

$$
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Numerical Values.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} &= 1 - \frac{278.15}{300.15} \\
&= 1 - 0.9267 \\
&= 0.0733
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Percentage.**

$$
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} \approx 7.33\,\%
$$

**Conclusion:** A tropical OTEC plant can convert at most **~7.3 %** of the absorbed solar-grade heat into work. Real plants achieve **2 – 3 %** due to turbine inefficiencies, pipe friction, heat-exchanger irreversibilities, and pumping losses.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Net Work Output & Required Working-Fluid Mass Flow Rate

**Given:**
- Net electrical power required: $W_{\text{net}} = 10\,\text{MW} = 10{,}000\,\text{kW}$
- Turbine inlet enthalpy: $h_1 = 1470\,\text{kJ/kg}$ (ammonia vapor at evaporator conditions)
- Turbine exit enthalpy: $h_2 = 1300\,\text{kJ/kg}$
- Pump exit enthalpy: $h_4 = 280\,\text{kJ/kg}$
- Condenser exit enthalpy: $h_3 = 280\,\text{kJ/kg}$ (saturated liquid — assumed same as $h_4$ for simplicity in isentropic pump work)
- Cycle efficiency (assumed practical): $\eta_{\text{cycle}} = 0.025$ (2.5 %)

**Step 1 — Turbine Work per kg of Working Fluid.**
The work extracted per kg is the enthalpy drop across the turbine.

$$
w_T = h_1 - h_2 = 1470 - 1300 = 170\ \text{kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 2 — Pump Work per kg of Working Fluid.**
The pump consumes a small fraction of the turbine's output to maintain loop pressure.

$$
w_P = h_4 - h_3 \approx 5\ \text{kJ/kg}\ (\text{typical for ammonia})
$$

**Step 3 — Net Specific Work Output.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_{\text{net}} &= w_T - w_P \\
&= 170 - 5 \\
&= 165\ \text{kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Required Ammonia Mass Flow Rate.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{wf} &= \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{w_{\text{net}}} \\
&= \frac{10{,}000\ \text{kW}}{165\ \text{kJ/kg}} \\
&= 60.6\ \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Heat Absorbed in Evaporator (for cross-check).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_H &= \dot{m}_{wf}\,(h_1 - h_4) \\
&= 60.6 \times (1470 - 280) \\
&= 60.6 \times 1190 \\
&= 72{,}114\ \text{kW} \approx 72.1\ \text{MW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification of Cycle Efficiency.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{\text{cycle}} &= \frac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_H} = \frac{10{,}000}{72{,}114} \\
&\approx 0.138\ \text{or}\ 13.8\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note for KTU Students:** The efficiency value derived here (~13.8 %) seems high because we used *enthalpy differences only*; a rigorous exam answer must also deduct **parasitic loads** (cold-water pump, warm-water pump, working-fluid pump, generator losses) from the gross output. Factoring these in (~30 % auxiliary consumption) brings realistic net efficiency into the **2–3 %** range expected by KTU examiners.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Required Seawater Flow Rate

**Given:** $\Delta T_{\text{eff}} = 20\,\text{K}$ (heat extracted from warm stream), $c_p = 4.18\,\text{kJ/kg·K}$, $\rho_{sw} = 1025\,\text{kg/m}^3$.

**Step 1 — Thermal Energy Equation.**

$$
Q_H = \dot{m}_{sw}\,c_p\,\Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\dot{m}_{sw}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{sw} &= \frac{Q_H}{c_p\,\Delta T} \\
&= \frac{72{,}114}{4.18 \times 20} \\
&= \frac{72{,}114}{83.6} \\
&\approx 862.6\ \text{kg/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Volumetric Flow.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{V}_{sw} &= \frac{\dot{m}_{sw}}{\rho_{sw}} \\
&= \frac{862.6}{1025} \\
&\approx 0.842\ \text{m}^3/\text{s} \\
&\approx 50.5\ \text{m}^3/\text{min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A 10 MW OTEC plant requires pumping nearly **one cubic meter per second of warm tropical seawater** through the evaporator. This colossal flow rate is a major reason OTEC plants are designed at **moderate scale (10–100 MW)** and located offshore or near coasts with deep-water access.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Closed-Cycle OTEC Plant (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of a typical Closed-Cycle Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (CC-OTEC) plant. Each block represents a major subsystem, and arrows represent the direction of fluid / energy flow.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Warm Surface Seawater Source 25 to 28 deg C Depth about 20 m] --> B[Warm Water Intake Pump]
    B --> C[Evaporator Heat Exchanger]
    D[Liquid Ammonia Working Fluid NH3] --> E[Working Fluid Feed Pump]
    E --> C
    C -->|Ammonia Vapor High Pressure| F[Turbine]
    F -->|Mechanical Shaft| G[Electric Generator]
    G --> H[Electrical Output to Grid or Load]
    F -->|Low Pressure Vapor| I[Condenser Heat Exchanger]
    J[Cold Deep Seawater Source 4 to 7 deg C Depth 800 to 1000 m] --> K[Cold Water Intake Pump]
    K --> I
    I -->|Condensed Liquid NH3| D
    L[Cold Water Discharge to Deep Ocean] --> I
    I --> M[Cold Water Outfall to Ocean Depth]
```

### 4.2 System Component Functions (Reference Table)

| Block ID | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Warm Surface Seawater Source | Provides the heat source (high-grade low-temp reservoir) |
| **B** | Warm Water Pump | Pushes warm seawater through the evaporator |
| **C** | Evaporator | Transfers heat from seawater to working fluid → vaporization |
| **D / E** | Liquid NH3 + Feed Pump | Pressurizes the working fluid for re-entry into evaporator |
| **F** | Turbine | Extracts work from expanding ammonia vapor |
| **G** | Generator | Converts mechanical shaft work to electrical energy |
| **I** | Condenser | Rejects heat to deep seawater → condenses NH3 vapor |
| **J / K** | Cold Deep Seawater Source + Pump | Supplies cold reservoir fluid |
| **M** | Cold Water Outfall | Discharges warmed deep water back to ocean |

### 4.3 T-S Diagram Reference (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[State 1 Saturated Vapor Exit Evaporator] -->|Isentropic Expansion| P2[State 2 Wet Vapor Exit Turbine]
    P2 -->|Isobaric Heat Rejection| P3[State 3 Saturated Liquid Exit Condenser]
    P3 -->|Isentropic Compression| P4[State 4 Subcooled Liquid Exit Pump]
    P4 -->|Isobaric Heat Addition| P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mapping to Rankine Cycle States (for KTU exam answers):**
> - **State 1 → 2:** Isentropic expansion in turbine (work output).
> - **State 2 → 3:** Isobaric condensation in condenser (heat rejected to cold seawater).
> - **State 3 → 4:** Isentropic compression in feed pump.
> - **State 4 → 1:** Isobaric vaporization in evaporator (heat absorbed from warm seawater).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> *Define Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC). State the minimum temperature difference required for its operation.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) is a method of generating electricity by exploiting the temperature difference between the warm surface water of tropical oceans and the cold deep water. A minimum temperature difference of **20 °C** between the warm surface water and the cold deep seawater (typically pumped from 800–1000 m depth) is required for the heat engine to produce net positive work. (3 Marks)

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> *Name the working fluid commonly used in a closed-cycle OTEC plant. Why is it preferred?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Ammonia (NH₃)** is the most commonly used working fluid in closed-cycle OTEC plants because of its (i) low boiling point (−33 °C at 1 atm), enabling vaporization at the low temperatures of warm seawater, (ii) high latent heat of vaporization (~1377 kJ/kg), allowing more energy transfer per kg, and (iii) high vapor density, which reduces turbine size. (3 Marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3(A). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> *With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a Closed-Cycle Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion (OTEC) plant. List the major components and state the function of each. Also discuss the principle of energy conversion involved.*

**(a) Block Diagram and Working Principle (7 Marks)**

A closed-cycle OTEC plant uses a low-boiling-point working fluid (ammonia) in a sealed Rankine cycle. The major components and their functions are:

| S.No. | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Warm seawater intake | Supplies ~26 °C water from ocean surface |
| 2 | Warm water pump | Circulates warm water through the evaporator |
| 3 | Evaporator | Transfers heat from seawater to liquid NH₃ → vaporization |
| 4 | Turbine | Ammonia vapor expands → rotates shaft |
| 5 | Generator (coupled) | Converts mechanical energy → electrical energy |
| 6 | Condenser | Rejects heat to deep cold seawater → NH₃ condenses |
| 7 | Cold water pump | Lifts ~5 °C water from 800–1000 m depth |
| 8 | Working-fluid pump | Pressurizes liquid NH₃ back to evaporator pressure |

**Working Principle (Logical Steps):**
1. Warm seawater enters the evaporator and gives up heat to liquid ammonia.
2. Ammonia vaporizes at low pressure (≈ 1 bar abs) and low temperature (~26 °C).
3. The vapor expands through a turbine, producing mechanical work.
4. The turbine drives a generator, producing electricity.
5. Spent vapor enters the condenser, where cold deep seawater absorbs its latent heat.
6. The condensed liquid ammonia is pumped back to the evaporator, completing the cycle.

**(b) Principle of Energy Conversion and Performance Discussion (7 Marks)**

The energy conversion follows the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** — specifically, a heat engine operating between a high-temperature reservoir (warm surface seawater at $T_H \approx 300\,\text{K}$) and a low-temperature reservoir (deep cold seawater at $T_C \approx 278\,\text{K}$). The **Carnot efficiency** sets the upper bound:

$$
\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{278}{300} \approx 7.3\,\%
$$

In practice, OTEC plants achieve **2–3 %** net efficiency because of:
- Turbine and pump mechanical inefficiencies.
- Heat-exchanger irreversibilities (fouling, finite $\Delta T$).
- Parasitic power consumption (cold-water pumping at high static head).
- Generator losses.

**Advantages:** Continuous 24/7 power, no fuel cost, no emissions, co-generation of fresh water (open cycle).
**Limitations:** Low efficiency, high capital cost, geographical restriction to tropical belt (20° N – 20° S), biofouling of heat exchangers, large intake pipe (~1 m diameter for 10 MW plant).

---

> **Q3(B). [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> *Explain the working of an Open-Cycle OTEC plant with a block diagram. How does it differ from the closed-cycle system? Discuss the co-generation of fresh water in this system.*

**(a) Open-Cycle OTEC — Working Principle & Block Diagram (7 Marks)**

In an **open-cycle (Claude cycle)** OTEC plant, **warm seawater itself acts as the working fluid**. There is no separate ammonia loop.

**Major Components & Flow:**

1. Warm seawater (~26 °C) is drawn from the surface using a flash evaporator at partial vacuum.
2. The reduced pressure causes the warm seawater to **flash evaporate** rapidly.
3. The resulting low-pressure steam drives a **low-pressure turbine** coupled to a generator.
4. The exhaust steam is condensed using cold deep seawater (~5 °C) — but **the condensation surface is kept below 25 °C**, so the steam condenses into **fresh water**, not salt water.
5. The fresh water is collected for potable use or agriculture.
6. Spent brine and condenser cooling water are discharged back to the ocean.

**Block Diagram (Schematic):**
$$\text{Warm SW} \rightarrow \text{Flash Evaporator} \rightarrow \text{Steam} \rightarrow \text{Turbine} \rightarrow \text{Generator} \rightarrow \text{Condenser (with CW)} \rightarrow \text{Fresh Water + Brine}$$

**(b) Comparison with Closed-Cycle + Co-Generation Discussion (7 Marks)**

| Parameter | Closed-Cycle OTEC | Open-Cycle OTEC |
|---|---|---|
| Working fluid | Ammonia (sealed loop) | Warm seawater itself |
| Output | Electricity only | Electricity **+** desalinated fresh water |
| Turbine type | Standard Rankine | Low-pressure axial (large diameter) |
| Vacuum required | Moderate (in condenser) | High (entire evaporator) |
| Fresh water by-product | No | Yes (~1 L per kWh typical) |

**Co-generation of Fresh Water:**
- The condenser must be operated **below the saturation temperature of fresh water at that pressure** so that only pure H₂O condenses.
- Salt ions remain in the liquid brine phase, which is discharged.
- For a 1 MW OC-OTEC plant, ~1000–1500 m³/day of fresh water can be produced — a huge benefit for island communities.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse the open-cycle "working fluid"** with ammonia; in OC-OTEC, seawater *is* the working fluid — this is a frequent 2-mark deduction point.
> 2. **Always convert °C to K** in Carnot efficiency problems. Using °C directly yields a negative denominator and zero marks.
> 3. **State depth values (800–1000 m)** for cold water source; examiners award marks for completeness.
> 4. **Draw the block diagram with arrows** showing fluid direction; a diagram without directional flow loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Mention the working fluid by name (ammonia)** in closed-cycle questions — vague references like "a low-temperature fluid" are penalized.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam (Module 2 — OTEC):**
> - ✅ OTEC = **Ocean Thermal Energy Conversion**; exploits $\Delta T$ between surface and deep ocean.
> - ✅ Minimum $\Delta T$ required = **20 K**; ideal tropical locations: 20° N – 20° S.
> - ✅ Three types: **Closed Cycle (CC), Open Cycle (OC), and Hybrid**.
> - ✅ **Closed Cycle** uses ammonia (boiling point −33 °C) in a Rankine loop.
> - ✅ **Open Cycle** uses warm seawater itself; produces **electricity + fresh water**.
> - ✅ **Carnot efficiency** ≈ 7 % for a 22 K span; **real OTEC efficiency** ≈ 2–3 %.
> - ✅ Key components: Warm water pump → Evaporator → Turbine → Generator → Condenser → Cold water pump → Feed pump.
> - ✅ Cold water intake depth: **800–1000 m**; warm water from ~**20 m**.
> - ✅ Advantages: Continuous baseload, no fuel, no emissions, multi-product (fresh water, AC, mariculture).
> - ✅ Disadvantages: Low efficiency, high capital cost, biofouling, geographically restricted.
> - ✅ Formula recall: $\eta = 1 - T_C/T_H$; $W_{\text{net}} = \dot{m}_{wf}(h_1 - h_2) - \dot{m}_{wf}(h_4 - h_3)$.
> - ✅ Always state **assumed standard tropical values** ($T_H$ = 27 °C, $T_C$ = 5 °C) unless the problem gives different data.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importance of Energy Storage - Means of Storing Energy - Principle of Operation and Performance Comparison
Compressed Air Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Compressed Air Storage ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES)** is a large-scale, grid-connected mechanical energy storage technology in which surplus electrical energy (typically off-peak or from intermittent renewables) is used to drive a multi-stage air compressor. The compressed air is then stored under high pressure in a suitable reservoir — most commonly a solution-mined salt cavern, hard-rock cavern, depleted gas/oil reservoir, or in advanced designs, an above-ground high-pressure vessel array. During peak demand periods, the stored pressurized air is released, pre-heated (using stored or external thermal energy), and expanded through a turbo-expander coupled to a generator to produce electricity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note (PERAT637 – Module 3):** CAES is classified as a **Bulk Energy Storage System (BESS-mechanical)**. Unlike Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS), CAES has **geographical flexibility** because it does not require two specific water elevations. The thermodynamic quality of the storage medium (high-pressure air) is preserved for hours to months with very low self-discharge (< 0.5 % per day). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Power Grid's Tire Pump"** > Imagine your bicycle uses a tire pump to compress air. You do work by pumping; energy is now *stored* in the pressurized air inside the tube. If you could extract that compressed air and push it through a tiny windmill, you would recover most of the pumping effort as mechanical work. > A **CAES plant is simply a "grid-scale bicycle pump"**: > - **Charging mode (Compression):** Cheap/renewable electricity $\rightarrow$ Motor $\rightarrow$ Compressor $\rightarrow$ Pressurized air pushed into a giant underground "tire" (salt cavern). > - **Discharging mode (Generation):** The cavern "tire" releases air $\rightarrow$ Air is heated $\rightarrow$ Hot air spins a turbine $\rightarrow$ Generator produces peak electricity. > The cave is the **battery**; the air is the **electrolyte**; the pressure is the **state of charge**. ### 1.3 Classification of CAES (Standard KTU Taxonomy) | CAES Type | Heat Management | Round-Trip Efficiency | Commercial Status | |---|---|---|---| | **Diabatic CAES (D-CAES)** | Compression heat is vented to atmosphere; fuel is burned during discharge to re-heat air | **≈ 42 – 54 %** | Commercial (Huntorf 1978, McIntosh 1991) | | **Adiabatic CAES (A-CAES)** | Compression heat is captured in a Thermal Energy Storage (TES) unit and reused during discharge (no fuel) | **≈ 60 – 70 %** | Pilot / Demonstration (ADELE, Polaris) | | **Isothermal CAES (I-CAES)** | Compression/expansion occurs at near-constant temperature using water spray or liquid pistons | **≈ 75 – 80 %** | Lab / Sub-scale R&D | | **Underwater CAES (UW-CAES)** | Air stored in flexible bags on seabed | **≈ 60 – 65 %** | Conceptual | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Metric Highlighted:** > - Storage pressure range: **40 – 80 bar** (utility scale) > - Typical cavern volume: **300 000 – 500 000 m³** > - Discharge duration: **8 – 26 hours** at rated power. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic The thermodynamic round-trip of a CAES plant can be broken down into **four primary sub-processes**: 1. **Charging (Compression Cycle):** - Ambient air (State 1: $P_1$, $T_1$) is drawn into an **inlet filter + multi-stage intercooled compressor** (typically 6–10 stages for utility scale). - Compression is modeled as a **polytropic / isentropic** process, with intermediate cooling to reduce specific work. - The pressurized air (State 2: $P_2$, $T_2$) is delivered into the storage cavern. 2. **Storage (Cavern Residence):** - Air sits in the cavern with negligible leakage. In salt caverns, the **salt creep** slowly reduces volume, providing slight self-pressurization. - Pressure is **quasi-constant** at cavern hydrostatic head ($P_2 \approx 50\text{–}80$ bar). 3. **Discharging (Expansion Cycle):** - Compressed air is withdrawn, mixed with fuel in a **recuperator / combustor** (D-CAES) or with stored heat (A-CAES). - High-pressure hot air expands through a **high-pressure (HP) turbine** and a **low-pressure (LP) turbine** (typically 2-stage with reheat in D-CAES). - Turbine exhausts to atmosphere; mechanical work drives the generator. 4. **Recuperation / Regeneration (Optional):** - Turbine exhaust heat may pre-heat incoming cavern air, boosting cycle efficiency by **4 – 8 %**. ### 2.2 Why the Heat Management Matters (The Core "Why") > The compression of air is a **non-isentropic** real process that inherently generates heat. In a **diabatic** system, this heat is *thrown away* — which means a huge fraction of the input electrical energy becomes waste. A modern **A-CAES** recovers 80 – 95 % of this heat in a packed-bed TES (using molten salt, ceramic pebbles, or concrete), making the plant fuel-free. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Specific work of isentropic compression | $w_c = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,R\,T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | kJ/kg | $n = c_p/c_v = 1.4$ (air) | | 2 | Specific work of isentropic expansion | $w_t = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,R\,T_3 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_4}{P_3}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}}\right]$ | kJ/kg | $T_3$ = pre-heated turbine inlet temp | | 3 | Energy stored in cavern (mass form) | $E_{stored} = m \cdot c_v (T_2 - T_1)$ | kJ | At constant cavern volume | | 4 | Energy stored (pressure form, ideal gas) | $E_{stored} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{n-1} \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$ | kJ | **Most used in board problems** | | 5 | Mass of air in cavern | $m = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{R T_2}$ | kg | Universal gas equation | | 6 | Compressor isentropic efficiency | $\eta_c = \dfrac{w_{s,\,c}}{w_{a,\,c}}$ | — | Subscript $a$ = actual | | 7 | Turbine isentropic efficiency | $\eta_t = \dfrac{w_{a,\,t}}{w_{s,\,t}}$ | — | — | | 8 | Round-trip (storage) efficiency | $\eta_{RT} = \dfrac{W_{turbine,\,out}}{W_{compressor,\,in}}$ | — | Diabatic: 0.42 – 0.54 | | 9 | Volume of salt cavern (cylindrical) | $V = \pi r^2 h$ | m³ | Plus dome correction factor | | 10 | Cavern pressure from depth | $P_2 \approx P_{atm} + \rho_{brine}\,g\,H$ | Pa | Brine compensation in salt caverns | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** When a KTU question states *ideal reversible CAES*, use the **isentropic** form directly. When it states *practical* or *actual* plant, multiply by $1/\eta_c$ (compressor) and $\eta_t$ (turbine). Never confuse absolute `|` notation in tables — use $\vert$ (rendered above). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Renewable firming:** CAES smooths the intermittency of solar/wind farms (e.g., the **Polaris A-CAES** project in Saskatchewan, Canada — 5 MW / 10 MWh). - **Transmission deferral:** Reduces need for new peaker plants. - **Ancillary services:** Provides spinning reserve, frequency regulation, voltage support. - **Hydrogen synergy:** Modern "LAES + CAES" hybrids use liquid air as a cold sink for hydrogen liquefaction. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Specific Work of Polytropic Compression **Starting premise:** Air behaves as an **ideal gas** undergoing a **reversible polytropic (≈ isentropic) compression** from inlet state $(P_1, V_1)$ to discharge state $(P_2, V_2)$. **Step 1 — Polytropic relation between states:** $$ P_1 V_1^{n} = P_2 V_2^{n} $$ **Step 2 — Boundary work for any reversible closed-system process:** $$ w = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV $$ **Step 3 — Substitute $P = P_1 V_1^{n} / V^{n}$ and integrate:** $$ w_c = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} \dfrac{P_1 V_1^{n}}{V^{n}} \, dV $$ **Step 4 — Perform the integration** (using $\int V^{-n} dV = \dfrac{V^{1-n}}{1-n}$): $$ w_c = P_1 V_1^{n} \left[\dfrac{V_2^{1-n} - V_1^{1-n}}{1-n}\right] $$ **Step 5 — Factor to expose the pressure ratio.** Multiply numerator and denominator by $(-1)$: $$ w_c = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^{n}}{n-1} \left[V_1^{1-n} - V_2^{1-n}\right] $$ **Step 6 — Recognize** $V_1^{1-n} = V_1 \cdot V_1^{-n}$ and use $P_1 V_1^{n} V_1^{1-n} = P_1 V_1$: $$ w_c = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{n-1} \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{n-1}\right] $$ **Step 7 — Convert volume ratio to pressure ratio** using $V_1/V_2 = (P_2/P_1)^{1/n}$: $$ \left(\dfrac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{n-1} = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} $$ **Step 8 — Final closed-form expression:** $$ \boxed{\,w_c = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,R\,T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]\,} $$ > The term $(P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}$ grows rapidly with pressure ratio — this is why multi-stage **intercooled compression** is essential above 4:1 pressure ratio. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Energy Stored in a Compressed-Air Cavern **Starting premise:** Cavern has fixed volume $V_2$. Air enters at state $(P_1, T_1)$ and is compressed into a smaller volume $V_2$ at the **same mass** $m$. **Step 1 — Mass of air stored** (using the *final* cavern state): $$ m = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{R T_2} $$ **Step 2 — Energy stored as internal-energy gain.** For an ideal gas, internal energy depends only on temperature, and the cavern is rigid (no boundary work is *recovered* from the wall — all the compression work is *inside* the gas as internal + flow energy): $$ E_{stored} = m \cdot c_v (T_2 - T_1) $$ **Step 3 — Substitute** $c_v = R/(n-1)$ and $T_2 = T_1 (P_2/P_1)^{(n-1)/n}$ (from the isentropic relation): $$ E_{stored} = \dfrac{m R}{n-1} \left[T_1 \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - T_1\right] $$ **Step 4 — Factor out $T_1$ and replace $m R T_1$ by $P_1 V_1$** (initial ideal-gas identity): $$ E_{stored} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{n-1} \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right] $$ **Step 5 — Final energy-storage equation (in units of Joules):** $$ \boxed{\,E_{stored} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{n-1} \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]\,} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **maximum theoretically recoverable** work. Real systems deliver only $\eta_t$ fraction of this through the turbine. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Performance Comparison of CAES Architectures | Performance Metric | Diabatic CAES (Huntorf) | Adiabatic CAES (Polaris-like) | Isothermal CAES (Lab) | |---|---|---|---| | Energy density (kWh/m³) | 3 – 6 | 6 – 12 | 8 – 15 | | Round-trip efficiency | 0.42 – 0.54 | 0.60 – 0.70 | 0.75 – 0.80 | | Fossil fuel input (LHV basis) | 0.40 – 0.45 kWh/kWh$_e$ | 0 | 0 | | CO₂ emissions (g/kWh$_e$) | 250 – 450 | ≈ 0 (recovered heat) | ≈ 0 | | Cold-start time | 10 – 15 min | 5 – 8 min | < 1 min | | Levelized cost ($/MWh) | 120 – 160 | 90 – 130 | Estimated 70 – 100 | | Lifetime cycles | > 30 years | > 30 years | 10 – 15 years (TBD) | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture — Diabatic CAES Plant The following Mermaid block maps the **charging (compression) loop** and the **discharging (expansion + generation) loop** of a classical diabatic CAES installation, including thermal-energy management pathways. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Grid Off-Peak Power] --> A2[Electric Motor] A2 --> A3[Multi-Stage Intercooled Compressor] A3 -- Stage 1 --> A4[Intercooler 1] A4 -- Stage 2 --> A5[Intercooler 2] A5 -- Stage 3 --> A6[Aftercooler] A6 --> B1[(Underground Salt Cavern
P = 50 to 80 bar)] B1 -- Charged Air --> C1[Throttle Valve] C1 --> C2[Recuperator Cold Side] C2 --> D1[Combustor / Natural Gas Burner] D1 -- Hot Pressurized Air --> E1[High Pressure Turbine] E1 --> E2[Reheater] E2 --> F1[Low Pressure Turbine] F1 --> F2[Exhaust to Stack] E1 --> G1[Generator Shaft] F1 --> G1 G1 --> H1[Step Up Transformer] H1 --> H2[Grid Peak Demand] F2 -- Hot Exhaust --> C3[Recuperator Hot Side] C3 -- Pre-heated Combustion Air --> D1 A3 -- Waste Heat Rejected --> X1[Cooling Tower] ``` ### 4.2 Functional Block Architecture — Adiabatic CAES (A-CAES) Plant ```mermaid flowchart TD G1[Grid Renewable Power] --> M1[Variable Speed Motor] M1 --> CMP[Multi-Stage Compressor] CMP -- Hot Compressed Air --> HEX1[Heat Exchanger 1] HEX1 -- High Grade Heat --> TES[(Thermal Energy Storage
Molten Salt or Ceramic Pebbles)] HEX1 -- Cooled Air --> CAV[(Salt Cavern
50 to 80 bar)] CAV -- Air at Discharge --> HEX2[Heat Exchanger 2] TES -- Stored Heat back --> HEX2 HEX2 -- Hot High Pressure Air --> TURB[Turbine Expander] TURB --> GEN[Generator] GEN --> G2[Grid Peak Demand] TURB -- Cool Exhaust --> HEX1 ``` ### 4.3 Process State-Point Diagram (T-s Representation) ```mermaid flowchart LR S1["1: Ambient Air
P1 = 1 bar, T1 = 298 K"] -- Isentropic Compression --> S2["2: Compressed Air
P2 = 70 bar, T2 high"] S2 -- Constant Pressure Cooling in Cavern --> S3["3: Cavern Storage
T3 approx T1"] S3 -- Pre-heating via Combustor or TES --> S4["4: Turbine Inlet
P4 = 70 bar, T4 approx 900 K"] S4 -- Isentropic Expansion --> S5["5: Turbine Exhaust
P5 = 1 bar"] S5 -- Heat Recovery to Recuperator --> HX[Recuperator] HX -- Preheat Return --> S4 ``` ### 4.4 State-Point Key (Read alongside Diagram 4.3) - **State 1 → 2:** Isentropic compression in charging mode. - **State 2 → 3:** Isobaric cooling inside cavern (heat rejected to surrounding salt). - **State 3 → 4:** Isobaric heating by combustor (D-CAES) or TES (A-CAES). - **State 4 → 5:** Isentropic expansion through turbine during discharge. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES). Mention any two advantages of CAES over Pumped Hydro Storage (PHS).* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1 mark):** Compressed Air Energy Storage (CAES) is a utility-scale energy storage technology in which surplus electrical energy is used to compress air and store it under high pressure (typically 40 – 80 bar) in an underground reservoir such as a salt cavern. During peak demand, the compressed air is released, heated, and expanded through a turbine to generate electricity. - **Advantages over PHS (2 marks):** 1. **Geographic flexibility** — CAES requires only *one* suitable underground formation, whereas PHS requires two water reservoirs at different elevations. 2. **Lower environmental footprint** — CAES does not submerge large tracts of land or displace communities (no large dam, no large upper reservoir). 3. *(any one of the above or any other valid advantage — e.g., faster ramp rate, longer storage duration)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *What is the role of a Thermal Energy Storage (TES) unit in an Adiabatic CAES (A-CAES) system? Why is it absent in a Diabatic CAES?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Role of TES (2 marks):** In an A-CAES, the TES unit captures the heat generated during the multi-stage compression of air and stores it in a thermal medium (molten salt, ceramic pebbles, or concrete). This stored heat is later transferred back to the compressed air before it enters the turbine, allowing the cycle to operate *without fossil-fuel combustion* and significantly improving round-trip efficiency. - **Why absent in Diabatic CAES (1 mark):** In a Diabatic CAES, the compression heat is intentionally **vented to the atmosphere** (typically via intercoolers and a cooling tower) to keep the cavern air cool; a separate natural-gas combustor is then used during discharge to re-heat the air. Hence, no TES is required. --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions --- **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]* **(a)** A CAES plant compresses ambient air at **1 bar and 300 K** to a cavern pressure of **70 bar** using a three-stage intercooled compressor. Assume isentropic compression with $\gamma = 1.4$. Calculate the **specific compression work** per kg of air and the **temperature at the end of the third stage** if intercooling returns the air to 300 K after every stage. **(7 marks)** **(b)** If the cavern has a usable volume of $3.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{m}^{3}$ and operates at the final pressure, calculate the **total energy stored** in the cavern and the **total mass of air** stored. State any assumption made. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Question A ##### Part (a) — Specific Compression Work (7 marks) **Given:** - $P_1 = 1$ bar, $T_1 = 300$ K - $P_2 = 70$ bar - 3-stage compression, intercooled back to 300 K - $\gamma = 1.4 \Rightarrow (n-1)/n = 0.2857$ **Step 1 — Optimum intermediate pressure ratios (equal work distribution):** $$ r = \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/3} = (70)^{1/3} $$ $$ r \approx 4.121 $$ **Step 2 — Stage pressure ratio computation:** $$ P_x = 4.121\ \text{bar}, \quad P_y = 16.98\ \text{bar}, \quad P_2 = 70\ \text{bar} $$ **Step 3 — Specific work of one stage (isentropic, with $T_{in} = 300$ K):** $$ w_{stage} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left(r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} - 1\right) $$ **Step 4 — Numerical evaluation** with $R = 0.287$ kJ/kg·K: $$ r^{0.2857} = 4.121^{0.2857} \approx 1.518 $$ $$ w_{stage} = \dfrac{1.4}{0.4} \times 0.287 \times 300 \times (1.518 - 1) $$ $$ w_{stage} = 3.5 \times 0.287 \times 300 \times 0.518 $$ $$ w_{stage} \approx 156.1\ \text{kJ/kg} $$ **Step 5 — Total specific compression work (3 stages):** $$ \boxed{\,w_{c,\,total} = 3 \times 156.1 \approx 468.3\ \text{kJ/kg}\,} $$ **Step 6 — Temperature at the end of the third stage (just before the final intercooler):** $$ T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times 1.518 \approx 455.4\ \text{K} $$ $$ \boxed{\,T_2 \approx 455.4\ \text{K}\,} $$ --- ##### Part (b) — Energy Stored and Mass of Air (7 marks) **Given:** - Cavern volume $V_2 = 3.5 \times 10^{5}\ \text{m}^{3}$ - Cavern pressure $P_2 = 70\ \text{bar} = 7 \times 10^{6}\ \text{Pa}$ - Cavern temperature $T_2 = 300$ K (assumed, due to perfect intercooling) - $R = 287$ J/kg·K **Step 1 — Mass of air stored (Ideal Gas Law):** $$ m = \dfrac{P_2 V_2}{R T_2} = \dfrac{7 \times 10^{6} \times 3.5 \times 10^{5}}{287 \times 300} $$ $$ \boxed{\,m \approx 2.846 \times 10^{6}\ \text{kg}\,} $$ **Step 2 — Energy stored (using derived formula with $T_1 = T_2 = 300$ K — note that *isothermal* storage gives the same value as the $c_v$ form):** $$ E_{stored} = m \cdot c_v (T_{after\,comp} - T_1) $$ Using $c_v = R/(\gamma - 1) = 287/0.4 = 717.5$ J/kg·K and $T_{after\,comp} = 455.4$ K: $$ E_{stored} = 2.846 \times 10^{6} \times 717.5 \times (455.4 - 300) $$ $$ E_{stored} = 2.846 \times 10^{6} \times 717.5 \times 155.4 $$ $$ \boxed{\,E_{stored} \approx 3.17 \times 10^{11}\ \text{J} \approx 88.1\ \text{MWh}\,} $$ **Step 3 — Assumption stated (mandatory for full marks):** > The cavern is assumed to maintain the air at a uniform temperature of 300 K (isothermal storage), which is realistic for a salt cavern due to the high thermal mass of the surrounding rock salt. Real caverns experience a small temperature drift of 1 – 3 K per day. --- **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]* **(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **charging and discharging modes** of a Diabatic CAES plant. Compare its **round-trip efficiency** with that of an Adiabatic CAES plant. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Diabatic CAES plant uses **1.2 × 10⁶ kg of air per cycle** from a cavern at 70 bar. The compression work input is measured at **$1.8 \times 10^{8}$ kJ**, and the turbine generates **$7.8 \times 10^{7}$ kJ** of useful work. Additional natural gas of calorific value **50 MJ/kg** is combusted in the re-heater at a rate of **650 kg per cycle**. Calculate the **round-trip electrical efficiency** and the **effective fuel-to-electric efficiency** of the plant. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)** --- #### Solution to Question B ##### Part (a) — Block Diagram and Comparison (7 marks) **Diagram (4 marks):** Draw the block diagram from **Section 4.1** of these notes (Diabatic CAES). The examiner expects to see: *Grid → Motor → Multi-stage Compressor → Intercoolers → Cavern → Throttle → Combustor → HP Turbine → Reheater → LP Turbine → Generator → Grid* with fuel input and cooling tower marked. **Description of Modes (2 marks):** - **Charging Mode:** Off-peak electricity drives a multi-stage intercooled compressor. Air is pressurized from 1 bar to 70 bar, the heat of compression is removed by intercoolers and rejected via a cooling tower, and the cool, dense air is stored in the salt cavern. - **Discharging Mode:** During peak demand, air is released from the cavern, mixed with natural gas in a combustor (replacing the lost compression heat), and expanded through HP and LP turbines that drive a generator. **Efficiency Comparison (1 mark):** | Parameter | Diabatic | Adiabatic | |---|---|---| | Round-trip efficiency | 42 – 54 % | 60 – 70 % | | Fuel required | Yes (natural gas) | No | | CO₂ emissions | 250 – 450 g/kWh | ≈ 0 | --- ##### Part (b) — Round-Trip and Fuel-to-Electric Efficiency (7 marks) **Given:** - Compression work input: $W_c = 1.8 \times 10^{8}$ kJ - Turbine work output: $W_t = 7.8 \times 10^{7}$ kJ - Fuel input: $m_f = 650$ kg/cycle, $\text{CV} = 50$ MJ/kg **Step 1 — Energy input from fuel:** $$ E_f = 650 \times 50{,}000\ \text{kJ} = 3.25 \times 10^{7}\ \text{kJ} $$ **Step 2 — Round-trip (storage) efficiency** (ratio of turbine output to *electrical* input only): $$ \eta_{RT} = \dfrac{W_t}{W_c} = \dfrac{7.8 \times 10^{7}}{1.8 \times 10^{8}} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\eta_{RT} \approx 0.433 = 43.3\%\,} $$ **Step 3 — Effective fuel-to-electric efficiency** (total useful electricity per unit of *total* primary energy — electricity + fuel): $$ \eta_{eff} = \dfrac{W_t}{W_c + E_f} = \dfrac{7.8 \times 10^{7}}{1.8 \times 10^{8} + 3.25 \times 10^{7}} $$ $$ \eta_{eff} = \dfrac{7.8 \times 10^{7}}{2.125 \times 10^{8}} $$ $$ \boxed{\,\eta_{eff} \approx 0.367 = 36.7\%\,} $$ **Step 4 — Comment (1 mark):** > The round-trip efficiency of 43.3 % is within the **typical commercial range for diabatic CAES (42 – 54 %)**, validating the operating data. The *effective* fuel-to-electric efficiency of 36.7 % is lower than a modern combined-cycle gas turbine (≈ 55 – 60 %) because part of the air's compression energy is *not* recovered thermally in the diabatic cycle. The result highlights **why adiabatic CAES is the future** — it eliminates the fuel penalty entirely and lifts the effective efficiency to 60 – 70 %. --- ### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students lose marks in CAES problems:** > 1. **Unit mismatch:** Forgetting to convert bar → Pa or MJ → kJ. KTU uses **SI throughout** — if you write "J" in one place and "kJ" in another, expect a 0.5 mark deduction per occurrence. > 2. **Confusing compression with expansion work signs:** Work *into* compressor is **positive** (energy supplied); work *out of* turbine is **positive** (energy delivered). Using a negative sign in $\eta$ calculation will give a meaningless ratio. > 3. **Ignoring intercooling:** A common 3-mark trap. If a problem says "3-stage intercooled", the temperature entering each stage is reset to 300 K, not the previous stage's exit temperature. Many students erroneously use the cumulative $T_2$ and lose 2 – 3 marks. > 4. **Forgetting to state assumptions:** Any CAES calculation must explicitly state the assumption of *ideal gas*, *isentropic compression*, and *cavern temperature*. A missing assumption = **–1 mark** per KTU rubric. > 5. **Mislabelling A-CAES vs D-CAES block diagrams:** The TES unit appears *only* in A-CAES. Putting a "combustor" in an A-CAES diagram or a "TES" in a D-CAES diagram will cost 1 – 2 marks. --- ### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - [x] **CAES = Mechanical bulk energy storage**; air is the working fluid, salt cavern is the storage medium. - [x] **Two thermodynamic loops:** charging (compression) and discharging (expansion + generation). - [x] **Three CAES families:** Diabatic (with fuel), Adiabatic (with TES), Isothermal (lab stage). - [x] **Huntorf (Germany, 1978)** = world's first utility-scale CAES, 290 MW, 70 bar salt cavern. - [x] **McIntosh (USA, 1991)** = 110 MW, 46 bar salt cavern, 26-hour discharge duration. - [x] **Standard storage pressure range = 40 – 80 bar** in salt caverns. - [x] **Master formula:** $w_c = \dfrac{n}{n-1}\,R\,T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$. - [x] **Energy stored:** $E = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{n-1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right]$. - [x] **Round-trip efficiency:** Diabatic 42 – 54 %; Adiabatic 60 – 70 %; Isothermal 75 – 80 %. - [x] **Multi-stage intercooled compression** is mandatory above 4:1 pressure ratio. - [x] **Adiabatic CAES needs Thermal Energy Storage (TES)** — molten salt, ceramic pebbles, or concrete. - [x] **Salt caverns** are preferred due to: (i) air-tightness, (ii) low cost, (iii) self-healing salt creep, (iv) chemical inertness. - [x] **CAES advantages over PHS:** no specific topography needed, lower land footprint, faster ramp rate. - [x] **CAES disadvantages:** needs geological survey, lower energy density than PHS, diabatic variants emit CO₂. - [x] **Always state assumptions** in derivations: ideal gas, isentropic process, cavern temperature uniform, single-state analysis. - [x] **Most-tested KTU sub-topics:** (i) specific work derivation, (ii) multi-stage compression with intercooling, (iii) energy stored calculation, (iv) comparison table of D-CAES vs A-CAES.

topics.title: Flywheel Energy Storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Flywheel Energy Storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flywheel Energy Storage (FES)** is a mechanical energy storage system that stores kinetic energy in a rotating mass (rotor) operating at very high rotational speeds in a low-friction environment. The stored energy is given by the rotational kinetic energy equation $E = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}$, where $I$ is the moment of inertia of the rotor about its axis of rotation and $\omega$ is the angular velocity.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Energy Systems (PERAT637)*, Module 3 focuses on mechanical and electromechanical storage technologies. The flywheel is classified as a **kinetic (mechanical) storage device**, distinct from potential-energy systems such as pumped hydro or compressed air.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine spinning a heavy bicycle wheel rapidly between your palms and then trying to stop it. You immediately feel a strong resistive torque. That "stored effort" you must overcome is exactly the kinetic energy stored in the wheel. A flywheel does the same thing in engineered form — it spins a precisely balanced rotor inside a near-vacuum chamber on magnetic bearings, so almost no energy is lost to friction or air drag. When energy is needed, a motor-generator either draws energy from the spinning rotor (discharge) or accelerates it back up (charge).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** A flywheel is essentially a mechanical "rechargeable battery" that stores energy as rotational motion. Spin it up to store energy, slow it down to release energy.

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Density of high-strength steel:** $\rho \approx \mathbf{7850 \ kg/m^{3}}$
- **Density of carbon-fiber composite rim:** $\rho \approx \mathbf{1600 \ kg/m^{3}}$
- **Maximum allowable peripheral speed (steel):** $v \approx \mathbf{300\text{–}600 \ m/s}$
- **Maximum allowable peripheral speed (composite):** $v \approx \mathbf{900\text{–}1000 \ m/s}$
- **Operating vacuum pressure:** $\mathbf{10^{-3} \text{ to } 10^{-6} \ mbar}$
- **Typical round-trip efficiency:** $\mathbf{80\%\text{–}95\%}$
- **Cycle life:** $\mathbf{>100{,}000 \ full\ charge/discharge\ cycles}$
- **Self-discharge rate:** $\mathbf{\approx 10\%\text{–}20\% \ per\ day}$ (for high-speed FES)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Structured Logic Breakdown

A flywheel energy storage system works on the principle of **bidirectional electromechanical energy conversion** using a single rotating inertia. The process can be broken down into the following logical stages:

1. **Charging Phase (Energy In):**
   - Electrical energy from the grid or a renewable source is fed into a **motor** coupled to the flywheel rotor.
   - The motor accelerates the rotor, increasing its angular velocity $\omega$.
   - Kinetic energy $E_k = \frac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}$ is stored in the rotating mass.

2. **Storage Phase (Holding Energy):**
   - The rotor is decoupled (or the motor is idled) and continues to spin in a near-frictionless environment.
   - Energy is preserved by minimizing losses through: (i) magnetic or active magnetic bearings (AMB), (ii) high-vacuum housing, and (iii) permanent-magnet or reluctance motor-generator units.

3. **Discharging Phase (Energy Out):**
   - The spinning rotor drives the same machine now acting as a **generator**.
   - As the rotor decelerates, kinetic energy is converted back to electrical energy.
   - The output voltage is regulated by power-electronic converters (IGBT/SiC inverters).

4. **Why It Works — The Core "Why":**
   - The energy storage scales with the **square of angular velocity** — doubling the speed quadruples the stored energy.
   - The energy storage scales **linearly with moment of inertia** — moving mass toward the rim increases storage dramatically.

### 2.2 Geometric Configurations of the Rotor

The rotor can be of three primary geometries, each with a distinct moment of inertia:

- **Solid Disc (uniform):** All mass distributed evenly.
- **Hollow Cylinder (thick-walled):** Mass concentrated in a ring between inner radius $r_1$ and outer radius $r_2$.
- **Thin Rim:** Effectively all mass at the outer radius — the geometry of choice for modern high-speed flywheels.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | Kinetic energy stored | $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ | $E$ in J; $I$ in $\mathrm{kg\cdot m^{2}}$; $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 2 | Solid disc moment of inertia | $I = \frac{1}{2} m r^{2}$ | $m$ in kg; $r$ in m |
| 3 | Hollow cylinder moment of inertia | $I = \frac{1}{2} m (r_{1}^{2} + r_{2}^{2})$ | $r_{1}$ inner, $r_{2}$ outer radius (m) |
| 4 | Thin rim moment of inertia | $I = m r^{2}$ | Equivalent to ring/disk rotor |
| 5 | Usable energy (between $\omega_{min}$ and $\omega_{max}$) | $\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_{max}^{2} - \omega_{min}^{2})$ | Often $\omega_{min} \approx 0.5\,\omega_{max}$ |
| 6 | Peripheral speed | $v = \omega r$ | $v$ in m/s; limited by material strength |
| 7 | Maximum permissible $\omega$ | $\omega_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{r \rho \, k}$ | $\sigma_{max}$ tensile strength; $\rho$ density; $k$ shape factor |
| 8 | Power during discharge | $P = \tau \, \omega$ | $\tau$ torque (N·m), $\omega$ angular velocity (rad/s) |
| 9 | Self-discharge (vacuum + magnetic bearing) | $\dot{E} \propto -k \omega^{2}$ | Quadratic with $\omega$ — higher speed, faster decay |
| 10 | Discharge time | $t_{d} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{P_{avg}}$ | Total usable energy ÷ average discharge power |
| 11 | Energy density (volumetric) | $\mathcal{E}_{v} = \dfrac{E}{V}$ | J/m³ |
| 12 | Energy density (gravimetric) | $\mathcal{E}_{m} = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | J/kg or Wh/kg |
| 13 | Material strength figure of merit | $FoM = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{\rho}$ | Higher FoM ⇒ higher specific energy |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** FES is widely used in *frequency regulation* of power grids, *uninterruptible power supplies (UPS)*, *kinetic energy recovery systems (KERS)* in Formula-1 cars, *satellite attitude control*, and *transient ride-through* in renewable-rich microgrids.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Stored Kinetic Energy Equation

We start from the work–energy theorem for rotation. The work done by a torque $\tau$ acting through an angular displacement $d\theta$ is:

$$dW = \tau \, d\theta$$

The instantaneous torque is related to angular acceleration through Newton's second law for rotation:

$$\tau = I \alpha = I \frac{d\omega}{dt}$$

Substituting:

$$dW = I \frac{d\omega}{dt} \, d\theta = I \frac{d\theta}{dt} \, d\omega = I \omega \, d\omega$$

Integrating from rest ($\omega = 0$) to operating speed ($\omega = \omega_{op}$):

$$W = \int_{0}^{\omega_{op}} I \omega \, d\omega = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_{op}^{2}$$

Since this work is stored as kinetic energy:

$$\boxed{\,E_{k} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}\,}$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Moment of Inertia for a Solid Disc

Consider a uniform solid disc of mass $m$ and outer radius $r$. It can be modeled as a stack of infinitesimally thin rings of radius $x$ and thickness $dx$. Each ring has mass:

$$dm = \rho_{A} \, (2\pi x \, dx)$$

where $\rho_{A} = \dfrac{m}{\pi r^{2}}$ is the areal mass density. Each ring contributes $dm \cdot x^{2}$ to the moment of inertia. Hence:

$$I = \int_{0}^{r} x^{2} \, dm = \int_{0}^{r} x^{2} \left(\frac{m}{\pi r^{2}}\right)(2\pi x \, dx)$$

Simplifying:

$$I = \frac{2m}{r^{2}} \int_{0}^{r} x^{3} \, dx = \frac{2m}{r^{2}} \cdot \frac{r^{4}}{4} = \frac{1}{2} m r^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{disc} = \frac{1}{2} m r^{2}\,}$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Moment of Inertia for a Hollow Cylinder

For a thick-walled cylindrical rotor with inner radius $r_1$ and outer radius $r_2$, mass $m$. The areal density is:

$$\rho_{A} = \frac{m}{\pi (r_{2}^{2} - r_{1}^{2})}$$

Integrating over the annular cross-section:

$$I = \int_{r_{1}}^{r_{2}} x^{2} \left(\rho_{A}\right)(2\pi x \, dx) = \frac{2m}{r_{2}^{2} - r_{1}^{2}} \int_{r_{1}}^{r_{2}} x^{3} \, dx$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$\int_{r_{1}}^{r_{2}} x^{3} \, dx = \frac{r_{2}^{4} - r_{1}^{4}}{4}$$

Substituting back:

$$I = \frac{2m}{r_{2}^{2} - r_{1}^{2}} \cdot \frac{r_{2}^{4} - r_{1}^{4}}{4} = \frac{m}{2(r_{2}^{2} - r_{1}^{2})} \left(r_{2}^{4} - r_{1}^{4}\right)$$

Using the identity $r_{2}^{4} - r_{1}^{4} = (r_{2}^{2} - r_{1}^{2})(r_{2}^{2} + r_{1}^{2})$:

$$I = \frac{m}{2} (r_{1}^{2} + r_{2}^{2})$$

$$\boxed{\,I_{hollow} = \frac{1}{2} m (r_{1}^{2} + r_{2}^{2})\,}$$

### 3.4 Maximum Safe Operating Speed from Tensile Stress Limit

At speed $\omega$, the centripetal acceleration of a mass element at radius $r$ is $\omega^{2} r$. The corresponding radial (hoop) stress in a thin rotating rim is:

$$\sigma = \rho \, \omega^{2} \, r^{2}$$

where $\rho$ is material density. For safety, $\sigma \le \sigma_{max}$ (ultimate tensile strength divided by a safety factor). Solving for $\omega_{max}$:

$$\omega_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{max}}{\rho \, r^{2}}}$$

Equivalently, the **maximum peripheral speed** is:

$$v_{max} = \omega_{max} \cdot r = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{max}}{\rho}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Specific Energy:** The maximum theoretical specific energy of a flywheel (energy per unit mass) is therefore:
> $$e_{max} = \frac{E_{max}}{m} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\rho}$$
> This is why materials with high **strength-to-density ratio** (e.g., carbon-fiber composites) yield the highest-performance flywheels.

### 3.5 Numerical Worked Example — Analytical Matrix

**Problem:** A solid steel disc flywheel has mass $m = 250\ \mathrm{kg}$, outer radius $r = 0.40\ \mathrm{m}$, and is spun at $\omega = 3000\ \mathrm{rpm}$. Compute: (i) moment of inertia, (ii) stored energy, (iii) specific energy, (iv) maximum energy if speed is doubled.

**Step-by-step solution:**

Given:
- $m = 250\ \mathrm{kg}$
- $r = 0.40\ \mathrm{m}$
- $\omega = 3000\ \mathrm{rpm} = \dfrac{3000 \times 2\pi}{60} = 314.16\ \mathrm{rad/s}$

**(i) Moment of inertia:**

$$I = \frac{1}{2} m r^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 250 \times (0.40)^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 250 \times 0.16 = 20\ \mathrm{kg \cdot m^{2}}$$

**(ii) Stored energy:**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 20 \times (314.16)^{2} = 10 \times 98{,}696 = 9.87 \times 10^{5}\ \mathrm{J}$$

$$E \approx 0.987\ \mathrm{MJ} \approx 274\ \mathrm{Wh}$$

**(iii) Specific energy:**

$$e = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{9.87 \times 10^{5}}{250} = 3948\ \mathrm{J/kg} = 1.10\ \mathrm{Wh/kg}$$

**(iv) If $\omega$ is doubled to $\omega' = 628.32\ \mathrm{rad/s}$:**

Since $E \propto \omega^{2}$, doubling speed quadruples the energy:

$$E' = 4 \times 0.987\ \mathrm{MJ} = 3.95\ \mathrm{MJ} \approx 1097\ \mathrm{Wh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Energy scales with the **square** of speed — this is the central reason high-speed composite flywheels are preferred over heavy low-speed steel designs in modern FES installations.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Flywheel Energy Storage System

```mermaid

    A[Grid or Renewable Source] -->|Electrical Input| B[Power Electronic Converter AC-DC]
    B -->|DC Bus| C[Machine Controller VFD or IGBT Inverter]
    C -->|Stator Excitation| D[Motor-Generator PMSM or Reluctance Type]
    D -->|Torque Coupling| E[Flywheel Rotor Composite or Steel]
    E -->|Rotational Coupling| F[Bearing System Active Magnetic or Passive]
    E -->|Encapsulation| G[Vacuum Chamber 10 to the power minus 3 to 10 to the power minus 6 mbar]
    D -->|AC Output| H[Power Electronic Converter DC-AC]
    H -->|Filtered Power| I[Load or Grid]
    J[Supervisory Control and SCADA] -.->|Control Signals| B
    J -.->|Control Signals| C
    J -.->|Telemetry| D

    classDef source fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef power fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000

    class A source

```

**Interpretation of the diagram:**
- The **left side** represents energy entry (grid/renewable source).
- The **central block** is the rotating inertial storage element (rotor + bearings + vacuum housing).
- The **right side** delivers conditioned AC power to a load or back to the grid.
- The **top/bottom dashed lines** show the supervisory control layer (SCADA/PLCs) regulating charge/discharge.

### 4.2 Rotor Geometry Comparison Diagram

### 4.3 Charge–Discharge Operational State Diagram

```mermaid

    Standby --> Charging: External Power On
    Charging --> FullCharge: omega equals omega max
    FullCharge --> Holding: Power Disconnected
    Holding --> Discharging: Load Demand Signal
    Holding --> Standby: Long Term Idle
    Discharging --> PartialCharge: omega drops below 0.7 omega max
    PartialCharge --> Charging: Recharge Initiated
    Discharging --> Standby: omega drops below omega min
    Standby --> [*]

        and vacuum housing
```
