## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q1]**

**Q1.** Define Flywheel Energy Storage (FES). State any two of its key advantages over electrochemical batteries.

**Model Answer:**

Flywheel Energy Storage (FES) is a mechanical energy storage device that stores energy in the form of kinetic energy of a rotating mass (rotor) operating at high angular velocity in a low-friction environment. The stored energy is given by $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$.

**Two key advantages over batteries:**
1. **Long cycle life:** FES can withstand >100,000 full charge–discharge cycles with negligible degradation, whereas Li-ion batteries degrade after ~3,000–5,000 cycles.
2. **High power density and fast response:** FES can deliver very high power pulses within milliseconds, ideal for frequency regulation and UPS, whereas batteries have slower electrochemical response.

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q2]**

**Q2.** Distinguish between **low-speed** and **high-speed** flywheel energy storage systems in terms of rotor material, bearings, and typical applications.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Low-Speed FES | High-Speed FES |
|---|---|---|
| Operating speed | 1,000–6,000 rpm | 20,000–100,000 rpm |
| Rotor material | Heavy steel | Carbon-fiber composite rim |
| Bearing type | Mechanical / roller bearings | Active or passive magnetic bearings |
| Containment | None / steel casing | Vacuum chamber + composite containment |
| Applications | UPS, ride-through, traction | Grid frequency regulation, KERS, space |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the kinetic energy stored in a rotating flywheel. A solid steel disc flywheel of mass **200 kg** and radius **0.5 m** rotates at **1500 rpm**. Calculate:
  (i) Moment of inertia.
  (ii) Stored kinetic energy in MJ.
  (iii) Specific energy in Wh/kg. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram the major subsystems of a modern flywheel energy storage system. Discuss the role of magnetic bearings and vacuum housing in minimizing losses. **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation + Numerical:**

**Derivation:**
The work done by torque $\tau$ in rotating the rotor through angle $d\theta$ is $dW = \tau \, d\theta$. Using $\tau = I \alpha = I(d\omega/dt)$ and $\omega = d\theta/dt$, we obtain $dW = I\omega \, d\omega$. Integrating from $0$ to $\omega$:

$$E_k = \int_{0}^{\omega} I \omega \, d\omega = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given:
- $m = 200\ \mathrm{kg}$
- $r = 0.5\ \mathrm{m}$
- $N = 1500\ \mathrm{rpm} \Rightarrow \omega = \dfrac{1500 \times 2\pi}{60} = 157.08\ \mathrm{rad/s}$

**(i) Moment of inertia (solid disc):**

$$I = \frac{1}{2} m r^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 200 \times (0.5)^{2} = 25\ \mathrm{kg \cdot m^{2}}$$

**(ii) Stored kinetic energy:**

$$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \times 25 \times (157.08)^{2} = 12.5 \times 24{,}674 = 3.08 \times 10^{5}\ \mathrm{J}$$

$$E \approx 0.308\ \mathrm{MJ}$$

**(iii) Specific energy:**

$$e = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{3.08 \times 10^{5}}{200} = 1542\ \mathrm{J/kg} = 0.428\ \mathrm{Wh/kg}$$

---

**(b) Block Diagram + Subsystem Explanation:**

```
   Grid/Renewable
        ↓
  AC-DC Converter → DC Bus → VFD Controller
        ↓
  Motor-Generator (PMSM)
        ↓
  Flywheel Rotor ↔ Magnetic Bearings
        ↓
  Vacuum Housing
        ↓
  Generator mode → DC-AC → Load
```

**Role of magnetic bearings:**
- Eliminate mechanical contact between rotor shaft and stator, removing **frictional losses**.
- Allow very high rotational speeds (up to 100,000 rpm).
- Active magnetic bearings (AMB) enable real-time damping and rotor-position control.

**Role of vacuum housing:**
- Removes air drag (aerodynamic losses), which scale as $\omega^{2}$.
- Reduces self-discharge; typical vacuum levels are $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}\ \mathrm{mbar}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model — Internal Choice]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the moment of inertia of a **hollow cylindrical flywheel** of mass $m$, inner radius $r_1$, and outer radius $r_2$. Hence obtain the moment of inertia of a **thin rim** as a limiting case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **specific energy** of a flywheel. Show that the maximum specific energy of a flywheel is $e_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{max}}{2\rho}$ and compute it for **carbon-fiber composite** ($\sigma_{max} = 4\ \mathrm{GPa}$, $\rho = 1600\ \mathrm{kg/m^{3}}$). Compare with steel ($\sigma_{max} = 1\ \mathrm{GPa}$, $\rho = 7850\ \mathrm{kg/m^{3}}$). **(7 Marks)**

---

##### Model Solution

**(a) Hollow cylinder + thin rim derivation:**

For a hollow cylinder with inner radius $r_1$ and outer radius $r_2$, the areal mass density is:

$$\rho_A = \frac{m}{\pi (r_2^{2} - r_1^{2})}$$

Consider an elemental ring at radius $x$ of thickness $dx$:

$$dI = x^{2} \, dm = x^{2} \rho_A (2\pi x \, dx)$$

Integrating:

$$I = \int_{r_1}^{r_2} 2\pi \rho_A x^{3} \, dx = \frac{\pi \rho_A}{2} (r_2^{4} - r_1^{4})$$

Substituting $\rho_A$ and using $r_2^{4} - r_1^{4} = (r_2^{2} - r_1^{2})(r_2^{2} + r_1^{2})$:

$$\boxed{\,I = \frac{1}{2} m (r_1^{2} + r_2^{2})\,}$$

**Limiting case — thin rim:** As $r_1 \to r_2 = r$, the rim thickness $\to 0$ and all mass lies at radius $r$:

$$I_{rim} = \frac{1}{2} m (r^{2} + r^{2}) = m r^{2}$$

This matches the result for a thin ring.

---

**(b) Specific energy derivation + numerical comparison:**

The maximum safe operating speed is obtained from the hoop-stress limit:

$$\sigma = \rho \omega_{max}^{2} r^{2} \le \sigma_{max} \Rightarrow \omega_{max}^{2} = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\rho r^{2}}$$

Substituting into the energy equation for a thin rim:

$$E_{max} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_{max}^{2} = \frac{1}{2} m r^{2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\rho r^{2}} = \frac{1}{2} m \cdot \frac{\sigma_{max}}{\rho}$$

Hence maximum specific energy:

$$\boxed{\,e_{max} = \frac{E_{max}}{m} = \frac{\sigma_{max}}{2\rho}\,}$$

**Carbon-fiber composite:**

$$e_{max}^{CFC} = \frac{4 \times 10^{9}}{2 \times 1600} = 1.25 \times 10^{6}\ \mathrm{J/kg} = 347.2\ \mathrm{Wh/kg}$$

**Steel:**

$$e_{max}^{steel} = \frac{1 \times 10^{9}}{2 \times 7850} = 6.37 \times 10^{4}\ \mathrm{J/kg} = 17.7\ \mathrm{Wh/kg}$$

**Comparison:** The carbon-fiber composite yields roughly **20× higher specific energy** than steel — this is why modern high-speed FES systems universally adopt composite rims.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion:** Many students forget to convert rpm → rad/s, leaving $\omega$ in rpm in the energy equation. Marks deducted: 1–2 per occurrence.
> 2. **Forgetting $\omega^{2}$:** When the question asks for "energy at double speed," students often double the energy instead of quadrupling it.
> 3. **Misapplying the moment of inertia formula:** Hollow cylinder ≠ thin rim; using $I = mr^{2}$ for a thick-walled rotor yields a wrong answer.
> 4. **Confusing $\sigma$ (stress) with $\rho$ (density):** In the specific-energy derivation, swapping these symbols leads to a drastically wrong result.
> 5. **Skipping the integration steps:** KTU examiners expect full derivation, not just the final boxed equation. Marks are awarded for each intermediate step.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flywheel Energy Storage**

- ☐ **Core equation:** $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ — energy scales with **square of speed**.
- ☐ **Solid disc:** $I = \frac{1}{2} m r^{2}$
- ☐ **Hollow cylinder:** $I = \frac{1}{2} m (r_1^{2} + r_2^{2})$
- ☐ **Thin rim:** $I = m r^{2}$ — preferred for high-speed FES.
- ☐ **Peripheral speed limit:** $v_{max} = \sqrt{\sigma_{max}/\rho}$ — material-driven cap.
- ☐ **Maximum specific energy:** $e_{max} = \sigma_{max}/(2\rho)$ — composite > steel by ~20×.
- ☐ **Usable energy:** $\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_{max}^{2} - \omega_{min}^{2})$, with $\omega_{min} \approx 0.5\,\omega_{max}$ typical.
- ☐ **Subsystems:** Rotor + Motor/Generator + Bearings + Vacuum Chamber + Power Electronics + SCADA.
- ☐ **Bearings:** Magnetic (active/passive) eliminate mechanical friction.
- ☐ **Vacuum:** $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ mbar eliminates aerodynamic drag.
- ☐ **Advantages:** Long cycle life (>100,000), fast response (ms), high power density, eco-friendly (no heavy metals).
- ☐ **Disadvantages:** High self-discharge (10–20 %/day), safety concerns (rotor burst containment), high initial cost.
- ☐ **Applications:** UPS, grid frequency regulation, KERS in motorsport, satellite attitude control, renewable integration.
- ☐ **Convert rpm → rad/s:** $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ — never plug rpm directly into $\omega^{2}$.
- ☐ **Remember the $1/2$ factor:** Easy to drop in derivations; always recheck before writing the final boxed result.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Battery Storage - Battery Specification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Battery Storage - Battery Specification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Battery Specification** refers to the comprehensive set of standardized electrical, mechanical, thermal, and performance parameters that quantitatively characterize an electrochemical storage device. These specifications define the **operating envelope**, **life expectancy**, **safety limits**, and **interoperability criteria** required for engineering selection in stationary, mobile, and renewable-integrated energy systems.

In the context of modern energy systems (renewable integration, EV powertrains, microgrids, and UPS networks), battery specification forms the **primary engineering contract** between the cell manufacturer and the system designer. It translates an electrochemical phenomenon into measurable engineering quantities.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water Tank Model"

Imagine a battery as a **water tank connected to a tap**:

| Battery Parameter | Water Tank Analogy |
|---|---|
| **Capacity (Ah)** | Total volume of water the tank can hold |
| **State of Charge (SoC)** | Current water level (%) |
| **Depth of Discharge (DoD)** | How much water has been drawn out |
| **C-rate** | How fast you open the tap |
| **Internal Resistance** | Pipe friction inside the tank |
| **Self-Discharge** | Slow evaporation when not in use |
| **Cycle Life** | Number of times you can fill/drain before leaks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Just as opening a tap too fast reduces the actual water delivered (pressure drop), drawing current too fast from a battery reduces its usable capacity. This is why the **C-rate** is one of the most critical specifications.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

The following universally accepted industry metrics form the backbone of any battery specification sheet:

- **Nominal Voltage (V)** — Typically **3.6 V–3.7 V** for Li-ion, **2.0 V** for Lead-acid, **1.2 V** for NiMH per cell.
- **Gravimetric Energy Density** — **Wh/kg** (mass-based)
- **Volumetric Energy Density** — **Wh/L** (volume-based)
- **Gravimetric Power Density** — **W/kg**
- **Round-Trip Efficiency** — Typically **85–95%** for Li-ion, **70–85%** for Lead-acid
- **Faraday's Constant:** **F = 96,485 C/mol** (used in theoretical capacity calculations)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Battery Specification Parameters

A complete battery specification is structured into **five functional blocks**:

#### Block 1 — Electrical Specifications
- **Nominal Capacity (Q):** The total charge a battery can deliver at a specified discharge rate (typically at C/5 or C/10 rate) under standard temperature conditions (**25 °C**).
- **Nominal Voltage (V_nom):** The midpoint voltage during a standard discharge cycle.
- **Cut-off Voltage:** The minimum allowable terminal voltage (e.g., **2.5 V** for LiFePO₄) below which the battery must be disconnected to prevent irreversible damage.
- **Maximum Charge Voltage:** Upper safety limit during charging (e.g., **4.2 V** for NMC cells).
- **Internal Resistance (R_int):** The ohmic resistance seen at the terminals, comprising ionic, contact, and electrolyte contributions.

#### Block 2 — Energy & Power Specifications
- **Specific Energy (Wh/kg):** Total energy per unit mass.
- **Energy Density (Wh/L):** Total energy per unit volume.
- **Specific Power (W/kg):** Instantaneous power delivery per unit mass.
- **Power Density (W/L):** Instantaneous power delivery per unit volume.

#### Block 3 — Lifetime Specifications
- **Cycle Life:** Number of complete charge-discharge cycles before capacity fades to **80% of nominal** (industry standard end-of-life criterion).
- **Calendar Life:** Total operational/storage lifetime in years (typically **5–15 years**).
- **State of Health (SoH):** Percentage ratio of current maximum capacity to the original rated capacity.

#### Block 4 — Operational Specifications
- **C-rate:** The discharge/charge current normalized to the battery capacity.
- **State of Charge (SoC):** Instantaneous available capacity as a percentage of full charge.
- **Depth of Discharge (DoD):** Complement of SoC, representing the percentage of capacity that has been withdrawn.
- **Self-Discharge Rate:** Percentage of capacity lost per month when idle.

#### Block 5 — Safety & Environmental Specifications
- **Operating Temperature Range** (e.g., **−20 °C to +60 °C** for discharge; **0 °C to +45 °C** for charge).
- **Thermal Runaway Threshold**
- **Memory Effect** (mainly NiCd/NiMH)
- **Toxicity & Recyclability Standards** (UN 38.3, IEC 62133)

### 2.2 Why Each Parameter Matters — The 'Why' Behind the 'How'

- **Capacity (Ah)** dictates **runtime** in autonomous systems (off-grid PV, EVs).
- **C-rate** determines **power capability**; a battery rated 50 Ah at 1C delivers 50 A for 1 hour.
- **Internal Resistance** causes **I²R heat losses** and **voltage sag** under load.
- **SoC** is the **master control variable** in Battery Management Systems (BMS).
- **SoH** is the **degradation indicator** that triggers warranty claims and replacement scheduling.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacity (Theoretical) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{M}$ | Ah/g | n = electrons transferred, M = molar mass |
| Capacity (Practical) | $Q_p = Q_{th} \cdot \eta_{Faradaic}$ | Ah | Accounts for side reactions |
| C-rate | $C_{rate} = \dfrac{I}{Q_{rated}}$ | $hr^{-1}$ | 1C = full discharge in 1 hour |
| Discharge Time | $t = \dfrac{Q_{rated}}{I}$ | h | Direct inverse of C-rate |
| Energy Stored | $E = V_{nom} \times Q_{rated}$ | Wh | Wh = V × Ah |
| State of Charge | $SoC(t) = SoC_0 - \dfrac{1}{Q_{rated}}\int_0^t I(\tau)\,d\tau$ | fraction or % | BMS core equation |
| Depth of Discharge | $DoD = 100\% - SoC$ | % | Always complements SoC |
| State of Health | $SoH = \dfrac{Q_{max,current}}{Q_{rated,new}} \times 100\%$ | % | End-of-life = 80% |
| Internal Heat Loss | $P_{loss} = I^2 \cdot R_{int}$ | W | Drives thermal management design |
| Coulombic Efficiency | $\eta_C = \dfrac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100\%$ | % | Should be $\vert > 99\% \vert$ for Li-ion |
| Energy Efficiency | $\eta_E = \dfrac{E_{discharge}}{E_{charge}} \times 100\%$ | % | Lower than $\eta_C$ due to voltage difference |
| Peukert's Law | $t = H \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{I \cdot H}\right)^k$ | h | k (Peukert exponent) > 1 for Lead-acid |
| Specific Energy | $E_{sp} = \dfrac{E}{m}$ | Wh/kg | Key for EV range |
| Power Density | $P_d = \dfrac{V \cdot I}{V}$ | W/L | Determines acceleration capability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Conversion Rule for KTU Exams:** $1\ \text{Ah} = 3600\ \text{C}$ (Coulombs). When a question gives charge in Coulombs, ALWAYS convert before applying capacity-based formulas.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **EV Design:** Specific energy (Wh/kg) directly determines driving range; specific power (W/kg) determines acceleration.
- **Solar Microgrid:** Round-trip efficiency and cycle life dictate the Levelized Cost of Storage (LCOS).
- **Grid-Scale BESS (Battery Energy Storage Systems):** Power density determines footprint and response time for frequency regulation.
- **Aerospace & Satellites:** Energy density per liter is the ultimate constraint for launch vehicle payload optimization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Capacity from Faraday's Laws

The theoretical capacity of an electrochemical cell is derived directly from Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis.

**Given:**
- Active material molecular weight: $M$ (kg/mol)
- Number of electrons transferred: $n$
- Faraday's constant: $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol

**Step 1:** Faraday's First Law states that the mass $m$ deposited/dissolved is proportional to charge $Q$:

$$m = \dfrac{Q \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for total charge:

$$Q_{total} = \dfrac{m \cdot n \cdot F}{M}$$

**Step 3:** Convert mass-specific charge to capacity per unit mass by dividing both sides by $m$:

$$\dfrac{Q_{total}}{m} = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{M}$$

**Step 4:** Convert C/g to Ah/g by dividing by 3600:

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{3600 \cdot M}\ \text{(Ah/g)}$$

**Step 5:** Final compact form (in Ah/kg):

$$Q_{th} = \dfrac{n \cdot F}{3.6 \cdot M}\ \text{(Ah/kg)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters:** This derivation is the foundation of any "battery specification" question. Examiner's almost always expect you to compute theoretical specific capacity given $n$ and $M$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Content of a Battery Pack

**Given:** A 48 V, 100 Ah Li-ion battery pack at 100% SoC.

**Step 1:** Identify nominal voltage: $V_{nom} = 48$ V

**Step 2:** Identify rated capacity: $Q_{rated} = 100$ Ah

**Step 3:** Apply the energy formula:

$$E = V_{nom} \times Q_{rated}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values:

$$E = 48 \times 100$$

**Step 5:** Compute final result:

$$E = 4800\ \text{Wh} = 4.8\ \text{kWh}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This 4.8 kWh figure is the **nominal stored energy**. The **usable energy** is $E \times DoD_{max}$, which for Li-ion is typically $E \times 0.8 = 3.84\ \text{kWh}$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — C-Rate and Discharge Time Relationship

**Given:** A battery rated 50 Ah at 1C.

**Step 1:** State the C-rate definition:

$$C_{rate} = \dfrac{I}{Q_{rated}}$$

**Step 2:** For a 1C discharge current:

$$I = C_{rate} \times Q_{rated} = 1 \times 50 = 50\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the discharge time formula:

$$t = \dfrac{Q_{rated}}{I}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute:

$$t = \dfrac{50\ \text{Ah}}{50\ \text{A}} = 1\ \text{hour}$$

**Step 5:** For 2C discharge (twice the current):

$$I = 2 \times 50 = 100\ \text{A},\quad t = \dfrac{50}{100} = 0.5\ \text{h} = 30\ \text{min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Inverse Relationship:** $t \propto \dfrac{1}{C_{rate}}$. This is the single most tested relationship in KTU module questions on battery specification.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Peukert's Law (Capacity Fade at High Discharge Rates)

**Given:** A Lead-acid battery with rated capacity $C = 100$ Ah at the 20-hour rate (i.e., 5 A discharge). Peukert exponent $k = 1.25$.

**Step 1:** State Peukert's Law:

$$t = H \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{I \cdot H}\right)^k$$

where $H$ is the rated discharge time in hours (= 20 h here).

**Step 2:** Compute the discharge current for a 5-hour (higher rate) discharge:

$$I = \dfrac{C}{5} = \dfrac{100}{5} = 20\ \text{A}$$

**Step 3:** Plug into Peukert's equation:

$$t = 20 \cdot \left(\dfrac{100}{20 \times 20}\right)^{1.25}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify inside the parenthesis:

$$t = 20 \cdot \left(\dfrac{100}{400}\right)^{1.25} = 20 \cdot (0.25)^{1.25}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the power term:

$$(0.25)^{1.25} = (0.25)^1 \times (0.25)^{0.25}$$

**Step 6:** Approximate numerically:

$$(0.25)^{0.25} = \sqrt[4]{0.25} \approx 0.707$$

$$(0.25)^{1.25} = 0.25 \times 0.707 \approx 0.1768$$

**Step 7:** Final time:

$$t = 20 \times 0.1768 \approx 3.54\ \text{hours}$$

**Conclusion:** Instead of a theoretical 5 hours, the battery actually delivers only **3.54 hours** at the higher 20 A discharge rate — a **capacity loss of ~29%**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Specification Comparison Across Battery Chemistries

| Specification | Lead-Acid (VRLA) | NiMH | Li-ion (NMC) | LiFePO₄ | Solid-State (emerging) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal Voltage (V) | 2.0 | 1.2 | 3.6–3.7 | 3.2 | 3.7 |
| Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | 30–50 | 60–120 | 150–220 | 90–160 | 250–400 |
| Energy Density (Wh/L) | 60–110 | 140–300 | 250–350 | 200–300 | 400–700 |
| Cycle Life (80% DoD) | 300–500 | 500–1000 | 1000–2500 | 2000–5000 | 5000+ |
| C-rate Capability | 0.2–0.5C | 0.5–2C | 1–3C | 1–5C | 1–10C |
| Self-Discharge (/month) | 3–20% | 20–30% | 1–3% | 1–3% | <1% |
| Memory Effect | No | Yes (mild) | No | No | No |
| Operating Temp (°C) | −15 to +45 | −20 to +45 | −20 to +60 | −20 to +60 | −30 to +80 |
| Round-Trip Efficiency | 70–85% | 70–80% | 92–96% | 95–98% | 95–99% |
| Cost ($/kWh) | 100–200 | 250–350 | 130–180 | 150–220 | 400–800 (est.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Selection Rule of Thumb (KTU-favorite question type):**
> - For **EVs**: Choose Li-ion NMC (high specific energy).
> - For **stationary storage**: Choose LiFePO₄ (long cycle life, safety).
> - For **starting/backup**: Choose Lead-Acid (cost-effective, reliable).
> - For **hybrid autos**: Choose NiMH (mature, moderate performance).

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define the terms **(i) State of Charge (SoC)** and **(ii) Depth of Discharge (DoD)** as applied to a battery. How are they mathematically related?

**Model Answer:**

**State of Charge (SoC):** SoC is the ratio of the remaining capacity in a battery at any instant to its rated maximum capacity, expressed as a percentage.

$$SoC(t) = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}(t)}{Q_{rated}} \times 100\%$$

**Depth of Discharge (DoD):** DoD is the percentage of the rated capacity that has been discharged from the battery.

**Mathematical Relationship:**

$$DoD = 100\% - SoC$$

When SoC = 100%, the battery is fully charged (DoD = 0%). When SoC = 20%, the battery has been discharged by 80% (DoD = 80%).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
Explain the significance of the **C-rate** in battery specification with a suitable example.

**Model Answer:**

The **C-rate** expresses the rate at which a battery is charged or discharged relative to its rated capacity. It is defined as:

$$C_{rate} = \dfrac{I}{Q_{rated}}$$

**Significance:**
- It determines **how quickly** energy can be drawn from a battery.
- Higher C-rates lead to reduced usable capacity (Peukert effect).
- It governs the **thermal load** on the battery — higher C-rate means more I²R heat.

**Example:** A 100 Ah battery discharged at 1C delivers 100 A for 1 hour. At 2C, it delivers 200 A for only 30 minutes. At 0.5C, it delivers 50 A for 2 hours.

---

### PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 | Module 3 | ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] List and briefly explain **any five** important electrical specifications of a rechargeable battery used in renewable energy storage systems.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A battery pack has the following ratings: **Nominal Voltage = 48 V, Rated Capacity = 200 Ah, Mass = 60 kg, Volume = 40 L.**
Calculate:
   - (i) Total stored energy in kWh.
   - (ii) Specific energy in Wh/kg.
   - (iii) Energy density in Wh/L.
   - (iv) Maximum discharge current at 0.5C.
   - (v) Discharge time at 0.5C.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A

**(a) Five Electrical Specifications:**

1. **Nominal Voltage (V_nom):** The standard operating voltage of the battery under typical load. For example, a LiFePO₄ cell is rated at **3.2 V**, while a Lead-acid cell is **2.0 V**.

2. **Rated Capacity (Ah):** The total charge a fully charged battery can deliver under standard discharge conditions (typically at C/5 rate and 25 °C). Determines runtime.

3. **Internal Resistance (R_int):** The total opposition to current flow within the cell, comprising electrolyte resistance, electrode resistance, and contact resistance. Lower R_int = better high-power performance.

4. **Cut-off Voltage:** The minimum terminal voltage at which discharge must be terminated to prevent permanent damage. For LiFePO₄, this is typically **2.5 V**; for Lead-acid, **1.75 V**.

5. **Maximum Charge Voltage:** The upper safety limit during charging. Exceeding this triggers electrolyte decomposition and rapid degradation. For NMC chemistry, this is **4.2 V** per cell.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Total Stored Energy:**

$$E = V_{nom} \times Q_{rated}$$

$$E = 48 \times 200 = 9600\ \text{Wh} = 9.6\ \text{kWh}$$

**(ii) Specific Energy:**

$$E_{sp} = \dfrac{E}{m} = \dfrac{9600\ \text{Wh}}{60\ \text{kg}}$$

$$E_{sp} = 160\ \text{Wh/kg}$$

**(iii) Energy Density:**

$$E_d = \dfrac{E}{V_{volume}} = \dfrac{9600}{40}$$

$$E_d = 240\ \text{Wh/L}$$

**(iv) Maximum Discharge Current at 0.5C:**

$$I_{max} = 0.5 \times Q_{rated} = 0.5 \times 200$$

$$I_{max} = 100\ \text{A}$$

**(v) Discharge Time at 0.5C:**

$$t = \dfrac{Q_{rated}}{I} = \dfrac{200}{100}$$

$$t = 2\ \text{hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert Wh to kWh in part (i). Full 2 marks are lost if units are wrong.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing specific energy (Wh/kg) with energy density (Wh/L). They are NOT interchangeable.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Writing 0.5C as 0.5 A (incorrect!). C-rate is dimensionless and must be multiplied by capacity to get current.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam – June 2023 | Module 3 | ESE Pattern]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] What is **Peukert's Law**? Why is it more relevant to Lead-acid batteries than to Lithium-ion batteries?

**(b)** [7 Marks] A Lead-acid battery has the following specifications: **Rated Capacity = 200 Ah (at the 20-hour rate), Peukert exponent k = 1.3, Rated discharge time H = 20 h.**
   - (i) Calculate the discharge current at the rated 20-hour rate.
   - (ii) If the battery is discharged at 50 A, estimate the actual discharge time using Peukert's law.
   - (iii) Comment on the capacity loss compared to the rated case.

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B

**(a) Peukert's Law — Conceptual Explanation:**

Peukert's Law is an empirical equation describing how the **usable capacity of a battery decreases as the discharge rate increases**. The law is stated as:

$$t = H \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{I \cdot H}\right)^k$$

where $k$ is the Peukert exponent. For an ideal battery, $k = 1$. For real Lead-acid batteries, $k$ typically lies between **1.1 and 1.3**.

**Why more relevant to Lead-acid than Li-ion:**
- Lead-acid batteries suffer significantly from **concentration polarization** and **sulfation** at high discharge rates.
- Li-ion batteries have **flat discharge curves** and much lower internal resistance, making capacity loss at high C-rates minimal.
- For Li-ion, $k$ is approximately **1.0–1.05**, while for Lead-acid, $k \approx 1.2–1.4$.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Discharge Current at the 20-hour Rate:**

$$I_{rated} = \dfrac{Q_{rated}}{H} = \dfrac{200\ \text{Ah}}{20\ \text{h}} = 10\ \text{A}$$

**(ii) Actual Discharge Time at I = 50 A using Peukert's Law:**

$$t = H \cdot \left(\dfrac{C}{I \cdot H}\right)^k$$

**Step 1:** Compute the dimensionless ratio inside the parenthesis:

$$\dfrac{C}{I \cdot H} = \dfrac{200}{50 \times 20} = \dfrac{200}{1000} = 0.2$$

**Step 2:** Apply the exponent $k = 1.3$:

$$(0.2)^{1.3} = e^{1.3 \cdot \ln(0.2)} = e^{1.3 \times (-1.6094)}$$

$$= e^{-2.0922} \approx 0.1234$$

**Step 3:** Compute discharge time:

$$t = 20 \times 0.1234 \approx 2.47\ \text{hours}$$

**(iii) Capacity Loss Comment:**

- **Expected (naive) time at 50 A without Peukert correction:** $t_{naive} = \dfrac{200}{50} = 4$ hours.
- **Actual time:** $t_{actual} \approx 2.47$ hours.
- **Capacity Loss:** 

$$\Delta t = 4 - 2.47 = 1.53\ \text{hours}$$

$$\text{Loss Percentage} = \dfrac{1.53}{4} \times 100\% \approx 38.3\%$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** At higher discharge rates, the Lead-acid battery loses more than **one-third** of its rated capacity. This is precisely why Peukert's exponent must be considered when sizing Lead-acid banks for high-current applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write $t = H \cdot (C/I)^k$ **without multiplying by H** inside the bracket. This is mathematically incorrect — Peukert's Law requires the normalized form $\frac{C}{I \cdot H}$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $k = 1$ in the calculation (which would give the ideal case). The question specifically says $k = 1.3$, so ignoring it leads to a full-marks loss.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to interpret the "capacity loss comment" — examiners award 1–2 marks for the qualitative conclusion.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Battery Specification:**

- ✅ **Capacity (Ah)** = charge stored; **Energy (Wh)** = V × Ah. Always quote both.
- ✅ **1 Ah = 3600 Coulombs** — the most tested conversion.
- ✅ **C-rate** is dimensionless: $C_{rate} = I / Q_{rated}$.
- ✅ **SoC + DoD = 100%** — universally complementary.
- ✅ **Specific energy (Wh/kg)** governs EV range; **energy density (Wh/L)** governs packaging.
- ✅ **Peukert's Law** is critical for **Lead-acid only** — Li-ion has near-ideal behavior.
- ✅ **SoH < 80%** = end-of-life (industry-standard retirement criterion).
- ✅ **Round-trip efficiency** = energy out / energy in × 100%. Li-ion > Lead-acid.
- ✅ **Internal resistance** causes I²R heating — drives thermal management design.
- ✅ **Cycle life** is specified at a given DoD (e.g., "2000 cycles at 80% DoD").
- ✅ Always state **temperature** and **C-rate** when quoting capacity (e.g., "100 Ah at C/5, 25 °C").
- ✅ **Faraday's constant** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol is required for theoretical capacity derivations.
- ✅ **Standards to remember:** IEC 62133 (safety), UN 38.3 (transport), IEEE 1188 (VRLA), IEC 62660 (Li-ion EV).
- ✅ **Self-discharge** ordering: Lead-acid < NiMH (Lead-acid actually ~3–20%/month, NiMH ~20–30%/month) — NiMH loses charge fastest among common chemistries.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Primary and Secondary Cells - Dry Cell
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Primary and Secondary Cells - Dry Cell

# ⚡ Primary and Secondary Cells — The Dry Cell

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Electrochemical Cells: The Foundation

An **electrochemical cell** is a device that converts chemical energy into electrical energy through spontaneous redox (oxidation-reduction) reactions, or vice versa. Two electrodes (anode and cathode) are immersed in an electrolyte that facilitates ionic conduction.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Electrochemical cells form the heart of all battery technologies. The classification into *Primary* and *Secondary* cells is based on **reversibility** of the cell reaction — a fundamental concept that distinguishes single-use from rechargeable energy storage.

### 1.2 Classification of Cells

| Cell Type | Reversibility | Rechargeable | Typical Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Cell** | Irreversible reaction | ❌ No | Dry Cell (Leclanché), Alkaline, Mercury cell |
| **Secondary Cell** | Reversible reaction | ✅ Yes | Lead-acid, Ni-Cd, Li-ion |
| **Fuel Cell** | Continuous feed | ✅ Yes (with fuel supply) | H₂-O₂ cell, PEMFC |

### 1.3 Primary Cell — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Cell:** A galvanic (voltaic) cell in which the chemical energy is converted into electrical energy as long as the active materials are consumed. Once exhausted, the cell **cannot be recharged** because the electrochemical reaction is **thermodynamically irreversible**. The original state of the electrodes cannot be restored by passing current in the reverse direction.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Chemical reaction is **irreversible** (ΔG < 0 only in one direction)
- Internal resistance rises steadily with use
- Must be discarded after depletion
- Generally has **higher energy density** than secondary cells
- Lower initial cost per unit

### 1.4 Secondary Cell — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Secondary Cell:** A rechargeable galvanic cell in which the chemical reaction is **reversible**. After discharge, electrical energy can be restored by passing a current through the cell in the reverse direction (a process called *charging*). The cell can undergo hundreds to thousands of charge-discharge cycles.

**Key Characteristics:**
- Chemical reaction is **reversible** (ΔG changes sign with current direction)
- Internal resistance is relatively stable
- Can be reused multiple times (cycle life: **500–2000+** cycles)
- Higher initial cost but lower long-term cost per cycle
- Lower self-discharge rate in modern variants

### 1.5 The Dry Cell (Leclanché Cell)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dry Cell:** A type of primary cell invented by French engineer Georges Leclanché in 1866. It is essentially a *modified Leclanché wet cell* in which the liquid electrolyte (ammonium chloride solution) is replaced by a **moist paste**, making the cell portable and spill-proof. The standard EMF is **1.5 V** per cell.

### 1.6 Real-World Analogy — Making it Intuitive 💡

> **Conceptual Analogy:**
> 
> Imagine a **water tank on a hill**:
> - **Primary Cell** = A tank with a **one-way valve** at the bottom. Water (energy) flows down (discharges) to turn a turbine. Once empty, you must physically carry water back up — you cannot pump it back through the same valve.
> 
> - **Secondary Cell** = A tank with a **two-way valve**. Water can flow down to generate power (discharge) and be pumped back up using external electricity (charge). This is exactly like a rechargeable battery in your phone or laptop.
> 
> - **Dry Cell** = Specifically, it's a *portable water gun* (the Leclanché design) — sealed, dry-looking on the outside, but containing wet paste inside. You can carry it anywhere, but once the "water" runs out, you throw it away.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of the Dry Cell (Leclanché Cell)

The dry cell has a cylindrical, sealed construction with the following components:

| Component | Material | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Container (Anode)** | **Zinc** (Zn) can | Serves as the negative electrode; gets oxidized |
| **Central Rod (Cathode)** | **Carbon (Graphite)** rod | Collects current; inert during reaction |
| **Cathode Mix** | **MnO₂** (Manganese dioxide) powder + carbon black | Depolarizer — removes H₂ gas bubbles |
| **Electrolyte Paste** | **NH₄Cl** (Ammonium chloride) + **ZnCl₂** + starch/agar | Provides ionic conductivity between electrodes |
| **Sealing** | Wax or pitch layer at top | Prevents drying of electrolyte paste |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The zinc container is *consumed* during discharge (zinc dissolves into the electrolyte). This is why old dry cells often leak — the container develops holes!

## 2.2 Chemical Reactions During Discharge

### Step-by-Step Reaction Mechanism:

**At the Anode (Negative Electrode — Oxidation):**
$$\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^{-}$$

**At the Cathode (Positive Electrode — Reduction):**
$$2\text{NH}_4^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2$$

**Depolarizer Reaction (Prevents Polarization):**
$$\text{H}_2 + 2\text{MnO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**
$$\text{Zn} + 2\text{MnO}_2 + 2\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} \rightarrow \text{Zn}(\text{NH}_3)_2\text{Cl}_2 + \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polarization Problem:** Without MnO₂, hydrogen gas would form bubbles on the carbon rod, creating an insulating layer that **increases internal resistance** and **drops the terminal voltage**. MnO₂ is the **depolarizer** that prevents this — critical for sustained current delivery.

## 2.3 EMF & Internal Resistance

The standard EMF of a fresh dry cell is approximately **1.5 V**. As the cell discharges:

- **EMF** remains nominally stable for most of the discharge curve
- **Internal resistance (r)** increases significantly toward end-of-life
- Terminal voltage: **V = E − I × r**

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | Cell EMF from standard electrode potentials | **Volts (V)** |
| 2 | $V = E - I \cdot r$ | Terminal voltage under load | **Volts (V)** |
| 3 | $\Delta G = -n \cdot F \cdot E$ | Gibbs free energy change | **Joules (J)** |
| 4 | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Charge capacity (Coulombs/Ampere-hours) | **Ah** |
| 5 | $E_{\text{stored}} = V \cdot Q$ | Energy stored in cell | **Wh** |
| 6 | $\eta = \frac{V_{\text{discharge}}}{V_{\text{charge}}} \times 100\%$ | Coulombic/Voltage efficiency | **%** |
| 7 | $P_{\text{loss}} = I^2 \cdot r$ | Power dissipated as heat internally | **Watts (W)** |
| 8 | $R_{\text{ext}} = \frac{E - V}{I}$ | External load resistance for given voltage | **Ohms (Ω)** |

> **Standard Constants for KTU Calculations:**
> - Faraday's Constant: **F = 96,485 C/mol**
> - Standard EMF of Dry Cell: **E° = 1.5 V**
> - Theoretical Energy Density of Dry Cell: **~100 Wh/kg**

## 2.5 Why Dry Cells are Preferred for Specific Applications

| Property | Engineering Implication |
|---|---|
| Portable & spill-proof | Flashlights, remote controls, toys |
| Low cost | Single-use consumer electronics |
| Stable voltage output | Suitable for low-current, intermittent loads |
| No maintenance required | Ideal for rural/remote installations |

### 2.6 Limitations of Dry Cell

> [!WARNING]
> - **Voltage drops** during continuous heavy discharge due to polarization
> - **Cannot be recharged** — attempting to do so may cause explosion due to gas buildup
> - **Shelf life is limited** (~2–3 years) due to gradual self-discharge and drying of paste
> - **Poor performance at low temperatures** — ionic mobility in paste decreases
> - **Environmental concern**: Zn and MnO₂ disposal poses heavy-metal pollution risk

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cell EMF from Standard Electrode Potentials

### Given:
- Standard Reduction Potential of Zn²⁺/Zn: **E°(Zn²⁺/Zn) = −0.76 V**
- Standard Reduction Potential of MnO₂/NH₃ couple (approx.): **E°(cathode) ≈ +0.74 V**

### Step 1: Identify Half-Cell Reactions

The anode reaction (oxidation) is the reverse of the standard Zn reduction:
$$\text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Zn}, \quad E^\circ_{\text{red}} = -0.76 \text{ V}$$

The cathode reaction (reduction) involves MnO₂:
$$2\text{MnO}_2 + 2\text{NH}_4^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3 + 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}, \quad E^\circ_{\text{red}} \approx +0.74 \text{ V}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Nernst-Based Cell EMF Formula

$$E_{\text{cell}} = E^\circ_{\text{cathode}} - E^\circ_{\text{anode}}$$

Substituting the values:
$$E_{\text{cell}} = (+0.74 \text{ V}) - (-0.76 \text{ V})$$

$$E_{\text{cell}} = 0.74 \text{ V} + 0.76 \text{ V}$$

$$E_{\text{cell}} = 1.50 \text{ V}$$

### Step 3: Verify via Gibbs Free Energy

Using the relationship:
$$\Delta G^\circ = -n \cdot F \cdot E^\circ_{\text{cell}}$$

With **n = 2** (electrons transferred) and **F = 96,485 C/mol**:
$$\Delta G^\circ = -(2)(96{,}485)(1.50)$$

$$\Delta G^\circ = -289{,}455 \text{ J/mol} \approx -289.5 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

> **Interpretation:** The negative ΔG confirms the reaction is **spontaneous** in the discharge direction — consistent with the dry cell being a galvanic cell.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Discharge Time for a Dry Cell

### Problem Setup:
A dry cell with rated capacity **Q = 1.5 Ah** supplies a constant current **I = 0.3 A**. Determine the discharge time.

### Step 1: Express the capacity-time relationship

$$t = \frac{Q}{I}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the numerical values

$$t = \frac{1.5 \text{ Ah}}{0.3 \text{ A}}$$

$$t = 5.0 \text{ hours}$$

### Step 3: Calculate the total energy delivered

$$E_{\text{delivered}} = V \cdot Q = 1.5 \text{ V} \times 1.5 \text{ Ah}$$

$$E_{\text{delivered}} = 2.25 \text{ Wh}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Terminal Voltage Under Load

### Problem Setup:
A dry cell with EMF **E = 1.5 V** and internal resistance **r = 0.5 Ω** delivers current **I = 0.4 A** through an external resistor.

### Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law around the loop

$$E = V_{\text{terminal}} + I \cdot r$$

### Step 2: Solve for terminal voltage

$$V_{\text{terminal}} = E - I \cdot r$$

$$V_{\text{terminal}} = 1.5 - (0.4)(0.5)$$

$$V_{\text{terminal}} = 1.5 - 0.2 = 1.3 \text{ V}$$

### Step 3: Power delivered to external load

$$P_{\text{load}} = V_{\text{terminal}} \cdot I = (1.3)(0.4) = 0.52 \text{ W}$$

### Step 4: Power lost as heat inside the cell

$$P_{\text{loss}} = I^2 \cdot r = (0.4)^2 (0.5) = 0.08 \text{ W}$$

### Step 5: Overall efficiency

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{load}}}{P_{\text{load}} + P_{\text{loss}}} \times 100\% = \frac{0.52}{0.60} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta = 86.67\%$$

---

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Primary vs Secondary Cells

| Parameter | Primary Cell (Dry Cell) | Secondary Cell (Lead-Acid) |
|---|---|---|
| **EMF per cell** | 1.5 V | 2.0 V |
| **Energy Density** | 40–100 Wh/kg | 30–40 Wh/kg |
| **Cycle Life** | 1 (single use) | 200–1000 cycles |
| **Internal Resistance** | Rises during discharge | Relatively stable |
| **Self-Discharge Rate** | High (~10%/year) | Moderate (~3–5%/month) |
| **Cost per kWh delivered** | High (single use) | Low (multi-cycle) |
| **Charging Required?** | No | Yes |
| **Maintenance** | None | Periodic (for flooded types) |
| **Environmental Impact** | High (single-use waste) | Lower (long-term use) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Dry Cell — Cross-Sectional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Top Sealing Wax Layer] --> B[Carbon Rod Cathode Center]
    B --> C[Cathode Mix MnO2 Powder with Carbon Black]
    C --> D[Porous Separator Paper Liner]
    D --> E[NH4Cl plus ZnCl2 Electrolyte Paste]
    E --> F[Zinc Metal Container Anode]
    F --> G[Bottom Metal Cap Negative Terminal]
    B --> H[Top Metal Cap Positive Terminal]

    A -. Sealing .-> F
    E -. Ionic Conduction .-> C
    F -. Oxidation Zn loses 2 electrons .-> E
    C -. Reduction MnO2 gains electrons .-> B
```

> **Caption:** Block-level functional architecture of a Leclanché dry cell showing the layered construction from top seal to bottom anode contact.

---

## 4.2 Electron & Ion Flow During Discharge

```mermaid
graph LR
    stepA[Zinc Anode Oxidation] -->|Electrons flow through external circuit| stepB[External Load Flashlight]
    stepB -->|Electrons arrive at cathode| stepC[Carbon Cathode Reduction]
    stepC -->|MnO2 accepts electrons becomes Mn2O3| stepD[Depolarizer Reaction]
    stepD -->|NH4 ions migrate to cathode| stepA

    A2[Electron Path External] --> stepB
    A3[Ion Path Internal Electrolyte] --> stepA
```

> **Caption:** Direction of electron flow (external circuit, conventional current opposite) and ion migration (internal electrolyte) during discharge.

```mermaid
    R --> R1[Dry Cell Low drain devices]
    R --> R2[Alkaline Cell Higher capacity]
    R --> R3[Lithium Primary Cell Long shelf life]

    S --> S1[Lead Acid Automotive backup]

```

```mermaid
    style V1 fill:#90EE90
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between primary and secondary cells. Give two examples of each."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

| Feature | Primary Cell | Secondary Cell |
|---|---|---|
| **Reversibility** | Reaction is irreversible | Reaction is reversible |
| **Recharging** | Cannot be recharged | Can be recharged |
| **Examples** | Dry cell, Alkaline cell, Mercury cell | Lead-acid cell, Ni-Cd cell, Li-ion cell |

**Mark Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- 1 mark: Definition + 1 distinguishing feature
- 1 mark: Two examples of primary cells
- 1 mark: Two examples of secondary cells

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**"What is the role of manganese dioxide (MnO₂) in a dry cell? Why is the cell called 'dry'?"**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**(a) Role of MnO₂ (2 marks):**
MnO₂ acts as the **depolarizer** in the dry cell. During discharge, hydrogen gas is generated at the cathode:
$$2\text{NH}_4^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2$$
This H₂ forms an insulating layer around the carbon rod, increasing internal resistance and reducing terminal voltage (a phenomenon called **polarization**). MnO₂ reacts with this H₂:
$$\text{H}_2 + 2\text{MnO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
This prevents gas accumulation and maintains cell performance.

**(b) Why "dry"? (1 mark):**
The cell is called "dry" because the electrolyte is held as a **moist paste** (NH₄Cl + ZnCl₂ + starch) rather than a free-flowing liquid, making the cell spill-proof and portable.

---

## 📚 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Module Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following two questions.

---

### ⭐ Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a Leclanché dry cell. Write the overall cell reaction.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A dry cell has EMF of 1.5 V and internal resistance of 0.4 Ω. It is connected to a load of 2 Ω. Calculate: (i) the current drawn, (ii) terminal voltage, (iii) power delivered to the load, and (iv) efficiency of the cell.

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Construction and Working of Dry Cell [7 Marks]:**

**Construction (3.5 marks):**
The dry cell consists of:
1. A **zinc container** that serves as the **anode** (negative electrode)
2. A **carbon rod** at the center acting as the **cathode** (positive electrode)
3. The space between is filled with a **cathode mix** of MnO₂ powder and carbon black
4. The electrolyte is a **paste of NH₄Cl + ZnCl₂** with starch/agar as binder
5. The top is sealed with **wax or pitch** to prevent drying
6. A porous **paper or cloth separator** prevents direct contact between the cathode mix and zinc wall

**Working (3.5 marks):**
During discharge, oxidation occurs at the zinc anode:
$$\text{Zn} \rightarrow \text{Zn}^{2+} + 2e^-$$
Electrons flow through the external circuit to the carbon cathode. At the cathode:
$$2\text{NH}_4^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow 2\text{NH}_3 + \text{H}_2$$
MnO₂ depolarizes by reacting with H₂:
$$\text{H}_2 + 2\text{MnO}_2 \rightarrow \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Overall Reaction:**
$$\text{Zn} + 2\text{MnO}_2 + 2\text{NH}_4\text{Cl} \rightarrow \text{Zn}(\text{NH}_3)_2\text{Cl}_2 + \text{Mn}_2\text{O}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The standard EMF produced is **1.5 V**.

---

**(b) Numerical Problem [7 Marks]:**

**Given:**
- EMF: E = 1.5 V
- Internal resistance: r = 0.4 Ω
- External load: R = 2 Ω

**(i) Current drawn (2 marks):**
By Ohm's law for the complete circuit:
$$I = \frac{E}{R + r} = \frac{1.5}{2 + 0.4} = \frac{1.5}{2.4}$$
$$I = 0.625 \text{ A}$$

**(ii) Terminal voltage (2 marks):**
$$V = E - I \cdot r = 1.5 - (0.625)(0.4)$$
$$V = 1.5 - 0.25 = 1.25 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Power delivered to load (1.5 marks):**
$$P_{\text{load}} = I^2 \cdot R = (0.625)^2 \times 2$$
$$P_{\text{load}} = 0.3906 \times 2 = 0.78125 \text{ W} \approx 0.781 \text{ W}$$

**(iv) Efficiency (1.5 marks):**
$$\eta = \frac{V}{E} \times 100\% = \frac{1.25}{1.5} \times 100\%$$
$$\eta = 83.33\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Many students write V = E × I instead of P = V × I — read the question carefully!
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing internal resistance (r) with external load (R). Remember: total resistance = R + r
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Efficiency formula — use V/E (voltage efficiency) or P_load/P_total depending on context. Don't mix up with coulombic efficiency.

---

### ⭐ Question B: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the terms: (i) EMF of a cell, (ii) Internal resistance, (iii) Polarization, and (iv) Depolarization. How does internal resistance affect the performance of a primary cell?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare primary and secondary cells on the basis of: reversibility, rechargeability, internal resistance variation, energy density, cycle life, and cost. Mention two applications of each type.

---

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Definitions and Internal Resistance Effects [7 Marks]:**

**(i) EMF (1.5 marks):**
The **electromotive force (EMF)** of a cell is the potential difference between the two electrodes when **no current flows** through the external circuit (open-circuit condition). It is measured in **volts (V)** and represents the maximum voltage the cell can deliver.

**(ii) Internal Resistance (1.5 marks):**
**Internal resistance (r)** is the resistance offered by the electrolyte and electrode materials to the flow of current *within* the cell. It is measured in **ohms (Ω)** and causes a voltage drop (I × r) when current is drawn.

**(iii) Polarization (1 mark):**
**Polarization** is the phenomenon where the terminal voltage of a cell drops below its EMF during discharge due to: (a) accumulation of H₂ bubbles at the cathode (concentration polarization), and (b) depletion of active ions near electrode surfaces.

**(iv) Depolarization (1 mark):**
**Depolarization** is the process of removing or preventing the accumulation of hydrogen at the cathode. In dry cells, **MnO₂** serves as the depolarizer by chemically reacting with H₂ to form Mn₂O₃ and water.

**(v) Effect of internal resistance (2 marks):**
- Reduces terminal voltage: V = E − I × r
- Causes power loss: P_loss = I² × r (converted to heat)
- Wastes energy and lowers efficiency
- Rises as cell ages, accelerating voltage decline
- Limits the maximum current that can be safely drawn (I_max = E/r)

---

**(b) Comparison Table [7 Marks]:**

| Parameter | Primary Cell | Secondary Cell |
|---|---|---|
| **Reversibility** | Irreversible | Reversible |
| **Rechargeable** | No | Yes |
| **Internal Resistance** | Increases with use | Relatively stable |
| **Energy Density** | Higher (40–250 Wh/kg) | Lower (30–150 Wh/kg) |
| **Cycle Life** | 1 use only | 200–3000+ cycles |
| **Cost per kWh** | High | Low (amortized over cycles) |

**Applications (2 marks — 1 each):**

**Primary Cells:**
1. Flashlights, remote controls, toys
2. Smoke detectors, watches, military applications

**Secondary Cells:**
1. Automobiles (lead-acid starter batteries)
2. Mobile phones, laptops, EVs (Li-ion)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning for Question B:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Writing "EMF = I × R" — this is Ohm's law, NOT the definition of EMF. EMF is a *source property*, defined at zero current.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing *polarization* with *internal resistance effect*. Polarization is specifically about H₂ buildup; internal resistance is a broader resistive phenomenon.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Listing only one example per cell type — KTU requires *minimum two* for full marks.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Dry Cell & Electrochemical Cells**

### ✅ Must-Know Concepts:
- **Primary cell** = irreversible, non-rechargeable, single-use (Dry cell, Alkaline)
- **Secondary cell** = reversible, rechargeable, multi-cycle (Lead-acid, Li-ion)
- **Dry cell EMF** = **1.5 V** (memorize this value)
- **Dry cell anode** = Zinc container (gets consumed)
- **Dry cell cathode** = Carbon rod (inert collector)
- **Dry cell electrolyte** = NH₄Cl + ZnCl₂ moist paste
- **Depolarizer** = MnO₂ (prevents H₂ polarization)
- **Inventor** = Georges Leclanché (1866)

### ✅ Must-Know Reactions:
- **Anode (oxidation):** Zn → Zn²⁺ + 2e⁻
- **Cathode (reduction):** 2NH₄⁺ + 2e⁻ → 2NH₃ + H₂
- **Depolarizer:** H₂ + 2MnO₂ → Mn₂O₃ + H₂O
- **Overall:** Zn + 2MnO₂ + 2NH₄Cl → Zn(NH₃)₂Cl₂ + Mn₂O₃ + H₂O

### ✅ Must-Know Formulas:
- Terminal voltage: **V = E − I × r**
- Cell EMF: **E_cell = E°_cathode − E°_anode**
- Capacity: **Q = I × t**
- Energy stored: **E = V × Q (Wh)**
- Power loss: **P_loss = I² × r**
- Efficiency: **η = (V/E) × 100%**

### ✅ Common KTU Exam Keywords:
- "With neat diagram explain..." → Always include labeled diagram + reactions
- "Distinguish between..." → Use a comparison table (preferred format)
- "Calculate the current/voltage/efficiency..." → Show step-by-step substitution

### ✅ Numerical Problem Strategy:
1. **List givens** explicitly (E, r, R, I, V, Q, t)
2. **Identify formula** based on what is asked
3. **Substitute with units** — never skip units in KTU board exams
4. **Box the final answer** with proper SI units

### ✅ Frequently Confused Concepts — Quick Clarifications:
- **EMF vs Terminal Voltage:** EMF is at open-circuit (I = 0); terminal voltage is under load (I > 0)
- **Polarization vs Internal Resistance:** Polarization is chemical (gas buildup); internal resistance is resistive (ohmic)
- **Wet vs Dry Cell:** Wet = liquid electrolyte (Leclanché original); Dry = paste electrolyte (modern portable version)

---

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Primary and Secondary Cells: Dry Cell**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme, NEP 2020 Outcome-Based Education Framework*

topics.title: Lithium Ion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lithium Ion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Lithium-Ion Battery (LIB)** is a rechargeable electrochemical energy storage device that exploits the reversible intercalation (insertion) of lithium ions into the crystal lattice of two host electrode materials to store and release electrical energy. The lithium ions shuttle between the **cathode** (positive electrode) and the **anode** (negative electrode) through a non-aqueous electrolyte, while electrons flow through the external circuit to balance the charge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A lithium-ion cell is a *closed electrochemical system* in which electrical energy is derived from the transfer of lithium ions (Li⁺) between two intercalation hosts separated by a Li⁺-conducting electrolyte and an electronically insulating, ionically permeable separator.

The standard cell architecture consists of four functional components:

| Component | Function | Typical Materials |
|-----------|----------|-------------------|
| **Cathode** | Li⁺ source during discharge | LiCoO₂, LiFePO₄, LiMn₂O₄, NMC, NCA |
| **Anode** | Li⁺ sink during discharge | Graphite (C), Silicon-Graphite composites, Li₄Ti₅O₁₂ (LTO) |
| **Electrolyte** | Li⁺ transport medium | LiPF₆ in EC/DMC (ethylene carbonate + dimethyl carbonate) |
| **Separator** | Physical isolation, ionic bridge | Polyethylene (PE) / Polypropylene (PP) microporous film |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rechargeable Bucket Brigade"

Imagine a **water reservoir system** with two tanks (the electrodes) connected by a pipe (the electrolyte). Water (representing **lithium ions**) can be pumped from one tank to the other. When water is moved from the *low tank* to the *high tank*, a pump (the **charger**) does work on the system — this is **charging**. When allowed to flow back naturally, the water spins a turbine (the **load**) producing useful work — this is **discharge**.

> [!NOTE]
> The key insight: **lithium ions do not get consumed or destroyed**; they simply migrate. The electrodes act as *sponge-like hosts* that absorb and release Li⁺ without dissolving — this property is called **intercalation**.

### 1.3 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Figures)

- **Nominal cell voltage:** **3.6 V – 3.7 V** (per cell)
- **Gravimetric energy density:** **100 – 265 Wh·kg⁻¹**
- **Volumetric energy density:** **250 – 700 Wh·L⁻¹**
- **Coulombic efficiency:** **>99 %** (after formation cycles)
- **Cycle life:** **500 – 3,000 cycles** (chemistry-dependent)
- **Self-discharge rate:** **1.5 % – 2 % per month** at 25 °C
- **Operating temperature window:** **−20 °C to +60 °C** (discharge); **0 °C to +45 °C** (charge)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**During Discharge (Spontaneous, G > 0 in cell):**

1. The anode (e.g., lithiated graphite, LiC₆) is **chemically energetic**. It wants to give up its lithium.
2. Lithium atoms at the anode surface lose an electron: $\text{LiC}_6 \rightarrow \text{C}_6 + \text{Li}^+ + e^-$
3. The **electron** is forced through the external circuit (powering the load) because the separator physically blocks it.
4. The **lithium ion** travels through the electrolyte (driven by concentration gradient and electric field) toward the cathode.
5. At the cathode (e.g., LiCoO₂), the Li⁺ inserts into the lattice while the arriving electron reduces a transition metal ion (e.g., Co⁴⁺ → Co³⁺).
6. The cell voltage appears at the terminals as a measurable potential difference.

**During Charge (Forced, V_applied > V_cell):**

All the above steps are **reversed** by an external power source. Lithium ions are **de-intercalated** from the cathode, traverse the electrolyte, and re-intercalate into the anode.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Rocking-Chair" Battery?** This terminology arises because the Li⁺ ions *rock back and forth* between the two electrodes — the battery itself does not undergo net chemical change in its bulk; only the *location* of Li⁺ changes.

### 2.2 Electrochemistry of a Generic LiCoO₂ / Graphite Cell

**Anode (Oxidation during discharge):**
$$\text{Li}_x\text{C}_6 \rightarrow x\text{Li}^+ + x e^- + 6\text{C}$$

**Cathode (Reduction during discharge):**
$$\text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 + x\text{Li}^+ + x e^- \rightarrow \text{LiCoO}_2$$

**Overall Cell Reaction:**
$$\text{LiCoO}_2 + 6\text{C} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 + \text{Li}_x\text{C}_6$$

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units |
|--------|----------|----------------|-------|
| $E_{\text{cell}}$ | Cell EMF (open circuit) | $E_{\text{cathode}} - E_{\text{anode}}$ | V |
| $Q$ | Charge capacity | $Q = n \cdot F \cdot m / M$ | C (or Ah) |
| $W_{\text{elec}}$ | Electrical energy stored | $W = \int_0^Q V(q)\,dq \approx V_{\text{nom}} \cdot Q$ | Wh |
| $\rho_E$ | Gravimetric energy density | $\rho_E = W / m_{\text{cell}}$ | Wh·kg⁻¹ |
| $\rho_{EV}$ | Volumetric energy density | $\rho_{EV} = W / V_{\text{cell}}$ | Wh·L⁻¹ |
| $\eta_C$ | Coulombic efficiency | $\eta_C = Q_{\text{discharge}} / Q_{\text{charge}}$ | dimensionless |
| $\eta_E$ | Energy efficiency | $\eta_E = W_{\text{dis}} / W_{\text{chg}}$ | dimensionless |
| $C\text{-rate}$ | Charge/discharge current | $I = C\text{-rate} \times C_{\text{nom}}$ | A |
| $F$ | Faraday constant | $\mathbf{96\,485}$ | C·mol⁻¹ |
| $n$ | Electrons transferred per mole | typically **1** for Li⁺ | — |
| $E^0$ | Standard reduction potential (Li⁺/Li) | $\mathbf{-3.04}$ | V vs SHE |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note for Tables:** In every KTU answer script, when using absolute value or modulus of any variable, write $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$ — never use the raw pipe character $\vert$ inside markdown tables, as it breaks table rendering and is a common valuation penalty trigger.

### 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering

Lithium-ion cells are the **dominant electrochemical storage technology** for three high-impact engineering sectors:

1. **Electric Mobility (EVs):** Energy density of ~250 Wh·kg⁻¹ at pack level enables 300–600 km driving range per charge. Tesla, BYD, and all major OEMs use NMC or LFP chemistries.
2. **Grid-Scale Energy Storage (BESS):** For renewable integration (solar/wind smoothing), LFP cells dominate because of their superior cycle life (>6,000 cycles) and thermal stability.
3. **Portable Electronics:** Smartphones, laptops, and medical devices rely on compact, high-voltage Li-ion cells (typically LiCoO₂).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Specific Capacity of an Electrode Material

The **specific capacity** $Q_{\text{sp}}$ of an electrode material is the amount of charge it can store per unit mass. We derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Start from the definition of electric charge:**
$$Q = I \cdot t \quad \text{[Coulombs]}$$

**Step 2 — Express the total charge stored by $n$ moles of a material that transfers $z$ electrons per mole:**
$$Q_{\text{total}} = n \cdot z \cdot F$$

where $F = 96\,485$ C·mol⁻¹ is Faraday's constant.

**Step 3 — Convert moles to mass using molar mass $M$ (kg·mol⁻¹):**
$$n = \frac{m}{M}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into Step 2 to obtain the specific capacity (per unit mass):**
$$Q_{\text{sp}} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{m} = \frac{(m/M) \cdot z \cdot F}{m} = \frac{z \cdot F}{M}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to the more practical unit of mAh·g⁻¹ using $1 \text{ C} = (1/3600) \text{ Ah}$:**
$$Q_{\text{sp}} = \frac{z \cdot F}{3600 \cdot M} \quad \text{[Ah·g}^{-1}\text{]}$$

**Step 6 — Apply to graphite anode (C₆, M = 72 g·mol⁻¹, z = 1, fully lithiated as LiC₆):**
$$Q_{\text{sp,graphite}} = \frac{1 \times 96\,485}{3600 \times 72} \;\text{Ah·g}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{sp,graphite}} = 372 \;\text{mAh·g}^{-1}}$$

**Step 7 — Apply to lithium cobalt oxide cathode (LiCoO₂, M = 97.87 g·mol⁻¹, z = 1):**
$$Q_{\text{sp,LCO}} = \frac{1 \times 96\,485}{3600 \times 97.87} \;\text{Ah·g}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{Q_{\text{sp,LCO}} = 274 \;\text{mAh·g}^{-1}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Energy Density of a LiCoO₂/Graphite Cell

**Step 1 — Use the average open-circuit voltage $V_{\text{avg}} \approx 3.7$ V (vs Li/Li⁺ for LCO half-cell ≈ 3.9 V; graphite half-cell ≈ 0.1 V):**
$$V_{\text{cell}} = V_{\text{cathode}} - V_{\text{anode}} = 3.9 - 0.1 = 3.8 \;\text{V (theoretical open-circuit)}$$

**Step 2 — The practical cell energy per unit mass is governed by the *limiting electrode* — typically the cathode. Compute it using the specific capacity of LCO:**
$$E_{\text{sp}} = V_{\text{cell}} \times Q_{\text{sp,LCO}} = 3.7 \;\text{V} \times 274 \;\text{mAh·g}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{sp, theoretical}} \approx 1014 \;\text{Wh·kg}^{-1} \text{ (cathode active material basis)}}$$

**Step 3 — Real-world cells achieve only 25–30 % of this theoretical value because:**
- Inactive mass (current collectors, electrolyte, separator, casing) — ~50 %
- Packing factor and porosity — ~15 %
- Kinetic overpotentials and depth-of-discharge limits — ~10 %

Therefore, **practical cell-level energy density ≈ 250 Wh·kg⁻¹**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Major Cathode Chemistries

| Chemistry | Formula | Nominal V (vs Li) | Theor. Cap. (mAh·g⁻¹) | Practical Energy (Wh·kg⁻¹) | Cycle Life | Key Trade-off |
|-----------|---------|-------------------|-----------------------|----------------------------|------------|--------------|
| LCO | LiCoO₂ | 3.9 V | 274 | 150 – 200 | 500 – 1,000 | High energy, low thermal stability |
| LFP | LiFePO₄ | 3.4 V | 170 | 90 – 110 | 2,000 – 6,000 | Safe, long-life, lower voltage |
| NMC | LiNiₓMnᵧCozO₂ | 3.8 V | 280 | 200 – 250 | 1,000 – 2,500 | Balanced, EV-dominant |
| NCA | LiNi₀.₈Co₀.₁₅Al₀.₀₅O₂ | 3.8 V | 280 | 220 – 260 | 500 – 1,500 | High energy, Tesla-favoured |
| LMO | LiMn₂O₄ | 4.1 V | 148 | 100 – 120 | 500 – 1,000 | Cheap, Mn dissolution issue |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for the Board Exam — "Co Feeds Nicks, Mn Lost Money":** *Co*balt powers phones, *Fe* (LFP) anchors grids, *Ni*ckel-Manganese-*Co*balt (NMC) drives EVs, *Mn* (LMO) lost out commercially due to capacity fade.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

> **Problem:** A 50 Ah LiFePO₄ cell is discharged at 0.5 C rate. Compute: (a) the discharge current, (b) the discharge time to 100 % Depth of Discharge (DoD), (c) the energy delivered if average voltage is 3.2 V, (d) the cost per kWh if cell price is ₹12,000 and mass is 1.4 kg.

**Solution:**

**(a) Discharge current:**
$$I = 0.5 \times C_{\text{nom}} = 0.5 \times 50 = 25 \;\text{A}$$

**(b) Discharge time:**
$$t = \frac{Q}{I} = \frac{50 \;\text{Ah}}{25 \;\text{A}} = 2.0 \;\text{h}$$

**(c) Energy delivered:**
$$W = V_{\text{avg}} \times Q = 3.2 \times 50 = 160 \;\text{Wh} = 0.16 \;\text{kWh}$$

**(d) Gravimetric energy density:**
$$\rho_E = \frac{160 \;\text{Wh}}{1.4 \;\text{kg}} \approx 114 \;\text{Wh·kg}^{-1}$$

**(e) Cost per kWh:**
$$\text{Cost} = \frac{12\,000}{0.16} = 75\,000 \;\text{₹·kWh}^{-1}$$

This is consistent with current Indian market retail pricing for LFP cells (2024).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Lithium-Ion Cell — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[POSITIVE TERMINAL] --> A2[Cathode Current Collector Aluminum Foil]
    A2 --> A3[Cathode Active Material LiCoO2 or NMC or LFP]

    A4 --> A5[Anode Active Material Graphite or SiC]
    A5 --> A6[Anode Current Collector Copper Foil]
    A6 --> A7[NEGATIVE TERMINAL]

    A4 -. Li+ ion path .-> A4
    A3 -. Electron path via external load .-> A6

    A8[Electrolyte LiPF6 in EC DMC] --> A4
```

### 4.2 Cell Operation State Machine — Charge / Discharge / Rest

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2

    Rest --> Charging: External V applied greater than V cell
    Rest --> Discharging: Load connected
    Charging --> Rest: V cell equals V source
    Discharging --> Rest: Load removed
    Charging --> Overcharge: V exceeds 4.2 V per cell
    Discharging --> Overdischarge: V drops below 2.5 V per cell
    Overcharge --> ThermalRunaway: Separator melts
    Overdischarge --> CapacityLoss: Cu dissolution
    ThermalRunaway --> [*]: Safety hazard
    CapacityLoss --> [*]: Permanent damage
```
