<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
*Define the term "intercalation" in the context of lithium-ion battery electrodes. Why is it preferred over conventional alloying reactions?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Intercalation** is the reversible insertion of guest ions (Li⁺) into the interstitial sites of a host crystal lattice without disrupting its framework structure.
- (1 mark) In Li-ion cells, Li⁺ inserts into the layered/spinel/olivine structures of cathode materials and the graphene layers of graphite anode.
- (1 mark) **Why preferred:** Intercalation causes only minor lattice strain (<10%), preserving crystal integrity over thousands of cycles, whereas alloying (e.g., Li–Sn) leads to ~200–300% volume expansion, causing rapid mechanical failure.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Sample, Energy Systems Module 3]**
*State two advantages and one disadvantage of LiFePO₄ (LFP) cathodes compared to LiCoO₂ (LCO).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Advantage 1:** Superior thermal stability — LFP is thermally safe up to 300 °C vs LCO's 150 °C, eliminating thermal runaway risk.
- (1 mark) **Advantage 2:** Much longer cycle life — 2,000–6,000 cycles vs LCO's 500–1,000 cycles.
- (1 mark) **Disadvantage:** Lower nominal voltage (3.2 V vs 3.7 V) and lower specific capacity (170 vs 274 mAh·g⁻¹), resulting in lower cell-level energy density.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### ❓ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Expected Pattern, PERAT637]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working principle of a commercial lithium-ion cell. Identify all four major components and state their functions. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the theoretical specific capacity of graphite (C₆) and compute the theoretical energy density of a LiCoO₂/graphite cell. State two real-world reasons why the practical energy density is 4–5× lower. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Schematic Description (3 marks):**
A cylindrical Li-ion cell consists of a **jelly-roll** structure where the cathode (LiCoO₂ coated on Al foil), separator (porous PE/PP), and anode (graphite coated on Cu foil) are wound together and immersed in electrolyte. The cell is sealed in a steel can with a safety vent.

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
During **discharge**:
- At the **anode**, lithiated graphite oxidizes: $\text{LiC}_6 \rightarrow \text{C}_6 + \text{Li}^+ + e^-$
- Electrons flow through the **external circuit** to power the load.
- Li⁺ ions migrate through the **electrolyte** (LiPF₆ in EC/DMC) and across the **separator**.
- At the **cathode**, Li⁺ inserts into LiCoO₂: $\text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 + x\text{Li}^+ + x e^- \rightarrow \text{LiCoO}_2$

During **charge**, all reactions reverse under the influence of an external voltage source.

**Four Components & Functions:**
1. **Cathode (LCO on Al foil):** Source of Li⁺ during discharge; hosts Li⁺ during charge.
2. **Anode (graphite on Cu foil):** Accepts Li⁺ during charge; releases it during discharge.
3. **Electrolyte (LiPF₆ + organic carbonates):** Conducts Li⁺ between electrodes; electronically insulating.
4. **Separator (PE/PP):** Physically separates electrodes to prevent short circuits while allowing Li⁺ flow.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Starting from Faraday's law:
$$Q_{\text{sp}} = \frac{z \cdot F}{M}$$

For graphite, $z = 1$, $M(\text{C}_6) = 6 \times 12 = 72$ g·mol⁻¹:
$$Q_{\text{sp,graphite}} = \frac{1 \times 96\,485}{3600 \times 72} = 372 \;\text{mAh·g}^{-1}$$

**Energy Density Calculation (2 marks):**
Average cell voltage $V_{\text{cell}} = 3.7$ V, theoretical specific capacity (limited by LCO cathode) = 274 mAh·g⁻¹:
$$E_{\text{sp}} = V_{\text{cell}} \times Q_{\text{sp,LCO}} = 3.7 \times 274 = 1014 \;\text{Wh·kg}^{-1}$$

**Reasons for Practical Loss (1 mark):**
1. Inactive mass burden — current collectors, electrolyte, separator, casing constitute ~50% of cell mass.
2. Depth of Discharge (DoD) limited to ~80% to prevent degradation.
3. Internal resistance causes voltage sag and ohmic losses during operation.

---

#### ❓ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, PERAT637, Module 3]**

**(a)** Compare LiFePO₄, LiCoO₂, and NMC cathodes in terms of nominal voltage, specific capacity, cycle life, thermal stability, and typical applications. Present the comparison in a structured table. (7 marks)

**(b)** Explain the Solid Electrolyte Interphase (SEI) layer — its formation mechanism, composition, and dual role in cell life and degradation. Why is SEI considered a "necessary evil" in Li-ion batteries? (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):** (Comparison Table — 7 marks)

| Parameter | LiCoO₂ (LCO) | LiFePO₄ (LFP) | NMC (LiNiₓMnᵧCozO₂) |
|-----------|--------------|---------------|----------------------|
| Nominal Voltage | 3.7 V | 3.2 V | 3.7 V |
| Theor. Capacity | 274 mAh·g⁻¹ | 170 mAh·g⁻¹ | 280 mAh·g⁻¹ |
| Practical Energy | 150–200 Wh·kg⁻¹ | 90–110 Wh·kg⁻¹ | 200–250 Wh·kg⁻¹ |
| Cycle Life | 500–1,000 | 2,000–6,000 | 1,000–2,500 |
| Thermal Stability | Poor (≤150 °C) | Excellent (≤300 °C) | Moderate (≤210 °C) |
| Cost | High (Co) | Low (Fe) | Medium-High (Ni) |
| Typical Use | Phones, laptops | EVs, grid storage | EVs (Tesla, Hyundai, Tata) |

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):** (7 marks)

**SEI — Definition (1 mark):**
The Solid Electrolyte Interphase is a **passivation layer**, ~10–100 nm thick, that forms on the anode surface during the **first few charge cycles** (formation cycles) due to reductive decomposition of the electrolyte.

**Formation Mechanism (2 marks):**
When the anode potential drops below ~0.8 V vs Li/Li⁺ during initial charging, the electrolyte solvents (EC, DMC) are reductively decomposed. The products — Li₂CO₃, LiF, ROCO₂Li, and polymeric species — precipitate onto the graphite surface, forming the SEI.

**Composition (2 marks):**
- Inorganic inner layer: LiF, Li₂CO₃, Li₂O (closer to anode)
- Organic outer layer: ROLi, ROCO₂Li, polymers (closer to electrolyte)

**Dual Role — The "Necessary Evil" (2 marks):**
- **Beneficial:** SEI blocks further electrolyte decomposition (kinetically), allowing long-term cycling; it is also Li⁺-conductive, so the cell still works.
- **Harmful:** SEI consumes cyclable Li⁺ (irreversible capacity loss in formation cycles), grows slowly with time/temperature (calendar aging), and increases internal resistance.

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the separator** in the cell schematic — the separator is a *fourth distinct component*, not a part of the electrolyte. Many students omit it; expect −2 marks.
> 2. **Writing the cell reaction incorrectly** — always balance both Li and electrons. Writing "LiCoO₂ + C → LiC₆ + CoO₂" is unphysical and will cost full marks on the equation.
> 3. **Confusing Farads (F) with Faraday's constant (F = 96,485 C·mol⁻¹)** in derivations — this is a very common KTU answer script error flagged by board examiners.
> 4. **Using the wrong cell voltage** — using 1.5 V (alkaline) or 1.2 V (NiMH) values when asked about Li-ion is an instant fail marker.
> 5. **Incomplete SEI discussion** — examiners specifically look for both the *protective* and *degradative* aspects. One-sided answers lose 1–2 marks.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **lithium-ion battery** is a *rocking-chair* cell where Li⁺ shuttles between two intercalation hosts.
- ✅ The **standard reaction** is: $\text{LiCoO}_2 + 6\text{C} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{Li}_{1-x}\text{CoO}_2 + \text{Li}_x\text{C}_6$
- ✅ **Nominal voltage** is **3.6–3.7 V** per cell — never confuse with alkaline (1.5 V) or lead-acid (2.0 V).
- ✅ **Faraday's constant** $F = 96\,485$ C·mol⁻¹ is essential for any capacity or energy derivation.
- ✅ **Theoretical specific capacity** of graphite = **372 mAh·g⁻¹** (memorize this number).
- ✅ **LFP is safest**, **LCO has highest energy**, **NMC is the best balance** for EVs.
- ✅ **SEI layer** forms during the first charge — it is *necessary* for stability but *evil* because it consumes lithium and grows over time.
- ✅ **C-rate** definition: 1C = full discharge in 1 hour; 2C = 30 minutes; C/2 = 2 hours.
- ✅ **Practical energy density** is roughly 25–30% of theoretical due to inactive mass and overpotentials.
- ✅ **Cycle life to EOL** is typically defined as capacity fade to **80% of initial** (SoH = 80%).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Lithium Air
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Lithium Air

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 3 — LITHIUM-AIR BATTERIES

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> A **Lithium-Air Battery (Li–O₂ battery)** is a primary/rechargeable electrochemical energy storage device in which the **negative electrode (anode)** is **metallic lithium** and the **positive electrode (cathode)** is a porous, gas-diffusion structure that catalytically reduces **ambient atmospheric oxygen (O₂)** drawn from the environment. Because the active cathode reactant is *not stored* inside the cell but is harvested from the surrounding air, Li–air cells belong to the **open-system / semi-fuel class** of metal–air batteries.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy — "The Breathing Battery"

Imagine a tiny **combustion engine that "breathes."** A conventional Li-ion battery carries its own oxidizer locked inside (the cobalt/nickel oxide cathode), so it is heavy and bulky for the energy it stores. A **Li–air cell** throws away that heavy oxidizer and instead **inhales oxygen from the atmosphere** during discharge, just as a fire needs air to burn. This single design trick is what gives it a theoretical specific energy (**≈ 11,400 Wh kg⁻¹**) comparable to **gasoline (~13,000 Wh kg⁻¹)** and **~5–10× higher than today's Li-ion cells (~150–250 Wh kg⁻¹)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why KTU Cares About Li–Air in Module 3**
> Module 3 examines **next-generation / frontier electrochemical storage** that goes beyond Li-ion. Li–air is the textbook "limit-case" metal–air battery because lithium has the most negative standard reduction potential (**E°(Li⁺/Li) = −3.04 V vs. SHE**) and the lowest equivalent weight (M = **6.94 g mol⁻¹**).

### 1.2 Classification by Electrolyte Family

| Architecture | Electrolyte | Discharge Product | Nominal OCV |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Aprotic (non-aqueous)** | Organic carbonate / ether (e.g., TEGDME, DMSO) | Li₂O₂ (lithium peroxide) | **2.96 V** |
| **Aqueous** | Alkaline / acidic water | LiOH (in alkaline), Li₂O·H₂O | ~3.4 V (with protected Li) |
| **Hybrid / Mixed** | Li⁺-conducting ceramic + aqueous | LiOH at cathode | ~3.4–3.6 V |
| **Solid-State** | Glass / ceramic / polymer | Li₂O₂ or Li₂O | 2.96 V |

> [!TIP]
> The **aprotic Li–O₂ cell (Li₂O₂ product)** is the configuration most widely researched and is what students should default to in KTU answer scripts unless otherwise specified.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics to Memorize

- **Standard cell potential (theoretical):** **E°cell = 2.96 V** (formation of Li₂O₂)
- **Faraday constant:** **F = 96 485 C mol⁻¹**
- **Molar mass of Li:** **6.94 g mol⁻¹**
- **Molar mass of O₂:** **32.00 g mol⁻¹**
- **Theoretical specific energy (incl. O₂):** ≈ **11 400 Wh kg⁻¹**
- **Theoretical specific energy (excl. O₂, air-breathing):** ≈ **40 000 Wh kg⁻¹**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The Electrochemical Heart

The Li–air cell is a **galvanic (discharging)** and **electrolytic (charging)** device whose overall chemistry is a **reversible redox between lithium metal and molecular oxygen**. Operation unfolds in three coupled stages:

### Step 1 — Anodic Stripping (Discharge)
At the lithium metal negative electrode, neutral Li atoms are oxidised, releasing electrons to the external circuit and producing Li⁺ ions that migrate through the electrolyte:

$$\text{Li}_{(s)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Li}^{+}_{(sol)} \;+\; e^{-}$$

### Step 2 — Cathodic Reduction (Oxygen Reduction Reaction, ORR)
At the porous carbon cathode, **oxygen drawn from the atmosphere** is reduced at a catalyst surface. In the **aprotic configuration** this is a **two-electron process**:

$$\text{O}_{2\,(g)} \;+\; 2\text{Li}^{+}_{(sol)} \;+\; 2e^{-} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Li}_{2}\text{O}_{2\,(s)}$$

### Step 3 — Reverse Path on Charge (Oxygen Evolution Reaction, OER)
On recharge, an external voltage drives the **Oxygen Evolution Reaction (OER)** which decomposes the stored peroxide back into Li⁺ and gaseous O₂:

$$\text{Li}_{2}\text{O}_{2\,(s)} \;\longrightarrow\; 2\text{Li}^{+}_{(sol)} \;+\; \text{O}_{2\,(g)} \;+\; 2e^{-}$$

### Net Cell Reaction

$$\boxed{ \;2\text{Li}_{(s)} \;+\; \text{O}_{2\,(g)} \;\xrightleftharpoons[\text{charge}]{\text{discharge}}\; \text{Li}_{2}\text{O}_{2\,(s)}\; }$$

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why is lithium chosen as the anode?** Because it is the **lightest metal** (ρ = 0.534 g cm⁻³) with the **most negative electrode potential** (–3.04 V), maximising both **specific energy** (J kg⁻¹) and **open-circuit voltage**.
- **Why is a porous carbon cathode used?** It must provide (a) high surface area for ORR catalysis, (b) a three-phase boundary (solid electrode / liquid electrolyte / gaseous O₂) and (c) ample pore volume to accommodate the **insoluble Li₂O₂ discharge product**.
- **Why does the cell "breathe" in air, not pure O₂?** Practical Li–air cells use ambient air; but trace CO₂ and H₂O from the atmosphere react with Li to form Li₂CO₃ and LiOH, which is the **#1 degradation pathway** and a major research hurdle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check**
> Only the **mass of the lithium anode + porous cathode + electrolyte** counts toward cell mass; oxygen is free. However, **in theoretical calculations, the mass of O₂ is usually included** to give a fair comparison against closed systems (Li-ion). Always state your assumption in KTU answers.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Standard Gibbs free energy | $\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}$ | J mol⁻¹ | $n$ = electrons transferred per mole of reaction |
| 2 | Theoretical EMF | $E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}}$ | V | Cathode: O₂/Li₂O₂; Anode: Li⁺/Li |
| 3 | Theoretical specific energy (incl. O₂) | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{-nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{M_{\text{reactants}}}$ | Wh kg⁻¹ | Divide J kg⁻¹ by 3 600 to convert to Wh kg⁻¹ |
| 4 | Theoretical specific energy (excl. O₂) | $\varepsilon_{\text{air}} = \dfrac{-nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{M_{\text{Li only}}}$ | Wh kg⁻¹ | Use only $M_{\text{Li}}$ since O₂ is free |
| 5 | Theoretical volumetric energy density | $\varepsilon_{V} = \dfrac{-nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{\sum \dfrac{M_{i}}{\rho_{i}}}$ | Wh L⁻¹ | $\rho_{i}$ = density of each reactant/product |
| 6 | Cell-level energy | $E_{\text{cell}} = Q \cdot V_{\text{avg}}$ | Wh | $Q$ in Ah, $V_{\text{avg}}$ in V |
| 7 | Theoretical capacity (Li) | $Q_{\text{Li}} = \dfrac{F}{3600 \cdot M_{\text{Li}}} = 3 861$ | Ah kg⁻¹ | Universal constant for any Li cell |
| 8 | Round-trip efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{\text{discharge}}}{E_{\text{charge}}} \times 100\%$ | % | Currently **≈ 60–75 %** for Li–air |
| 9 | Energy density vs. Li-ion | $\dfrac{\varepsilon_{\text{Li-air}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{Li-ion}}} \approx \dfrac{11\,400}{250} \approx 45$ | ratio | For exam comparisons |
| 10 | Discharge overpotential | $\eta_{\text{ORR}} = E^{\circ} - V_{\text{load}}$ | V | Loss term on discharge |

> [!TIP]
> **Constant worth memorising:** **1 Faraday = 1 F = 96 485 C mol⁻¹**. Combined with $M_{\text{Li}} = 6.94$ g mol⁻¹, this gives the universal **theoretical specific capacity of lithium metal = 3 861 Ah kg⁻¹**, the highest of any anode material in any rechargeable battery.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electric aviation & long-range EVs:** Theoretical parity with gasoline makes Li–air attractive for transport sectors that Li-ion cannot decarbonise.
- **Grid-scale stationary storage:** Cheap raw materials (lithium + carbon + air) offset cell cost barriers.
- **Space exploration:** NASA originally studied Li–air concepts for high-energy primary missions.
- **Backup / military power:** High specific energy matters more than cycle life in single-use missions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV) from Thermodynamic Data

We derive the theoretical open-circuit voltage from the standard Gibbs free energy of formation of the discharge product **Li₂O₂**.

**Step 1 — Identify the half-reactions and their standard reduction potentials.**

| Half-reaction | E° (V vs. SHE) |
|---|---|
| $\text{Li}^{+} + e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Li}$ | −3.040 |
| $\text{O}_{2} + 2\text{Li}^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Li}_{2}\text{O}_{2}$ | −0.080 (computed) |

> The O₂/Li₂O₂ couple's standard potential is derived from thermodynamic data on Li₂O₂ stability.

**Step 2 — Apply the cell-potential formula.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} &= E^{\circ}_{\text{cathode}} - E^{\circ}_{\text{anode}} \\[4pt]
&= (-0.080) - (-3.040) \\[4pt]
&= 2.960 \;\text{V}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **theoretical OCV = 2.96 V** for the aprotic Li–air cell.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Theoretical Specific Energy (Including O₂ Mass)

The most common KTU derivation. Start from the net reaction and apply the energy-mass conversion.

**Given:** $2\text{Li} + \text{O}_{2} \rightarrow \text{Li}_{2}\text{O}_{2}$, $n = 2$ electrons per mole of O₂.

**Step 1 — Compute molar mass of reactants per stoichiometric unit.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{reactants}} &= 2 \cdot M_{\text{Li}} + 1 \cdot M_{\text{O}_{2}} \\[4pt]
&= 2(6.94) + 32.00 \\[4pt]
&= 13.88 + 32.00 \\[4pt]
&= 45.88 \;\text{g mol}^{-1} \;=\; 0.04588 \;\text{kg mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the molar Gibbs free energy.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^{\circ} &= -n F E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} \\[4pt]
&= -2 \times 96\,485 \times 2.96 \\[4pt]
&= -571\,191 \;\text{J mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Divide by molar mass to obtain specific energy in J kg⁻¹.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{M_{\text{reactants}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{571\,191}{0.04588} \\[4pt]
&= 12\,449\,585 \;\text{J kg}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Wh kg⁻¹ (1 Wh = 3 600 J).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\text{Wh/kg}} &= \frac{12\,449\,585}{3\,600} \\[4pt]
&= 3\,458 \;\text{Wh kg}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Discrepancy notice for students.** The figure most textbooks cite — **≈ 11 400 Wh kg⁻¹** — uses $n = 4$ (per mole of O₂) instead of $n = 2$. The most rigorous modern convention is:
> - **Per 2 Li + 1 O₂ (n = 2):** $\varepsilon \approx$ **3 458 Wh kg⁻¹** (specific energy per mole of O₂)
> - **Per 4 Li + 2 O₂ (n = 4):** $\varepsilon \approx$ **11 600 Wh kg⁻¹** (specific energy of the *full* balanced reaction mass)
>
> In KTU answer scripts, **explicitly state the stoichiometric basis** and you'll always be credited.

## 3.3 Derivation III — "Air-Breathing" Specific Energy (Oxygen Excluded)

When comparing Li–air to Li-ion on a **fair, practical basis** (since O₂ is "free" from atmosphere), we count only the lithium mass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\text{air}} &= \frac{-nFE^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{M_{\text{Li only}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{2 \times 96\,485 \times 2.96}{0.01388} \\[4pt]
&= 41\,150\,086 \;\text{J kg}^{-1} \\[4pt]
&= 11\,431 \;\text{Wh kg}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **"headline" number** ≈ **11 400 Wh kg⁻¹** that you should write in KTU 14-mark answers.

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Theoretical Specific Capacity of Li Metal

**Step 1 — Faraday's law.** Capacity per kilogram of Li = (Faraday constant) / (molar mass of Li), converted to Ah.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{\text{Li}} &= \frac{F}{3600 \cdot M_{\text{Li}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{96\,485}{3600 \times 0.00694} \\[4pt]
&= 3\,861 \;\text{Ah kg}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **universal Li-anode capacity number** (often quoted as **3 862 Ah kg⁻¹**).

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison With Competing Chemistries

| Metric | **Li–Air** | Li-ion (NMC) | Zn–Air | Pb-acid |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Theoretical specific energy | **11 400 Wh kg⁻¹** | 580 Wh kg⁻¹ | 1 350 Wh kg⁻¹ | 170 Wh kg⁻¹ |
| Practical specific energy | 200–800 Wh kg⁻¹ (lab) | 150–250 Wh kg⁻¹ | 350–500 Wh kg⁻¹ | 30–50 Wh kg⁻¹ |
| Nominal cell voltage | **2.96 V** | 3.6–3.7 V | 1.65 V | 2.0 V |
| Anode equivalent weight | **6.94 g mol⁻¹** | Graphite: 72 g mol⁻¹ | Zn: 65 g mol⁻¹ | Pb: 207 g mol⁻¹ |
| Rechargeable? | Yes (limited cycles) | Yes (1 000–5 000) | Hard | Yes |
| Energy density gap vs. gasoline | ≈ 1.1× | ≈ 0.02× | ≈ 0.1× | ≈ 0.005× |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cell-Level Functional Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LiAnode["Li METAL ANODE Stripping Oxidation Source of Li plus ions"]
    Electrolyte["ELECTROLYTE Lithium ion conductor Aprotic solvent or solid"]
    Cathode["POROUS CARBON CATHODE Three Phase Boundary Gas Liquid Solid"]
    AirIn["ATMOSPHERIC AIR INLET O2 molecules diffuse in"]
    AirOut["VENTED GAS OUTLET CO2 and H2O contamination rejected"]
    Product["DISCHARGE PRODUCT Li2O2 Solid Insoluble Stored in cathode pores"]
    Load["EXTERNAL LOAD OR CHARGER Electron path"]

    LiAnode -- Li plus ions migrate --> Electrolyte
    Electrolyte -- Li plus ions arrive --> Cathode
    AirIn -- ORR catalysis at triple point --> Cathode
    Cathode -- Insoluble deposit grows in pores --> Product
    LiAnode -- Electrons flow through circuit --> Load
    Load -- Returns on charge with OER --> Cathode
    Cathode -- O2 evolved back to atmosphere --> AirOut
```

## 4.2 Process Flow During One Discharge Half-Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Step 1 Li metal atom at anode"] --> B["Step 2 Li oxidised to Li plus and electron"]
    B --> C["Step 3 Li plus migrates through electrolyte"]
    C --> D["Step 4 O2 enters porous cathode from air"]
    D --> E["Step 5 ORR combines O2 Li plus and electrons at catalyst site"]
    E --> F["Step 6 Li2O2 solid deposits in cathode pores"]
    F --> G["Step 7 Electrons deliver energy to external load"]
```

## 4.3 Degradation Pathway Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Root["Li Air Cell Operating in Real Air"] --> C1["CO2 reacts with Li2O2 to form Li2CO3"]
    Root --> C2["H2O hydrolyses Li2O2 to LiOH and O2"]
    Root --> C3["O2 crossover attacks Li anode forms LiOH"]
    Root --> C4["Electrolyte decomposition from radicals superoxide O2 minus"]
    C1 --> Degrade["Capacity fade and voltage hysteresis"]
    C2 --> Degrade
    C3 --> Degrade
    C4 --> Degrade
```

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation tip for exam answers:** In KTU 14-mark questions on Li–air limitations, the **block diagram above** gives you a clean way to organise an answer into the four-failure-mode framework (carbonates, hydroxides, anode corrosion, electrolyte decay) — a structure examiners reward with full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

> **"Define the term Lithium-Air battery. Mention the overall cell reaction and theoretical open-circuit voltage for the aprotic configuration."**

### Model Answer (A1)

A Lithium-Air (Li–O₂) battery is a **rechargeable electrochemical cell** in which the **anode is metallic lithium** and the **cathode is a porous gas-diffusion electrode** that reduces **atmospheric oxygen** as the active material. Because the oxidiser is drawn from the environment, the cell has an exceptionally high theoretical specific energy.

**Overall cell reaction (aprotic, Li₂O₂):**

$$
2\text{Li}_{(s)} \;+\; \text{O}_{2\,(g)} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{Li}_{2}\text{O}_{2\,(s)}
$$

**Theoretical open-circuit voltage: E°cell ≈ 2.96 V.**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**

> **"List any four major technical challenges that currently prevent commercial deployment of rechargeable Lithium-Air batteries."**

### Model Answer (A2)

1. **Poor cycle life** — typically < 100 cycles due to cathode clogging by insulating Li₂O₂.
2. **Low round-trip efficiency** — large voltage gap between charge (~4.2 V) and discharge (~2.6 V) due to sluggish ORR/OER kinetics.
3. **Electrolyte instability** — superoxide (O₂⁻) radicals degrade organic carbonate/ether electrolytes.
4. **Atmospheric contamination** — CO₂ and H₂O from ambient air form Li₂CO₃ and LiOH, irreversibly consuming active lithium.
5. *(Optional 5th)* Dendritic Li growth on the anode during recharge, causing short circuits.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Provision (KTU ESE Pattern):**
> Answer **ONE** of the following 14-mark questions. Each contains two 7-mark sub-parts.

### Question B-A
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Adapted]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **aprotic Lithium-Air battery**. Write the half-reactions at the anode and cathode and the overall discharge reaction. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **theoretical specific energy** of the Li–Air cell (Li₂O₂ product) in **Wh kg⁻¹**, clearly stating all assumptions. Compare the result with a typical commercial Li-ion cell (≈ 200 Wh kg⁻¹) and comment on the engineering significance. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for B-A(a)

**Construction:** A Li–air cell is assembled in a sealed coin or pouch housing. It consists of:

1. **Lithium metal anode** — a thin foil (typical 50–200 µm) acting as the Li⁺ source.
2. **Porous carbon cathode** — a high-surface-area carbon (e.g., Super P, Ketjenblack, or graphene) loaded with an ORR/OER catalyst (Pt, Au, MnO₂, RuO₂). The pores must be **biphobic / hydrophilic tuned** to host both liquid electrolyte and gaseous O₂.
3. **Aprotic electrolyte** — a lithium-salt-in-organic-solvent (e.g., 1 M LiTFSI in TEGDME) soaked in a separator (glass fibre, polymer).
4. **Air-breathing window** — a gas-diffusion layer on the cathode face that admits O₂ while excluding H₂O and CO₂.

**Working (Discharge):**

At the **anode** (oxidation):

$$
\text{Li}_{(s)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Li}^{+}_{(sol)} \;+\; e^{-}
$$

At the **cathode** (ORR at triple-phase boundary):

$$
\text{O}_{2\,(g)} \;+\; 2\text{Li}^{+} \;+\; 2e^{-} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Li}_{2}\text{O}_{2\,(s)}
$$

**Overall:**

$$
2\text{Li} + \text{O}_{2} \rightarrow \text{Li}_{2}\text{O}_{2} \quad (E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = 2.96 \;\text{V})
$$

Li⁺ travels through the electrolyte; electrons flow through the external load; Li₂O₂ deposits inside the porous cathode (causing eventual pore clogging — a key failure mode).

#### Model Solution for B-A(b)

**Step 1 — Identify stoichiometry and electron count.**
Reaction: $2\text{Li} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{Li}_2\text{O}_2$. Electrons transferred per mole of O₂: $n = 2$.

**Step 2 — Molar mass of reactants.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{reactants}} &= 2 M_{\text{Li}} + M_{\text{O}_2} = 2(6.94) + 32.00 = 45.88 \;\text{g mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical EMF from half-cell potentials.**

$$
E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}} = E^{\circ}_{\text{O}_2/\text{Li}_2\text{O}_2} - E^{\circ}_{\text{Li}^+/\text{Li}} = -0.080 - (-3.040) = 2.96 \;\text{V}
$$

**Step 4 — Molar Gibbs free energy.**

$$
\Delta G^{\circ} = -nFE^{\circ} = -2 \times 96\,485 \times 2.96 = -571\,191 \;\text{J mol}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Specific energy in Wh kg⁻¹.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{-\Delta G^{\circ}}{M_{\text{reactants}} \times 3\,600} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{571\,191}{0.04588 \times 3\,600} \\[4pt]
&\approx 3\,458 \;\text{Wh kg}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Comparison.**

$$
\frac{\varepsilon_{\text{Li-air}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{Li-ion}}} = \frac{3\,458}{200} \approx 17.3\times
$$

> **Engineering significance:** Li–air theoretically stores **~17× more energy per kg** than commercial Li-ion, making it attractive for electric aviation and long-range EVs where weight is critical. *Caveat:* practical cells achieve only **200–800 Wh kg⁻¹** in labs due to parasitic mass (cathode, electrolyte, packaging) and incomplete O₂ utilisation.

---

### Question B-B
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Explain **four major failure mechanisms** that limit the cycle life of rechargeable Li–Air batteries, with reference to the chemical species involved. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Li–air cell is discharged at a constant current of **0.5 A** for **20 hours** at an average voltage of **2.6 V**. The mass of lithium consumed is **2.16 g**. Calculate:
> (i) the actual specific energy delivered, and (ii) the percentage utilisation of lithium. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for B-B(a)

1. **Cathode pore clogging by Li₂O₂** — Li₂O₂ is electronically insulating and insoluble; it accumulates in the porous carbon, blocks O₂ diffusion and deadens active sites, ending discharge prematurely.

2. **Electrolyte decomposition by superoxide radicals (O₂⁻)** — During ORR, intermediate superoxide attacks organic solvents (carbonates, ethers) via nucleophilic substitution, producing Li₂CO₃, HCO₂Li, and polyethers that clog the cell.

3. **Lithium anode corrosion by crossover O₂ and H₂O** — O₂ (and H₂O) crossing the electrolyte reach the Li metal anode, forming Li₂O, LiOH, and Li₂CO₃, irreversibly consuming the anode.

4. **Dendritic lithium deposition on charge** — On recharge, Li⁺ does not plate uniformly; it forms dendrites that may pierce the separator, causing internal short circuits and thermal runaway.

5. *(Optional 5th)* **Atmospheric CO₂ poisoning** — CO₂ reacts with Li₂O₂ to form Li₂CO₃, a poorly reversible product.

#### Model Solution for B-B(b)

**Step 1 — Energy delivered.**

$$
E = V \cdot I \cdot t = 2.6 \times 0.5 \times 20 = 26 \;\text{Wh}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass of Li consumed = 2.16 g = 0.00216 kg.**

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{actual}} = \frac{26 \;\text{Wh}}{0.00216 \;\text{kg}} = 12\,037 \;\text{Wh kg}^{-1}
$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical specific energy of Li alone (Li is the limiting reagent at the anode):**

$$
\varepsilon_{\text{theor}} = \frac{F \cdot E^{\circ}_{\text{cell}}}{3\,600 \cdot M_{\text{Li}}} = \frac{96\,485 \times 2.96}{3\,600 \times 0.00694} = 11\,431 \;\text{Wh kg}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4 — Percentage utilisation of lithium.**

$$
\eta_{\text{Li}} = \frac{\varepsilon_{\text{actual}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{theor}}} \times 100\% = \frac{12\,037}{11\,431} \times 100\% \approx 105.3\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity-check note for students:** A value > 100 % in this calculation indicates that **some energy contribution is being counted from O₂** (mass = 0 assumed). For a fair utilisation figure, compare only on a **per-Li basis using E° (2.96 V)** against the average delivered voltage (2.6 V), not against the ideal specific energy number. The voltage efficiency itself is **2.6 / 2.96 ≈ 87.8 %** — this is the more meaningful metric.

**Voltage (round-trip-ish) efficiency:**

$$
\eta_{V} = \frac{V_{\text{discharge}}}{E^{\circ}} = \frac{2.6}{2.96} \times 100\% \approx 87.8\%
$$

This shows that even in ideal chemistry, **12 % of energy is lost to polarisation/overpotentials** in real Li–air operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT mix units** mid-calculation. Always convert grams → kg before dividing Wh, or you will get answers 1 000× too large and lose 3–4 marks.
> - **Always quote n explicitly** in specific-energy derivations. If $n$ is omitted, the examiner will mark the derivation step as incomplete.
> - **Never cite the Li–air voltage as 3.7 V** (that's Li-ion's voltage). The correct aprotic OCV is **2.96 V**.
> - **Do not confuse "specific energy" with "energy density."** The former is Wh/kg, the latter is Wh/L.
> - In Part A 3-mark questions, **draw the cell diagram** — KTU examiners allot ~1 mark for a labelled schematic even when not explicitly asked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Li–Air = Li metal anode + porous carbon cathode that breathes O₂ from air; product is Li₂O₂ in aprotic electrolytes.
- **Theoretical OCV:** **2.96 V** (Li₂O₂ pathway).
- **Net reaction:** $2\text{Li} + \text{O}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{Li}_2\text{O}_2$.
- **Theoretical specific energy:** ≈ **11 400 Wh kg⁻¹** (when O₂ mass is included) or **3 458 Wh kg⁻¹** (per mole O₂ basis) — **state your assumption explicitly.**
- **Faraday constant:** $F = 96\,485$ C mol⁻¹ — always use this exact number.
- **Li anode theoretical capacity:** **3 861 Ah kg⁻¹** — universal constant.
- **Half-reactions to memorise:**
   * Anode (discharge): $\text{Li} \rightarrow \text{Li}^{+} + e^{-}$
   * Cathode (discharge, ORR): $\text{O}_2 + 2\text{Li}^{+} + 2e^{-} \rightarrow \text{Li}_2\text{O}_2$
- **Four failure modes (very high-yield for KTU):** pore clogging · superoxide electrolyte attack · Li anode corrosion · dendrite formation.
- **Practical efficiency:** current lab cells achieve **200–800 Wh kg⁻¹** with < 100 cycles and **60–75 %** round-trip efficiency.
- **Comparison anchor:** Li–air is theoretically **~17–45×** more energetic than commercial Li-ion depending on basis — memorise this ratio.
- **Environmental appeal:** Uses cheap, abundant carbon + lithium + atmospheric O₂ — no cobalt, no nickel.
- **Why not yet commercial?** Cycle life, efficiency, and air-purity sensitivity remain unsolved at the materials level.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nickel Cadmium
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nickel Cadmium

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Nickel-Cadmium (Ni-Cd) battery** is a rechargeable (secondary) electrochemical energy storage device that operates on an alkaline electrolyte (typically potassium hydroxide, KOH) with a nickel hydroxide cathode [NiO(OH)] and a metallic cadmium anode. It is classified as a **secondary alkaline battery** and represents one of the earliest commercially successful rechargeable battery chemistries, introduced commercially by **Waldemar Jungner in 1899**.

The standard nominal cell voltage is **1.2 V**, with an open-circuit voltage (OCV) ranging between **1.25 V (discharged)** and **1.35 V (fully charged)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Ni-Cd batteries are studied under *Electrochemical Energy Storage Systems* because of their robustness, high cycle life (≥ 2000 cycles), and ability to deliver high discharge currents, making them critical for aerospace, emergency backup, and traction applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **rechargeable water tank system**:
- The **cadmium electrode (anode)** is like the *outlet pipe* that releases water (electrons) when the system supplies power.
- The **nickel hydroxide electrode (cathode)** is like the *inlet reservoir* that accepts the electrons during discharge.
- The **KOH electrolyte** is the *connecting pipework* — it only transports ions (not water), but never gets consumed.
- When you recharge (pump water back), the direction reverses and the system restores its original state.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Ni-Cd?**
> Because both nickel and cadmium can reversibly switch oxidation states in alkaline media, the cell can be **discharged and recharged hundreds to thousands of times** without significant degradation — unlike a regular dry cell which dies after one use.

## 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Nominal Cell Voltage | **1.2 V** |
| Specific Energy | **40 – 60 Wh/kg** |
| Energy Density | **50 – 150 Wh/L** |
| Operating Temperature | **−20 °C to +60 °C** |
| Cycle Life | **> 2000 cycles** |
| Self-Discharge Rate | **~10 % per month** |
| Internal Resistance | **Very Low (≈ 10–40 mΩ)** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Construction of a Ni-Cd Cell

A Ni-Cd cell consists of three main functional components:

1. **Positive Electrode (Cathode):** Nickel hydroxide — Ni(OH)₂ in discharged state, converted to NiOOH (nickel oxy-hydroxide) when charged.
2. **Negative Electrode (Anode):** Metallic cadmium (Cd) in discharged state, converted to Cd(OH)₂ when discharged.
3. **Electrolyte:** Aqueous **potassium hydroxide (KOH)** solution of concentration ~20–30 % by weight, with a small addition of LiOH to improve cycle life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Construction Variants:**
> - **Sealed (Sintered/Pocket Plate):** Used in portable electronics and aerospace.
> - **Vented (Flooded):** Used in aviation, railway signalling, and standby power.
> - **Pocket Plate Type:** Used for high discharge rate applications like diesel engine starting.

## 2.2 Working Principle — Discharge & Charge Reactions

### Discharge Reactions

During discharge (battery supplies current), the following reactions occur:

- **At the Anode (Oxidation, Cd → Cd²⁺):**
Cd + 2OH⁻ → Cd(OH)₂ + 2e⁻

- **At the Cathode (Reduction, NiOOH → Ni(OH)₂):**
2NiOOH + 2H₂O + 2e⁻ → 2Ni(OH)₂ + 2OH⁻

- **Net Cell Reaction (Discharge):**
Cd + 2NiOOH + 2H₂O → Cd(OH)₂ + 2Ni(OH)₂

### Charge Reactions

During charge (current forced into the battery), the reactions reverse exactly:

- **At the Anode (Reduction):**
Cd(OH)₂ + 2e⁻ → Cd + 2OH⁻

- **At the Cathode (Oxidation):**
2Ni(OH)₂ + 2OH⁻ → 2NiOOH + 2H₂O + 2e⁻

- **Net Cell Reaction (Charge):**
Cd(OH)₂ + 2Ni(OH)₂ → Cd + 2NiOOH + 2H₂O

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The **electrolyte (KOH) does NOT participate in the overall reaction** — only its ions serve as charge carriers. This is why electrolyte specific gravity remains nearly constant, eliminating the need for electrolyte-level monitoring in sealed Ni-Cd cells.

## 2.3 Voltage Characteristics

The voltage profile during discharge is remarkably **flat**, hovering near **1.2 V for over 80 % of the discharge cycle**. This flatness makes Ni-Cd cells ideal for applications requiring stable voltage under varying load.

## 2.4 The Memory Effect (A KTU Favourite!)

The **Memory Effect** (also called *voltage depression* or *lazy battery effect*) is a phenomenon where a Ni-Cd cell that is repeatedly only partially discharged before recharging **"remembers" the shallower discharge depth** and delivers progressively less capacity over time.

**Mechanism:** Crystalline cadmium hydroxide grows into large, stable structures that are harder to re-dissolve during discharge, reducing active surface area.

> [!WARNING]
> **Exam Pitfall:** Memory Effect occurs ONLY in Ni-Cd (and Ni-MH), NOT in lead-acid or lithium batteries. Full discharge cycles periodically (e.g., once every 10 cycles) reverse the effect.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula / Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Nominal EMF | $E_{cell} = E_{cathode} - E_{anode} \approx 1.2$ | V |
| Specific Energy | $E_s = \dfrac{V \times Q}{m}$ | Wh/kg |
| Energy Density | $E_v = \dfrac{V \times Q}{V_{cell}}$ | Wh/L |
| Coulombic Efficiency | $\eta_C = \dfrac{Q_{discharge}}{Q_{charge}} \times 100$ | % |
| Energy Efficiency | $\eta_E = \dfrac{V_{avg,dis} \times Q_{dis}}{V_{avg,ch} \times Q_{ch}} \times 100$ | % |
| State of Charge (SoC) | $SoC = \dfrac{Q_{remaining}}{Q_{rated}} \times 100$ | % |
| Depth of Discharge | $DoD = 100 - SoC$ | % |
| Self-Discharge (per month) | $Q_{lost} = Q_0 \times e^{-k t}$ | Ah |
| Capacity (Peukert-adjusted) | $C_p = I^n \times t$ (for lead-acid; Ni-Cd follows near-ideal) | Ah |
| Charge Time (typical) | $t_{charge} = \dfrac{C_{rated}}{I_{charge}}$ | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For Ni-Cd batteries, the **standard charge rate is C/10 for 14–16 hours**, while a fast charge can be done at **C/3 to 1C** with controlled voltage cutoff.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Cell EMF from Half-Cell Potentials

The Ni-Cd cell EMF can be derived from the standard reduction potentials of the half-cells.

**Step 1 — Identify Standard Reduction Potentials:**

At the cathode (NiOOH / Ni(OH)₂ couple):
$$
E^{\circ}_{NiOOH/Ni(OH)_2} = +0.49 \text{ V vs SHE}
$$

At the anode (Cd(OH)₂ / Cd couple):
$$
E^{\circ}_{Cd(OH)_2/Cd} = -0.81 \text{ V vs SHE}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Nernst-Derived Cell EMF Equation:**

$$
E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values:**

$$
E^{\circ}_{cell} = (+0.49) - (-0.81)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate:**

$$
E^{\circ}_{cell} = 1.30 \text{ V (theoretical)}
$$

The experimentally observed nominal voltage is **1.2 V** due to internal resistance, over-potentials, and concentration polarization.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the most frequently asked analytical question in KTU 2024 Energy Systems exams for this topic.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Capacity & Energy of a Ni-Cd Battery Pack

**Problem:** A Ni-Cd battery pack consists of **10 cells in series**. Each cell has a rated capacity of **5 Ah** and a nominal voltage of **1.2 V**. Calculate the (a) total pack voltage, (b) total pack capacity, (c) total stored energy, and (d) specific energy if the pack weighs **0.9 kg**.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**(a) Total Pack Voltage (Series Connection):**

For cells in series, voltages add while current remains constant.

$$
V_{pack} = N_{series} \times V_{cell}
$$

$$
V_{pack} = 10 \times 1.2 = 12.0 \text{ V}
$$

**(b) Total Pack Capacity (Series Connection):**

For series connection, capacity is the same as a single cell.

$$
Q_{pack} = Q_{cell} = 5 \text{ Ah}
$$

**(c) Total Stored Energy:**

Energy is computed as the product of voltage and charge capacity.

$$
E_{pack} = V_{pack} \times Q_{pack}
$$

$$
E_{pack} = 12.0 \times 5 = 60 \text{ Wh}
$$

**(d) Specific Energy:**

Specific energy divides total energy by total mass.

$$
E_{s} = \dfrac{E_{pack}}{m_{pack}}
$$

$$
E_{s} = \dfrac{60}{0.9} = 66.67 \text{ Wh/kg}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Interpretation:** A specific energy of **66.67 Wh/kg** falls comfortably within the typical Ni-Cd range of **40–60 Wh/kg** (slight overshoot indicates premium-grade cells or ideal conditions).

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: Ni-Cd vs Other Batteries

| Property | **Ni-Cd** | **Lead-Acid** | **Ni-MH** | **Li-Ion** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Nominal Voltage (V) | **1.2** | 2.0 | 1.2 | 3.6–3.7 |
| Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | **40–60** | 30–40 | 60–120 | 100–250 |
| Cycle Life | **2000+** | 300–500 | 500–1000 | 500–2000 |
| Memory Effect | **YES (Severe)** | NO | Mild | NO |
| Self-Discharge / Month | **10 %** | 4–6 % | 30 % | 2–3 % |
| Toxicity | **HIGH (Cd)** | HIGH (Pb) | Low | Low |
| Cost | **Medium** | Low | Medium | High |
| Operating Temp Range | **−20 to +60 °C** | −15 to +50 °C | −20 to +45 °C | −20 to +60 °C |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — Ni-Cd Cell Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Load] -->|Discharge Current| B[Cell Terminal -]
    B --> C[Cadmium Anode -ve]
    C --> D[Alkaline KOH Electrolyte]
    D --> E[Nickel Hydroxide Cathode +ve]
    E --> F[Cell Terminal +]
    F --> A
    G[DC Charger] -.->|Charge Current| F
    G -.-> B
    H[Electron Flow during Discharge] -.-> C
    I[OH- Ion Migration] -.-> D
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Charge/Discharge Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start] --> P2{Operation Mode?}
    P2 -->|Discharge| Q1[Cd oxidized to CdOH2]
    Q1 --> Q2[NiOOH reduced to NiOH2]
    Q2 --> Q3[Electrons flow via external load]
    Q3 --> R1[Voltage drops from 1.35V to 1.0V]
    R1 --> S1[Cutoff at 1.0V per cell]
    P2 -->|Charge| Q4[External DC source applied]
    Q4 --> Q5[CdOH2 reduced to Cd]
    Q5 --> Q6[NiOH2 oxidized to NiOOH]
    Q6 --> R2[Voltage rises to 1.45V]
    R2 --> S2[Termination via -dV detection or timer]
    S1 --> T[End of Cycle]
    S2 --> T
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Ni-Cd Battery Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BAT[(Ni-Cd Battery Pack)] --> BMS[Battery Management System]
    BMS -->|Voltage Monitoring| Vmon[Voltage Sensor]
    BMS -->|Current Monitoring| Imon[Current Shunt]
    BMS -->|Temperature Monitoring| Tmon[Thermal Sensor]
    BMS -->|Cell Balancing| Cbal[Equalizer Circuit]
    BMS --> PROT[Protection Switch]
    PROT --> OUT[DC Load / Inverter]
    IN[DC Charger / Solar MPPT] --> BMS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Insight:** Notice how the electrolyte (KOH) is the *bridge* between both electrodes — it only carries OH⁻ ions and never gets consumed. This makes Ni-Cd batteries *maintenance-free* in sealed configurations.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Nickel-Cadmium battery and state its nominal voltage. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer:**
A Nickel-Cadmium (Ni-Cd) battery is a rechargeable alkaline secondary cell that uses **nickel hydroxide as the positive electrode (cathode)**, **metallic cadmium as the negative electrode (anode)**, and an **aqueous potassium hydroxide (KOH) solution as the electrolyte**.

**Nominal Cell Voltage = 1.2 V.**

### Question 2: What is the Memory Effect in Ni-Cd batteries? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**
The Memory Effect is a phenomenon in which a Ni-Cd battery, when repeatedly subjected to *shallow discharge cycles* before recharging, gradually **"remembers" the partial discharge depth** and progressively delivers a *lower usable capacity* than its rated value. It occurs due to the growth of large, stable cadmium hydroxide crystals on the negative electrode that are difficult to dissolve during subsequent discharge.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Choose ONE)

### Question A (14 Marks): Ni-Cd Battery Construction, Working, and Merits [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

#### Part (a) — With the help of neat sketches, describe the construction and working of a Nickel-Cadmium cell. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

A Ni-Cd cell is constructed using two dissimilar electrodes immersed in an alkaline electrolyte:

1. **Positive Electrode (Cathode):** Made of nickel hydroxide Ni(OH)₂ mixed with graphite or nickel flakes for conductivity. The active material is held in a nickel-plated steel grid or in sintered nickel plaques for sealed types.
2. **Negative Electrode (Anode):** Made of metallic cadmium, often blended with iron oxide or graphite to enhance conductivity. Active material is impregnated in sintered plaques for sealed cells.
3. **Electrolyte:** Aqueous KOH (~30 % w/w) with small additions of LiOH for cycle life enhancement. It acts purely as an ionic conductor — its specific gravity remains essentially unchanged during operation.
4. **Separator:** A porous nylon or polypropylene sheet that physically separates the two electrodes while permitting ionic flow.
5. **Container & Vent (for vented types):** Nickel-plated steel casing with a resealable vent to release overpressure during fast charging.

**Working — Discharge Mode:**

When a load is connected, oxidation occurs at the cadmium anode releasing electrons, which travel through the external circuit and re-enter at the nickel cathode where reduction takes place. The OH⁻ ions migrate through the electrolyte to balance charge.

$$
Cd + 2OH^- \rightarrow Cd(OH)_2 + 2e^- \quad \text{(Anode)}
$$

$$
2NiOOH + 2H_2O + 2e^- \rightarrow 2Ni(OH)_2 + 2OH^- \quad \text{(Cathode)}
$$

**Working — Charge Mode:**

When an external DC source is applied, the reactions reverse: cadmium hydroxide is reduced back to metallic cadmium, and nickel hydroxide is oxidized back to nickel oxy-hydroxide.

#### Part (b) — Discuss the advantages, disadvantages, and applications of Ni-Cd batteries. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages:**
1. **Long cycle life** (2000+ cycles) — far superior to lead-acid.
2. **Wide operating temperature range** (−20 °C to +60 °C).
3. **Robust construction** — tolerant to overcharge, deep discharge, and mechanical shock.
4. **Low internal resistance** — enables high discharge current (up to 20C).
5. **Flat discharge voltage curve** — provides stable output voltage.
6. **Long shelf life** when stored in any state of charge.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Memory effect** — capacity loss under partial discharge cycling.
2. **Toxicity of cadmium** — environmental hazard; restricted under EU RoHS directive.
3. **Lower specific energy** compared to Ni-MH and Li-ion.
4. **Higher self-discharge** rate than Li-ion.
5. **Higher cost** than lead-acid equivalents.

**Applications:**
1. **Aerospace** — emergency power units (EPU) for aircraft and satellites.
2. **Railway Signalling & Locomotive Starting** — robust and reliable.
3. **Emergency Lighting & UPS Systems** — standby power.
4. **Portable Power Tools** — high discharge capability.
5. **Two-way Radios & Medical Equipment** — historically dominant.
6. **Military and Defence Equipment** — temperature-tolerant and rugged.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Electrochemistry, Voltage Characteristics, and Comparison [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

#### Part (a) — Derive the EMF of a Ni-Cd cell from its half-cell potentials and discuss its discharge characteristics. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Half-Cell Reactions (Standard Reduction Potentials vs SHE):**

- Cathode: $NiOOH + H_2O + e^- \rightarrow Ni(OH)_2 + OH^-$, $\quad E^{\circ} = +0.49$ V
- Anode: $Cd(OH)_2 + 2e^- \rightarrow Cd + 2OH^-$, $\quad E^{\circ} = -0.81$ V

**Net Cell EMF:**

$$
E^{\circ}_{cell} = E^{\circ}_{cathode} - E^{\circ}_{anode}
$$

$$
E^{\circ}_{cell} = (+0.49) - (-0.81) = 1.30 \text{ V}
$$

**Observed Nominal Voltage = 1.2 V** (slightly lower due to overpotentials and internal resistance).

**Discharge Characteristics:**
- Open-circuit voltage (OCV) of a fully charged cell ≈ 1.35 V.
- OCV of a fully discharged cell ≈ 1.0 V.
- The discharge curve is **remarkably flat** between **1.25 V and 1.15 V** for ~80 % of capacity.
- Voltage drops sharply only at the end of discharge (below 1.1 V).

#### Part (b) — Compare Ni-Cd batteries with Lead-Acid and Lithium-Ion batteries on the basis of specific energy, cycle life, and environmental impact. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

| Criterion | Ni-Cd | Lead-Acid | Li-Ion |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Specific Energy** | 40–60 Wh/kg | 30–40 Wh/kg | 100–250 Wh/kg |
| **Cycle Life** | 2000+ | 300–500 | 500–2000 |
| **Energy Efficiency** | 70–80 % | 75–85 % | 90–95 % |
| **Memory Effect** | Severe | None | None |
| **Toxicity / Environmental Impact** | High (Cd is carcinogenic) | High (Pb toxicity, acid spills) | Low to Moderate (electrolyte flammable) |
| **Cost per kWh** | Medium | Low | High |
| **Temperature Range** | −20 to +60 °C | −15 to +50 °C | −20 to +60 °C |

**Summary Verdict:**
- **Ni-Cd** wins on **robustness, cycle life, and low-temperature operation**, but loses on **specific energy and environmental concerns**.
- **Lead-Acid** wins on **cost** but is inferior in cycle life and energy density.
- **Li-Ion** dominates in **specific energy, energy efficiency, and absence of memory effect**, but requires complex BMS and is sensitive to overcharge.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Ni-Cd with Ni-MH:** Ni-MH uses a hydrogen-absorbing alloy anode, not cadmium — memory effect is mild but not as severe as Ni-Cd.
> 2. **Forgetting the electrolyte's role:** Many students wrongly state that KOH is "consumed" during discharge — actually KOH is a *catalyst-grade carrier*; its concentration remains unchanged.
> 3. **Wrong EMF calculation:** The cathode potential is taken as **+0.49 V**, and the anode as **−0.81 V**. Many students confuse signs and obtain a negative value.
> 4. **Omitting applications:** A 14-mark answer without real-world applications (aerospace, railway, UPS) typically loses 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the Memory Effect:** A defining feature of Ni-Cd — failing to mention it in either definition or comparison is a guaranteed mark loss.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Ni-Cd** = *Nickel hydroxide cathode + Cadmium anode + KOH electrolyte*.
- ✅ **Nominal voltage = 1.2 V**, theoretical EMF = **1.30 V**.
- ✅ **Overall discharge reaction:** $Cd + 2NiOOH + 2H_2O \rightarrow Cd(OH)_2 + 2Ni(OH)_2$.
- ✅ **Specific energy:** **40–60 Wh/kg**.
- ✅ **Cycle life:** **> 2000 cycles** (highest among conventional rechargeables).
- ✅ **Memory Effect** is *unique to Ni-Cd* (severe) and Ni-MH (mild); fixable by full discharge cycles.
- ✅ **Electrolyte (KOH) is unchanged** during operation — no need for water topping in sealed cells.
- ✅ **Operating temperature:** **−20 °C to +60 °C** — exceptional cold-weather performance.
- ✅ **Major applications:** Aerospace EPU, railway signalling, UPS, emergency lighting, portable tools.
- ✅ **Cadmium toxicity** is the primary reason for declining use in consumer electronics (RoHS-restricted).
- ✅ **Comparison shortcut:** Ni-Cd = *robust + long-cycle + low-temp tolerant*; loses on energy density and environment.
- ✅ **Charge rule:** C/10 for 14–16 hours; fast charge at 1C with ΔV cutoff; never overcharge sealed types beyond 1.45 V/cell.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nickel Metal Hydride Fuel Cell: Working Principle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Nickel Metal Hydride Fuel Cell: Working Principle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Technical Clarification: Battery vs. Fuel Cell

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Terminology Note for KTU Examiners:** The Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH) system is technically a **rechargeable (secondary) electrochemical cell (battery)**, not a true fuel cell in the conventional sense (like PEMFC, SOFC, AFC). It is grouped with fuel cell/advanced energy storage modules in the KTU 2024 Energy Systems syllabus because it shares **alkaline electrolyte electrochemistry** principles and **hydrogen-mediated energy conversion** pathways. Examiners expect the student to discuss its **electrochemical working principle** as a reversible hydrogen storage system.

## 1.2 Formal KTU-Standard Definition

The **Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH) cell** is a secondary (rechargeable) alkaline electrochemical energy storage device in which the positive electrode (cathode during discharge) is **nickel hydroxide [NiO(OH)]** and the negative electrode (anode during discharge) is a **hydrogen-absorbing intermetallic alloy (MH)**. The electrolyte is a **concentrated aqueous solution of potassium hydroxide (KOH), typically 20–30% w/w, with a small addition of LiOH**. The nominal cell voltage is **1.2 V**, and energy density ranges from **60–120 Wh/kg**.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Hydrogen Sponge"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Rechargeable Hydrogen Sponge**
> Imagine a sponge that soaks up hydrogen gas when you pour water (charge it), and slowly releases that hydrogen to do work (discharge it) — without ever producing free hydrogen gas. The negative electrode in NiMH is literally a **metallic alloy sponge (AB₅ type, e.g., LaNi₅ or MmNi₅ where Mm = Mischmetal)** that absorbs hydrogen atoms into its crystal lattice to form a **metal hydride (MH)**. The positive electrode cycles between nickel hydroxide and nickel oxyhydroxide. The KOH electrolyte acts as the **ionic highway** for OH⁻ ions to shuttle between the two electrodes.

## 1.4 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| **Nominal Cell Voltage** | **1.2 V** |
| **Theoretical Specific Energy** | **≈ 240 Wh/kg** (practical: 60–120 Wh/kg) |
| **Electrolyte** | **KOH (20–30% aqueous)** |
| **Operating Temperature** | **−20 °C to +45 °C** |
| **Anode Alloy (Hydrogen Storage)** | **AB₅ (LaNi₅)** or **AB₂ (ZrNi₂)** type |
| **Faraday Constant (F)** | **96,485 C/mol** |
| **Cycle Life** | **500–2000 cycles** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electrochemistry of the NiMH Cell

The NiMH cell is based on a **reversible electrochemical reaction** between nickel hydroxide and a metal hydride. The cell operates via the **transport of hydroxyl ions (OH⁻)** through the alkaline electrolyte.

### 2.1.1 The Core Active Species

- **Positive Electrode (Cathode during discharge):** Nickel hydroxide / Nickel oxyhydroxide redox couple
  $$\text{Ni(OH)}_2 \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{NiO(OH)} + \text{H}^+ + e^-$$
  In alkaline medium, this is rewritten using OH⁻:
  $$\text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^- \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{NiO(OH)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^-$$

- **Negative Electrode (Anode during discharge):** Metal hydride / Metal alloy redox couple
  $$\text{MH} + \text{OH}^- \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^-$$
  Or equivalently:
  $$\text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{MH} + \text{OH}^-$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:** Notice that during discharge, **no net water or KOH is consumed**. The water formed at the positive electrode is consumed at the negative electrode, and vice versa during charge. This makes NiMH a **sealed, maintenance-free** system — a major advantage over NiCd and lead-acid cells.

## 2.2 Net Cell Reaction

The overall cell reaction (reversible) is:

$$\text{NiO(OH)} + \text{MH} \;\underset{\text{charge}}{\overset{\text{discharge}}{\rightleftharpoons}}\; \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{M}$$

### Verification of Net Reaction:
Summing the two half-reactions (electrons cancel):

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Cathode:} \quad & \text{NiO(OH)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^- \\
\text{Anode:} \quad & \text{MH} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \\
\hline
\text{Net:} \quad & \text{NiO(OH)} + \text{MH} \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{M}
\end{aligned}$$

This confirms the **closed-loop reversibility** of the NiMH chemistry.

## 2.3 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Operation

### 2.3.1 During DISCHARGE (Energy Release)

1. **At the Negative Electrode (Anode):** The metal hydride (MH) is oxidized. Hydrogen leaves the alloy lattice and combines with OH⁻ to form water, releasing one electron per hydrogen atom.
   $$\text{MH} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^-$$

2. **Electron Flow:** Electrons travel through the external circuit from anode to cathode, providing **electrical work** to the load (this is the "useful" current).

3. **Ion Migration:** Hydroxyl ions (OH⁻) migrate through the KOH electrolyte from the negative electrode to the positive electrode to maintain charge balance.

4. **At the Positive Electrode (Cathode):** NiO(OH) is reduced by the incoming electrons and OH⁻ ions to form Ni(OH)₂.
   $$\text{NiO(OH)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^-$$

5. **Net Effect:** The hydrogen stored in the metal alloy is "transferred" (in ionic form via OH⁻) to the nickel electrode, and electrical energy is liberated.

### 2.3.2 During CHARGE (Energy Storage)

1. **At the Positive Electrode (Anode during charge):** Ni(OH)₂ is oxidized back to NiO(OH) with the release of one electron and one water molecule.
   $$\text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{NiO(OH)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^-$$

2. **At the Negative Electrode (Cathode during charge):** The metal alloy (M) absorbs hydrogen generated by water reduction, reforming the metal hydride.
   $$\text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \rightarrow \text{MH} + \text{OH}^-$$

3. **Electron Flow:** External power source (charger) drives electrons from positive to negative terminal, forcing the reverse reaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why It Is Reversible:** The metal alloy (e.g., LaNi₅) has the unique property of **reversibly absorbing and desorbing large quantities of hydrogen** at ambient temperature and pressure. This is the heart of NiMH technology — it is essentially a **solid-state hydrogen storage** device used electrochemically.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$ | Cell EMF (Standard) | **V (Volts)** |
| 2 | $E^0_{NiO(OH)/Ni(OH)_2} = +0.49$ V vs. SHE | Standard potential of Ni electrode | V |
| 3 | $E^0_{OH^-/H_2} = -0.83$ V vs. SHE | Standard potential of MH electrode | V |
| 4 | $E^0_{cell} = 0.49 - (-0.83) = 1.32$ V (theoretical) | Theoretical open-circuit voltage | V |
| 5 | $V_{nominal} \approx 1.2$ V | Practical nominal voltage | V |
| 6 | $Q = n \cdot F \cdot m / M$ | Theoretical capacity (n = electrons) | **C (Coulombs)** or **Ah** |
| 7 | $E_{specific} = \frac{V \times Q}{m}$ | Specific energy | **Wh/kg** |
| 8 | $\eta = \frac{E_{discharge}}{E_{charge}} \times 100\%$ | Coulombic / Energy efficiency | **%** |
| 9 | $P = V \times I$ | Power delivered | **W (Watts)** |
| 10 | $W = \int V \, dQ$ | Energy delivered (area under V-Q curve) | **Wh** or **J** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Use `\vert` not `|` in equations:** For absolute value or magnitude, write $\vert x \vert$ — never the raw pipe character which breaks markdown tables.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs):** Toyota Prius, Honda Insight (first-generation NiMH packs).
- **Consumer Electronics:** Rechargeable AA/AAA batteries.
- **Spacecraft Power Systems:** Combined with solar PV for LEO/MEO satellites.
- **Emergency Power Backup:** UPS systems for telecom and data centers.
- **Medical Devices:** Power sources for portable diagnostic equipment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Open-Circuit Voltage of NiMH Cell

### Step 1 — Identify the Half-Cell Potentials (Standard Conditions, 25 °C, 1 M)

**Positive Electrode Half-Reaction (Cathode, discharge):**
$$\text{NiO(OH)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^-$$
$$E^0_{+} = +0.49 \text{ V vs. SHE}$$

**Negative Electrode Half-Reaction (Anode, discharge):**
$$\text{MH} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^-$$
$$E^0_{-} = -0.83 \text{ V vs. SHE}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the Nernst-Derived Cell EMF Equation

The standard cell EMF is given by:
$$E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute the Values

$$\begin{aligned}
E^0_{cell} &= E^0_{+} - E^0_{-} \\
&= (+0.49) - (-0.83) \\
&= 0.49 + 0.83 \\
&= 1.32 \text{ V}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 4 — Compare with Practical Voltage

The **theoretical** open-circuit voltage of **1.32 V** is reduced in practice to a **nominal 1.2 V** due to:
- Internal resistance (IR drop) under load
- Polarization losses (activation + concentration)
- Purity and state-of-charge of the active materials
- Cell temperature variation

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic Note:** The reduction step adds the magnitude of the negative electrode potential because subtracting a negative value is mathematically equivalent to adding its absolute value. This is a **frequently examined KTU step** — always show the sign convention explicitly.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Theoretical Specific Energy of the NiMH Cell

### Step 1 — Theoretical Capacity of the Positive Electrode (Ni(OH)₂)

The relevant reaction involves **one electron per Ni atom** (n = 1).

$$Q_{Ni(OH)_2} = \frac{n \cdot F}{M_{Ni(OH)_2}}$$

where:
- $n = 1$ electron
- $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (Faraday constant)
- $M_{Ni(OH)_2} = 92.71$ g/mol (molar mass)

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{Ni(OH)_2} &= \frac{1 \times 96{,}485}{92.71} \text{ C/g} \\
&= 1040.7 \text{ C/g} \\
&= \frac{1040.7}{3600} \text{ Ah/g} \\
&= 0.289 \text{ Ah/g}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 2 — Convert Capacity × Voltage to Specific Energy

$$E_{specific} = V_{nominal} \times Q_{theoretical}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
E_{specific} &= 1.32 \text{ V} \times 289 \text{ mAh/g} \\
&\approx 381 \text{ mWh/g} \\
&= 381 \text{ Wh/kg} \;(\text{per gram of Ni(OH)}_2 \text{ basis})
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 3 — Account for Practical Cell Mass

Real NiMH cells include inert components (current collectors, separator, casing, electrolyte). The **practical cell-level specific energy** is therefore:
$$E_{practical} = \eta_{packaging} \times E_{theoretical} \approx 0.25 \times 381 \approx 95 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

This matches the experimentally observed range of **60–120 Wh/kg**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Logic:** A specific energy value computed per unit active mass is always **higher** than the cell-level specific energy, because the cell mass includes non-active structural components. The packaging factor ($\eta_{packaging}$) typically falls between 0.20 and 0.35 for commercial NiMH cells.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis Table

| Parameter | NiMH | NiCd | Lead-Acid | Li-ion (for context) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Voltage (V)** | 1.2 | 1.2 | 2.0 | 3.6–3.7 |
| **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** | 60–120 | 40–60 | 30–50 | 150–250 |
| **Cycle Life** | 500–2000 | 1000–2000 | 300–800 | 500–3000 |
| **Memory Effect** | Mild | **Severe** | None | None |
| **Toxicity** | Low (no Cd) | **High (Cd)** | **High (Pb)** | Low |
| **Self-Discharge/Month** | 20–30% | 10–15% | 4–6% | 2–3% |
| **Operating Temp (°C)** | −20 to +45 | −20 to +45 | −15 to +45 | −20 to +60 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** The comparison between NiMH, NiCd, and Lead-Acid is a high-weightage question (often Part A 3-mark or Part B 14-mark). Memorize the nominal voltages and specific energy values — they are **directly testable**.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

> **Problem (Typical KTU Pattern):** A NiMH cell rated at 1.2 V delivers a steady current of 2.5 A for 3 hours during discharge. Calculate (a) the total charge transferred, (b) the energy delivered, and (c) the specific energy if the cell mass is 50 g.

### Solution:

**Part (a): Total Charge**
$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= I \times t \\
&= 2.5 \text{ A} \times 3 \text{ hr} \\
&= 7.5 \text{ Ah} \\
&= 7.5 \times 3600 \text{ C} \\
&= 27{,}000 \text{ C}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b): Energy Delivered**
$$\begin{aligned}
W &= V \times Q \\
&= 1.2 \text{ V} \times 7.5 \text{ Ah} \\
&= 9.0 \text{ Wh} \\
&= 9.0 \times 3600 \text{ J} \\
&= 32{,}400 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (c): Specific Energy**
$$\begin{aligned}
E_{specific} &= \frac{W}{m} = \frac{9.0 \text{ Wh}}{0.050 \text{ kg}} \\
&= 180 \text{ Wh/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

This value is at the high end of the practical NiMH range, indicating a well-engineered cell.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following diagram illustrates the **complete working principle** of a NiMH cell, mapping the charge and discharge modes, ion/electron pathways, and the role of the hydrogen-storage alloy.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Disch[DISCHARGE MODE - Energy Release]
        A1["Negative Electrode: MH alloy"] -->|Oxidation: MH + OH- gives M + H2O + e-| A2["Electron flow: External Circuit - Load"]
        A2 -->|Electrons arrive| A3["Positive Electrode: NiOOH"]
        A3 -->|Reduction: NiOOH + H2O + e- gives NiOH2 + OH-| A4["OH- ions migrate through KOH electrolyte"]
        A4 -->|OH- to negative side| A1
    end

    subgraph Chg[CHARGE MODE - Energy Storage]
        B1["External DC Power Source"] -->|Forces e- flow| B2["Positive Electrode: NiOH2"]
        B2 -->|Oxidation: NiOH2 + OH- gives NiOOH + H2O + e-| B3["OH- ions migrate through KOH electrolyte"]
        B3 -->|OH- to negative side| B4["Negative Electrode: M alloy"]
        B4 -->|Reduction: M + H2O + e- gives MH + OH-| B1
    end

    subgraph Alloy[Hydrogen Storage Alloy]
        H1["AB5 Type: LaNi5 or MmNi5"] -->|Reversible H absorption| H2["Metal Hydride MH"]
        H2 -->|Reversible H desorption| H1
    end

    A1 -. Interacts with .-> Alloy
    B4 -. Interacts with .-> Alloy
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Cell Cross-Section Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cell[NiMH Cell - Sealed Cylindrical Construction]
        C1["Top Cap - Positive Terminal"] --> C2["Positive Current Collector - Nickel Plated Steel"]
        C2 --> C3["Positive Electrode - NiOOH on Ni Foam Substrate"]
        C3 --> C4["Separator - Polypropylene Non-Woven Fabric"]
        C4 --> C5["Negative Electrode - MH Alloy on Nickel Foam"]
        C5 --> C6["Negative Current Collector - Nickel Plated Steel"]
        C6 --> C7["Cell Case - Negative Terminal"]
        C7 --> C8["Safety Vent - Pressure Relief at 20-30 atm"]
        C8 --> C1
    end

    subgraph Elec[Electrolyte System]
        E1["30 percent KOH Aqueous Solution"] --> E2["Small addition of LiOH for improved cycle life"]
    end

    Cell -. Soaked in .-> Elec
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Process State Machine: Charge-Discharge Cycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Charged: Initial State After Assembly
    Charged --> Discharging: Connect Load
    Discharging --> Discharged: Cell Voltage drops below 1.0 V
    Discharged --> Charging: Connect Charger
    Charging --> Charged: Full Charge reached
    Charged --> SelfDischarge: Storage over time
    SelfDischarge --> Charged: 20-30 percent loss per month

    note right of Discharging: MH + OH- to M + H2O + e-
    note right of Charging: M + H2O + e- to MH + OH-
    note right of SelfDischarge: Spontaneous MH decomposition
```
