# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question, Module 3, 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Write the overall electrochemical reaction of a Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH) cell during discharge. State the function of the metal hydride electrode.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
During discharge, the overall reaction of a NiMH cell is:

$$\text{NiO(OH)} + \text{MH} \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{M}$$

The positive electrode is **nickel oxyhydroxide [NiO(OH)]**, which is reduced to nickel hydroxide [Ni(OH)₂] during discharge. The negative electrode is a **metal hydride (MH)**, where M is a hydrogen-absorbing alloy (typically AB₅ type such as LaNi₅). The function of the metal hydride electrode is to **store hydrogen reversibly** within its crystal lattice and release it electrochemically during discharge, effectively acting as a **solid-state hydrogen reservoir**. The electrolyte is concentrated KOH solution, and the nominal cell voltage is **1.2 V**.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 3, 3 Marks]**

**Q2. Why is the NiMH cell preferred over the NiCd cell in modern hybrid electric vehicles? Give two reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The NiMH cell is preferred over NiCd in modern HEVs for the following two reasons:

1. **Environmental / Toxicological Advantage:** NiMH cells do not contain toxic **cadmium (Cd)**, a heavy metal that poses severe environmental and disposal hazards. NiCd is restricted under RoHS directives, whereas NiMH is exempt.

2. **Higher Specific Energy:** NiMH delivers **60–120 Wh/kg** compared to NiCd's **40–60 Wh/kg**, which translates directly into greater driving range and lower battery mass for the same energy storage requirement — critical for vehicle fuel economy.

*(Third optional point, for completeness)*: NiMH has a **milder memory effect** than NiCd, requiring less stringent full-discharge cycling maintenance.

---

## 5.2 PART B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme Pattern, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**Q(A). (a)** Explain the construction and working principle of a Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH) cell with suitable reactions. Discuss the role of the hydrogen-storage alloy in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A NiMH battery pack rated at 201.6 V and 6.5 Ah powers an electric vehicle. Calculate the total energy stored in kWh and the theoretical range if the vehicle consumes 150 Wh/km. Compare this pack with a lead-acid pack of the same voltage and capacity in terms of mass, assuming NiMH specific energy is 80 Wh/kg and lead-acid is 35 Wh/kg. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction and Working Principle of NiMH Cell — 7 Marks**

**Construction (3 Marks):**
A NiMH cell consists of:
- **Positive Electrode:** Nickel hydroxide [Ni(OH)₂] coated on a nickel-foam or nickel-plated steel substrate. This electrode is converted to nickel oxyhydroxide [NiO(OH)] on first charge.
- **Negative Electrode:** A hydrogen-absorbing intermetallic alloy, most commonly **AB₅ type (LaNi₅)** or **AB₂ type (ZrNi₂, TiNi₂)**, coated on a nickel substrate. This alloy reversibly absorbs hydrogen to form the metal hydride MH.
- **Separator:** A porous **polypropylene non-woven fabric** that electrically isolates the two electrodes while permitting ionic transport.
- **Electrolyte:** **30% aqueous KOH** with a small addition of **LiOH** to improve cycle life and reduce oxygen evolution at the positive electrode.
- **Cell Case:** Nickel-plated steel cylindrical case (negative terminal), with a **safety vent** that opens at 20–30 atm to prevent rupture from overcharge gas buildup.

**Working Principle (4 Marks):**
The NiMH cell operates on a **reversible alkaline electrochemical reaction**. During discharge:

- At the negative electrode (anode), the metal hydride is oxidized:
$$\text{MH} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^-$$
- Electrons flow through the external load to the positive electrode.
- Hydroxyl ions migrate through the KOH electrolyte to the positive electrode.
- At the positive electrode (cathode), NiO(OH) is reduced:
$$\text{NiO(OH)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^-$$

The overall reaction is:
$$\text{NiO(OH)} + \text{MH} \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{M}$$

During charging, the reaction is reversed by the external power source. The **role of the hydrogen-storage alloy** is central: it acts as a **reversible hydrogen sink**, storing hydrogen in atomic form within its crystal lattice (forming the β-phase hydride) at ambient temperature and pressure. This eliminates the need for gaseous hydrogen storage, making the NiMH cell **safe, compact, and maintenance-free**.

---

**(b) Numerical Calculation — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Energy Stored in the NiMH Pack**
$$\begin{aligned}
E &= V \times Q \\
&= 201.6 \text{ V} \times 6.5 \text{ Ah} \\
&= 1310.4 \text{ Wh} \\
&= 1.3104 \text{ kWh}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Theoretical Range of the EV**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Range} &= \frac{E}{\text{Consumption per km}} = \frac{1310.4 \text{ Wh}}{150 \text{ Wh/km}} \\
&= 8.736 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Mass of the NiMH Pack**
$$\begin{aligned}
m_{NiMH} &= \frac{E}{E_{specific}} = \frac{1310.4 \text{ Wh}}{80 \text{ Wh/kg}} \\
&= 16.38 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4: Mass of the Equivalent Lead-Acid Pack**
$$\begin{aligned}
m_{Pb} &= \frac{1310.4 \text{ Wh}}{35 \text{ Wh/kg}} \\
&= 37.44 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5: Mass Comparison**
$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass Saving} &= 37.44 - 16.38 = 21.06 \text{ kg} \\
\text{Percentage Saving} &= \frac{21.06}{37.44} \times 100\% \approx 56.25\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The NiMH pack offers the **same energy storage** at only **43.75% of the mass** of an equivalent lead-acid pack, making it vastly superior for EV applications where vehicle mass directly affects range, acceleration, and energy efficiency.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**Q(B). (a)** Describe the half-cell reactions at both electrodes of a NiMH cell during charging and discharging. Derive the theoretical open-circuit voltage of the cell given $E^0_{NiO(OH)/Ni(OH)_2} = +0.49$ V and $E^0_{OH^-/H_2} = -0.83$ V versus SHE. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare the NiMH cell with the NiCd and lead-acid cells in terms of nominal voltage, specific energy, environmental impact, and memory effect. State any two practical limitations of NiMH technology. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Half-Cell Reactions and OCV Derivation — 7 Marks**

**During DISCHARGE:**

*Positive electrode (cathode) — Reduction:*
$$\text{NiO(OH)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \rightarrow \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^- \quad \quad (E^0 = +0.49 \text{ V})$$

*Negative electrode (anode) — Oxidation:*
$$\text{MH} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \quad \quad (E^0 = -0.83 \text{ V})$$

**During CHARGE** (reactions reverse):

*Positive electrode (anode during charge) — Oxidation:*
$$\text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{NiO(OH)} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^-$$

*Negative electrode (cathode during charge) — Reduction:*
$$\text{M} + \text{H}_2\text{O} + e^- \rightarrow \text{MH} + \text{OH}^-$$

**Net Reversible Reaction (both modes):**
$$\text{NiO(OH)} + \text{MH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{M}$$

**Derivation of Theoretical OCV:**

The standard cell EMF is calculated as:
$$\begin{aligned}
E^0_{cell} &= E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode} \\
&= (+0.49) - (-0.83) \\
&= 0.49 + 0.83 \\
&= \mathbf{1.32 \text{ V}}
\end{aligned}$$

The **practical nominal voltage** is **1.2 V** due to internal resistance, polarization, and operating-condition losses.

---

**(b) Comparative Analysis and Limitations — 7 Marks**

**Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Parameter | NiMH | NiCd | Lead-Acid |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Nominal Voltage (V)** | 1.2 | 1.2 | 2.0 |
| **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** | 60–120 | 40–60 | 30–50 |
| **Environmental Impact** | **Low** (no heavy metals in active material) | **High** (cadmium toxic) | **High** (lead toxic) |
| **Memory Effect** | **Mild** | **Severe** | None |
| **Cycle Life** | 500–2000 | 1000–2000 | 300–800 |
| **Self-Discharge** | High (20–30%/month) | Moderate (10–15%/month) | Low (4–6%/month) |

**Two Practical Limitations of NiMH (2 Marks):**

1. **High Self-Discharge Rate:** NiMH cells lose **20–30% of their charge per month** even when idle, which is significantly higher than NiCd (10–15%) or lead-acid (4–6%). This makes them unsuitable for low-drain standby applications.

2. **Lower Specific Energy than Li-ion:** With practical specific energy capped at **60–120 Wh/kg**, NiMH is being rapidly displaced by **Li-ion (150–250 Wh/kg)** in modern EVs (e.g., post-2015 Toyota Prius switched to Li-ion) and portable electronics, where energy density is critical.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Error in EMF Calculation:** Forgetting to subtract a negative value — writing $0.49 - 0.83 = -0.34$ V instead of $0.49 + 0.83 = 1.32$ V. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 2. **Confusing Anode and Cathode:** During discharge, the **negative electrode is the anode** (oxidation occurs) and the **positive electrode is the cathode** (reduction occurs). Many students reverse this. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 3. **Forgetting the Role of the Hydrogen-Storage Alloy:** Simply writing the net reaction without explaining the **reversible hydrogen absorption/desorption** mechanism of the AB₅ or AB₂ alloy is incomplete. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 4. **Unit Conversion Mistakes in Numerical Problems:** Mixing Wh, kWh, Ah, and mAh without explicit conversion. Always show the unit conversion steps. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 5. **Omitting the Comparison with Other Battery Types:** KTU 14-mark questions on NiMH almost always require a comparative discussion with NiCd and/or Lead-Acid. Skipping this loses **3–4 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- ☐ **Definition:** NiMH is a **rechargeable alkaline secondary cell**, NOT a true fuel cell. Uses **NiO(OH)/Ni(OH)₂** at cathode and **MH/M alloy** at anode, in **KOH electrolyte**.

- ☐ **Nominal Voltage:** **1.2 V** (theoretical OCV: **1.32 V**).

- ☐ **Net Reaction:** $\text{NiO(OH)} + \text{MH} \rightleftharpoons \text{Ni(OH)}_2 + \text{M}$

- ☐ **Hydrogen-Storage Alloy:** **AB₅ (LaNi₅)** is most common; AB₂ is the alternative. Acts as a **reversible hydrogen sponge**.

- ☐ **Electrolyte Ion:** **OH⁻** (hydroxyl ion) is the charge carrier, migrating through KOH electrolyte.

- ☐ **Specific Energy:** Theoretical ~**240–381 Wh/kg**; Practical **60–120 Wh/kg**.

- ☐ **Cycle Life:** **500–2000 cycles**.

- ☐ **Self-Discharge:** High, **20–30% per month**.

- ☐ **Advantages over NiCd:** No toxic Cd, higher specific energy, milder memory effect.

- ☐ **Disadvantages vs. Li-ion:** Lower specific energy, higher self-discharge, heavier for same energy.

- ☐ **Real-World Applications:** Toyota Prius HEVs (older models), AA/AAA rechargeable batteries, spacecraft NiMH batteries, UPS backup.

- ☐ **Key Formula:** $E^0_{cell} = E^0_{cathode} - E^0_{anode} = 0.49 - (-0.83) = 1.32$ V.

- ☐ **Memorize the Discharge Mode Mapping:** Anode = MH oxidation; Cathode = NiO(OH) reduction; OH⁻ flows from negative to positive electrode inside the cell.

- ☐ **Memorize the Charge Mode Mapping:** Reverse of discharge; external DC source forces electrons from positive to negative terminal.

- ☐ **No Net Water Consumption:** Water formed at one electrode is consumed at the other — hence **sealed, maintenance-free** design.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Efficiency
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Efficiency

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
In the context of **Energy Systems (PERAT637)**, *Efficiency* ($\eta$) is defined as the dimensionless ratio of the useful energy or power output delivered by a system, device, or process to the energy or power input supplied to it. It is a fundamental performance metric used to quantify how effectively an energy conversion device transforms input energy into the desired form of output energy.

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Useful Energy Output}}{\text{Energy Input}} = \frac{E_{out}}{E_{in}}$$

For continuous-flow devices (turbines, pumps, compressors, engines), efficiency is expressed in terms of **power**:

$$\eta = \frac{\dot{W}_{out}}{\dot{E}_{in}} = \frac{\dot{W}_{out}}{\dot{Q}_{in}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Efficiency is the backbone of Module 3, covering the analysis of thermal power plants, IC engines, refrigeration cycles, and renewable energy systems. Mastery of first-law (energy-based) and second-law (exergy-based) efficiencies is mandatory.

### 1.2 Types of Efficiencies in Energy Systems
| **Category** | **Type** | **Application** |
|--------------|----------|-----------------|
| First Law | Thermal Efficiency | Heat engines, power cycles |
| First Law | Mechanical Efficiency | Turbines, pumps, compressors |
| First Law | Electrical Efficiency | Generators, motors |
| First Law | Overall/Plant Efficiency | Power stations |
| Second Law | Exergy Efficiency | All energy systems (advanced) |
| Component | Boiler Efficiency | Steam generators |
| Component | Combustion Efficiency | Furnaces, boilers |
| Component | Cycle Efficiency | Rankine, Brayton, Otto, Diesel |
| Component | Isentropic Efficiency | Nozzles, diffusers, turbines, compressors |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Leaky Bucket**
> Imagine you are filling a bucket (the *input energy*) but the bucket has several small holes at the bottom. The water that successfully reaches the next container represents *useful output energy*. **Efficiency is simply the ratio of water collected to water poured in.** If you pour 100 litres and collect only 35 litres, your efficiency is 35%. The rest is "leaked" as waste heat, friction, exhaust gases, or irreversibilities. The more holes (losses) you plug, the closer you get to 100% — but you can never quite reach it because the Second Law of Thermodynamics guarantees that some energy will always be *degraded* into unusable low-temperature heat.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics
- **Carnot (Maximum Theoretical) Efficiency** uses the absolute temperature ratio: $T_H$ and $T_L$ in **Kelvin (K)**.
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** and **Higher Heating Value (HHV)** are measured in **kJ/kg** or **MJ/kg**.
- **Standard Ambient/Reference Temperature:** $T_0 = 298.15 \, \text{K}$ (25 °C).
- **Standard Pressure:** $p_0 = 101.325 \, \text{kPa}$.
- A 100% efficient device is called a **reversible device** (theoretical ideal, never achievable in practice).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of Efficiency Concepts

**Step 1 — Why Efficiency is Always ≤ 1:**
- The **First Law of Thermodynamics** (energy conservation) requires that energy input equals useful output plus losses.
- The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** (entropy increase) guarantees that real processes are irreversible, creating additional losses.
- Therefore, $E_{out} \leq E_{in}$ always, making $\eta \leq 1$ (or $\eta \leq 100\%$).

**Step 2 — First Law vs Second Law Efficiency:**
- **First Law (Energy) Efficiency:** Based purely on energy conservation; does not account for the *quality* of energy.
- **Second Law (Exergy) Efficiency:** Based on *exergy* (available work potential); accounts for the quality degradation of energy. Exergy efficiency can be greater than 1 when a system uses low-quality waste heat and converts it usefully.

**Step 3 — Component-wise vs Overall Efficiency:**
- A power plant has multiple stages: boiler → turbine → generator → transformer.
- **Overall efficiency** = product of individual component efficiencies.
- Example: $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{boiler} \times \eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{generator} \times \eta_{transformer}$

**Step 4 — Isentropic (Adiabatic) Efficiency:**
- Compares the actual work/process of a device to the ideal isentropic (reversible adiabatic) process.
- **For turbines:** $\eta_T = \dfrac{W_{actual}}{W_{isentropic}} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$
- **For compressors:** $\eta_C = \dfrac{W_{isentropic}}{W_{actual}} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}$

**Step 5 — Carnot Efficiency — The Ultimate Theoretical Limit:**
- For any heat engine operating between a hot reservoir at $T_H$ and a cold reservoir at $T_L$:
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$
- This is the **maximum possible** efficiency for *any* heat engine, regardless of working fluid or cycle design.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Efficiency Type** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|-------|---------------------|-------------|-----------|-------------|
| 1 | Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{th} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$ | Dimensionless | Heat engines |
| 2 | Carnot Efficiency | $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Dimensionless | Theoretical max; $T$ in K |
| 3 | Rankine Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{Rankine} = \dfrac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \dfrac{(h_1 - h_2) + (h_3 - h_4)}{h_1 - h_4}$ | Dimensionless | Steam power plants |
| 4 | Brayton Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{Brayton} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ | Dimensionless | Gas turbines; $r_p$ = pressure ratio |
| 5 | Otto Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{(\gamma-1)}}$ | Dimensionless | Petrol engines; $r$ = compression ratio |
| 6 | Diesel Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{Diesel} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ | Dimensionless | Diesel engines; $\rho$ = cutoff ratio |
| 7 | Boiler Efficiency | $\eta_{boiler} = \dfrac{Q_{absorbed}}{Q_{fuel}} \times 100$ | % | Direct/indirect method |
| 8 | Generator Efficiency | $\eta_{gen} = \dfrac{P_{electrical\,out}}{P_{mechanical\,in}} \times 100$ | % | Electrical output |
| 9 | Overall Plant Efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{boiler} \times \eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{gen}$ | Dimensionless | Product of stages |
| 10 | Isentropic Efficiency (Turbine) | $\eta_{T} = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$ | Dimensionless | Expansion devices |
| 11 | Isentropic Efficiency (Compressor) | $\eta_{C} = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}$ | Dimensionless | Compression devices |
| 12 | Exergy (Second Law) Efficiency | $\eta_{II} = \dfrac{\dot{E}x_{useful\,out}}{\dot{E}x_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{\dot{E}x_{destroyed}}{\dot{E}x_{in}}$ | Dimensionless | Quality-based |
| 13 | COP (Refrigeration) | $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W_{net}} = \dfrac{1}{Q_H/Q_L - 1}$ | Dimensionless | Refrigerators/ACs |
| 14 | COP (Heat Pump) | $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W_{net}} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ | Dimensionless | Heating devices |
| 15 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{W_{shaft}}{W_{indicated}} \times 100$ | % | Engines, pumps |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Power Plant Design:** Engineers use overall efficiency to size boilers, turbines, and condensers optimally. A 1% increase in efficiency of a 500 MW plant saves thousands of tonnes of coal annually.
- **IC Engine Development:** Compression ratio, cutoff ratio, and combustion timing are tuned using Otto/Diesel efficiency equations.
- **Renewable Energy:** Solar PV efficiency (~15–22%), wind turbine efficiency (Betz limit = 59.3%), and fuel cell efficiency (~40–60%) are all critical performance metrics.
- **HVAC & Refrigeration:** COP (Coefficient of Performance) determines energy savings for air conditioners and refrigerators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Carnot Efficiency from the Second Law

The Carnot cycle consists of two isothermal and two isentropic processes operating between $T_H$ and $T_L$.

**Step 1 — Heat transfer during isothermal expansion at $T_H$ (Hot reservoir):**
$$Q_H = T_H \cdot \Delta S$$
The system absorbs heat $Q_H$ from the high-temperature reservoir, and entropy change is $\Delta S$.

**Step 2 — Heat rejection during isothermal compression at $T_L$ (Cold reservoir):**
$$Q_L = T_L \cdot \Delta S$$
Since the Carnot cycle is reversible, the magnitude of entropy change in both isothermal steps is equal: $\vert \Delta S_H \vert = \vert \Delta S_L \vert = \Delta S$.

**Step 3 — Net work output (First Law applied to the cycle):**
$$W_{net} = Q_H - Q_L$$

**Step 4 — Substitute Step 1 and Step 2 into Step 3:**
$$W_{net} = T_H \cdot \Delta S - T_L \cdot \Delta S$$
$$W_{net} = (T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S$$

**Step 5 — Apply the definition of thermal efficiency:**
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_H}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute $W_{net}$ and $Q_H$:**
$$\eta_{th} = \frac{(T_H - T_L) \cdot \Delta S}{T_H \cdot \Delta S}$$

**Step 7 — Cancel $\Delta S$ (since the cycle is reversible):**
$$\eta_{Carnot} = \frac{T_H - T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** The temperatures **must be in Kelvin (K)** for the Carnot equation. Mixing °C and K is a common error that costs marks.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Brayton Cycle Efficiency (Gas Turbine)

The ideal Brayton cycle consists of: (1–2) isentropic compression, (2–3) constant-pressure heat addition, (3–4) isentropic expansion, (4–1) constant-pressure heat rejection.

**Step 1 — Apply the isentropic relation for an ideal gas:**
$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}$$
where $r_p$ is the pressure ratio and $\gamma = c_p / c_v$ is the specific heat ratio.

**Step 2 — For constant-pressure heat addition and rejection:**
$$q_{in} = c_p (T_3 - T_2)$$
$$q_{out} = c_p (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 3 — Net work output:**
$$w_{net} = q_{in} - q_{out} = c_p[(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$$

**Step 4 — Thermal efficiency:**
$$\eta_{Brayton} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

**Step 5 — Using the isentropic relations for processes 1–2 and 3–4:**
$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{T_4}{T_3} \cdot \text{(rearranged from isentropic equations)}$$

**Step 6 — After algebraic manipulation (using $T_2/T_1 = T_3/T_4$ for isentropic processes with the same pressure ratio):**
$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = \frac{T_4}{T_3}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute to simplify the efficiency expression:**
$$\eta_{Brayton} = 1 - \frac{T_1}{T_2} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$$

This is the final closed-form Brayton efficiency equation, which depends only on the **pressure ratio** and **specific heat ratio**.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Otto Cycle Efficiency (Petrol Engine)

The ideal Otto cycle consists of: (1–2) isentropic compression, (2–3) constant-volume heat addition, (3–4) isentropic expansion, (4–1) constant-volume heat rejection.

**Step 1 — Define the compression ratio:**
$$r = \frac{V_1}{V_2}$$
where $V_1$ is the maximum (BDC) volume and $V_2$ is the minimum (TDC) volume.

**Step 2 — Isentropic relations for processes 1–2 and 3–4:**
$$\frac{T_2}{T_1} = \left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)^{\gamma-1} = r^{\gamma-1}$$
$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = r^{\gamma-1}$$

**Step 3 — Heat addition (constant volume):**
$$q_{in} = c_v (T_3 - T_2)$$

**Step 4 — Heat rejection (constant volume):**
$$q_{out} = c_v (T_4 - T_1)$$

**Step 5 — Net work:**
$$w_{net} = q_{in} - q_{out} = c_v[(T_3 - T_2) - (T_4 - T_1)]$$

**Step 6 — Thermal efficiency:**
$$\eta_{Otto} = \frac{w_{net}}{q_{in}} = 1 - \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2}$$

**Step 7 — Using isentropic relations $T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$ and $T_3 = T_4 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}$:**
$$\frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_3 - T_2} = \frac{T_4 - T_1}{T_4 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} - T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1}} = \frac{T_4 - T_1}{r^{\gamma-1}(T_4 - T_1)} = \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$

**Step 8 — Final Otto efficiency equation:**
$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing the compression ratio $r$ increases Otto efficiency. This is why high-performance engines use $r = 10$ to $12$ (limited by knocking in petrol engines; diesel engines have no such limit due to compression ignition).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Efficiency vs Working Conditions

| **Cycle** | **Key Parameter** | **Efficiency Increases When** | **Limiting Factor** |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------------------|---------------------|
| Carnot | $T_H$ and $T_L$ | $T_H$ rises or $T_L$ drops | Material limits (metallurgy) |
| Rankine | Boiler pressure, superheat | Boiler pressure, superheat temperature rise | Turbine blade temp., moisture in turbine |
| Brayton | Pressure ratio $r_p$ | $r_p$ increases (up to optimum) | Compressor work at high $r_p$ |
| Otto | Compression ratio $r$ | $r$ increases | Knocking in SI engines |
| Diesel | $r$ and $\gamma$ | $r$ increases, cutoff ratio decreases | Combustion efficiency, heat loss |
| Refrigeration | $T_L$ and $T_H$ | $T_L$ rises, $T_H$ drops | Compressor performance |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy Flow in a Thermal Power Plant (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL INPUT Chemical Energy] --> B[BOILER Thermal Energy]
    B --> C[STEAM TURBINE Mechanical Work]
    C --> D[GENERATOR Electrical Energy]
    D --> E[TRANSFORMER Step-up Voltage]
    E --> F[GRID Distribution]
    B --> G[FLUE GAS LOSS Q_loss1]
    C --> H[CONDENSER LOSS Q_loss2]
    D --> I[MECHANICAL LOSS Q_loss3]
    F --> J[USEFUL OUTPUT Net Power]
    A -.η_boiler.-> B
    B -.η_turbine.-> C
    C -.η_gen.-> D
    D -.η_trans.-> E
```

### 4.2 Efficiency Hierarchy of a Power Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[OVERALL PLANT EFFICIENCY η_overall] --> P2[Boiler Efficiency η_b]
    P1 --> P3[Turbine Isentropic Efficiency η_T]
    P1 --> P4[Generator Efficiency η_g]
    P1 --> P5[Auxiliary Consumption η_aux]
    P2 --> P21[Combustion Efficiency]
    P2 --> P22[Heat Transfer Efficiency]
    P3 --> P31[Stage-wise Efficiency]
    P4 --> P41[Electrical Conversion Efficiency]
```

### 4.3 Energy Degradation and Exergy Destruction Pathway

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[High Quality Energy Exergy] --> X2[Process Conversion]
    X2 --> X3[Medium Quality Energy]
    X3 --> X4[End Use Conversion]
    X4 --> X5[Low Quality Waste Heat to Environment]
    X2 -.Exergy destroyed.-> X6[Irreversibilities]
    X4 -.Exergy destroyed.-> X6
    X1 --> A1[η_II approaches 100 percent]
    X5 --> A2[η_II approaches 0 percent]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Efficiency Ranges for Real Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[REAL WORLD EFFICIENCY RANGES] --> R1[Steam Power Plant 33 to 42 percent]
    R0 --> R2[Gas Turbine Plant 35 to 45 percent]
    R0 --> R3[Combined Cycle Plant 55 to 62 percent]
    R0 --> R4[Petrol Engine 25 to 35 percent]
    R0 --> R5[Diesel Engine 35 to 45 percent]
    R0 --> R6[Solar PV 15 to 22 percent]
    R0 --> R7[Wind Turbine Betz Limit 59 percent]
    R0 --> R8[Fuel Cell 40 to 60 percent]
    R0 --> R9[Carnot Limit 100 percent theoretical]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Define thermal efficiency and overall efficiency of a thermal power plant. Why is the overall efficiency always less than the Carnot efficiency?**

**Model Answer:**

**Thermal Efficiency:** It is the ratio of net work output of the cycle to the heat input supplied to the working fluid.

$$\eta_{th} = \frac{W_{net}}{Q_{in}}$$

**Overall Efficiency:** It is the ratio of net electrical energy output delivered to the grid to the energy content of the fuel input.

$$\eta_{overall} = \frac{E_{electrical\,out}}{E_{fuel\,in}} = \eta_{boiler} \times \eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{generator}$$

**Why Overall < Carnot:** The overall efficiency is the product of individual component efficiencies, each of which is less than 1. Additionally, the working cycle is not reversible; irreversibilities in combustion, heat transfer, and fluid friction make it inferior to the ideal Carnot cycle. Practical constraints like material temperature limits and condenser cooling temperatures further reduce efficiency.

*(3 marks — 1 mark each for definitions + 1 mark for comparison justification)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Distinguish between isentropic efficiency of a turbine and isentropic efficiency of a compressor. Why are the numerators and denominators interchanged?**

**Model Answer:**

For a **turbine** (expansion device — output is work):

$$\eta_{T} = \frac{W_{actual}}{W_{isentropic}} = \frac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$$

For a **compressor** (compression device — input is work):

$$\eta_{C} = \frac{W_{isentropic}}{W_{actual}} = \frac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}$$

**Reason for Interchange:**
- In a **turbine**, the actual work output is always *less* than the ideal isentropic work because of internal irreversibilities. Hence, the actual value goes in the numerator.
- In a **compressor**, the actual work input is always *more* than the ideal isentropic work because additional work is required to overcome irreversibilities. Hence, the isentropic (ideal) value goes in the numerator.

In both cases, the *desired* quantity is placed in the numerator so that efficiency is always $\leq 1$.

*(3 marks — 1.5 marks for each formula + 1 mark for justification)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** A Carnot heat engine operates between a hot reservoir at $T_H = 800$ K and a cold reservoir at $T_L = 300$ K. The engine receives 1000 kJ of heat from the hot reservoir per cycle. Calculate:
(i) The thermal efficiency.
(ii) The net work output per cycle.
(iii) The heat rejected to the cold reservoir per cycle.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steam power plant operates on the ideal Rankine cycle. Steam enters the turbine at 3 MPa and 350 °C, and condenser pressure is 10 kPa. Determine:
(i) The thermal efficiency of the cycle.
(ii) The work output of the turbine per kg of steam.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — Carnot Engine:**

**(i) Thermal Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_L}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{800} = 1 - 0.375 = 0.625$$
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 62.5\%$$

**(ii) Net Work Output:**
$$W_{net} = \eta_{Carnot} \times Q_H = 0.625 \times 1000 = 625 \text{ kJ}$$

**(iii) Heat Rejected:**
$$Q_L = Q_H - W_{net} = 1000 - 625 = 375 \text{ kJ}$$

**Part (b) — Rankine Cycle:**

**State 1:** Turbine inlet: $p_1 = 3$ MPa, $T_1 = 350$ °C (superheated steam)
From steam tables: $h_1 = 3115.3$ kJ/kg, $s_1 = 6.7428$ kJ/(kg·K)

**State 2:** Turbine exit: $p_2 = 10$ kPa, $s_2 = s_1 = 6.7428$ kJ/(kg·K)
At 10 kPa: $s_f = 0.6493$, $s_{fg} = 7.5009$, $h_f = 191.83$, $h_{fg} = 2392.8$ kJ/kg
$$x_2 = \frac{s_2 - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.7428 - 0.6493}{7.5009} = 0.8128$$
$$h_2 = h_f + x_2 \cdot h_{fg} = 191.83 + 0.8128 \times 2392.8 = 2136.0 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**State 3:** Condenser exit (saturated liquid at 10 kPa):
$$h_3 = h_f = 191.83 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**State 4:** Pump exit (after isentropic pumping to 3 MPa):
$$w_p = v_3 (p_4 - p_3) = 0.00101 \times (3000 - 10) = 3.01 \text{ kJ/kg}$$
$$h_4 = h_3 + w_p = 191.83 + 3.01 = 194.84 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(i) Work Output of Turbine:**
$$w_T = h_1 - h_2 = 3115.3 - 2136.0 = 979.3 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**(ii) Thermal Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{Rankine} = \frac{w_T - w_p}{h_1 - h_4} = \frac{979.3 - 3.01}{3115.3 - 194.84} = \frac{976.29}{2920.46} = 0.3343$$
$$\eta_{Rankine} = 33.43\%$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** An air-standard Brayton cycle has a pressure ratio of 8. The air enters the compressor at 300 K and the turbine at 1300 K. Assuming $\gamma = 1.4$ and $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K), determine:
(i) The temperature at the compressor exit and turbine exit.
(ii) The thermal efficiency of the cycle.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A petrol engine works on the Otto cycle with a compression ratio of 9:1. The conditions at the beginning of compression are 100 kPa and 35 °C. If the maximum temperature in the cycle is 2000 K, determine:
(i) The temperature and pressure at the end of compression.
(ii) The thermal efficiency of the cycle.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — Brayton Cycle:**

Given: $r_p = 8$, $T_1 = 300$ K, $T_3 = 1300$ K, $\gamma = 1.4$, $c_p = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Temperature exponent:**
$$\frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \frac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857$$

**Compressor exit (State 2 — isentropic):**
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 300 \times 8^{0.2857}$$
$$8^{0.2857} = 1.8114$$
$$T_2 = 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.4 \text{ K}$$

**Turbine exit (State 4 — isentropic expansion with same $r_p$):**
$$\frac{T_3}{T_4} = r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma} = 1.8114$$
$$T_4 = \frac{T_3}{1.8114} = \frac{1300}{1.8114} = 717.7 \text{ K}$$

**Thermal efficiency (Brayton closed-form):**
$$\eta_{Brayton} = 1 - \frac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}} = 1 - \frac{1}{1.8114} = 1 - 0.5521 = 0.4479$$
$$\eta_{Brayton} = 44.79\%$$

**Part (b) — Otto Cycle:**

Given: $r = 9$, $T_1 = 35 + 273 = 308$ K, $T_3 = 2000$ K, $p_1 = 100$ kPa, $\gamma = 1.4$

**(i) End of compression (State 2 — isentropic):**
$$T_2 = T_1 \cdot r^{\gamma-1} = 308 \times 9^{0.4}$$
$$9^{0.4} = 2.4082$$
$$T_2 = 308 \times 2.4082 = 741.7 \text{ K}$$

Pressure at State 2:
$$p_2 = p_1 \cdot r^{\gamma} = 100 \times 9^{1.4}$$
$$9^{1.4} = 21.67$$
$$p_2 = 100 \times 21.67 = 2167 \text{ kPa} = 2.167 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Thermal Efficiency:**
$$\eta_{Otto} = 1 - \frac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{9^{0.4}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2.4082} = 1 - 0.4152 = 0.5848$$
$$\eta_{Otto} = 58.48\%$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Temperature in °C vs K:** Carnot and Brayton efficiency equations **MANDATORILY** require absolute temperature in Kelvin. Using Celsius will give a *wrong answer* and zero marks.
> 2. **Steam Table Interpolation:** In Rankine cycle problems, students often read steam tables at the wrong pressure or skip interpolation. Always show the state points clearly (1, 2, 3, 4) and cite the source values.
> 3. **Moisture Check:** If quality $x < 0.88$ at turbine exit, the KTU examiner expects you to mention excessive moisture and suggest superheating. Skipping this commentary can cost 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Sign Convention Confusion:** In compressor efficiency, the work input is *positive* into the system. For turbine, work output is *positive* out. Mixing signs leads to a completely wrong formula.
> 5. **Pump Work Approximation:** Many students ignore pump work in the Rankine cycle. Although small, including $w_p = v_3(p_4 - p_3)$ shows engineering rigor and earns full marks.
> 6. **Units Consistency:** Always report answers with units (kJ/kg, kPa, K) — KTU 2024 marking scheme penalizes unit-less answers by 0.5 marks per instance.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **⚡ RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — EFFICIENCY**

- **Definition:** $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Useful Output}}{\text{Input}}$; always $\leq 1$ (or 100%) for real systems.
- **Carnot Limit:** $\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ — the absolute maximum; temperatures in **Kelvin**.
- **Brayton:** $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r_p^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}}$ — depends on pressure ratio only.
- **Otto:** $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}}$ — increases with compression ratio; limited by knock.
- **Diesel:** $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{r^{\gamma-1}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho^\gamma - 1}{\gamma(\rho - 1)}$ — depends on compression and cutoff ratios.
- **Rankine:** $\eta = \dfrac{w_T - w_p}{q_{in}}$ — improved by superheating, reheating, regeneration.
- **Overall Plant:** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{boiler} \times \eta_{turbine} \times \eta_{generator} \times \eta_{transformer} \times \eta_{aux}$.
- **Isentropic Efficiency (Turbine):** $\eta_T = \dfrac{h_1 - h_2}{h_1 - h_{2s}}$.
- **Isentropic Efficiency (Compressor):** $\eta_C = \dfrac{h_{2s} - h_1}{h_2 - h_1}$.
- **Exergy (Second Law) Efficiency:** $\eta_{II} = 1 - \dfrac{\dot{E}x_{destroyed}}{\dot{E}x_{in}}$ — the *true* thermodynamic measure of performance.
- **COP:** $\text{COP}_R = \dfrac{Q_L}{W}$ for refrigeration; $\text{COP}_{HP} = \dfrac{Q_H}{W} = \text{COP}_R + 1$ for heat pumps.
- **Key Real-World Values:** Modern coal plant ~ 38–42%; Combined cycle ~ 60%; Petrol engine ~ 30%; Diesel ~ 40%; Solar PV ~ 20%; Wind turbine Betz limit 59.3%.
- **Units Rule:** Use **Kelvin** for all temperature ratios, **kJ/kg** for specific quantities, **kPa or MPa** for pressure.
- **The 1% Rule:** A 1% increase in efficiency of a 1000 MW plant saves ~₹50 crores annually in fuel cost.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Significance and General Principles of Energy Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Significance and General Principles of Energy Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4 — Significance and General Principles of Energy Management

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy Management (EM)** is the proactive, organized, and systematic process of **planning, operating, controlling, and monitoring** energy consumption in a building, industrial facility, process, or organization to ensure that the required services are delivered with the **least amount of energy use, lowest operating cost, and minimal environmental impact**, while maintaining occupant comfort, productivity, and safety standards.

According to the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India** and standard KTU 2024 syllabus nomenclature, Energy Management is a structured function that integrates engineering principles, economic analysis, and managerial commitment to achieve **sustained energy performance improvement**.

**Key Terminology (KTU High-Frequency Vocabulary):**
- **Energy Manager**: A certified professional responsible for implementing the energy management program.
- **Energy Policy**: A documented statement of intent by top management regarding energy performance.
- **Energy Audit**: A systematic review of energy use to identify wastage and improvement opportunities.
- **Energy Monitoring & Targeting (M&T)**: Continuous tracking of energy consumption against a baseline.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)**: Energy used per unit of output (e.g., kWh/ton, MJ/kg).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Family Budget" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Energy Management like managing a family's monthly household budget.**

Imagine a family earning ₹50,000 per month. Without any tracking, money disappears on unnecessary purchases — eating out, idle subscriptions, electricity wastage, etc.

Now apply **Energy Management principles**:
1. **Set a financial policy** ("We will save 20% every month") ↔ This is the **Energy Policy**.
2. **Track every expense** in a notebook or app ↔ This is **Energy Monitoring**.
3. **Find where money leaks** (audit) ↔ This is the **Energy Audit**.
4. **Take corrective action** (switch off lights, efficient appliances) ↔ This is **Implementation of ECMs (Energy Conservation Measures)**.
5. **Review monthly** whether savings are sustained ↔ This is **Management Review & Continual Improvement** (ISO 50001 PDCA Cycle).

In the same way, an industry or building that consumes huge amounts of energy needs a **structured, ongoing, top-management-driven** program to control, conserve, and optimize energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Energy management is NOT a one-time project. It is a **continuous, iterative, management-driven discipline** — like hygiene for a business.

---

### 1.3 Why Energy Management is Significant — The "5-Pillar" Justification

| Pillar | Significance |
|---|---|
| **1. Economic (Cost Reduction)** | Energy typically accounts for **15–40%** of factory operating costs. Even a 10% reduction directly boosts profit. |
| **2. Environmental (Sustainability)** | Reduced fossil-fuel use → reduced **CO₂ emissions**, lower carbon footprint, ESG compliance. |
| **3. Legal/Regulatory** | Compliance with the **Energy Conservation Act 2001**, BEE star labelling, Perform Achieve Trade (PAT) scheme. |
| **4. Operational (Reliability)** | Efficient systems run cooler, last longer, and have lower breakdown rates. |
| **5. Strategic (Competitive Edge)** | Lower energy intensity → lower product cost → stronger market position. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Metric:** The **Energy Cost Share (ECS)** in Indian manufacturing averages around **25–30%** of total production cost — making it the **second-largest controllable expense** after raw materials.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & General Principles of Energy Management

## 2.1 The 10 General Principles of Energy Management (KTU Board Favourite)

Energy Management rests on **10 universally accepted principles**, originally formalized by the **Energy Management Centre, India** and widely tested in KTU university exams:

### Principle 1: **Top Management Commitment & Leadership**
- Energy management must begin with a **written, signed Energy Policy** issued by the CEO/MD.
- Without leadership commitment, no energy program survives beyond a few months.
- Manifests as: budget allocation, appointing an Energy Manager, regular reviews.

### Principle 2: **Establish an Energy Management Cell (EMC)**
- A dedicated cross-functional team with defined **Roles & Responsibilities (R&R)**.
- Members: Plant Head, Maintenance Engineer, Electrical Engineer, Production Manager, Finance Officer.

### Principle 3: **Energy Audit — Know Your Baseline**
- **Type 1 (Walk-through Audit)**: Quick visual survey; identifies obvious losses.
- **Type 2 (Detailed Audit)**: Instrumented survey with measurements; produces quantified savings.
- **Type 3 (Investment-grade Audit)**: Detailed engineering + financial modelling for CAPEX decisions.

### Principle 4: **Energy Monitoring & Targeting (M&T)**
- Install **sub-meters** at every major load centre.
- Compute **CUSUM** (Cumulative Sum) charts to detect deviations from baseline.
- Use **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** techniques.

### Principle 5: **Benchmarking**
- Compare with **internal historical data** (own plant last year) and **external peers** (industry best practices).
- Metric: **kWh per unit of production** (Specific Energy Consumption).

### Principle 6: **Prioritization of Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)**
- Rank ECMs by **Simple Payback Period (SPP)** or **Net Present Value (NPV)**.
- Implement **"low-hanging fruits" first** (no-cost/low-cost measures).

### Principle 7: **Implementation of ECMs**
- Engineering measures (VFDs on motors, LED lighting, waste heat recovery).
- Operational measures (switching off idle equipment, tariff optimization).
- Maintenance measures (steam leak repair, compressed air leak audit).

### Principle 8: **Training & Awareness**
- Conduct **Energy Awareness Programs** for all employees.
- Use **Display boards**, posters, suggestion schemes.

### Principle 9: **Documentation & Record Keeping**
- Maintain **Energy Consumption Database**.
- Document all M&V (Measurement & Verification) reports using **IPMVP protocol**.

### Principle 10: **Continual Improvement & Management Review**
- Apply the **PDCA Cycle**: Plan → Do → Check → Act.
- Re-set targets every year — the **ISO 50001 EnMS** philosophy.

---

## 2.2 The Energy Management PDCA Cycle (ISO 50001 Framework)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** is the foundational management system approach tested in KTU ESE questions.

- **Plan**: Conduct energy review, establish baseline, set objectives & KPIs.
- **Do**: Implement the energy action plan and operational controls.
- **Check**: Monitor, measure, and analyse energy performance against KPIs.
- **Act**: Take corrective actions to address non-conformities and drive continual improvement.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following equations are the most-tested formulas in KTU 2024 Scheme for Energy Systems.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Legend | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P_{output}}$ | Specific Energy Consumption = Total Energy ÷ Production Output | $\text{MJ/kg}$, $\text{kWh/ton}$ |
| 2 | $ECS = \dfrac{C_{energy}}{C_{total}} \times 100$ | Energy Cost Share (%) | $\\%$ |
| 3 | $SPP = \dfrac{I}{\Delta C}$ | Simple Payback Period = Investment ÷ Annual Cost Saving | $\text{years}$ |
| 4 | $ROI = \dfrac{\Delta C}{I} \times 100$ | Return on Investment (%) | $\%$ |
| 5 | $NPV = \sum_{t=1}^{n}\dfrac{\Delta C_t}{(1+r)^t} - I_0$ | Net Present Value (Discounted Cash Flow) | ₹ (currency) |
| 6 | $E_{saved} = E_{baseline} - E_{actual}$ | Energy Saving (baseline method) | $\text{kWh}$, $\text{MJ}$ |
| 7 | $\% E_{saved} = \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{baseline}} \times 100$ | Percent Energy Saving | $\%$ |
| 8 | $CO_{2,reduced} = E_{saved} \times EF_{grid}$ | CO₂ Emission Reduction (Grid EF = 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh for India) | $\text{kg CO}_2$ |
| 9 | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{T \times N}{9550}$ | Motor Power = Torque × Speed / 9550 (SI) | $\text{kW}$ |
| 10 | $CUSUM_t = \sum_{i=1}^{t}(E_i - E_{baseline})$ | Cumulative Sum (deviation from baseline) | $\text{kWh}$ |

> **Symbols:** $I$ = Investment (₹), $\Delta C$ = Annual cost saving (₹/yr), $r$ = Discount rate, $t$ = year, $E$ = Energy, $EF$ = Emission Factor, $T$ = Torque (N·m), $N$ = Speed (RPM).

### Critical Boundary Conditions & Constants
- **Indian Grid Emission Factor (CEEF, 2023)**: $EF_{grid} = 0.79 \text{ to } 0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kWh}$ (use **0.82** unless otherwise stated).
- **Minimum attractive rate of return** in Indian industry practice: $r = 12 \text{ to } 15\%$.
- **Discounted Payback Period** = Year in which cumulative discounted cash flow first becomes non-negative.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These principles and formulas are the **operational backbone** of:
- **BEE-certified Energy Auditors** performing mandatory audits under PAT scheme.
- **ISO 50001 EnMS implementation** in large industries (steel, cement, fertilizer, paper).
- **ESCO (Energy Service Company)** business models using **shared savings** contracts.
- **Green Building certifications** such as IGBC, LEED, GRIHA — all require energy M&T.
- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0)** where IoT sensors feed real-time energy dashboards.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Simple Payback Period (SPP)

The Simple Payback Period is the most fundamental financial metric in energy management. It represents the **time required to recover the initial investment** purely from annual cost savings, ignoring the time value of money.

### Step 1 — State the Variables
Let:
- $I_0$ = Initial capital investment for the Energy Conservation Measure (₹)
- $\Delta C$ = Net annual monetary saving (₹/year)
- $t$ = Time elapsed since implementation (years)

### Step 2 — Set Up the Recovery Condition
The investment is recovered when the **cumulative undiscounted savings equal the initial investment**:

$$
C_{cumulative}(t) = \Delta C \times t
$$

Setting cumulative savings equal to initial investment:

$$
\Delta C \times t = I_0
$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Time $t$

$$
t = \frac{I_0}{\Delta C}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Realistic Engineering Context
In practice, we also adjust for:
- Annual **inflation in energy cost** ($i_e$)
- Annual **Operation & Maintenance (O&M) cost increase** ($\Delta C_{OM}$)

Thus the engineering-corrected payback formula is:

$$
SPP_{adj} = \frac{I_0}{\Delta C \cdot (1 + i_e) - \Delta C_{OM}}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

> **Problem Statement:** A textile mill replaces 200 conventional 100 W incandescent lamps with 25 W LED lamps. The lamps operate 12 hours/day, 300 days/year. Electricity tariff is ₹8.50/kWh. Initial investment is ₹2,00,000. Find: **(a)** Annual Energy Saving, **(b)** Annual Cost Saving, **(c)** Simple Payback Period, **(d)** % Energy Saved, **(e)** Annual CO₂ Avoided.

### Given Data
- $N_{lamps} = 200$
- $P_{conv} = 100 \text{ W}$, $P_{LED} = 25 \text{ W}$
- Hours of operation $h = 12 \times 300 = 3600 \text{ hr/year}$
- Tariff $T_r = ₹8.50/\text{kWh}$
- Investment $I_0 = ₹2,00,000$
- Emission Factor $EF_{grid} = 0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2 / \text{kWh}$

---

### (a) Annual Energy Saving

**Step 1:** Compute power saved per lamp.

$$
\Delta P = P_{conv} - P_{LED} = 100 - 25 = 75 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 2:** Total power saved across all lamps.

$$
P_{total} = N_{lamps} \times \Delta P = 200 \times 75 = 15{,}000 \text{ W} = 15 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 3:** Annual energy saved.

$$
\Delta E = P_{total} \times h = 15 \text{ kW} \times 3600 \text{ hr} = 54{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

---

### (b) Annual Cost Saving

$$
\Delta C = \Delta E \times T_r = 54{,}000 \times 8.50 = ₹4{,}59{,}000/\text{year}
$$

---

### (c) Simple Payback Period

$$
SPP = \frac{I_0}{\Delta C} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{4{,}59{,}000} = 0.4357 \text{ years} \approx 5.23 \text{ months}
$$

This is an **extremely attractive payback** — typically any SPP < 2 years is a "no-brainer" for management approval.

---

### (d) % Energy Saved

Baseline energy consumption per year:

$$
E_{baseline} = N \times P_{conv} \times h = 200 \times 0.1 \times 3600 = 72{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

Percentage saving:

$$
\% E_{saved} = \frac{\Delta E}{E_{baseline}} \times 100 = \frac{54{,}000}{72{,}000} \times 100 = 75\%
$$

---

### (e) Annual CO₂ Avoided

$$
CO_{2,avoided} = \Delta E \times EF_{grid} = 54{,}000 \times 0.82 = 44{,}280 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

$$
CO_{2,avoided} \approx 44.28 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Ranking of Energy Conservation Measures

| ECM | Capital (₹) | Annual Saving (₹) | SPP (yr) | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Replace 200 LEDs | 2,00,000 | 4,59,000 | 0.44 | **A (Top)** |
| Install VFD on 50 HP motor | 3,50,000 | 1,20,000 | 2.92 | **B (Medium)** |
| Waste heat recovery (boiler) | 25,00,000 | 6,00,000 | 4.17 | **C (Long-term)** |
| Solar PV 100 kWp rooftop | 50,00,000 | 7,50,000 | 6.67 | **D (Strategic)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb:** Implement ECMs in the order **A → B → C → D**. Quick-payback projects fund the long-payback ones through internal accruals.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Energy Management Process — Top-Level Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Top Management Commitment] --> B[Energy Policy Declaration]
    B --> C[Form Energy Management Cell EMC]
    C --> D[Conduct Energy Audit]
    D --> E[Identify Baseline Specific Energy Consumption]
    E --> F[Set Energy Performance Indicators EnPIs]
    F --> G[Prioritise ECMs by Payback]
    G --> H[Implement ECMs]
    H --> I[Monitor Measure and Verify MMV]
    I --> J{Performance vs Target}
    J -- Below Target --> K[Corrective Action]
    J -- Above Target --> L[Document Best Practices]
    K --> I
    L --> M[Management Review and PDCA Continual Improvement]
    M --> F
```

---

## 4.2 ISO 50001 EnMS — PDCA Architecture Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plan: Energy Review and Baseline] --> P2[Plan: Energy Objectives and Targets]
    P2 --> D1[Do: Operational Controls and Training]
    D1 --> D2[Do: Implement Action Plan]
    D2 --> C1[Check: Monitor and Measure]
    C1 --> C2[Check: Analyse and Audit]
    C2 --> A1[Act: Non Conformity Corrective Action]
    A1 --> A2[Act: Management Review and Improvement]
    A2 --> P1
```

---

## 4.3 Energy Audit Decision Matrix (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Energy Audit Trigger]) --> T1{Investment Decision Required}
    T1 -- No --> Type1[Walk Through Audit]
    T1 -- Yes --> T2{Detailed Savings Estimate Needed}
    T2 -- Basic --> Type2[Detailed Audit with Instrumentation]
    T2 -- Accurate --> Type3[Investment Grade Audit with M and V]
    Type1 --> Out1[Report Low Cost No Cost ECMs]
    Type2 --> Out2[Report with Quantified Savings]
    Type3 --> Out3[Bankable Report for ESCO Funding]
```

---

## 4.4 Energy Monitoring & Targeting (M&T) Information Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Source1[Main LT Panel] --> Meter1[Utility Main Energy Meter kWh]
    Source2[Sub Panel 1] --> Meter2[Sub Meter 1 - HVAC]
    Source3[Sub Panel 2] --> Meter3[Sub Meter 2 - Lighting]
    Source4[Sub Panel 3] --> Meter4[Sub Meter 3 - Compressed Air]
    Source5[Sub Panel 4] --> Meter5[Sub Meter 4 - Process Loads]
    Meter1 --> EMS[EMS Data Acquisition Server]
    Meter2 --> EMS
    Meter3 --> EMS
    Meter4 --> EMS
    Meter5 --> EMS
    EMS --> Dashboard[Cloud Dashboard and Analytics]
    Dashboard --> KPI1[Specific Energy Consumption KPI]
    Dashboard --> KPI2[CUSUM Deviation KPI]
    Dashboard --> KPI3[Cost per Unit KPI]
    KPI1 --> Review[Monthly Energy Review Meeting]
    KPI2 --> Review
    KPI3 --> Review
```

---

## 4.5 Hierarchy of Energy Management Decisions (Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[L1 Strategic: Energy Policy and Targets] --> L2[L2 Tactical: Energy Audit and Planning]
    L2 --> L3[L3 Operational: Equipment Controls and ECMs]
    L3 --> L4[L4 Maintenance: Monitoring and Verification]
    L4 --> L5[L5 Behavioral: Awareness and Training]
    L5 --> L1
```
