# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Energy Management and list any four of its general principles.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Energy Management is the systematic and coordinated process of planning, controlling, and monitoring energy use in an organization to optimize consumption, reduce costs, and minimize environmental impact while maintaining the desired level of service.

**Four General Principles (any 4 of the following):**

1. **Top Management Commitment** — issuing a formal Energy Policy and allocating resources.
2. **Establishment of Energy Management Cell** — a dedicated cross-functional team.
3. **Energy Audit** — conducting walk-through, detailed, or investment-grade audits to identify saving opportunities.
4. **Energy Monitoring and Targeting (M&T)** — continuous tracking of consumption vs. baseline.
5. **Benchmarking** — comparing SEC against internal history and external industry standards.
6. **Implementation of Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** — prioritized by payback period.
7. **Training and Awareness** — engaging all employees in energy-saving behavior.
8. **Continual Improvement** — using the Plan-Do-Check-Act cycle.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often *define* energy management but **forget to list the principles**. Always ensure both parts of the question are answered.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the significance of Energy Management in modern industry.**

**Model Answer:**

Energy Management is significant because it delivers **five major benefits** to modern industry:

1. **Economic Benefit:** Energy typically accounts for **20–30% of production costs** in Indian manufacturing. Reducing it directly improves profit margins.
2. **Environmental Benefit:** Reduces greenhouse gas emissions (CO₂, SO₂, NOₓ), helping meet **ESG and climate commitments**.
3. **Regulatory Compliance:** Fulfils obligations under the **Energy Conservation Act 2001**, BEE star labelling, and the **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade)** scheme for designated consumers.
4. **Operational Reliability:** Efficient systems operate at lower temperatures, reducing wear and tear and unplanned downtime.
5. **Strategic Competitiveness:** Lower energy intensity means **lower product cost**, better market positioning, and eligibility for **green financing**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Avoid vague statements like "it saves money." Always support with **specific data** (e.g., % of cost share, grid emission factor, PAT scheme name).

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Provided)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **ten general principles of Energy Management** as adopted by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A foundry proposes to install a **VFD (Variable Frequency Drive)** on a 75 kW induction motor driving a centrifugal pump. The motor currently runs at constant full speed for 6000 hours/year. The VFD costs ₹6,00,000. By using the affinity law, the average speed reduction is 20% during operation, leading to an estimated power reduction of **49%** (cube law). Electricity tariff is ₹9/kWh.
Calculate: **(i)** Annual energy saved in kWh, **(ii)** Annual cost saving in ₹, **(iii)** Simple Payback Period, **(iv)** % energy saved. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

##### Part (a) — Ten Principles of Energy Management

The Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India, has formalized **ten general principles** that constitute the foundation of an effective energy management program:

1. **Top Management Commitment & Energy Policy:** A written, signed energy policy must be issued by the top management, committing the organization to continual improvement.

2. **Energy Management Cell (EMC):** A dedicated, cross-functional team headed by a certified Energy Manager must be set up with clear R&R (Roles and Responsibilities).

3. **Energy Audit:** Conduct **Type-1 (Walk-through)**, **Type-2 (Detailed)**, or **Type-3 (Investment-grade)** audits to identify savings.

4. **Energy Monitoring & Targeting (M&T):** Sub-metering and continuous data logging to track consumption against a baseline.

5. **Benchmarking:** Compare **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** with historical and industry-best data.

6. **Prioritization of ECMs:** Rank Energy Conservation Measures using **SPP, NPV, IRR**.

7. **Implementation of ECMs:** Execute engineering (technology), operational (process), and maintenance measures.

8. **Training and Awareness:** Conduct programs for all employee levels.

9. **Documentation:** Maintain energy database, M&V reports using **IPMVP** protocol.

10. **Continual Improvement (PDCA):** Re-set targets annually, apply management review for sustained gains.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- Motor rating $P_{rated} = 75 \text{ kW}$
- Annual operating hours $h = 6000 \text{ hr}$
- VFD Investment $I_0 = ₹6,00,000$
- Power reduction at average load $r = 49\% = 0.49$
- Tariff $T_r = ₹9/\text{kWh}$

---

**(i) Annual Energy Saved**

**Step 1:** Compute annual baseline energy consumption at full load.

$$
E_{baseline} = P_{rated} \times h = 75 \times 6000 = 4{,}50{,}000 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply 49% reduction.

$$
\Delta E = E_{baseline} \times r = 4{,}50{,}000 \times 0.49 = 2{,}20{,}500 \text{ kWh/year}
$$

---

**(ii) Annual Cost Saving**

$$
\Delta C = \Delta E \times T_r = 2{,}20{,}500 \times 9 = ₹19{,}84{,}500/\text{year}
$$

---

**(iii) Simple Payback Period**

$$
SPP = \frac{I_0}{\Delta C} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{19{,}84{,}500} = 0.3023 \text{ years}
$$

Converting to months:

$$
SPP \approx 0.3023 \times 12 = 3.63 \text{ months}
$$

---

**(iv) % Energy Saved**

$$
\% E_{saved} = 49\% \text{ (as per affinity cube law)}
$$

(Verification: $\Delta E / E_{baseline} = 2,20,500 / 4,50,000 = 49\%$.)

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse the affinity law. Remember — **Power ∝ (Speed)³**, so a 20% speed reduction = $1 - (0.80)^3 = 1 - 0.512 = 0.488 \approx 49\%$ power reduction. Always cube the speed ratio, not the speed difference.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Describe the **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA) cycle** as applied to ISO 50001 Energy Management System (EnMS). How does it enable continual improvement in energy performance? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process industry spends ₹85,00,000/year on electrical energy, producing 4,20,000 units of output. After implementing several ECMs, the annual energy bill drops to ₹72,00,000 for the same output. Calculate: **(i)** Energy Cost Share reduction, **(ii)** Specific Energy Cost before and after, **(iii)** Annual cost saving, **(iv)** % cost saving, **(v)** Annual CO₂ reduction assuming $EF = 0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$ and blended average tariff ₹8/kWh. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution**

##### Part (a) — PDCA Cycle in ISO 50001 EnMS

The **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** is the structural framework behind **ISO 50001:2018** (Energy Management System). It drives continual improvement in **Energy Performance (EnP)**.

**1. PLAN (Establish the Energy Management System):**
- Conduct an **energy review** of all current and past energy uses.
- Identify **Significant Energy Uses (SEUs)**.
- Establish an **energy baseline (EnB)** and **Energy Performance Indicators (EnPIs)**.
- Set **objectives, targets, and action plans**.

**2. DO (Implement the EnMS):**
- Execute the action plans and operational controls.
- Provide **training and competency** to employees.
- Implement **communication, documentation, and operational controls**.

**3. CHECK (Monitor and Review):**
- Monitor, measure, and analyse **EnPIs** vs. the **energy baseline**.
- Conduct **internal audits** and **non-conformity evaluations**.
- Compare actual performance against targets.

**4. ACT (Continual Improvement):**
- Address non-conformities through **corrective actions**.
- Conduct **management reviews** to decide on changes to policy, objectives, or resources.
- Update the energy baseline if a structural change has occurred.
- **Iterate** back to PLAN for the next cycle.

**How it enables continual improvement:**
- Each cycle **raises the baseline** and tightens the target.
- Findings from CHECK drive refinements in PLAN.
- Creates a **closed-loop, self-correcting management system** — preventing regression.
- Ensures **top management engagement** through periodic management reviews.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- Initial annual cost $C_1 = ₹85,00,000$
- Final annual cost $C_2 = ₹72,00,000$
- Production output $P = 4,20,000 \text{ units}$
- Average tariff $T_r = ₹8/\text{kWh}$
- Emission Factor $EF = 0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$

---

**(i) Energy Cost Share (ECS) Reduction**

The absolute reduction in energy cost:

$$
\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = 85,00,000 - 72,00,000 = ₹13,00,000/\text{year}
$$

---

**(ii) Specific Energy Cost (SECost) Before and After**

**Before ECMs:**

$$
SEC_{before} = \frac{C_1}{P} = \frac{85,00,000}{4,20,000} = ₹20.24 \text{ per unit}
$$

**After ECMs:**

$$
SEC_{after} = \frac{C_2}{P} = \frac{72,00,000}{4,20,000} = ₹17.14 \text{ per unit}
$$

---

**(iii) Annual Cost Saving** (already calculated)

$$
\Delta C = ₹13,00,000/\text{year}
$$

---

**(iv) % Cost Saving**

$$
\% \text{Cost Saving} = \frac{\Delta C}{C_1} \times 100 = \frac{13,00,000}{85,00,000} \times 100 = 15.29\%
$$

---

**(v) Annual CO₂ Reduction**

**Step 1:** Compute initial annual energy consumption in kWh.

$$
E_1 = \frac{C_1}{T_r} = \frac{85,00,000}{8} = 10,62,500 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute final annual energy consumption in kWh.

$$
E_2 = \frac{C_2}{T_r} = \frac{72,00,000}{8} = 9,00,000 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute energy reduction.

$$
\Delta E = E_1 - E_2 = 10,62,500 - 9,00,000 = 1,62,500 \text{ kWh}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute CO₂ reduction.

$$
CO_{2,reduced} = \Delta E \times EF = 1,62,500 \times 0.82 = 1,33,250 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

Converting:

$$
CO_{2,reduced} = 133.25 \text{ tonnes CO}_2/\text{year}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to convert the **cost in ₹ to kWh** using the tariff before multiplying by the **emission factor**. Skipping this conversion step loses 2 full marks. Also, always state the **CEEF (Central Electricity Authority Emission Factor)** value used — 0.82 kg CO₂/kWh is the standard for the Indian grid in 2023.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- **Definition (must remember):** Energy Management = systematic planning, control & monitoring of energy use to deliver required services at minimum cost & environmental impact.
- **Significance rests on 5 pillars:** Economic, Environmental, Regulatory, Operational, Strategic.
- **10 Principles of Energy Management** (BEE) — memorize the sequence: *Commitment → EMC → Audit → M&T → Benchmarking → Prioritization → Implementation → Training → Documentation → Continual Improvement*.
- **Three Audit Types:** Walk-through (Type-1), Detailed (Type-2), Investment-grade (Type-3).
- **PDCA Cycle** = ISO 50001 backbone — *Plan-Do-Check-Act* — exam-favourite topic.
- **SPP Formula:** $SPP = I_0 / \Delta C$ — in years.
- **Affinity Law (Affinity Cube Law):** $P \propto N^3$ — 20% speed reduction = ~49% power reduction.
- **Indian Grid Emission Factor:** $EF_{grid} \approx 0.82 \text{ kg CO}_2/\text{kWh}$.
- **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** = Energy ÷ Production Output — the **most cited KPI**.
- **Energy Cost Share** in Indian industry = **20–30%** of operating cost.
- **"Low-hanging fruits" first** — ECMs with SPP < 2 years are quick wins.
- **Discounting matters for long-life projects** — use NPV for projects > 5 years.
- **Always carry units** through every calculation step in the answer script.
- **Always end with a managerial/strategic statement** — KTU examiners reward application-oriented answers.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Audit - Types and Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Audit - Types and Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (Energy Audit)**
> An **Energy Audit** is a systematic, planned, and documented procedure carried out by qualified energy auditors/engineers to evaluate the energy efficiency, energy consumption profile, and energy wastage of a system, building, process, or facility. It involves **measurement, analysis, and verification** of energy flows with the objective of identifying **Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** and recommending a cost-effective Energy Conservation Plan.

According to the **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India** — referenced under the **Energy Conservation Act, 2001 (EC Act)** — an energy audit is the *“systematic verification, monitoring, and analysis of the use of energy”* to identify energy losses and recommend improvements, ensuring that energy input is optimally utilized.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (PERAT637), the energy audit is the **bridge between theoretical energy management principles (Modules 1–3) and actual on-site implementation (Module 5: Renewable and Alternative Systems).**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🏥 Analogy: The "Energy Doctor"**
> Think of an energy audit exactly like a **medical check-up for a building or factory**:
> - The **building/factory** is the *patient*.
> - The **energy bill** is the *symptom* (the ache/pain).
> - The **Energy Auditor** is the *doctor*.
> - The **instruments (power meters, thermometers, flow meters)** are the doctor's *stethoscope, BP monitor, and blood test kit*.
> - The **Audit Report** is the *medical prescription*.
> - **Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** are the *medicines and lifestyle changes* recommended.
>
> Just as you wouldn't take random medicines without a diagnosis, a company should not randomly invest in solar panels or LED lights without first understanding **where, how, and why** energy is being wasted.

## 1.3 Primary Objectives of an Energy Audit

| Objective | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :--- |
| Identify **wasteful energy use** | Quantifies kWh/MJ losses in equipment, processes, lighting |
| Establish an **energy baseline** | Reference for future performance comparison |
| Recommend **Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** | Actionable engineering modifications |
| Quantify **savings potential (kWh + ₹)** | Justifies capital investment decisions |
| Ensure **regulatory compliance** | Adheres to EC Act 2001, ISO 50001, BEE Star ratings |
| Improve **Energy Performance Index (EPI)** | Lower EPI = Greater efficiency |

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics Used in Audits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following metrics are **MANDATORY for KTU numericals** (frequently asked in ESE):
> - **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC):** Energy used per unit of production → $\frac{kWh}{tonne}$, $\frac{MJ}{kg}$
> - **Energy Performance Index (EPI):** Energy used per unit built-up area per month → $\frac{kWh}{m^2/month}$
> - **Plant Load Factor (PLF):** Actual energy used / Maximum possible energy
> - **Energy Cost to Production Ratio:** Energy cost as % of total production cost
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Energy Audits

Energy audits are classified into **four standard types** based on the **depth of analysis, cost, and time required**. The classification follows **ASHRAE Standard 100 (Energy Conservation in Existing Buildings)** and the **BEE Certified Energy Auditor (CEA) framework** accepted in India.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The most frequently asked classification question in ESE is the **comparison between Walk-through, Mini-audit, Comprehensive, and Investment-Grade Audit** — especially tabulating their differences.

### 2.1.1 Type 1 — Walk-Through (Preliminary) Audit

| Feature | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Depth** | Surface-level visual inspection |
| **Duration** | 1–3 days |
| **Cost** | Lowest (₹20,000 – ₹50,000 typical) |
| **Instruments** | Minimal — only visual + utility bills |
| **Output** | List of low-cost/no-cost ECMs |
| **Auditor Skill** | Junior energy auditor or intern |
| **When Used** | First-time site visit; pre-screening before a detailed audit |

### 2.1.2 Type 2 — Mini Energy Audit (Mini Audit)

| Feature | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Depth** | Slightly deeper; short-term measurements (1–2 weeks) |
| **Duration** | 1–2 weeks |
| **Cost** | Moderate (₹1–3 lakh typical) |
| **Instruments** | Portable power analyzer, tachometer, basic logging |
| **Output** | Major ECMs with rough payback estimates |
| **Auditor Skill** | Certified Energy Auditor (CEA) |
| **When Used** | Small/medium industries; hospitals; hotels |

### 2.1.3 Type 3 — Comprehensive (Detailed) Audit

| Feature | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Depth** | In-depth engineering analysis |
| **Duration** | 2–6 weeks |
| **Cost** | High (₹3–10 lakh typical) |
| **Instruments** | Power quality analyzers, thermal imagers, flow meters, flue gas analyzers, lux meters, data loggers |
| **Output** | Detailed report with calibrated energy models, cost-benefit analysis, monitoring & verification (M&V) plan |
| **Auditor Skill** | Lead Certified Energy Auditor (CEA) + multi-disciplinary team |
| **When Used** | Large industries, ESCO projects, ISO 50001 implementation |

### 2.1.4 Type 4 — Investment-Grade Audit (IGA)

| Feature | Description |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Depth** | Highest precision; bankable report |
| **Duration** | 1–6 months |
| **Cost** | Very high (₹10–50 lakh typical) |
| **Instruments** | Full range + long-term data loggers (3–6 months) |
| **Output** | Investment-grade report with NPV, IRR, risk analysis, financing options |
| **Auditor Skill** | Lead CEA + financial analyst + process specialist |
| **When Used** | ESCO contracts, large capital projects, Public-Private Partnerships (PPP) |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are HIGH-YIELD for KTU 2024 ESE numericals — memorize all units and standard conditions.**

| Sl. | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Units | Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) | $SEC = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P}$ | $\frac{kWh}{unit}$ or $\frac{MJ}{kg}$ | Compare process efficiency |
| 2 | Energy Performance Index (EPI) | $EPI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{A \times t}$ | $\frac{kWh}{m^{2} \cdot month}$ | Buildings, hospitals |
| 3 | Plant Load Factor (PLF) | $PLF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{max}}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Power plants |
| 4 | Plant Utilization Factor (PUF) | $PUF = \dfrac{E_{actual}}{E_{rated} \times hours}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Boilers, motors |
| 5 | Simple Payback Period (SPP) | $SPP = \dfrac{I}{\Delta C}$ | years | Quick screening of ECMs |
| 6 | Net Present Value (NPV) | $NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{B_{t}}{(1+r)^{t}} - I$ | ₹ or $ | Investment-grade analysis |
| 7 | Internal Rate of Return (IRR) | $0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{B_{t}}{(1+IRR)^{t}} - I$ | % | Compare competing projects |
| 8 | Energy Cost Reduction (%) | $\%Saving = \dfrac{E_{old} - E_{new}}{E_{old}} \times 100$ | % | Audit impact evaluation |
| 9 | Power Factor Penalty/Correction | $Q = P \tan(\phi)$ | kVAr | Electricity bills |
| 10 | Carbon Emission Reduction | $CO_{2} = E_{saved} \times EF$ | tonnes | Sustainability reporting |
| 11 | Boiler Efficiency (Direct) | $\eta_{b} = \dfrac{m_{s}(h_{s} - h_{fw})}{m_{f} \times GCV_{f}} \times 100$ | % | Steam systems |
| 12 | Motor Efficiency | $\eta_{m} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100$ | % | Electric motors |

> **Notation used in formulas:**
> - $E_{total}$ = Total energy consumed, $P$ = Production output
> - $A$ = Floor area, $t$ = time period
> - $I$ = Initial investment (₹), $\Delta C$ = Annual cost saving (₹/year)
> - $B_{t}$ = Net cash flow in year $t$, $r$ = Discount rate, $n$ = project life
> - $EF$ = Emission Factor (≈ $0.82\ \frac{kg\ CO_2}{kWh}$ for Indian grid)
> - $m_s, m_f$ = mass flow of steam / fuel, $h_s, h_{fw}$ = enthalpy of steam / feed water
> - $GCV_f$ = Gross Calorific Value of fuel

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Applications

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry relevance of energy audits in KTU 2024 perspective:**
> 1. **Industrial Sector** (Cement, Steel, Fertilizer): SEC reduction of 10–20% achievable.
> 2. **Commercial Buildings** (Malls, IT Parks): EPI benchmarking against ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code).
> 3. **Healthcare** (Hospitals): Continuous load monitoring for life-safety critical equipment.
> 4. **ESCO (Energy Service Company) Projects**: Audits form the legal and financial foundation for **shared-savings** contracts.
> 5. **ISO 50001 Energy Management Systems**: Energy audit is a mandatory **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** step.
> 6. **PAT (Perform, Achieve, Trade) Scheme** by BEE: Industries exceeding SEC targets earn **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)** — directly tied to audit data.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Procedure & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard Energy Audit Methodology (BEE 7-Phase Approach)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India**, recommends a **7-phase systematic procedure** for conducting a comprehensive energy audit. This is the **exact framework KTU expects** in Part B (14-mark) questions.

### **Phase 1 — Pre-Audit Phase (Planning & Scoping)**

| Step | Activity | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1.1 | Define **audit scope, boundaries, and objectives** | Scope document |
| 1.2 | Collect **12 months of electricity/fuel bills** | Energy cost database |
| 1.3 | Gather **plant process flow diagrams (PFDs)** | System map |
| 1.4 | List all **major energy-consuming equipment** | Equipment inventory |
| 1.5 | Identify **key personnel** and assign audit team | Team charter |

### **Phase 2 — Planning & Detailed Data Collection**

| Step | Activity | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 2.1 | Develop **data collection forms** (DCS) | Standardized forms |
| 2.2 | Plan **measurement campaign** (sensors, loggers) | Measurement matrix |
| 2.3 | Decide **measurement duration** (min. 1 week for variable loads) | Monitoring plan |
| 2.4 | Coordinate **shutdown windows** for tests | Test schedule |

### **Phase 3 — Site Survey and Measurements**

| Step | Activity | Instruments Used |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 3.1 | **Visual inspection** of all major equipment | Checklist |
| 3.2 | **Power quality analysis** (V, I, kW, kVA, kVAr, PF, THD) | Power analyzer (e.g., Fluke 435) |
| 3.3 | **Light level measurement** in workspaces | Lux meter |
| 3.4 | **Temperature profiling** (surfaces, steam lines) | Infrared thermometer / thermal imager |
| 3.5 | **Flow measurement** of water, steam, air | Ultrasonic flow meter, pitot tube |
| 3.6 | **Combustion analysis** in boilers/furnaces | Flue gas analyzer (O₂, CO, CO₂, temperature) |
| 3.7 | **Compressed air leak audit** | Ultrasonic leak detector |
| 3.8 | **HVAC performance check** | Manometer, anemometer |

### **Phase 4 — Data Analysis & Energy Balance**

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **analytical heart** of the audit. An **Energy Balance** is developed to track 100% of energy input and distribute it across all end-uses (lighting, motors, HVAC, process heating, etc.).

**Step-by-step construction of an Energy Balance:**

1. Total energy input $E_{in} = E_{elec} + E_{fuel} + E_{steam} + E_{renewable}$ (in **MJ/year** or **toe/year**)
2. Convert all forms to a common unit (usually **MJ**):

$$
1\ kWh = 3.6\ MJ \quad ; \quad 1\ toe = 41.868\ GJ
$$

3. Allocate to **end-uses** $E_{i}$:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_{i} = E_{in}
$$

4. Identify **losses** $L$ (waste heat, leaks, inefficiencies):

$$
\eta_{system} = \frac{E_{useful}}{E_{in}} = 1 - \frac{L}{E_{in}}
$$

### **Phase 5 — Identification of Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)**

ECMs are classified into three categories:

| Category | Description | Typical SPP | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Low-Cost / No-Cost** | Operational, zero-investment | < 6 months | Switching off idle motors, thermostat setpoints |
| **Medium-Cost** | Retrofit modifications | 6 months – 2 years | VFD installation, insulation, capacitor banks |
| **High-Cost** | Major capital investment | 2 – 10 years | Boiler replacement, cogeneration, roof-top solar |

### **Phase 6 — Economic Analysis of ECMs**

**Step-by-step Payback Calculation (Sample):**

> A factory installs a **Variable Frequency Drive (VFD)** on a 30 kW motor.
> - Motor runs 6000 hours/year at 80% average load
> - Estimated energy saving = **25%**
> - Investment = ₹2,50,000
> - Electricity cost = ₹8/kWh

**Step 1: Calculate annual energy saved**

$$
E_{saved} = P \times Load\ Factor \times Hours \times \% Saving
$$

$$
E_{saved} = 30 \times 0.80 \times 6000 \times 0.25 = 36000\ kWh/year
$$

**Step 2: Calculate annual monetary saving**

$$
\Delta C = 36000 \times 8 = \text{₹ } 2{,}88{,}000/year
$$

**Step 3: Compute Simple Payback Period (SPP)**

$$
SPP = \frac{I}{\Delta C} = \frac{250000}{288000} = 0.87\ years \approx 10.4\ months
$$

**Step 4: Decision rule**
> Since $SPP \leq 2$ years $\Rightarrow$ **Recommended for immediate implementation.**

### **Phase 7 — Reporting, Implementation & Follow-up (M&V)**

| Deliverable | Content |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Executive Summary** | Top 5 ECMs with ROI |
| **Detailed Report** | Energy balance, calculations, baselines |
| **M&V Plan** | ISO 50015 / IPMVP framework for verification |
| **Implementation Roadmap** | Phased action plan with timelines |
| **Training Module** | Awareness for plant operators |
| **Post-Implementation Review** | Verify actual vs. predicted savings |

## 3.2 Sample Numerical Problem with Full Derivation

> **Question:** A textile mill consumes 8,50,000 kWh/year and produces 4,500 tonnes of fabric/year. After implementing audit recommendations, the consumption drops to 7,20,000 kWh/year with the same output. Calculate: (a) Original SEC, (b) New SEC, (c) % reduction in energy use, (d) Annual monetary saving if electricity cost is ₹7.50/kWh.

**Solution:**

**(a) Original SEC:**

$$
SEC_{old} = \frac{8{,}50{,}000\ kWh}{4500\ tonnes} = 188.89\ \frac{kWh}{tonne}
$$

**(b) New SEC:**

$$
SEC_{new} = \frac{7{,}20{,}000\ kWh}{4500\ tonnes} = 160.00\ \frac{kWh}{tonne}
$$

**(c) Percentage Reduction in Energy:**

$$
\% Reduction = \frac{850000 - 720000}{850000} \times 100 = 15.29\%
$$

**(d) Annual Monetary Saving:**

$$
\Delta C = (850000 - 720000) \times 7.50 = 130000 \times 7.50 = \text{₹ } 9{,}75{,}000/year
$$

**Conclusion:** The energy audit delivered a **15.29% energy reduction** and saved the mill **₹9.75 lakh per year**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students confuse the **four audit types** — remember the *depth, duration, and cost* increase progressively from Walk-through → Mini → Comprehensive → IGA.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using **wrong units** in SEC (use kWh/tonne, NOT kWh/tonne/hour).
> - **Common Mistake 3:** In Payback problems, **confusing "Investment" with "Annual Saving"** — write $SPP = \frac{Investment}{Annual\ Saving}$ in formula and explanation.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Forgetting to convert kWh to MJ when computing total energy balance — examiners deduct 1 mark per unit mismatch.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Writing NPV formula without explaining the discount rate **r** — always define all variables.
> - **Common Mistake 6:** Confusing **EPI (buildings)** with **SEC (industry/processes)** — both have kWh in numerator but different denominators.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**"Define the term 'Energy Audit'. List any two types of energy audits."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** An **energy audit** is a systematic, planned, and documented procedure carried out to evaluate the energy consumption pattern of a system, building, or industrial process, identify energy wastage, and recommend **Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)** to improve efficiency and reduce operating cost.
>
> **Two types (1 mark):**
> 1. **Walk-Through (Preliminary) Audit** — quick visual inspection for low-cost ECMs.
> 2. **Comprehensive Energy Audit** — detailed engineering analysis with measurements, energy balance, and M&V plan.

---

### **Question A2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between a Walk-Through Audit and a Comprehensive Energy Audit (any four points)."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark for each correct point, 0.5 for partial):**

| Parameter | Walk-Through Audit | Comprehensive Audit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Depth** | Surface-level visual | In-depth engineering analysis |
| **Duration** | 1–3 days | 2–6 weeks |
| **Instruments** | Minimal; mostly utility bills | Full range (power analyzer, thermal imager, flue gas analyzer) |
| **Output** | Preliminary list of low-cost ECMs | Calibrated energy model, M&V plan, NPV/IRR analysis |
| **Cost** | Lowest (₹20k–50k) | High (₹3L–10L) |
| **Auditor** | Junior/trained auditor | Lead Certified Energy Auditor (CEA) + team |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

### **Question B-A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **7-phase procedure of a comprehensive energy audit** as recommended by the Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), India. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A small-scale industry consumes **5,00,000 kWh per year** and produces **2500 units** of finished product. After an energy audit, the consumption reduces to **4,10,000 kWh/year** with the same output. Electricity tariff is **₹9.00/kWh**. Calculate: **(i)** Original SEC, **(ii)** New SEC, **(iii)** % energy reduction, **(iv)** Annual monetary savings, **(v)** Simple Payback Period if the audit cost was **₹3,50,000**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) 7-Phase BEE Energy Audit Procedure (7 marks — 1 mark per phase):**

1. **Phase 1 — Pre-Audit Planning:** Define scope, boundaries, objectives; collect 12 months of utility bills; form the audit team.
2. **Phase 2 — Detailed Data Collection:** Develop data collection forms, plan instrument deployment, schedule shutdown windows.
3. **Phase 3 — Site Survey & Measurements:** Use power analyzers, lux meters, thermal imagers, flue gas analyzers, flow meters, leak detectors for on-site measurements.
4. **Phase 4 — Data Analysis & Energy Balance:** Construct a Sankey diagram; compute SEC, EPI, PLF; identify losses.
5. **Phase 5 — Identification of ECMs:** Classify into Low-Cost (no investment), Medium-Cost (VFDs, insulation), and High-Cost (boiler replacement, solar).
6. **Phase 6 — Economic Analysis:** Compute Simple Payback Period (SPP), Net Present Value (NPV), and Internal Rate of Return (IRR) for each ECM.
7. **Phase 7 — Reporting, Implementation & M&V:** Submit detailed report, implement priority ECMs, monitor & verify actual savings using IPMVP / ISO 50015.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks — 1.4 marks per sub-part):**

**(i) Original SEC:**

$$
SEC_{old} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{2500} = 200\ \frac{kWh}{unit}
$$

**(ii) New SEC:**

$$
SEC_{new} = \frac{4{,}10{,}000}{2500} = 164\ \frac{kWh}{unit}
$$

**(iii) % Energy Reduction:**

$$
\%\ Reduction = \frac{500000 - 410000}{500000} \times 100 = 18.00\%
$$

**(iv) Annual Monetary Savings:**

$$
\Delta C = (500000 - 410000) \times 9 = 90000 \times 9 = \text{₹ } 8{,}10{,}000/year
$$

**(v) Simple Payback Period (SPP):**

$$
SPP = \frac{3{,}50{,}000}{8{,}10{,}000} = 0.432\ years \approx 5.19\ months
$$

> **Conclusion:** The audit paid back its entire cost in **less than 6 months** — a high-ROI project.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** With neat schematic diagrams, classify the **types of energy audits** based on the **ASHRAE framework**. Compare their **depth, duration, cost, instruments, and output.** **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A commercial building has a **built-up area of 5000 m²**. Its annual electricity consumption is **6,00,000 kWh**. After implementing ECMs from the audit, the consumption becomes **4,80,000 kWh/year**.
> Calculate: **(i)** Original EPI (in $\frac{kWh}{m^2 \cdot month}$), **(ii)** New EPI, **(iii)** Annual savings in ₹ (tariff ₹8.50/kWh), **(iv)** Annual CO₂ reduction in tonnes (use $EF = 0.82\ \frac{kg\ CO_2}{kWh}$). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) ASHRAE-based Classification of Energy Audits (7 marks):**

The four types are:

1. **Walk-Through (Preliminary) Audit** — Lowest depth, 1–3 days, minimal instruments, output: list of low-cost ECMs.
2. **Mini Energy Audit** — 1–2 weeks, portable instruments, output: major ECMs with rough payback.
3. **Comprehensive Energy Audit** — 2–6 weeks, full instrument suite, output: calibrated energy model, M&V plan, NPV/IRR.
4. **Investment-Grade Audit** — 1–6 months, long-term monitoring, output: bankable report for ESCO/PPP contracts.

**(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for schematic.)**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Original EPI:**

$$
EPI_{old} = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{5000 \times 12} = 10.00\ \frac{kWh}{m^2 \cdot month}
$$

**(ii) New EPI:**

$$
EPI_{new} = \frac{4{,}80{,}000}{5000 \times 12} = 8.00\ \frac{kWh}{m^2 \cdot month}
$$

**(iii) Annual Monetary Savings:**

$$
\Delta C = (600000 - 480000) \times 8.50 = 120000 \times 8.50 = \text{₹ } 10{,}20{,}000/year
$$

**(iv) Annual CO₂ Emission Reduction:**

$$
CO_{2\ saved} = 120000 \times 0.82 = 98400\ kg = 98.4\ tonnes\ CO_{2}/year
$$

> **Conclusion:** The audit not only saves **₹10.2 lakh/year** but also reduces the building's **carbon footprint by 98.4 tonnes CO₂ annually** — supporting both economic and ESG goals.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚡ Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 ESE**

- ✅ **Energy Audit** = Systematic measurement, analysis & verification of energy use to recommend ECMs.
- ✅ **Four Audit Types (in increasing depth):** Walk-Through → Mini → Comprehensive → Investment-Grade.
- ✅ **7-Phase BEE Procedure:** Pre-Audit → Planning → Survey → Analysis → ECMs → Economics → Reporting/M&V.
- ✅ **Key Metrics:** SEC (industry), EPI (buildings), PLF (power plants), SPP, NPV, IRR.
- ✅ **Standard Conversion:** $1\ kWh = 3.6\ MJ$; $1\ toe = 41.868\ GJ$.
- ✅ **Simple Payback Period:** $SPP = \frac{Investment}{Annual\ Saving}$ (years).
- ✅ **Carbon Emission Factor (India):** $EF \approx 0.82\ \frac{kg\ CO_2}{kWh}$.
- ✅ **Boiler Efficiency (Direct Method):** $\eta_{b} = \frac{m_{s}(h_{s} - h_{fw})}{m_{f} \times GCV_{f}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Audit Instruments:** Power analyzer, thermal imager, lux meter, flue gas analyzer, ultrasonic leak detector, flow meter.
- ✅ **M&V Standard:** IPMVP (International Performance Measurement & Verification Protocol) / ISO 50015.
- ✅ **Indian Regulatory Body:** Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE) under EC Act 2001.
- ✅ **Voluntary Standard:** ISO 50001 (Energy Management System) — uses PDCA cycle.
- ✅ **PAT Scheme:** Industries saving more SEC than targets earn **Energy Saving Certificates (ESCerts)**.
- ✅ **Common mistake trap:** Always state **units** in SEC/EPI/PLF/SPP answers — examiners deduct marks for missing units.
- ✅ **Examiner tip:** When asked "compare audit types," always use a **markdown table** with at least 5 rows of comparison.
- ✅ **Numerical tip:** In payback problems, ensure investment and annual savings are in the **same currency** before division.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Audit Report
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Energy Audit Report

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

An **Energy Audit Report** is a structured, systematic, and documented technical document that comprehensively records the findings, methodologies, analyses, and recommendations arising from a formal energy audit conducted at a facility, plant, building, or industrial process. It serves as the official deliverable of the energy auditing process and acts as a roadmap for energy management decisions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** An Energy Audit Report is a formal technical document prepared after systematically surveying, analyzing, and benchmarking the energy use of a system, identifying Energy Conservation Opportunities (ECOs), quantifying potential savings, and recommending actionable measures with cost-benefit justification.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an Energy Audit Report like a **doctor's medical prescription after a full-body checkup**:

| Medical Checkup | Energy Audit Report |
|---|---|
| Patient's health history | Facility's energy consumption history |
| Diagnostic tests (blood, X-ray) | Measurements (power, temperature, flow) |
| Doctor's diagnosis | Identification of energy wastage |
| Prescription of medicines | Recommended Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs) |
| Estimated recovery time | Payback period of investments |
| Follow-up appointments | Monitoring & Verification (M&V) plan |

Just as a patient cannot self-diagnose accurately, an organization cannot fully identify its energy inefficiencies without a structured audit followed by a professional report.

### 1.3 Purpose & Importance

The Energy Audit Report is important for:

- **Quantifying** current energy consumption patterns
- **Identifying** wasteful practices and inefficient equipment
- **Recommending** cost-effective Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)
- **Prioritizing** investments based on economic viability
- **Complying** with statutory regulations (e.g., Energy Conservation Act, 2001 of India)
- **Establishing** baseline data for continuous monitoring

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference:** The **Bureau of Energy Efficiency (BEE), Government of India** mandates energy audits for designated consumers under the **Perform, Achieve and Trade (PAT) scheme**. The report must conform to the format specified in **BEE's Manual on Energy Audit**.

### 1.4 Types of Energy Audits (Recap from Module Context)

The report format and depth vary with the audit type:

- **Walk-through Audit (Level 1):** Quick visual inspection, minimal instrumentation, qualitative findings.
- **Standard Audit (Level 2):** Detailed measurements, energy balance, payback analysis.
- **Investment-grade Audit (Level 3):** Engineering-grade precision with detailed financial modeling, risk analysis, and M&V protocols.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structure of a Standard Energy Audit Report

A KTU-standard Energy Audit Report follows a logical top-down structure. Each section builds upon the previous one.

#### Section A: Executive Summary
- Concise overview (typically **1–2 pages**)
- Key findings: total energy consumption, cost, % savings potential
- Recommendations with highest ROI
- Intended for senior management

#### Section B: Introduction & Facility Description
- Plant layout, processes, working hours
- Major equipment inventory
- Operational profile (daily, seasonal variation)

#### Section C: Methodology
- Audit scope and objectives
- Measurement instruments used (with accuracy)
- Data collection procedures
- Reference standards followed (IS, ISO 50002, BEE guidelines)

#### Section D: Energy Consumption Analysis
- Historical energy data (last 3 years minimum)
- Specific Energy Consumption (SEC) benchmarks
- Energy flow diagrams (Sankey diagrams)
- Load profiles

#### Section E: Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)
Each ECM should detail:
- Description of the measure
- Baseline energy use
- Estimated energy savings
- Implementation cost
- Annual monetary savings
- Simple Payback Period (SPP)
- Net Present Value (NPV) / Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

#### Section F: Implementation Plan & M&V
- Phased implementation schedule
- Monitoring & Verification methodology
- Roles and responsibilities

#### Section G: Appendices
- Raw measurement data
- Instrument calibration certificates
- Detailed calculation sheets

### 2.2 Key Economic Indicators Used in Reports

#### 2.2.1 Simple Payback Period (SPP)

The simplest financial metric used to evaluate ECMs:

$$SPP = \frac{\text{Total Implementation Cost}}{\text{Annual Monetary Savings}}$$

#### 2.2.2 Net Present Value (NPV)

Accounts for the time value of money over the project life:

$$NPV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + r)^t}$$

Where:
- $CF_t$ = Cash flow in year $t$ (savings − operating costs)
- $r$ = Discount rate (typically 12–15% in India)
- $n$ = Project lifetime in years

#### 2.2.3 Internal Rate of Return (IRR)

The discount rate at which NPV equals zero:

$$0 = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1 + IRR)^t}$$

#### 2.2.4 Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)

$$SEC = \frac{\text{Total Energy Consumed (kWh or MJ)}}{\text{Unit of Production or Output}}$$

For example: **kWh/ton** of cement, **MJ/kg** of steel, **kWh/m³** of water pumped.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Unit / Typical Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Simple Payback Period | $\frac{C_{inv}}{S_{annual}}$ | years | Quick screening of ECMs |
| 2 | Net Present Value | $\sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | ₹ / currency unit | Long-term profitability |
| 3 | Internal Rate of Return | Solve $NPV=0$ for $r$ | % | Compare with MARR (12–15%) |
| 4 | Specific Energy Consumption | $\frac{E_{total}}{P_{output}}$ | kWh/unit | Benchmarking |
| 5 | Energy Cost Reduction % | $\frac{C_{baseline}-C_{new}}{C_{baseline}} \times 100$ | % | Reporting impact |
| 6 | Annual Energy Savings | $E_{baseline}-E_{new}$ | kWh / MJ / toe | Quantify savings |
| 7 | CO₂ Emission Reduction | $\Delta E \times EF$ | tCO₂/year | Environmental reporting |
| 8 | Avoided Cost | $S_{annual}-O_{inc}$ | ₹/year | Net annual benefit |

Where:
- $C_{inv}$ = Investment cost
- $S_{annual}$ = Annual energy cost savings
- $CF_t$ = Net cash flow in year $t$
- $MARR$ = Minimum Attractive Rate of Return
- $EF$ = Emission factor (e.g., **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** for Indian grid average)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Energy Audit Reports are essential deliverables in:

- **Industrial Plants:** Cement, steel, fertilizers, paper, textiles
- **Commercial Buildings:** IT parks, hospitals, hotels, malls
- **Utilities:** Water pumping stations, sewage treatment, HVAC systems
- **Compliance:** PAT scheme, ECBC (Energy Conservation Building Code), ISO 50001 Energy Management Systems

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Sample Worked Example — Preparing an Energy Audit Report Section

**Problem Statement:**
A textile unit consumed **1,20,000 kWh** in one month. The baseline specific energy consumption is **2.5 kWh/kg** of fabric produced. After installing VFDs on motors, the new SEC becomes **2.1 kWh/kg**. Investment cost is **₹5,00,000**. Annual production is **5,00,000 kg**. Electricity tariff is **₹8/kWh**.

**Task:** Calculate annual energy savings, monetary savings, simple payback period, and NPV (assume $r = 12\%$, $n = 5$ years, annual maintenance = ₹20,000).

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Solution

#### Step 1: Calculate Annual Energy Consumption (Baseline)

$$E_{baseline, annual} = SEC_{baseline} \times \text{Annual Production}$$

$$E_{baseline, annual} = 2.5 \text{ kWh/kg} \times 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ kg}$$

$$E_{baseline, annual} = 12{,}50{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

#### Step 2: Calculate Annual Energy Consumption (After ECM)

$$E_{new, annual} = SEC_{new} \times \text{Annual Production}$$

$$E_{new, annual} = 2.1 \text{ kWh/kg} \times 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ kg}$$

$$E_{new, annual} = 10{,}50{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

#### Step 3: Calculate Annual Energy Savings

$$\Delta E_{annual} = E_{baseline, annual} - E_{new, annual}$$

$$\Delta E_{annual} = 12{,}50{,}000 - 10{,}50{,}000 = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

#### Step 4: Calculate Annual Monetary Savings

$$S_{annual} = \Delta E_{annual} \times \text{Tariff}$$

$$S_{annual} = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 8 = \text{₹}16{,}00{,}000/\text{year}$$

#### Step 5: Calculate Simple Payback Period

$$SPP = \frac{C_{inv}}{S_{annual}}$$

$$SPP = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{16{,}00{,}000} = 0.3125 \text{ years}$$

$$SPP \approx 3.75 \text{ months}$$

#### Step 6: Calculate Net Cash Flow Per Year

$$CF_t = S_{annual} - O_{inc}$$

$$CF_t = 16{,}00{,}000 - 20{,}000 = \text{₹}15{,}80{,}000$$

This is uniform for each year.

#### Step 7: Calculate NPV over 5 Years

$$NPV = -C_{inv} + \sum_{t=1}^{5} \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$$

Since cash flows are uniform, we use the **Present Worth of Annuity Factor**:

$$P/A = \frac{(1+r)^n - 1}{r(1+r)^n}$$

$$P/A = \frac{(1.12)^5 - 1}{0.12 \times (1.12)^5}$$

Computing $(1.12)^5$:
$$(1.12)^1 = 1.12$$
$$(1.12)^2 = 1.2544$$
$$(1.12)^3 = 1.4049$$
$$(1.12)^4 = 1.5735$$
$$(1.12)^5 = 1.7623$$

Substituting:
$$P/A = \frac{1.7623 - 1}{0.12 \times 1.7623}$$

$$P/A = \frac{0.7623}{0.2115} = 3.6048$$

#### Step 8: Compute NPV

$$NPV = -5{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}80{,}000 \times 3.6048$$

$$NPV = -5{,}00{,}000 + 56{,}95{,}584$$

$$NPV = \text{₹}51{,}95{,}584$$

Since NPV is **strongly positive**, the ECM is economically attractive.

#### Step 9: Reportable Metrics Summary

| Metric | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Annual Energy Savings | 2,00,000 kWh | 16% reduction |
| Annual Monetary Savings | ₹16,00,000 | Significant |
| Simple Payback | 0.3125 years | Very fast (< 3 years is excellent) |
| NPV @ 12%, 5 yrs | ₹51,95,584 | Highly profitable |
| CO₂ Avoided | 2,00,000 × 0.82 = 164 tCO₂/year | Environmental benefit |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Typical ECM Categories in Reports

| ECM Category | Typical SEC Reduction | Typical SPP | Priority |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lighting upgrade (LED) | 50–70% | 1–3 years | High |
| Motor VFD installation | 20–40% | 1–3 years | High |
| Boiler efficiency improvement | 5–15% | 2–4 years | High |
| Waste heat recovery | 10–30% | 1.5–3 years | High |
| Power factor correction | 1–3% direct, 5–10% indirect | < 1 year | Very High |
| HVAC optimization | 15–25% | 2–5 years | Medium |
| Renewable integration (solar) | Varies | 4–7 years | Strategic |
| Compressed air leak reduction | 10–30% | < 1 year | Very High |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Energy Audit Report

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cover Page and Document Control] --> B[Executive Summary]
    B --> C[Facility Description]
    C --> D[Audit Methodology and Instruments]
    D --> E[Energy Data Collection]
    E --> F[Energy Consumption Analysis]
    F --> G[Baseline Benchmarking]
    G --> H[Identification of ECMs]
    H --> I[Cost Benefit Analysis]
    I --> J[Simple Payback and NPV]
    J --> K[Prioritization Matrix]
    K --> L[Recommendations and Implementation Plan]
    L --> M[Monitoring and Verification Plan]
    M --> N[Appendices Raw Data and Certificates]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow for ECM Prioritization (Within Report)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[ECM Identified] --> q1{SPP less than 3 years?}
    q1 -- yes --> q2{NPV positive at MARR?}
    q1 -- no --> q4{Strategic or Mandatory ECM?}
    q2 -- yes --> rec1[Recommend Immediate Implementation]
    q2 -- no --> rec2[Reanalyze Assumptions]
    q4 -- yes --> rec3[Recommend with Justification]
    q4 -- no --> defer[Defer or Reject]
    rec1 --> monitor[Add to Monitoring Plan]
    rec2 --> monitor
    rec3 --> monitor
    defer --> monitor
```

### 4.3 Information Flow During Report Preparation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Field Measurements] --> A2[Data Logger Outputs]
    B1[Utility Bills] --> A2
    C1[Equipment Nameplates] --> A2
    A2 --> D1[Data Validation]
    D1 --> E1[Energy Balance Diagram]
    E1 --> F1[Sankey and Load Profile]
    F1 --> G1[ECM Identification]
    G1 --> H1[Economic Analysis]
    H1 --> I1[Final Report]
```

### 4.4 Sankey-Style Energy Flow Concept (Textual Representation)

```
Energy Input (100%)
    ├── Useful Output (55%)
    ├── Wastage as Heat (20%)
    ├── Losses in Distribution (10%)
    ├── Inefficient Equipment (10%)
    └── Miscellaneous (5%)
```

> [!TIP]
> When drawing Sankey diagrams in reports, ensure the **width of each flow band is proportional to the energy quantity**. This provides an immediate visual cue of where the major losses occur.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any six essential sections of a standard energy audit report.*

**Model Answer:**
A standard energy audit report contains the following essential sections:
1. Executive Summary
2. Introduction and Facility Description
3. Audit Methodology and Instrumentation
4. Energy Consumption Analysis (with Sankey diagrams)
5. Energy Conservation Measures (ECMs)
6. Cost-Benefit Analysis (SPP, NPV, IRR)
7. Recommendations and Implementation Plan
8. Monitoring and Verification Plan
9. Appendices (raw data, calibration certificates)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is the role of an executive summary in an energy audit report?*

**Model Answer:**
The executive summary provides a concise overview of the audit findings for senior management and decision-makers. It typically includes:
- Total energy consumption and cost
- Major energy-saving opportunities identified
- Recommended ECMs with payback periods
- Estimated investment required and annual savings

It allows management to take quick decisions without reading the entire detailed report.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the standard structure and content of a comprehensive energy audit report. Discuss each section in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing facility consumes **8,00,000 kWh** per year at ₹7/kWh. An energy audit suggests replacing inefficient motors with IE3 premium efficiency motors costing **₹6,00,000**, which will reduce consumption by **15%**. Annual maintenance is ₹15,000. Evaluate the project using Simple Payback and NPV over 6 years at 12% discount rate. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**
A comprehensive energy audit report follows a hierarchical structure designed for both technical and managerial readers:

1. **Cover Page & Document Control:** Title, audit date, auditor details, document version, confidentiality notice.
2. **Executive Summary:** Key findings, recommended actions, investment, and savings—concise enough for top management.
3. **Facility Description:** Plant layout, processes, operating hours, production data, equipment inventory.
4. **Audit Methodology:** Scope, instruments used (power analyzer, flue gas analyzer, lux meter), reference standards (ISO 50002, BEE guidelines).
5. **Energy Data Analysis:** Historical consumption (3-year minimum), energy balance, Sankey diagrams, load profiles, specific energy consumption.
6. **Identification of ECMs:** Description, baseline energy, proposed savings, implementation cost, payback.
7. **Economic Analysis:** SPP, NPV, IRR for each ECM with priority ranking.
8. **Implementation Plan:** Phased schedule, responsibilities, training needs.
9. **Monitoring & Verification:** KPIs, M&V methods, reporting frequency.
10. **Appendices:** Raw data, instrument calibration certificates, calculation sheets.

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given:**
- Annual consumption $E = 8{,}00{,}000$ kWh
- Tariff = ₹7/kWh
- Investment $C_{inv}$ = ₹6,00,000
- Energy reduction = 15%
- Annual maintenance = ₹15,000
- Project life $n = 6$ years, Discount rate $r = 12\%$

**Step 1: Annual Energy Savings**

$$\Delta E = 0.15 \times 8{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}20{,}000 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 2: Annual Monetary Savings**

$$S_{annual} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 7 = \text{₹}8{,}40{,}000$$

**Step 3: Simple Payback Period**

$$SPP = \frac{6{,}00{,}000}{8{,}40{,}000} = 0.7143 \text{ years}$$

$$SPP \approx 8.57 \text{ months}$$

**Step 4: Net Annual Cash Flow**

$$CF = 8{,}40{,}000 - 15{,}000 = \text{₹}8{,}25{,}000$$

**Step 5: NPV using Annuity Factor**

Compute $(1.12)^6$:
$$1.12^2 = 1.2544$$
$$1.12^3 = 1.4049$$
$$1.12^4 = 1.5735$$
$$1.12^5 = 1.7623$$
$$1.12^6 = 1.9738$$

$$P/A = \frac{1.9738 - 1}{0.12 \times 1.9738} = \frac{0.9738}{0.2369} = 4.1114$$

**Step 6: NPV**

$$NPV = -6{,}00{,}000 + 8{,}25{,}000 \times 4.1114$$

$$NPV = -6{,}00{,}000 + 33{,}91{,}905 = \text{₹}27{,}91{,}905$$

**Conclusion:** Highly attractive project — fast payback (~8.5 months) and strongly positive NPV.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Discuss the various financial evaluation methods used in an energy audit report to prioritize ECMs. Compare their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pump operates **5000 hours/year** with an efficiency of **60%**. After replacement with a high-efficiency pump (efficiency **80%**), the input power reduces. The motor rating is **20 kW**. Calculate the annual energy saved, monetary savings (₹8/kWh), simple payback (cost of new pump = ₹1,50,000), and payback in years. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

The principal financial metrics used in energy audit reports are:

1. **Simple Payback Period (SPP):**
   - $SPP = \frac{C_{inv}}{S_{annual}}$
   - *Advantage:* Simple, quick screening.
   - *Limitation:* Ignores time value of money, project life, and operating costs.

2. **Net Present Value (NPV):**
   - $NPV = -C_{inv} + \sum \frac{CF_t}{(1+r)^t}$
   - *Advantage:* Accounts for time value and full lifecycle.
   - *Limitation:* Requires discount rate estimate; sensitive to assumptions.

3. **Internal Rate of Return (IRR):**
   - Discount rate at which NPV = 0.
   - *Advantage:* Easy comparison with MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return).
   - *Limitation:* May have multiple solutions for non-conventional cash flows.

4. **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR):**
   - $BCR = \frac{PV\ of\ Benefits}{PV\ of\ Costs}$
   - *Advantage:* Indicates return per unit investment.
   - *Limitation:* Same assumptions as NPV.

5. **Life Cycle Cost (LCC):**
   - Total cost of ownership including maintenance.
   - *Advantage:* Comprehensive view.
   - *Limitation:* Data-intensive.

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- Operating hours $H = 5000$ hr/year
- Existing efficiency $\eta_1 = 60\%$
- New efficiency $\eta_2 = 80\%$
- Motor rating = 20 kW
- Tariff = ₹8/kWh
- Cost of new pump = ₹1,50,000

**Step 1: Input Power (Baseline)**

$$P_{in,1} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_1} = \frac{20}{0.60} = 33.33 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2: Input Power (After Replacement)**

$$P_{in,2} = \frac{20}{0.80} = 25.00 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Power Saved**

$$\Delta P = 33.33 - 25.00 = 8.33 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4: Annual Energy Saved**

$$\Delta E = 8.33 \times 5000 = 41{,}650 \text{ kWh/year}$$

**Step 5: Annual Monetary Savings**

$$S_{annual} = 41{,}650 \times 8 = \text{₹}3{,}33{,}200$$

**Step 6: Simple Payback Period**

$$SPP = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{3{,}33{,}200} = 0.4502 \text{ years}$$

$$SPP \approx 5.4 \text{ months}$$

**Conclusion:** Excellent project — energy savings of 41,650 kWh/year and payback in under 6 months.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes in Energy Audit Report Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include investment cost with negative sign** in NPV equation — leads to wrong sign for NPV.
> 2. **Using monthly data as annual** without conversion — produces 12× error.
> 3. **Not subtracting annual maintenance/operating cost** from savings before computing net cash flow.
> 4. **Confusing discount rate with inflation rate** — use MARR (typically 12–15% in Indian context).
> 5. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — KTU examiners strictly deduct marks for missing units.
> 6. **Listing report sections without explaining their content** — only naming sections earns partial marks; brief explanations are required.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Energy Audit Report**

- ✅ An Energy Audit Report is the **final deliverable** of the energy auditing process.
- ✅ Standard structure: **Executive Summary → Methodology → Analysis → ECMs → Cost-Benefit → M&V → Appendices**.
- ✅ Always include **Sankey diagrams** for visual energy flow representation.
- ✅ Report must contain **Specific Energy Consumption (SEC)** benchmarks.
- ✅ Key economic indicators: **SPP, NPV, IRR, BCR**.
- ✅ SPP < **3 years** is generally considered attractive in Indian industry.
- ✅ MARR (Minimum Attractive Rate of Return) for India ≈ **12–15%**.
- ✅ Standard grid emission factor ≈ **0.82 kg CO₂/kWh** (CEA, India).
- ✅ Always **validate raw data** before including it in the report.
- ✅ The report must be **action-oriented** — clear recommendations with timelines.
- ✅ Follow **BEE Manual** or **ISO 50002** format for credibility.
- ✅ M&V plan must specify **baseline, reporting period, and verification method**.
- ✅ Always include **assumptions, exclusions, and limitations** transparently.
- ✅ Appendices must contain **calibration certificates** of instruments used.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Economics: Traditional Types of Rates - Single-Part Rates - Two-Part Rates - Three-Part Rates - Numerical Problems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT637 - ENERGY SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Energy Economics: Traditional Types of Rates - Single-Part Rates - Two-Part Rates - Three-Part Rates - Numerical Problems ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition of Electrical Tariff / Rate An **electrical tariff** is a document containing the rates, charges, terms, and conditions under which electrical energy is supplied to a consumer by a utility. In the context of **Energy Economics**, the *traditional types of rates* refer to the three classical pricing structures historically used to recover the cost of generation, transmission, distribution, and a reasonable return on capital investment. These are universally recognized in power system engineering literature as: 1. **Single-Part Rate (Flat Rate / Uniform Rate)** 2. **Two-Part Rate (Hopkinson Demand Rate)** 3. **Three-Part Rate (Block Rate / Doherty Rate)** The governing cost-recovery philosophy is: the **fixed costs** of the utility (interest, depreciation, salaries) must be recovered from consumers, while the **running/energy costs** (fuel, variable O&M) must be billed in proportion to actual energy consumed. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Traditional rates are foundational to *Energy Auditing and Demand Side Management (DSM)*. Every energy auditor must understand how a consumer's bill is constructed under each rate structure to recommend cost-saving measures. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Analogy Imagine you visit a restaurant with three pricing models: - **Single-Part Rate** = A roadside *thattukada* (small eatery) that charges **one flat price per item** (say ₹20 per dosa). You pay exactly for what you eat — no separation of rent, gas, or chef salary. - **Two-Part Rate** = An AC restaurant charging a **separate entry/cover charge** (fixed, for ambience and seating) **plus** a per-dish rate (variable, for the actual food). The bigger your group (demand), the higher the cover charge. - **Three-Part Rate** = A buffet with **three slabs** — the first plate costs more, the second costs less, and beyond a certain point you get a heavy discount (block rate designed to encourage moderation). > [!NOTE] > In power engineering, the **"cover charge" is the demand charge (₹/kW)** and the **"per-dish" cost is the energy charge (₹/kWh)**. The fixed charge in a 3-part rate is like a *reservation fee* — paid regardless of consumption. ### 1.3 Why Traditional Rates Matter - They form the **revenue base** of the electricity utility. - They **shape consumer behaviour** (load shifting, demand reduction). - They are the **starting point** for advanced tariffs like Time-of-Use (ToU), peak-off-peak, and real-time pricing. - Energy auditors use them to evaluate **cost-saving opportunities** under a DSM framework. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Single-Part Rate (Flat / Uniform Rate) **Operational Logic:** - The rate (in ₹/kWh or ₹/unit) is **uniform** for all consumers of a class. - The bill is directly proportional to the energy consumed. - **No fixed charge** is levied; the per-unit rate is inflated to recover all costs (fixed + variable). - Most commonly used for **domestic (residential) consumers** because meters at homes record only energy (kWh), not maximum demand (kW or kVA). **Cost-Recovery Philosophy:** The utility must set the per-unit rate such that: $$ \text{Unit Rate} = \frac{\text{Total Annual Cost of Supply (Fixed + Variable)}}{\text{Total kWh Sold Annually}} $$ **Advantages:** - Simplest to understand, compute, and bill. - Easy to implement with simple kWh meters. - Promotes **equity for low-consumption** consumers. **Disadvantages:** - Discriminates against small consumers (they subsidize large consumers). - Does not recover the cost of providing **standby capacity** (the utility must keep the maximum demand of every consumer ready, even if used rarely). - Inefficient — does not incentivize load management. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric:** Rate is expressed in **₹/kWh** (or Rs./unit, where 1 unit = 1 kWh). **1 kWh = 1000 Wh = 3.6 MJ** of electrical energy. ### 2.2 Two-Part Rate (Hopkinson Demand Rate) **Operational Logic:** Introduced by **Dr. John Hopkinson (1892)** in England, this rate divides the bill into: 1. A **fixed charge** based on the **Maximum Demand (MD)** of the consumer, billed in **₹/kW** (or ₹/kVA). 2. A **variable (running) charge** based on the **energy consumed**, billed in **₹/kWh**. **Why Two Parts?** - The **fixed cost** of the utility (capital investment, depreciation, salaries) is independent of how much energy is sold — it depends on the **size of the plant** required to meet the peak demand. - The **running cost** (fuel) depends purely on the units generated. - So a fair tariff must recover the two costs via two corresponding charges. **Cost-Recovery Philosophy:** $$ \text{Total Bill} = (R_b \times \text{MD in kW}) + (R_e \times \text{Units in kWh}) $$ where: - $R_b$ = Rate per kW of maximum demand (₹/kW/month) - $R_e$ = Rate per kWh of energy consumed (₹/kWh) - MD = Maximum demand recorded in the billing period **Measurement Requirement:** - Requires an **MD meter** (in addition to a kWh meter) or a TOD meter that records the highest 15-minute (or 30-minute) average demand in the month. **Advantages:** - Fair to both utility and consumer. - Encourages consumers to **flatten their load curve** (lower MD → lower fixed charge). - Better cost-reflectivity. **Disadvantages:** - A small consumer with low MD but moderate energy pays a high proportion of fixed charge. - MD-based charge punishes consumers with seasonal peaks (e.g., rice mills operating only 3 months). ### 2.3 Three-Part Rate (Block Rate / Doherty Rate) **Operational Logic:** A three-part tariff contains **three components**: 1. **Fixed Charge** — A lump-sum amount (₹/month) independent of MD or units; covers metering, billing, and a portion of fixed costs. 2. **Demand Charge** — Based on maximum demand (₹/kW or ₹/kVA). 3. **Energy Charge** — Applied in **slabs (blocks)** where the per-unit rate changes with consumption level. **Slab Structure (Most Common Form):** | Slab | Consumption Range | Rate (₹/kWh) | |------|------------------|---------------| | Block 1 | 0 to 100 kWh | $r_1$ (highest) | | Block 2 | 101 to 300 kWh | $r_2$ (medium) | | Block 3 | Above 300 kWh | $r_3$ (lowest) | This is called a **decreasing block rate** — it penalizes the first units of consumption at a higher rate and rewards higher consumption at a lower rate. (Note: Inverted/declining forms also exist, where higher consumption is charged more to discourage waste — used in DSM programs.) **Cost-Recovery Philosophy:** $$ \text{Total Bill} = F + (R_b \times \text{MD}) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} (r_i \times U_i) $$ where: - $F$ = Fixed monthly charge (₹) - $R_b$ = Demand rate (₹/kW) - $r_i$ = Rate of the $i$-th block (₹/kWh) - $U_i$ = Units consumed in the $i$-th block (kWh) **Advantages:** - Most equitable of the three traditional structures. - Promotes energy conservation at the margin (the marginal kWh is expensive until the slab is crossed). - Combines simplicity of fixed charge with accuracy of block pricing. **Disadvantages:** - Most complex to compute and explain to consumers. - Requires MD metering and slab-aware billing software. - The "kink" at slab boundaries creates a discontinuity that some consumers find unfair. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner's Note:** In KTU 2024 Scheme papers, the "three-part rate" most commonly refers to the **Hopkinson rate with block energy charge**, which has three components: (1) Fixed charge, (2) Demand charge, and (3) Block-wise energy charge. Some textbooks call a *Block Tariff* (only block energy charges, no demand charge) a "two-part rate" — be precise in your answer. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Tariff Type | Formula | Unit | Components | Typical Application | |---|------------|---------|------|------------|---------------------| | 1 | Single-Part (Flat) | $\text{Bill} = r \times U$ | ₹/kWh | 1 (energy only) | Domestic, small shops | | 2 | Two-Part (Hopkinson) | $\text{Bill} = (R_b \times \text{MD}) + (R_e \times U)$ | ₹/kW + ₹/kWh | 2 (demand + energy) | Industrial, commercial | | 3 | Three-Part (Block + Demand + Fixed) | $\text{Bill} = F + (R_b \times \text{MD}) + \sum r_i U_i$ | ₹ + ₹/kW + ₹/kWh | 3 (fixed + demand + slab energy) | HT industrial, bulk consumers | | 4 | Average Rate | $\text{Avg Rate} = \dfrac{\text{Total Bill}}{\text{Total kWh}}$ | ₹/kWh | — | Used to compare tariffs | | 5 | Load Factor | $\text{LF} = \dfrac{\text{Avg Demand}}{\text{MD}}$ | ratio (0–1) | — | Determines billing efficiency | | 6 | Diversity Factor | $\text{DF} = \dfrac{\sum \text{Individual MDs}}{\text{Maximum System Demand}}$ | ratio (>1) | — | System planning | | 7 | MD = kVA (Apparent) | $\text{MD (kVA)} = \dfrac{\text{MD (kW)}}{\text{pf}}$ | kVA | — | When rate is in ₹/kVA | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion Reminder:** When the rate is specified in **₹/kVA**, the demand in kW must be converted using the operating **power factor (pf)**: > $$\text{MD (kVA)} = \frac{\text{MD (kW)}}{\cos\phi}$$ ### 2.5 Real-World Utility of These Rate Structures - **Utility Revenue Planning:** Each structure gives the utility a different cost-recovery profile. CESC, KSEB, Tata Power use combinations of these for different consumer categories. - **DSM & Energy Auditing:** Energy auditors advise clients to **shift loads off-peak** to reduce the demand charge in a Hopkinson tariff. - **Renewable Integration:** Modern tariffs (ToU, dynamic pricing) are evolutions of these traditional structures, adding a *time dimension* to the energy charge. - **Cross-Subsidy:** The block rate is a deliberate tool for **lifeline pricing** — the first block (50–100 units) is subsidized for BPL (Below Poverty Line) households. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Numerical Problem 1 (KTU Standard — Compare All Three Rates) > **Problem Statement (KTU Model):** > A small industry has the following consumption data: > - Maximum Demand (MD) = **200 kW** > - Average Load = **100 kW** > - Operating hours per month = **400 hours** > - Power Factor = **0.8 lagging** > > Compute the monthly electricity bill and the **average rate per kWh** under each of the following tariff options: > 1. **Single-Part (Flat) Rate:** ₹ 3.50 / kWh > 2. **Two-Part (Hopkinson) Rate:** ₹ 75 / kW of MD + ₹ 0.80 / kWh > 3. **Three-Part (Block + Demand + Fixed) Rate:** > - Fixed charge: ₹ 500 / month > - Demand charge: ₹ 50 / kW > - First 5,000 kWh @ ₹ 1.20 / kWh > - Next 20,000 kWh @ ₹ 1.00 / kWh > - Beyond 25,000 kWh @ ₹ 0.70 / kWh --- #### **Step A — Compute Total Energy Consumed per Month** $$ \begin{aligned} E_{\text{monthly}} &= \text{Average Load} \times \text{Operating Hours per Month} \\ &= 100\ \text{kW} \times 400\ \text{h} \\ &= 40{,}000\ \text{kWh} \end{aligned} $$ #### **Step B — Bill under Single-Part (Flat) Rate** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bill}_1 &= r \times E_{\text{monthly}} \\ &= 3.50\ \text{₹/kWh} \times 40{,}000\ \text{kWh} \\ &= ₹\ 1{,}40{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ Average rate = ₹ 1,40,000 / 40,000 = **₹ 3.50 / kWh** (trivially equal to the flat rate). #### **Step C — Bill under Two-Part (Hopkinson) Rate** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bill}_2 &= (R_b \times \text{MD}) + (R_e \times E_{\text{monthly}}) \\ &= (75\ \text{₹/kW} \times 200\ \text{kW}) + (0.80\ \text{₹/kWh} \times 40{,}000\ \text{kWh}) \\ &= ₹\ 15{,}000 + ₹\ 32{,}000 \\ &= ₹\ 47{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ Average rate = ₹ 47,000 / 40,000 = **₹ 1.175 / kWh**. #### **Step D — Bill under Three-Part (Block + Demand + Fixed) Rate** **Step D1 — Compute demand and fixed charges:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Fixed Charge} &= ₹\ 500 \\ \text{Demand Charge} &= ₹\ 50\ /\ \text{kW} \times 200\ \text{kW} = ₹\ 10{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step D2 — Compute the energy charge using slabs (cumulative 40,000 kWh):** - First 5,000 kWh: $5{,}000 \times 1.20 = ₹\ 6{,}000$ - Next 20,000 kWh (cumulative → 25,000 kWh): $20{,}000 \times 1.00 = ₹\ 20{,}000$ - Beyond 25,000 kWh: $(40{,}000 - 25{,}000) = 15{,}000\ \text{kWh}$ remaining. $15{,}000 \times 0.70 = ₹\ 10{,}500$ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Energy Charge (slab total)} &= ₹\ 6{,}000 + ₹\ 20{,}000 + ₹\ 10{,}500 = ₹\ 36{,}500 \end{aligned} $$ **Step D3 — Total Three-Part Bill:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bill}_3 &= F + (R_b \times \text{MD}) + \sum r_i U_i \\ &= ₹\ 500 + ₹\ 10{,}000 + ₹\ 36{,}500 \\ &= ₹\ 47{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ Average rate = ₹ 47,000 / 40,000 = **₹ 1.175 / kWh**. #### **Step E — Comparative Analysis Matrix** | Tariff Option | Total Bill (₹) | Average Rate (₹/kWh) | Verdict | |---------------|---------------:|---------------------:|---------| | Single-Part (Flat @ ₹3.50) | 1,40,000 | 3.500 | **Most expensive** for this consumer | | Two-Part (Hopkinson) | 47,000 | 1.175 | **Best for high-MD, high-energy** consumers | | Three-Part (Block + Demand + Fixed) | 47,000 | 1.175 | **Same effective rate** for this load profile | > [!NOTE] > **Analytical Insight:** For a *high-load-factor* industry (LF = 100/200 = **0.5** here, which is typical), the Two-Part and Three-Part rates yield equivalent average rates because the block structure in option (3) was designed to be revenue-neutral at this consumption level. The Single-Part rate is **3× more expensive** because it loads ALL fixed costs onto the variable per-unit rate. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem 2 (Slab Energy Only — Without Demand Charge) > **Problem Statement (KTU Model):** > A residential consumer uses **350 kWh** in a month under the following tariff: > - Fixed charge: ₹ 80 / month > - Energy charge: First 100 kWh @ ₹ 4.00 / kWh; Next 100 kWh @ ₹ 5.50 / kWh; Above 200 kWh @ ₹ 7.00 / kWh > > Calculate the bill and the average cost per unit. #### **Solution — Step-by-Step:** **Step 1: Apply slabs to the 350 kWh consumption:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Block 1 (first 100 kWh):} &\quad 100\ \text{kWh} \times ₹\ 4.00 = ₹\ 400 \\ \text{Block 2 (next 100 kWh):} &\quad 100\ \text{kWh} \times ₹\ 5.50 = ₹\ 550 \\ \text{Block 3 (above 200 kWh):} &\quad (350 - 200) = 150\ \text{kWh} \times ₹\ 7.00 = ₹\ 1{,}050 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Sum the energy charge components:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Energy Charge} &= ₹\ 400 + ₹\ 550 + ₹\ 1{,}050 = ₹\ 2{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Add the fixed charge:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Bill} &= F + \text{Energy Charge} \\ &= ₹\ 80 + ₹\ 2{,}000 \\ &= ₹\ 2{,}080 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Compute the average rate:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Average Rate} &= \frac{₹\ 2{,}080}{350\ \text{kWh}} = ₹\ 5.943\ /\ \text{kWh} \approx ₹\ 5.94\ /\ \text{unit} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inverted Block Rate Notice:** This is an *increasing block rate* (rate rises with consumption). It is **anti-promotional** — used by utilities to **discourage wastage** in residential DSM programs. KSEB, Tata Power Delhi use this design. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Problem 3 (kVA-Based Demand Charge) > **Problem Statement (KTU Model):** > A factory has: > - MD (kW basis) = 500 kW > - Power Factor = 0.85 lagging > - Monthly energy = 1,20,000 kWh > > Tariff: ₹ 200 / kVA of MD + ₹ 0.60 / kWh. Compute the bill. #### **Solution — Step-by-Step:** **Step 1: Convert kW demand to kVA:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{MD (kVA)} &= \frac{\text{MD (kW)}}{\text{pf}} = \frac{500}{0.85} = 588.24\ \text{kVA} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Apply the demand charge:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Demand Charge} &= ₹\ 200\ /\ \text{kVA} \times 588.24\ \text{kVA} \\ &= ₹\ 1{,}17{,}647.06 \\ &\approx ₹\ 1{,}17{,}647 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Apply the energy charge:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Energy Charge} &= ₹\ 0.60\ /\ \text{kWh} \times 1{,}20{,}000\ \text{kWh} = ₹\ 72{,}000 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Total Bill:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Total Bill} &= ₹\ 1{,}17{,}647 + ₹\ 72{,}000 = ₹\ 1{,}89{,}647 \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why Use kVA Instead of kW?** The utility's lines, transformers, and switchgear carry **apparent power (kVA)**, not real power (kW). A consumer with poor power factor (say 0.7) uses the same physical capacity as a consumer with 0.95 pf at the same kW. Charging in ₹/kVA forces the consumer to **improve pf** to lower their bill — a direct DSM tool. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Tariff (KTU POE Perspective) | Parameter | Single-Part | Two-Part | Three-Part | |-----------|:-----------:|:--------:|:----------:| | Number of consumers covered | Domestic | Small industrial, commercial | Large industrial, HT consumers | | Metering complexity | kWh meter only | kWh + MD meter | kWh + MD + block logic | | Fixed cost recovery | Loaded in unit rate | Direct | Direct | | Load factor impact | None | Penalises low LF | Penalises low LF | | DSM incentive | None | Yes (reduce MD) | Strong (reduce MD + energy) | | BPL subsidy mechanism | Block 1 at high rate | Not suitable | Ideal (lifeline block) | | Revenue predictability for utility | Low (weather-dependent) | High | Highest | ## 4.
