KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define a "two-part tariff" and state its two components. Why is it called a "Hopkinson rate"?** **Model Answer:** A two-part tariff is a method of charging electricity consumers in two parts: (i) a **fixed charge** based on the maximum demand (₹/kW or ₹/kVA) and (ii) a **variable (running) charge** based on the energy actually consumed (₹/kWh). It is called the **Hopkinson rate** because it was first proposed by **Dr. John Hopkinson in 1892** in England to equitably recover the fixed cost (capacity cost) and running cost (energy cost) of the supply. *(3 marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between a single-part tariff and a block tariff. Which one is more equitable and why?** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Single-Part Tariff | Block Tariff | |--------|-------------------|--------------| | Rate structure | Uniform single rate (₹/kWh) | Stepped (slab) rates per block | | Fixed cost recovery | Loaded into the unit rate | Separate fixed/demand charge | | Equity | Low — small consumers pay disproportionately | Higher — first block can be subsidized | | Metering | kWh meter only | kWh meter (and MD for full block tariff) | | BPL subsidy | Difficult | Easy via subsidized first block | The **block tariff is more equitable** because the marginal cost of additional units reflects the utility's incremental cost, and the lowest consumption block can be subsidized to protect economically weaker consumers. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4, 2024 Scheme]** A factory operates for **300 hours per month** with the following data: - Maximum demand = **500 kW** - Average load = **250 kW** - Power factor = **0.8 lagging** **(a)** Compute the monthly energy consumed and the load factor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Calculate the total monthly bill under the following three tariff options and identify the most economical one: **(7 marks)** 1. Flat rate: ₹ 4.00 / kWh 2. Two-part rate: ₹ 100 / kW + ₹ 0.50 / kWh 3. Three-part rate: Fixed ₹ 200 + ₹ 60 / kW + first 20,000 kWh @ ₹ 1.20 / kWh; next 30,000 kWh @ ₹ 0.90 / kWh; balance @ ₹ 0.60 / kWh. --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Step 1: Monthly energy consumed.** $$ E = \text{Average Load} \times \text{Hours} = 250\ \text{kW} \times 300\ \text{h} = 75{,}000\ \text{kWh} $$ **Step 2: Load factor.** $$ \text{LF} = \frac{\text{Avg Load}}{\text{MD}} = \frac{250}{500} = 0.5\ \text{or}\ 50\% $$ *(7 marks for both quantities correctly derived with units and interpretation.)* --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Tariff 1 — Flat Rate:** $$ \text{Bill}_1 = 4.00 \times 75{,}000 = ₹\ 3{,}00{,}000 $$ **Tariff 2 — Two-Part (Hopkinson):** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bill}_2 &= (100 \times 500) + (0.50 \times 75{,}000) \\ &= ₹\ 50{,}000 + ₹\ 37{,}500 = ₹\ 87{,}500 \end{aligned} $$ **Tariff 3 — Three-Part (Block + Demand + Fixed):** **Demand + Fixed:** $$ F + R_b \times \text{MD} = 200 + (60 \times 500) = ₹\ 200 + ₹\ 30{,}000 = ₹\ 30{,}200 $$ **Slab-wise energy charge (75,000 kWh):** - First 20,000 kWh @ ₹ 1.20 = ₹ 24,000 - Next 30,000 kWh (cumulative 50,000 kWh) @ ₹ 0.90 = ₹ 27,000 - Balance: $(75{,}000 - 50{,}000) = 25{,}000\ \text{kWh}$ @ ₹ 0.60 = ₹ 15,000 - Total energy charge = ₹ 24,000 + ₹ 27,000 + ₹ 15,000 = **₹ 66,000** **Tariff 3 — Total Bill:** $$ \text{Bill}_3 = ₹\ 30{,}200 + ₹\ 66{,}000 = ₹\ 96{,}200 $$ **Comparison Table:** | Tariff | Bill (₹) | Avg Rate (₹/kWh) | |--------|---------:|----------------:| | 1 — Flat | 3,00,000 | 4.000 | | 2 — Two-Part | 87,500 | 1.167 | | 3 — Three-Part | 96,200 | 1.283 | **Conclusion:** **Tariff 2 (Two-Part Hopkinson)** is the most economical for this consumer with LF = 0.5. *(7 marks: 2 for demand+fixed, 3 for slab energy, 1 for total, 1 for comparison + conclusion.)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 4, 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Explain the three traditional types of electrical tariffs with neat formulae and a real-world analogy for each. Mention the type of consumer each is best suited for. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A commercial complex has an MD of **150 kW**, monthly energy consumption of **36,000 kWh**, and a power factor of **0.9 lagging**. Calculate the monthly bill and average cost per kWh under: **(7 marks)** - Two-part tariff: ₹ 90 / kVA of MD + ₹ 1.20 / kWh. --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** 1. **Single-Part (Flat) Rate:** Formula: $\text{Bill} = r \times U$. Analogy: a roadside shop charging one flat price per item, no cover charge. Suited for **domestic consumers** with simple kWh metering. 2. **Two-Part (Hopkinson) Rate:** Formula: $\text{Bill} = (R_b \times \text{MD}) + (R_e \times U)$. Analogy: an AC restaurant's cover charge + per-dish rate. Suited for **industrial and commercial** consumers. 3. **Three-Part (Block) Rate:** Formula: $\text{Bill} = F + (R_b \times \text{MD}) + \sum r_i U_i$. Analogy: a buffet with slab-based pricing and reservation fee. Suited for **HT industries and bulk residential** with DSM objectives. *(7 marks: 1 per type, 1 for the consumer mapping, 1 for the unified analogy theme.)* --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — Convert MD to kVA:** $$ \text{MD (kVA)} = \frac{150\ \text{kW}}{0.9} = 166.67\ \text{kVA} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the two components:** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Demand Charge} &= ₹\ 90\ /\ \text{kVA} \times 166.67\ \text{kVA} = ₹\ 15{,}000 \\ \text{Energy Charge} &= ₹\ 1.20\ /\ \text{kWh} \times 36{,}000\ \text{kWh} = ₹\ 43{,}200 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3 — Total Bill:** $$ \text{Total Bill} = ₹\ 15{,}000 + ₹\ 43{,}200 = ₹\ 58{,}200 $$ **Step 4 — Average rate per kWh:** $$ \text{Average Rate} = \frac{₹\ 58{,}200}{36{,}000\ \text{kWh}} = ₹\ 1.617\ /\ \text{kWh} $$ *(7 marks: 2 for kVA conversion, 2 for demand, 2 for energy, 1 for total and average.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert kW → kVA** when the rate is in ₹/kVA. A 0.85 pf consumer with 500 kW must be billed on 588 kVA — losing 1–2 marks. > 2. **Mixing up "block" semantics**: A *decreasing* block rate rewards high consumption; an *increasing* block rate penalizes it. State which is being used. > 3. **MD vs Units confusion**: MD is in **kW or kVA** (power), Units are in **kWh** (energy). They are **not interchangeable**. > 4. **Slab boundary errors**: Always apply slabs **cumulatively** — the "next 20,000 kWh" means the *21st thousand to the 40th thousand*, not the *0 to 20,000th* again. > 5. **Units in final answer**: A KTU 2024 answer without units (₹, kWh, kW) loses 1 mark per sub-part as per marking scheme rubrics. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Single-Part (Flat) Rate** = `Bill = rate × Units`. Used for domestic; loaded with all fixed costs. - **Two-Part (Hopkinson) Rate** = `(₹/kW × MD) + (₹/kWh × Units)`. **John Hopkinson, 1892.** Recovers fixed cost via demand charge and running cost via energy charge. - **Three-Part Rate** = `Fixed + Demand + Slab Energy`. Three independent components. - **kVA conversion** is mandatory when tariff is in ₹/kVA: `kVA = kW / pf`. - **Load Factor** determines consumer's *favourability* under Hopkinson: **higher LF → lower average rate** (because fixed cost is spread over more units). - **Block rate design** can be *decreasing* (utility promotional) or *increasing* (DSM / conservation). - **Average rate** = `Total Bill / Total kWh` — used to compare different tariffs on a common basis. - **Demand charge** is the primary DSM tool — lowering MD directly lowers the bill. - For KTU 2024, remember the **three Ps of tariff design**: **Predictable revenue, Per-unit fairness, and Performance incentive**. - When the problem gives *hours of use* and *average load*, compute units as: `Units (kWh) = Avg Load (kW) × Hours (h)`. - The **maximum demand** in billing is usually the *highest 15-min or 30-min average* recorded in the month, **not** the instantaneous peak.
